```mermaid
    MIC["Microsyn AC"]:::leafNode

    ELEC2["Electronic Type"]:::branchNode
    OP["Op-Amp Summer DC"]:::leafNode
    PSD["Phase Sensitive Demodulator AC"]:::leafNode
    DIG["Digital XOR or Flip-Flop Detector"]:::leafNode

    ROOT --> MECH
    ROOT --> ELEC
    ROOT --> ELEC2

    MECH --> LEV
    MECH --> DIFF
    MECH --> CAM

    ELEC --> POT
    ELEC --> SYN
    ELEC --> LVDT
    ELEC --> MIC

    ELEC2 --> OP
    ELEC2 --> PSD
    ELEC2 --> DIG

    classDef rootNode fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef branchNode fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef leafNode fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#33691E,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Functional Flow: Signal Transformation Inside an Error Detector

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Stage 1: Signal Acquisition"]:::stageNode
    B["Stage 2: Reference Comparison"]:::stageNode
    C["Stage 3: Error Quantification"]:::stageNode
    D["Stage 4: Sign Determination"]:::stageNode
    E["Stage 5: Error Signal Output to Controller"]:::stageNode

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E

    classDef stageNode fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Detailed Functional Architecture of a Phase-Sensitive Demodulator (AC Error Detector)

```mermaid
graph TD
    IN_AC["AC Error Signal from CT or LVDT"]:::inNode
    REF["Reference Carrier from Local Oscillator"]:::inNode
    MIX["Multiplier or Balanced Modulator"]:::procNode
    LPF["Low Pass Filter Cutoff fc"]:::procNode
    DC_OUT["DC Error Voltage Verror"]:::outNode

    IN_AC --> MIX
    REF --> MIX
    MIX --> LPF
    LPF --> DC_OUT

    classDef inNode fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef procNode fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outNode fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

> **Why this matters:** In AC servos, the error from a synchro or LVDT is a **modulated** AC waveform. A controller cannot directly use an AC signal for DC motor drive. The **Phase-Sensitive Demodulator** "unwraps" this modulated error into a clean DC voltage whose magnitude equals the error amplitude and whose polarity (sign) indicates the direction of correction needed.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**
**Define an error detector. State its role in a closed-loop control system.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
An error detector is a device that continuously compares the **reference input (set-point)** with the **feedback signal** from the output and produces an **error signal** proportional to their difference: $e(t) = r(t) - c(t)$. 

**Role in closed-loop control:** (1 mark each point)
1. It **quantifies the deviation** between the desired and actual value.
2. It **indicates the direction** of correction through the sign of the error.
3. It **activates the controller** by providing the actuating signal that drives the actuator to reduce the error to zero.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**
**List any three types of error detectors commonly used in servomechanisms. Mention one application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark each):**

| **Error Detector** | **Application** |
|---------------------|-----------------|
| Potentiometric pair | Robotic arm position control |
| Synchro pair (CX–CT) | Radar antenna azimuth tracking |
| LVDT | Fuel injector valve position feedback |
| Op-amp summing amplifier | Process control loop in chemical plant |

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> Choose **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B.

---

#### **✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **synchro pair as an error detector** in a position servo. Derive the expression for the error voltage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **role of a phase-sensitive demodulator** as an error detector in AC servomechanisms. Why is it preferred over a simple diode rectifier? **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Synchro Pair Error Detector (7 Marks):**

A synchro pair consists of two units: a **Control Transmitter (CX)** and a **Control Transformer (CT)**, each with a single-phase rotor and a three-phase Y-connected stator. **(1 mark)**

**Working:**
- The CX rotor is supplied with a single-phase AC reference $v_r(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$. **(0.5 mark)**
- The input shaft mechanically rotates the CX rotor to angle $\theta_i$, producing three stator voltages $v_{s1}, v_{s2}, v_{s3}$ that are phase-shifted by $120^\circ$ and modulated by $\cos(\theta_i)$. **(1 mark)**
- These three stator voltages are transmitted to the CT stator via three interconnecting wires. **(0.5 mark)**
- The CT rotor is mechanically coupled to the output shaft at angle $\theta_o$. By transformer action, the CT rotor produces an AC error voltage whose envelope is proportional to $\sin(\theta_i - \theta_o)$. **(1 mark)**

**Derivation:**

Starting from the rotor excitation and using the spatial coupling integral of the three stator phases on the CT rotor:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{syn}(t) &= V_{CT,max} \cdot \sin(\theta_i - \theta_o) \cdot \sin(\omega t) \\
&= K_s \cdot \theta_e \cdot \sin(\omega t) \quad \text{(for small } \theta_e \text{)} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The DC-equivalent error (envelope detected) is $\vert e_{syn} \vert = K_s (\theta_i - \theta_o)$, which is then fed to a phase-sensitive demodulator to recover the polarity information. **(1.5 marks)**

**Part (b) — Phase-Sensitive Demodulator (PSD) (7 Marks):**

**Block Diagram:** *(See Section 4.4)*

**Role and Working:** **(3 marks)**
- The PSD receives two inputs: the **modulated AC error signal** from the CT rotor and the **reference carrier** from the same AC source that excites the CX rotor.
- A **multiplier (balanced modulator)** multiplies the two signals:

$$e_{PSD}(t) = e_{syn}(t) \cdot \sin(\omega t) = K_s \theta_e \sin^2(\omega t)$$

- Using the identity $\sin^2(\omega t) = \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2}$:

$$e_{PSD}(t) = \frac{K_s \theta_e}{2} - \frac{K_s \theta_e}{2}\cos(2\omega t)$$

- The **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** removes the $2\omega$ component, leaving a clean DC error:

$$V_{error} = \frac{K_s \theta_e}{2}$$

The **sign** of $\theta_e$ is preserved because the PSD output's polarity depends on the *phase relationship* between the two inputs. **(2 marks)**

**Why PSD over a simple diode rectifier:** A simple diode rectifier only produces the **magnitude** of the error (it always gives a positive output), losing the directional information. The PSD, however, gives a **bipolar DC output** whose sign indicates whether the output is below or above the set-point, enabling corrective action in the right direction. **(2 marks)**

---

#### **✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **potentiometric error detector** in a DC position servo. Derive the expression for the error voltage and explain how the direction of rotation is determined. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a circuit diagram, explain how an **operational amplifier configured as a difference amplifier** acts as an error detector. Derive the output voltage expression. State any two advantages of electronic error detectors. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Potentiometric Error Detector (7 Marks):**

**Construction:** Two identical linear potentiometers $P_1$ and $P_2$ are mounted on a common base. $P_1$'s wiper is mechanically coupled to the **input shaft** (reference), and $P_2$'s wiper is coupled to the **output shaft** (load). Both pots are supplied with the same DC voltage $V_s$, but with **opposite polarity** so that their wipers produce voltages of opposite sign for the same direction of rotation. **(2 marks)**

**Working:** As the input shaft rotates by $\theta_i$, the wiper of $P_1$ produces $V_r = K_p \theta_i$. Simultaneously, the output shaft produces $V_c = K_p \theta_o$ with opposite polarity. The wiper terminals are connected in series opposition, so the net error voltage at the junction is:

$$e_{pot} = V_r - V_c = K_p(\theta_i - \theta_o) \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

**Direction Determination:** When $\theta_i > \theta_o$, the error is positive, signalling that the motor must rotate in a direction that increases $\theta_o$. When $\theta_i < \theta_o$, the error is negative, and the controller reverses the motor. **(1 mark)**

**Practical Issues:** The output has a **discontinuous jump** at the point where the wiper crosses the supply-end terminal (the "dead zone"), causing a small but noticeable glitch in the servo response. This is mitigated by using a "make-before-break" or by offsetting the two pots by $180^\circ$. **(1.5 marks)**

**Numerical Example:** If $V_s = 10$ V, $\theta_{max} = 5\pi$ rad (2.5 turns), and $\theta_i - \theta_o = 0.1$ rad, then $K_p = 10/5\pi = 0.637$ V/rad, and $e_{pot} = 0.0637$ V. **(1 mark)**

---

**Part (b) — Op-Amp Difference Amplifier as Error Detector (7 Marks):**

**Circuit Description:** A standard difference amplifier uses one op-amp with four precisely matched resistors: $R_1, R_2$ on the input side and $R_f, R_g$ on the feedback side. The reference input $V_r$ is applied to the non-inverting terminal via $R_1$ (with $R_g$ to ground), and the feedback $V_c$ is applied to the inverting terminal via $R_2$ (with $R_f$ as feedback). **(2 marks)**

**Derivation:** Apply superposition — the output is the sum of the inverting amplifier response and the non-inverting amplifier response.

- **Inverting contribution** (with non-inverting input grounded):

$$V_{out,inv} = -\frac{R_f}{R_2} V_c$$

- **Non-inverting contribution:** Voltage at non-inverting node:

$$V^+ = V_r \cdot \frac{R_g}{R_1 + R_g}$$

$$V_{out,non-inv} = V^+ \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_2}\right) = V_r \cdot \frac{R_g}{R_1 + R_g} \cdot \frac{R_2 + R_f}{R_2}$$

- **Total output** (using $R_1 = R_2$ and $R_f = R_g$):

$$V_{out} = \frac{R_f}{R_1}(V_r - V_c) = e(t) \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

This gives a **true linear subtraction** with adjustable gain by varying $R_f/R_1$.

**Advantages of Electronic Error Detectors:** **(2 marks)**
1. **High input impedance** and **low output impedance** — no loading effect on the source or downstream stages.
2. **Infinite resolution** (no wire-wound quantization as in potentiometers) and very high accuracy (resistor matching to 0.01%).
3. **Fast dynamic response** — bandwidth up to MHz range, suitable for high-speed servos.
4. **Compact and inexpensive** — single IC package replaces bulky mechanical assemblies.

**Disadvantages:** Sensitive to **noise**, requires **stable DC power supply**, and resistor matching is critical for high CMRR. **(1 mark for any two disadvantages)**

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the sign convention** — Many students write $e(t) = c(t) - r(t)$ instead of $r(t) - c(t)$. The KTU-marked scheme follows the standard convention $e = r - c$. Marks deducted: **0.5 to 1 per occurrence**.
> 
> 2. **Confusing "error detector" with "controller"** — The error detector only **produces** the error signal; it does **not** take corrective action. Marks deducted: **1–2 marks** for blurring this distinction.
> 
> 3. **Missing the carrier-signal explanation for AC detectors** — In synchro/LVDT problems, students often write only the DC envelope and forget to mention the $\sin(\omega t)$ carrier term. Marks deducted: **1 mark**.
> 
> 4. **Not stating the assumption $\theta_e$ is small** — When linearising $\sin\theta_e \approx \theta_e$, you **must** state this assumption explicitly. Marks deducted: **0.5 mark**.
> 
> 5. **Skipping units in derivations** — KTU examiners deduct **0.5 mark per missing unit** in final answers. Always write $K_p = 0.637$ **V/rad**, not just $0.637$.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — "Role of Error Detectors"**
> 
> ✅ Error detector is the **comparing element** in a closed-loop system.
> 
> ✅ Fundamental equation: $e(t) = r(t) - c(t)$.
> 
> ✅ It performs three functions: **comparison, error generation, sign indication**.
> 
> ✅ **Mechanical types:** lever, differential gear, cam.
> 
> ✅ **Electrical types:** potentiometric (DC), synchro (AC), LVDT (AC), microsyn (AC).
> 
> ✅ **Electronic types:** op-amp summer/difference amplifier (DC), phase-sensitive demodulator (AC).
> 
> ✅ **Potentiometric error:** $e_{pot} = K_p (\theta_i - \theta_o)$, linear, but suffers from wiper-discontinuity noise.
> 
> ✅ **Synchro error:** $e_{syn}(t) = K_s \sin(\theta_i - \theta_o) \cdot \sin(\omega t)$, sinusoidal, AC-modulated.
> 
> ✅ **LVDT error:** $e_{lvdt} = K_L \cdot \Delta x$, linear over a wide range, used for displacement.
> 
> ✅ **Op-amp error:** $V_{out} = (R_f/R_1)(V_r - V_c)$, requires matched resistors for high CMRR.
> 
> ✅ **Phase-Sensitive Demodulator (PSD)** is needed to convert AC carrier-modulated errors into bipolar DC errors while **preserving polarity**.
> 
> ✅ AC servos need a PSD because controllers (e.g., DC motors) cannot accept AC input directly.
> 
> ✅ **Sensitivity** $K$ of any error detector = (output voltage) / (input error magnitude) — units depend on input (V/rad, V/mm, V/V).
> 
> ✅ Error detector is the **first block** in the forward path of the closed-loop block diagram, **before** the controller.
> 
> ✅ **Dead zone** and **resolution** are the two key performance metrics of mechanical error detectors.
> 
> ✅ **Without the error detector, feedback control is impossible** — this is the central role.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Block Diagram for a Control System with Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT205 - BASIC OF INSTRUMENTATION ENGINEERING (INSTRUMENT AND CONTROL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Block Diagram for a Control System with Examples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Block Diagram** is a graphical, high-level pictorial representation of a control system in which the functional relationship between the input, the output, and the system components is depicted using interconnected **functional blocks**, **summing points**, and **take-off points**. Each block represents a subsystem (or component) described mathematically by its **Transfer Function** $G(s)$, while the arrows represent the unidirectional flow of signals and the summing points implement algebraic operations (addition or subtraction) on multiple signals.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, the block diagram is the *primary* analytical tool used to derive the overall input–output relationship (Closed-Loop Transfer Function, CLTF) of a feedback control system without resorting to mesh or nodal electrical analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A block diagram shows only the *functional relationship* — it does **not** show the physical wiring, internal circuitry, or energy flow of the system. It is a *signal-flow* diagram.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Cruise Control of a Car

Imagine you are driving a car and want to maintain a constant speed of **60 km/h** on a highway. Your brain performs the following control actions every second:

1. **Reference Input (R):** The desired speed = 60 km/h.
2. **Summing Point (Comparator):** Your brain subtracts the actual speed from the desired speed → **Error** $e(t) = R - C$.
3. **Controller (Block $G_c$):** Your foot presses the accelerator based on the error.
4. **Plant (Block $G_p$):** The car engine produces the actual speed.
5. **Feedback Sensor (Block $H$):** The speedometer measures actual speed and sends it back.
6. **Output (C):** The actual car speed.

If the slope of the road changes (disturbance), the speedometer (feedback) reports the new speed, your brain (summing point) recalculates the error, and your foot adjusts — automatically! This entire feedback chain **is** a block diagram.

### 1.3 Fundamental Building Elements of a Block Diagram

> [!NOTE]
> A KTU examiner may ask the question *"List the basic elements of a block diagram."* Memorize these four elements verbatim:

| # | Element | Symbol | Function |
|---|---------|--------|----------|
| 1 | **Functional Block** | Rectangle with $G(s)$ | Represents the transfer function (input-output relationship) of a subsystem. |
| 2 | **Summing Point (Comparator)** | Circle with $\Sigma$ | Adds or subtracts two or more signals algebraically. |
| 3 | **Take-off Point (Branch Point)** | Solid dot | Splits a single signal into two or more parallel paths without modification. |
| 4 | **Signal Flow Arrow** | Unidirectional arrow | Indicates the direction in which the signal variable flows. |

### 1.4 The Concept of a "Block" in Greater Depth

A block is essentially an *operator* — a mathematical function (in the Laplace domain, $G(s)$) that maps an input signal $X(s)$ to an output signal $Y(s)$ according to:

$$Y(s) = G(s) \cdot X(s)$$

Here, $G(s)$ is a **rational function** of $s$ (the complex Laplace variable), typically of the form:

$$G(s) = \frac{N(s)}{D(s)} = \frac{b_m s^m + b_{m-1} s^{m-1} + \cdots + b_1 s + b_0}{a_n s^n + a_{n-1} s^{n-1} + \cdots + a_1 s + a_0}$$

where $N(s)$ is the numerator polynomial and $D(s)$ is the characteristic denominator polynomial.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The block **assumes linearity and time-invariance (LTI)**. Non-linear effects (saturation, dead-zone, hysteresis) are typically linearized first, **then** represented as a block.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Canonical Forms of a Block Diagram

There are two principal canonical configurations that form the *backbone* of every KTU-level block diagram problem:

**A. Open-Loop (Feed-Forward) System**
The output has **no influence** on the input.

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

**B. Closed-Loop (Feedback) System — Single-Loop (Canonical)**
The output is sensed and subtracted (or added) from the reference.

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)} \quad \text{(Negative Feedback)}$$

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 - G(s) H(s)} \quad \text{(Positive Feedback)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The term $1 + G(s)H(s)$ is called the **Return Difference**; the term $G(s)H(s)$ is called the **Loop Transfer Function** (LTF). The roots of $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ give the **characteristic equation** of the closed-loop system.

### 2.2 Rules for Interconnection of Blocks

When multiple blocks are present, they can be interconnected in three fundamental ways:

#### Rule 1 — Cascade (Series) Connection
Blocks placed one after another with no loading effect. The overall transfer function is the **product** of the individual transfer functions.

$$G_{eq}(s) = G_1(s) \cdot G_2(s) \cdot G_3(s) \cdots G_n(s)$$

#### Rule 2 — Parallel Connection
Blocks receiving the same input and their outputs are summed (or differenced) at a summing point. The overall transfer function is the **algebraic sum** of the individual transfer functions.

$$G_{eq}(s) = G_1(s) \pm G_2(s) \pm G_3(s) \cdots \pm G_n(s)$$

#### Rule 3 — Feedback Connection
A portion of the output is returned to the input through a feedback block $H(s)$.

$$G_{eq}(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 \pm G(s) H(s)}$$

### 2.3 Block Diagram Reduction — The 10 Standard Rules

When a block diagram contains *cascaded* summers, take-off points, and feedback loops, we apply **algebraic transformation rules** to progressively simplify it into a single canonical block. The 10 most-tested KTU rules are:

| Rule # | Manipulation | Formula / Effect |
|--------|--------------|-----------------|
| R1 | Combine cascade blocks | $G = G_1 G_2$ |
| R2 | Combine parallel blocks | $G = G_1 \pm G_2$ |
| R3 | Combine feedback loop | $G_{cl} = G/(1 \pm GH)$ |
| R4 | Move summing point **beyond** a block | $G(R - X) = GR - GX$ |
| R5 | Move summing point **ahead of** a block | $G(R - X) = G(R - X/G)$ |
| R6 | Move take-off point **beyond** a block | Add compensating block $1/G$ |
| R7 | Move take-off point **ahead of** a block | Add compensating block $G$ |
| R8 | Move block **from** feedback path to forward path | Multiply by $1/G$ |
| R9 | Move block **into** feedback path from forward path | Multiply by $G$ |
| R10 | Exchange two adjacent summing points | No change in CLTF |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** When moving a block across a take-off point, students forget the **compensating block**. This single error invalidates the entire reduction.

### 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Application |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | Closed-Loop TF (Negative FB) | $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | Servo position control, motor speed control |
| 2 | Closed-Loop TF (Positive FB) | $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 - G(s)H(s)}$ | Schmitt trigger, regenerative feedback |
| 3 | Loop Transfer Function (LTF) | $L(s) = G(s)H(s)$ | Stability analysis via Nyquist / Bode |
| 4 | Characteristic Equation | $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ | Root locus, pole locations |
| 5 | Error Transfer Function | $\dfrac{E(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | Steady-state error computation |
| 6 | Sensitivity | $S_G^T = \dfrac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | Robustness analysis |
| 7 | Cascade Equivalent | $G_{eq} = G_1 G_2 G_3$ | Series subsystems |
| 8 | Parallel Equivalent | $G_{eq} = G_1 + G_2$ | Actuator summation |
| 9 | Unity Feedback Reduction | $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$ | When $H(s) = 1$ |
| 10 | Feed-Forward Summation | $G_{eq} = G_1 + G_2$ | Disturbance rejection paths |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All transfer functions are functions of the **complex Laplace variable** $s = \sigma + j\omega$. Replace $s$ with $j\omega$ for frequency response analysis.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Block diagrams are indispensable in:

- **Aerospace:** Autopilot control loops (pitch, roll, yaw).
- **Process Industries:** Temperature, pressure, flow, and level control in petrochemical plants.
- **Robotics:** Joint-angle position control in robotic arms.
- **Automotive:** Anti-lock Braking Systems (ABS), Electronic Stability Control (ESC).
- **Biomedical:** Drug-infusion pumps, artificial pancreas (insulin delivery).
- **Power Systems:** Automatic Voltage Regulators (AVR) for synchronous generators.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Hover-stabilization in DSLR cameras and drones.

The **single most important engineering reason** for using block diagrams is the **modular analysis** they enable — each block can be designed, simulated, and tested independently before being integrated into the full system.

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — The Canonical Closed-Loop Transfer Function

Consider the negative-feedback system with forward path $G(s)$ and feedback path $H(s)$.

**Step 1:** Identify the error signal at the summing point.

$$E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$$

Here $B(s)$ is the feedback signal, and $R(s)$ is the reference input. The minus sign indicates negative feedback.

**Step 2:** Identify the feedback signal.

$$B(s) = H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

**Step 3:** Identify the output signal through the forward path.

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)$$

**Step 4:** Substitute (Step 1) into (Step 3).

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot \left[ R(s) - B(s) \right] = G(s) \cdot R(s) - G(s) \cdot B(s)$$

**Step 5:** Substitute (Step 2) into the result of (Step 4).

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s) - G(s) \cdot H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

**Step 6:** Collect $C(s)$ terms on the left-hand side.

$$C(s) + G(s) H(s) \cdot C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

**Step 7:** Factor out $C(s)$.

$$C(s) \cdot \left[ 1 + G(s) H(s) \right] = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

**Step 8:** Solve for the ratio $C(s)/R(s)$.

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}$$

This is the **Closed-Loop Transfer Function (CLTF)** for negative feedback.

> [!NOTE]
> For *positive* feedback, the sign of $H(s) C(s)$ at the summing point becomes *positive*, and the denominator becomes $1 - G(s)H(s)$.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Reduction of a Multi-Loop Block Diagram

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (Typical KTU 14-mark pattern):**
> Reduce the following block diagram to a single block representing the closed-loop transfer function $C(s)/R(s)$:
>
> - Forward path: $R(s) \to [\, \Sigma_1 \,] \xrightarrow{G_1} \to [\, \Sigma_2 \,] \xrightarrow{G_2} \xrightarrow{G_3} C(s)$
> - Inner feedback: $H_1$ from output of $G_2$ back to $\Sigma_1$ (negative)
> - Outer feedback: $H_2$ from output $C(s)$ back to $\Sigma_2$ (negative)
> - Feed-forward: $G_4$ from input of $G_1$ to $\Sigma_2$ (positive)

#### STEP 1 — Identify the inner loop

The inner loop is formed by $G_2$ in the forward path and $H_1$ in the feedback path. Using the canonical feedback formula:

$$G_{2,eq} = \frac{G_2}{1 + G_2 H_1}$$

#### STEP 2 — Replace $G_2$ with $G_{2,eq}$ in the diagram

The block diagram now reduces to:

$$R(s) \to \Sigma_1 \xrightarrow{G_1} \to \Sigma_2 \xrightarrow{G_{2,eq}} \xrightarrow{G_3} C(s)$$

with:
- Outer feedback $H_2$ from $C(s)$ to $\Sigma_2$ (negative)
- Feed-forward $G_4$ from input of $G_1$ to $\Sigma_2$ (positive)

#### STEP 3 — Move the take-off point to allow feed-forward combination

The feed-forward $G_4$ originates *before* $G_1$ but needs to combine with the signal *after* $G_1$ at $\Sigma_2$. We move the take-off point *beyond* $G_1$ and insert a compensating block of $1/G_1$ in the feed-forward path.

This is equivalent to keeping the same signal at the take-off point.

The signal at the input to $G_1$ (after summing point $\Sigma_1$) is $E_1(s)$. Moving the take-off point *beyond* $G_1$ gives $G_1 \cdot E_1(s)$. To preserve equality at $\Sigma_2$, the feed-forward block becomes:

$$G_4' = \frac{G_4}{G_1}$$

#### STEP 4 — Combine the parallel paths

At $\Sigma_2$, two signals now arrive:
- From $G_1 \cdot G_{2,eq}$ (forward)
- From $G_4' \cdot G_{2,eq}$ (feed-forward)

The combined signal entering $G_3$ is:

$$\left( G_1 + \frac{G_4}{G_1} \right) \cdot G_{2,eq} = \frac{G_1^2 + G_4}{G_1} \cdot G_{2,eq}$$

#### STEP 5 — Substitute $G_{2,eq}$

$$G_{eq, forward} = \frac{G_1^2 + G_4}{G_1} \cdot \frac{G_2}{1 + G_2 H_1} = \frac{G_2 (G_1^2 + G_4)}{G_1 (1 + G_2 H_1)}$$

#### STEP 6 — Apply the outer feedback formula

The forward path is $G_{eq, forward}$ and the feedback path is $H_2$. Applying the closed-loop reduction rule:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G_{eq, forward}}{1 + G_{eq, forward} \cdot H_2}$$

**Step 6a:** Compute the product $G_{eq, forward} \cdot H_2$.

$$G_{eq, forward} \cdot H_2 = \frac{G_2 (G_1^2 + G_4) \cdot H_2}{G_1 (1 + G_2 H_1)}$$

**Step 6b:** Form the denominator.

$$1 + G_{eq, forward} \cdot H_2 = 1 + \frac{G_2 (G_1^2 + G_4) H_2}{G_1 (1 + G_2 H_1)}$$

**Step 6c:** Bring to a common denominator.

$$1 + G_{eq, forward} \cdot H_2 = \frac{G_1 (1 + G_2 H_1) + G_2 (G_1^2 + G_4) H_2}{G_1 (1 + G_2 H_1)}$$

**Step 6d:** Expand the numerator.

$$G_1 + G_1 G_2 H_1 + G_2 G_1^2 H_2 + G_2 G_4 H_2$$

#### STEP 7 — Final Result

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{ \dfrac{G_2 (G_1^2 + G_4)}{G_1 (1 + G_2 H_1)} }{ \dfrac{G_1 + G_1 G_2 H_1 + G_1^2 G_2 H_2 + G_2 G_4 H_2}{G_1 (1 + G_2 H_1)} }$$

The denominator $G_1 (1 + G_2 H_1)$ cancels:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G_2 (G_1^2 + G_4)}{G_1 + G_1 G_2 H_1 + G_1^2 G_2 H_2 + G_2 G_4 H_2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the standard final form. Further simplification is possible by collecting like terms in the denominator if the student wishes, but the above expression is sufficient for a full 14-mark KTU answer.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Block Diagram Reduction of Common Topologies

| Topology | Forward Path | Feedback Path | Equivalent Block |
|----------|--------------|---------------|------------------|
| Single loop | $G$ | $H$ | $G / (1 + GH)$ |
| Cascade | $G_1, G_2$ | — | $G_1 G_2$ |
| Parallel (+) | $G_1, G_2$ | — | $G_1 + G_2$ |
| Inner + Outer loop | $G_1$ outer, $G_2$ inner, $H_1$ inner, $H_2$ outer | — | $\dfrac{G_1 G_2}{1 + G_2 H_1 + G_1 G_2 H_2}$ |
| Feed-forward | $G_1$ main, $G_f$ parallel feed-forward, $G_2$ post | — | $G_2 (G_1 + G_f)$ |
| Disturbance Rejection | $G_1$, disturbance $D$ at summing point after $G_1$, $G_2$ | $H$ | $\dfrac{C}{R} = \dfrac{G_1 G_2}{1 + G_1 G_2 H}$ |

### 3.4 Worked Example — Armature-Controlled DC Motor (Application)

The armature-controlled DC motor is one of the **most-tested KTU applications** for block diagram reduction. Its block diagram consists of three cascaded blocks:

**Block 1 — Armature Circuit (Electrical Subsystem):**
$$G_1(s) = \frac{1}{L_a s + R_a}$$
where $L_a$ is armature inductance (**Henry, H**) and $R_a$ is armature resistance (**Ohm, $\Omega$**).

**Block 2 — Torque Production (Electromechanical Coupling):**
$$G_2(s) = K_t$$
where $K_t$ is the motor torque constant (**N·m/A**).

**Block 3 — Mechanical Load:**
$$G_3(s) = \frac{1}{J s + B}$$
where $J$ is moment of inertia (**kg·m²**) and $B$ is viscous friction coefficient (**N·m·s/rad**).

**Feedback Block — Back-EMF Constant:**
$$H(s) = K_b$$
where $K_b$ is the back-EMF constant (**V·s/rad**).

**Overall Open-Loop Transfer Function:**

$$G_{ol}(s) = G_1(s) \cdot G_2(s) \cdot G_3(s) = \frac{K_t}{(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B)}$$

**Overall Closed-Loop Transfer Function (Velocity):**

$$\frac{\omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + K_t K_b}$$

This is a **second-order system** whose characteristic equation $D(s) = 0$ determines the dynamic response (damping ratio $\zeta$, natural frequency $\omega_n$) of the motor speed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The product $K_t K_b$ in the denominator is the *stabilizing effect* of the back-EMF feedback. Without it, the motor speed would run away under any applied voltage.

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Canonical Negative-Feedback System

```mermaid
graph LR
    rin[R_s] --> sum1{sum1}
    sum1 -->|e_s| gainA[G_s]
    gainA --> cout[C_s]
    cout --> outpoint((out_point))
    outpoint --> fbblock[H_s]
    fbblock --> sum1
    sum1 -.->|negative| sum1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The reference input $R(s)$ enters the summing point `sum1`.
- The summing point produces the error signal $e(s) = R(s) - B(s)$.
- $e(s)$ passes through the forward path block $G(s)$.
- The output $C(s)$ is sensed at the take-off point `out_point`.
- A fraction $H(s)$ of $C(s)$ is fed back and subtracted at the summing point.

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Three-Block System with Multiple Feedbacks

```mermaid
graph LR
    rin[R_s] --> sum1{sum1_inner}
    sum1_inner -->|after_sum1| gainA[G1_s]
    gainA -->|after_g1| takeoff1((takeoff1))
    takeoff1 --> sum2{sum2_outer}
    takeoff1 --> feedfwd[G4_s]
    feedfwd --> sum2
    sum2 --> gainB[G2_s]
    gainB --> sum3{sum3_innerFB}
    sum3 --> gainC[G3_s]
    gainC --> cout[C_s]
    cout --> outp((out_p))
    outp --> fbouter[H2_s]
    fbouter --> sum2
    gainB --> fbInner((takeoffInner))
    fbInner --> fbInner1[H1_s]
    fbInner1 --> sum1_inner
    sum3 --> fbin2((takeoff2))
    fbin2 --> fbin2b[H1b_s]
    fbin2b --> sum1_inner
```

**Explanation of signals:**
- `R_s` is the reference input.
- `sum1_inner` is the first summing point where $H_1$ feedback enters.
- `G1_s`, `G2_s`, `G3_s` are the three forward-path blocks.
- `G4_s` is the feed-forward block.
- `H1_s` and `H1b_s` represent the inner feedback.
- `H2_s` is the outer feedback from the output.
- `sum2_outer` is the second summing point where the feed-forward and outer feedback combine.
- `sum3_innerFB` is the third summing point for any disturbance or input to $G_3$.

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram Reduction Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    start([Complex Multi_Loop Diagram]) --> step1{Step1_Identify_Inner_Feedback_Loop}
    step1 -->|Apply| r1[R3_feedback_rule]
    r1 --> step2{Step2_Replace_Inner_Loop_with_Equivalent_Block}
    step2 --> step3{Step3_Move_Takeoff_or_Summing_Point_If_Needed}
    step3 -->|If needed| r2[R4_R5_R6_R7_move_rules]
    r2 --> step4{Step4_Combine_Parallel_or_Cascade_Blocks}
    step3 -->|No move needed| step4
    step4 --> r3[R1_R2_combine_rules]
    r3 --> step5{Step5_Identify_Outer_Feedback_Loop}
    step5 -->|Apply| r4[R3_feedback_rule_again]
    r4 --> final([Single_Block_T_s])
```

**Interpretation:**
- **Step 1:** Identify and reduce the *innermost* feedback loop first.
- **Step 2:** Replace it with its equivalent single block.
- **Step 3:** Use move rules to eliminate *crossing signals* (summing/takeoff point conflicts).
- **Step 4:** Combine cascade or parallel blocks.
- **Step 5:** Identify and reduce the *outer* feedback loop.
- **Final:** Obtain a single canonical block.

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Armature-Controlled DC Motor

```mermaid
graph LR
    vin[V_a_s] --> sum1{M_s}
    sum1 --> gainA[1_over_La_s_plus_Ra]
    gainA --> gainB[K_t]
    gainB --> gainC[1_over_Js_plus_B]
    gainC --> omegaout[omega_s]
    omegaout --> outp((takeoff))
    outp --> fbb[K_b]
    fbb --> sum1
    sum1 -.->|negative| sum1
```

**Components in sequence:**
1. `1_over_La_s_plus_Ra` — Armature circuit transfer function.
2. `K_t` — Torque constant block.
3. `1_over_Js_plus_B` — Mechanical load transfer function.
4. `K_b` — Back-EMF feedback.

The output is the angular velocity $\omega(s)$, and the input is the applied armature voltage $V_a(s)$.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**Define a block diagram. List its basic elements with their functions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A block diagram is a graphical representation of a control system in which the input-output relationship of each component is represented by a block containing its transfer function, and the signals flow between the blocks via unidirectional arrows.

**Basic Elements (1½ marks):**

1. **Functional Block:** A rectangle labelled with a transfer function $G(s)$ showing the input-output relationship of a subsystem.
2. **Summing Point:** A circle with a $\Sigma$ sign where two or more signals are added or subtracted.
3. **Take-off Point (Branch Point):** A solid dot from which a signal branches into multiple parallel paths without any change in value.
4. **Signal Flow Arrow:** A unidirectional arrow indicating the direction of signal flow.

**Function (1½ marks):**
The block diagram visually shows the *signal flow*, *causality*, and *interconnection* of subsystems, and enables the engineer to derive the overall closed-loop transfer function through algebraic block diagram reduction.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sessional Exam II]**

**Derive the closed-loop transfer function of a negative feedback control system with forward path transfer function $G(s)$ and feedback path transfer function $H(s)$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Let $R(s)$ be the reference input, $C(s)$ the output, $E(s)$ the error, and $B(s)$ the feedback signal.

**Error signal (1 mark):**
$$E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$$

**Feedback signal (½ mark):**
$$B(s) = H(s) C(s)$$

**Output (½ mark):**
$$C(s) = G(s) E(s)$$

Substituting and solving:
$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently forget to **square the inner-loop gain** when the inner loop has unity feedback with a nested block. Always verify the inner-loop reduction first.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]

**Q. (a)** Derive the closed-loop transfer function $C(s)/R(s)$ of a negative feedback system with forward path transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{K(s+2)}{s(s+1)(s+3)}$ and feedback path $H(s) = \dfrac{1}{s+4}$. Also determine the characteristic equation and the order of the system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A block diagram has the following structure:
- $R(s) \to \Sigma_1 \to G_1 \to \Sigma_2 \to G_2 \to \Sigma_3 \to G_3 \to C(s)$
- Feedback $H_1$ from output of $G_2$ to $\Sigma_1$
- Feedback $H_2$ from output of $G_3$ to $\Sigma_2$
- Feed-forward $G_4$ from output of $G_1$ to $\Sigma_3$

Reduce this block diagram step-by-step to a single block. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

The closed-loop transfer function is:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}$$

**Step 1 — Compute $G(s) H(s)$:** **(1 mark)**

$$G(s) H(s) = \frac{K(s+2)}{s(s+1)(s+3)} \cdot \frac{1}{s+4} = \frac{K(s+2)}{s(s+1)(s+3)(s+4)}$$

**Step 2 — Form the CLTF:** **(2 marks)**

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{\dfrac{K(s+2)}{s(s+1)(s+3)}}{1 + \dfrac{K(s+2)}{s(s+1)(s+3)(s+4)}}$$

**Step 3 — Common denominator:** **(2 marks)**

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{K(s+2)(s+4)}{s(s+1)(s+3)(s+4) + K(s+2)}$$

**Step 4 — Characteristic equation:** **(1 mark)**

$$s(s+1)(s+3)(s+4) + K(s+2) = 0$$

Expanding:
$$(s^2 + s)(s^2 + 7s + 12) + K(s+2) = 0$$
$$s^4 + 7s^3 + 12s^2 + s^3 + 7s^2 + 12s + K(s+2) = 0$$
$$s^4 + 8s^3 + 19s^2 + (12 + K)s + 2K = 0$$

**Step 5 — Order of the system:** **(1 mark)**

The highest power of $s$ in the characteristic equation is **4**, so the system is a **4th-order** system.

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Identify the inner loop** formed by $G_2$ in forward path and $H_1$ in feedback path: **(2 marks)**

$$G_{2,eq} = \frac{G_2}{1 + G_2 H_1}$$

**Step 2 — The reduced diagram** now has:
- $R \to \Sigma_1 \to G_1 \to \Sigma_2 \to G_{2,eq} \to \Sigma_3 \to G_3 \to C$
- Feedback $H_2$ from $C$ to $\Sigma_2$
- Feed-forward $G_4$ from output of $G_1$ to $\Sigma_3$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Move the take-off point** at output of $G_1$ to *beyond* $G_1$ (already is), so the feed-forward signal is the same. Combine the parallel paths $G_1$ and $G_4$ at $\Sigma_3$: **(2 marks)**

$$G_{1,eq} = G_1 + G_4 \quad \text{(Parallel combination)}$$

**Step 4 — The diagram now** has:
- $R \to \Sigma_1 \to G_{1,eq} \to G_{2,eq} \to \Sigma_3 \to G_3 \to C$
- Outer feedback $H_2$ from $C$ to $\Sigma_3$

**Step 5 — Combine the cascade:** **(1 mark)**

$$G_{cascade} = G_{1,eq} \cdot G_{2,eq} \cdot G_3 = (G_1 + G_4) \cdot \frac{G_2}{1 + G_2 H_1} \cdot G_3$$

$$G_{cascade} = \frac{G_3 G_2 (G_1 + G_4)}{1 + G_2 H_1}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the closed-loop feedback rule with $H_2$:** **(1 mark)**

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G_{cascade}}{1 + G_{cascade} H_2}$$

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{ \dfrac{G_3 G_2 (G_1 + G_4)}{1 + G_2 H_1} }{ 1 + \dfrac{G_3 G_2 (G_1 + G_4) H_2}{1 + G_2 H_1} }$$

**Step 7 — Simplify:** **(0 marks — final expression)**

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G_2 G_3 (G_1 + G_4)}{1 + G_2 H_1 + G_2 G_3 (G_1 + G_4) H_2}$$

---

#### Question B — [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**Q. (a)** Explain the procedure to reduce a complex block diagram into a simplified single block form. List any 5 reduction rules. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A unity feedback system has a forward path transfer function:

$$G(s) = \frac{10(s+2)}{s^2(s+5)(s+10)}$$

Determine: (i) The closed-loop transfer function. (ii) The characteristic equation. (iii) The order of the system. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Procedure to reduce a complex block diagram:** **(3 marks)**

1. **Combine cascade blocks** — Multiply all blocks in series into a single equivalent block.
2. **Combine parallel blocks** — Sum (or subtract) all parallel-path blocks at a summing point.
3. **Reduce inner feedback loops first** — Start from the innermost loop and work outward.
4. **Move summing points or take-off points** — If a summing point is *inside* a feedback loop and the take-off point is *outside*, shift one of them to align with the other (using the compensating block rule).
5. **Repeat steps 1–4** until only a single forward path and a single feedback path remain.
6. **Apply the canonical closed-loop formula** to obtain $C(s)/R(s) = G/(1+GH)$.

**Five Reduction Rules:** **(4 marks — 0.8 each)**

1. **Cascade Rule:** $G = G_1 G_2 G_3$
2. **Parallel Rule:** $G = G_1 + G_2$
3. **Feedback Rule:** $G_{cl} = G/(1+GH)$
4. **Summing-Point Exchange Rule:** Two adjacent summing points can be interchanged without altering the output.
5. **Take-off Point Movement Rule:** A take-off point can be moved ahead of a block by introducing a compensating block of $1/G$ in the branching path.

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Given:** $H(s) = 1$ (unity feedback) and $G(s) = \dfrac{10(s+2)}{s^2(s+5)(s+10)}$

**(i) Closed-Loop Transfer Function:** **(3 marks)**

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)} = \frac{\dfrac{10(s+2)}{s^2(s+5)(s+10)}}{1 + \dfrac{10(s+2)}{s^2(s+5)(s+10)}}$$

$$= \frac{10(s+2)}{s^2(s+5)(s+10) + 10(s+2)}$$

**(ii) Characteristic Equation:** **(2 marks)**

$$s^2(s+5)(s+10) + 10(s+2) = 0$$

Expanding:
$$(s^2 + 5s)(s+10) + 10s + 20 = 0$$
$$s^3 + 10s^2 + 5s^2 + 50s + 10s + 20 = 0$$
$$s^3 + 15s^2 + 60s + 20 = 0$$

**(iii) Order of the System:** **(2 marks)**

The highest power of $s$ in the characteristic equation is **3**, hence the system is a **3rd-order** system.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting parentheses** in the canonical formula — many students write $1 + G(s)H(s)$ as $1 + G(s) \cdot H(s)$ and lose a mark.
> 2. **Sign confusion in feedback:** The sign in the denominator is **+** for *negative* feedback and **−** for *positive* feedback. Mixing them is the #1 reason for full-mark failure.
> 3. **Not expanding the characteristic equation** when asked — partial marks are awarded only for full expansion.
> 4. **Skipping the "move compensating block" step** — examiners explicitly look for this in the 14-mark question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the KTU 2024 Exam**

- A **block diagram** is a *signal-flow* representation; it does not depict physical wiring.
- The **four basic elements** are: *Block, Summing Point, Take-off Point, Arrow.*
- The **canonical closed-loop transfer function** for negative feedback is $T(s) = G/(1+GH)$; for positive feedback it is $T(s) = G/(1-GH)$.
- The **denominator** $1+G(s)H(s)$ is called the *Return Difference*; setting it to zero gives the **characteristic equation** which determines **stability**.
- The **three canonical interconnections** are: **Cascade** (product), **Parallel** (sum), **Feedback** (canonical CLTF formula).
- **Block diagram reduction rules:** Always reduce *innermost* loop first, then move outward.
- **Compensating blocks** are mandatory when moving a *take-off point* or *summing point* across a block — without them, the CLTF is wrong.
- The **armature-controlled DC motor** is a 2nd-order system with three cascaded blocks and a back-EMF feedback $K_b$.
- **Order of the system** = highest power of $s$ in the *characteristic equation*, **not** in the open-loop transfer function.
- **Unity feedback** means $H(s) = 1$; the CLTF simplifies to $G/(1+G)$.
- **Sensitivity to parameter variation** is reduced by the factor $1/(1+GH)$ — this is the *engineering reason* for using feedback.
- **Block diagrams ≠ Signal Flow Graphs (SFG):** SFG uses *nodes* for signals and *branches* for transfer functions, while block diagrams use *rectangular blocks*.
- The most-tested **KTU application** is the *armature-controlled DC motor* — memorize its block structure and CLTF.
- Always **label the axes** of any graphical response (e.g., step response, Bode plot) and mention the **time-domain** vs **frequency-domain** context.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement System Performance: Static Characteristics of Instruments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT205 - BASIC OF INSTRUMENTATION ENGINEERING (INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement System Performance: Static Characteristics of Instruments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Static Characteristics** of a measuring instrument are those performance attributes that describe its behaviour when the measurand (input) is **constant (steady-state)** or **varying very slowly with time**, so that the dynamic/inertial effects (transient response, time lag, etc.) are negligible.

In formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, static characteristics are a set of **quantitative descriptors** that establish the relationship between the **input (stimulus/measurand)** and the **output (response/reading)** of an instrument under **equilibrium** conditions. They are obtained from the **static calibration curve**, which is a plot of output versus input after the transient has died out.

Mathematically, the static model of an instrument is represented as:

$$y = f(x)$$

where:
- $x$ = measurand (true input signal)
- $y$ = output (reading of the instrument)
- $f(\cdot)$ = static transfer function of the instrument

For an ideal linear instrument, this reduces to:

$$y = K \cdot x + b$$

where $K$ is the **static sensitivity (gain)** and $b$ is the **bias (offset)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Weighing Scale" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a bathroom weighing scale.** If you stand still on it (static condition), the scale tells you your weight. The way the scale *performs* while you stand still — does it read correctly? Does it read the same value every time you step off and on? Does it give the same reading if you shift slightly? Does the needle drift when nothing changes? — All of these are **static characteristics**.

| Real-world (Weighing Scale) | Instrumentation Term |
|---|---|
| How close the reading is to your *real* weight | **Accuracy** |
| How consistently it shows the same value on repeated weighing | **Precision** |
| Smallest weight difference the scale can detect | **Resolution** |
| The full dial swing (0 to 150 kg) | **Range / Span** |
| Whether the dial moves in equal steps for equal weight increments | **Linearity** |
| Difference in reading when you step on vs. when you step off | **Hysteresis** |
| Drift of the dial to "0" over months | **Drift** |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Takeaway for the Student:** If the input is *not changing with time* (or changing negligibly slowly), the performance you care about is **static**. If the input is *changing rapidly*, you must study **dynamic characteristics** (covered later in Module 2/3).

---

## 1.3 Why Study Static Characteristics?

1. **Selection of instrument** for a given measurement task.
2. **Error budgeting** in cascaded measurement systems.
3. **Calibration** of instruments against standards.
4. **Compliance with quality standards** (ISO/IEC 17025, NABL accreditation).
5. **Trades-off analysis** between cost, accuracy, and reliability.

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU exam pitfall: students confuse **static** with **steady-state error** in control systems. Here, "static" means the *measurand is not time-varying*, NOT that the system is open-loop or closed-loop.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Complete Taxonomy of Static Characteristics

Static characteristics can be broadly classified into:

### **A. Performance Characteristics (Performance Indices)**

These describe *how well* the instrument measures under ideal, calibrated conditions.

#### 1. **Accuracy**
- **Definition:** Closeness of the measured value to the *true* value.
- **Mathematical Expression:**

$$A = 1 - \frac{\vert x_m - x_t \vert}{x_t}$$

- Where $x_m$ = measured value, $x_t$ = true value.
- Accuracy is often expressed as a **percentage of full-scale (FS)** or **percentage of true value**.
- **Units:** Dimensionless (or %).

#### 2. **Precision**
- **Definition:** Closeness of *two or more* measurements to each other (repeatability of a single value). A precise instrument may NOT be accurate (e.g., a miscalibrated but consistent scale).
- Precision is a measure of **reproducibility / scatter**, NOT of correctness.

#### 3. **Sensitivity (Static Sensitivity, K)**
- **Definition:** Ratio of the change in output to the change in input that produces it.

$$K = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \frac{dy}{dx}\bigg\vert_{\text{static}}$$

- **High sensitivity** = small input change gives large output change.
- Sensitivity drift is a separate static characteristic.

#### 4. **Resolution (Discrimination)**
- **Definition:** The **smallest measurable change** in input that produces a detectable change in output.
- Example: A digital voltmeter with 3½ digit display has a resolution of 0.1 V on the 200 V range.

#### 5. **Range and Span**
- **Range:** Total set of values the instrument can measure (e.g., 0–150 °C).
- **Span (Full-Scale Deflection, FSD):** The difference between maximum and minimum values.

$$\text{Span} = x_{\max} - x_{\min}$$

#### 6. **Linearity**
- **Definition:** Constancy of the slope (sensitivity) over the entire range.
- An instrument is **linear** if the calibration curve is a **straight line**.
- **Linearity (Independent) Linearity:** Maximum deviation of the actual curve from the *best-fit straight line* expressed as % of FS.
- **Linearity (Zero-based) Linearity:** Deviation from a straight line passing through zero.
- **Linearity (Terminal) Linearity:** Deviation from a line joining the end-points of the calibration curve.

$$e_L = \frac{(y_{\text{actual}} - y_{\text{ideal line}})_{\max}}{y_{\max} - y_{\min}} \times 100\%$$

#### 7. **Threshold**
- **Definition:** The smallest input that produces a measurable output. It is the *dead zone* in mechanical instruments.

#### 8. **Dead Zone / Dead Band**
- **Definition:** The total range of input values for which there is **no change in output**. It is larger than or equal to the threshold.

#### 9. **Hysteresis**
- **Definition:** The maximum difference in output for the same input, when the input is **approached from opposite directions** (increasing vs. decreasing).

$$H = \vert y_{\text{increasing}} - y_{\text{decreasing}} \vert_{\max}$$

- Caused by friction, backlash, magnetic hysteresis, elastic after-effect, etc.

#### 10. **Backlash**
- A specific type of hysteresis caused by **mechanical looseness** (e.g., gear play in a rack-and-pinion).

#### 11. **Repeatability**
- **Definition:** Ability of the instrument to produce the **same output** for the **same input**, repeatedly, under **identical conditions of use**, over a short period, by the **same observer**, with the **same instrument**.
- Expressed as % of FS.
- Quantified by the standard deviation of multiple readings.

#### 12. **Reproducibility**
- **Definition:** Ability to produce the same output for the same input under **changed conditions of measurement** (different observer, different time, different location, after recalibration).
- Reproducibility is always *worse* than repeatability.

#### 13. **Drift**
- **Definition:** Change of output with **time** for a constant input, with all environmental conditions held constant.
- **Zero Drift:** Drift that appears as a change in the zero reading.
- **Sensitivity Drift (Span Drift):** Drift that appears as a change in the slope of the calibration curve.
- Caused by aging, temperature effects, component degradation.

#### 14. **Stability**
- **Definition:** Ability of an instrument to **retain its performance** (calibration) over a long period of time.
- An instrument with **low drift = high stability**.

#### 15. **Selectivity**
- **Definition:** Ability of the instrument to respond **only to the desired measurand** and reject all other inputs.
- Example: A pH meter selective to H⁺ ions.

#### 16. **Tolerance**
- **Definition:** The **maximum permissible error** in the reading, specified by the manufacturer.
- The actual error must lie within the tolerance band.

#### 17. **Bias (Offset / Zero Error)**
- **Definition:** The systematic error where the output is consistently offset from the true value by a fixed amount.
- The output is $y = Kx + b$, where $b$ is the bias.

#### 18. **Calibration**
- **Definition:** The process of comparing the instrument output against a **standard of known accuracy** under specified conditions, and adjusting (if needed) so that the output accurately represents the input.
- Calibration establishes the **static calibration curve**.

#### 19. **Static Error**

$$e_s = x_m - x_t$$

- Or, in normalized form:

$$\text{Static Error (\%FS)} = \frac{x_m - x_t}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}} \times 100\%$$

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 ESE** — Memorize this table thoroughly.

| # | Characteristic | Formula / Expression | Typical Unit / Form |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Static Sensitivity | $K = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \dfrac{dy}{dx}$ | output/input |
| 2 | Span | $\text{Span} = x_{\max} - x_{\min}$ | unit of $x$ |
| 3 | Static Error (Absolute) | $e_s = x_m - x_t$ | unit of $x$ |
| 4 | Static Error (% FS) | $e_s(\%FS) = \dfrac{x_m - x_t}{\text{Span}} \times 100$ | % |
| 5 | Accuracy (Absolute) | $A = 1 - \dfrac{\vert x_m - x_t\vert}{x_t}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Accuracy (% of true) | $A(\%) = \left(1 - \dfrac{\vert x_m - x_t\vert}{x_t}\right) \times 100$ | % |
| 7 | Linearity Error (% FS) | $e_L = \dfrac{\vert y_{act} - y_{ideal}\vert_{\max}}{\text{Span}} \times 100$ | % |
| 8 | Hysteresis Error (% FS) | $e_H = \dfrac{\vert y_{up} - y_{down}\vert_{\max}}{\text{Span}} \times 100$ | % |
| 9 | Repeatability | $R = \dfrac{\sigma}{\bar{y}} \times 100$ (or % FS) | % |
| 10 | Resolution | $\Delta x_{\min}$ (smallest detectable input change) | unit of $x$ |
| 11 | Dead Zone | $\Delta x_{DZ}$ (input range with no output change) | unit of $x$ |
| 12 | Drift | $D = y(t) - y(t_0)$ at constant $x$ | unit of $y$ |
| 13 | Bias / Offset | $b$ in $y = Kx + b$ | unit of $y$ |
| 14 | Combined Error | $e_{\text{total}} = e_L + e_H + e_R + e_D$ (worst case) | % FS |

---

## 2.3 The Static Calibration Curve — Conceptual Map

> [!NOTE]
> A **static calibration curve** is a plot of instrument output ($y$-axis) versus known standard input ($x$-axis), taken under static conditions. **Every static characteristic can be extracted from this single curve.**

| What to look for in the calibration curve | What it tells you |
|---|---|
| Slope of the best-fit line | **Sensitivity** |
| Intercept of the line on the y-axis | **Bias / Zero error** |
| Deviation of points from a straight line | **Linearity error** |
| Difference between upscale and downscale readings | **Hysteresis** |
| Smallest step the curve shows | **Resolution** |
| Total x-axis range covered | **Range / Span** |
| Scatter of multiple readings at same x | **Repeatability** |
| Shift of curve after days/months | **Drift** |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Static Characteristics |
|---|---|
| **Process Industry** | Selecting a pressure gauge for a reactor based on accuracy, range, and linearity. |
| **Medical Devices** | Defibrillators and infusion pumps demand tight accuracy, low hysteresis, and high stability. |
| **Aerospace** | Altimeters and air-data sensors must have negligible drift at high altitude. |
| **Metrology Labs** | Calibration of reference standards requires quantified repeatability and reproducibility (ISO 5725). |
| **Automotive** | Engine control unit (ECU) sensors — MAP, MAF, oxygen — need high selectivity and stability. |
| **Power Systems** | Energy meters are specified by their accuracy class (e.g., Class 0.5 = 0.5 % error). |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📕 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation: From Calibration Data to Sensitivity & Linearity Error

### **Problem Setup**
An instrument is calibrated against 6 known input standards, giving the following readings:

| Standard Input $x_i$ | Reading (Up-scale) $y_{u,i}$ | Reading (Down-scale) $y_{d,i}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.0 | 0.2 |
| 2 | 4.0 | 4.3 |
| 4 | 8.2 | 8.4 |
| 6 | 12.1 | 12.4 |
| 8 | 16.3 | 16.5 |
| 10 | 20.2 | 20.3 |

We want to find: **(a)** static sensitivity $K$, **(b)** bias $b$, **(c)** linearity error, **(d)** hysteresis error, **(e)** repeatability estimate.

---

### **Step 1: Average Up-scale and Down-scale Readings**

For each input, compute the **mean reading** to remove hysteresis effect:

$$y_{avg,i} = \frac{y_{u,i} + y_{d,i}}{2}$$

| $x_i$ | $y_{u,i}$ | $y_{d,i}$ | $y_{avg,i}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.0 | 0.2 | 0.10 |
| 2 | 4.0 | 4.3 | 4.15 |
| 4 | 8.2 | 8.4 | 8.30 |
| 6 | 12.1 | 12.4 | 12.25 |
| 8 | 16.3 | 16.5 | 16.40 |
| 10 | 20.2 | 20.3 | 20.25 |

---

### **Step 2: Best-Fit Straight Line (Linear Regression)**

Assume a linear model: $y = Kx + b$.

The **least-squares estimates** of $K$ and $b$ are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= \frac{N \sum x_i y_i - \left(\sum x_i\right)\left(\sum y_i\right)}{N \sum x_i^2 - \left(\sum x_i\right)^2} \\[6pt]
b &= \frac{\sum y_i - K \sum x_i}{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $N = 6$.

Compute the sums:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum x_i &= 0 + 2 + 4 + 6 + 8 + 10 = 30 \\[4pt]
\sum y_i &= 0.10 + 4.15 + 8.30 + 12.25 + 16.40 + 20.25 = 61.45 \\[4pt]
\sum x_i^2 &= 0 + 4 + 16 + 36 + 64 + 100 = 220 \\[4pt]
\sum x_i y_i &= 0(0.10) + 2(4.15) + 4(8.30) + 6(12.25) + 8(16.40) + 10(20.25) \\
&= 0 + 8.30 + 33.20 + 73.50 + 131.20 + 202.50 = 448.70
\end{aligned}
$$

Now compute $K$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= \frac{6(448.70) - (30)(61.45)}{6(220) - (30)^2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{2692.20 - 1843.50}{1320 - 900} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{848.70}{420} \\[4pt]
&= 2.0207 \;\; \text{units of output per unit of input}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $b$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
b &= \frac{61.45 - 2.0207(30)}{6} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{61.45 - 60.621}{6} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.829}{6} = 0.1382
\end{aligned}
$$

So the calibration equation is:

$$y = 2.0207 \, x + 0.1382$$

---

### **Step 3: Sensitivity**

From the calibration equation, **sensitivity is the slope**:

$$\boxed{K = 2.0207 \; \text{output/input}}$$

The **bias (offset)** is:

$$\boxed{b = 0.1382 \; \text{units of output}}$$

---

### **Step 4: Linearity Error (Independent Linearity)**

The maximum deviation of the actual mean readings $y_{avg,i}$ from the best-fit line gives the linearity error.

First, compute $y_{ideal,i} = Kx_i + b$:

| $x_i$ | $y_{avg,i}$ | $y_{ideal,i} = 2.0207 x_i + 0.1382$ | $\vert y_{avg} - y_{ideal}\vert$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.10 | 0.1382 | 0.0382 |
| 2 | 4.15 | 4.1796 | 0.0296 |
| 4 | 8.30 | 8.2210 | 0.0790 |
| 6 | 12.25 | 12.2624 | 0.0124 |
| 8 | 16.40 | 16.3038 | 0.0962 |
| 10 | 20.25 | 20.3452 | 0.0952 |

Maximum deviation: $0.0962$ (at $x_i = 8$).

Linearity error as % of full-scale span:

$$e_L = \frac{0.0962}{20.3452 - 0.1382} \times 100\% = \frac{0.0962}{20.207} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{e_L \approx 0.476\% \; \text{of Full Scale}}$$

---

### **Step 5: Hysteresis Error**

For each input, the hysteresis is $\vert y_{u,i} - y_{d,i}\vert$:

| $x_i$ | $\vert y_u - y_d\vert$ |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0.2 |
| 2 | 0.3 |
| 4 | 0.2 |
| 6 | 0.3 |
| 8 | 0.2 |
| 10 | 0.1 |

Maximum hysteresis: $0.3$ (at $x_i = 2$ and $x_i = 6$).

$$e_H = \frac{0.3}{20.207} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{e_H \approx 1.485\% \; \text{of FS}}$$

---

### **Step 6: Repeatability (Standard Deviation at one input)**

Assume 5 repeated readings at $x = 6$ are: $\{12.20, 12.25, 12.22, 12.27, 12.24\}$.

Mean: $\bar{y} = 12.236$

Sample standard deviation:

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}{N-1}}
$$

Deviations: $\{-0.036, 0.014, -0.016, 0.034, 0.004\}$

Squared deviations: $\{0.001296, 0.000196, 0.000256, 0.001156, 0.000016\}$

Sum: $0.002920$

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{0.002920}{4}} = \sqrt{0.00073} = 0.0270
$$

Repeatability as % FS:

$$R = \frac{0.0270}{20.207} \times 100\% \approx 0.134\% \; \text{FS}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Accuracy, Error, and Percentage Error

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU Typical 3-mark question):** A voltmeter reads 51.4 V when measuring a standard voltage of 50.0 V. The full-scale range of the voltmeter is 100 V. Find: (a) absolute error, (b) % error of full scale, (c) % accuracy.

### **Solution:**

**(a) Absolute Static Error:**

$$e_s = x_m - x_t = 51.4 - 50.0 = +1.4 \; \text{V}$$

**(b) Percentage Error of Full Scale:**

$$e_s(\%FS) = \frac{+1.4}{100} \times 100\% = 1.4\% \; \text{FS}$$

**(c) Percentage Accuracy (of true value):**

$$A(\%) = \left(1 - \frac{\vert e_s\vert}{x_t}\right) \times 100 = \left(1 - \frac{1.4}{50.0}\right) \times 100 = 97.2\%$$

> [!TIP]
> Notice: **1.4 % FS error** does NOT mean 1.4 % accuracy. The accuracy **depends on where you are on the scale.** At full scale (100 V), this instrument is highly accurate; at 5 V it would be terribly inaccurate. This is why KTU emphasizes specifying accuracy as % FS.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Static Characteristics

| Characteristic | Quantifies | Affected by | Reduced by |
|---|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | Systematic + Random error | Calibration error, bias | Better calibration, correction factors |
| Precision | Random error / scatter | Noise, observer skill | Averaging, shielding |
| Sensitivity | Slope of calibration | Design of transducer | Cannot easily change after design |
| Resolution | Smallest detectable Δx | ADC bits, display design | Higher ADC resolution |
| Linearity | Non-linearity | Sensor physics (e.g., thermistor) | Linearization circuits, look-up tables |
| Hysteresis | Energy loss / friction | Backlash, magnetic domains | Use of low-friction bearings, software compensation |
| Repeatability | Inherent scatter | Noise, environmental variation | Stable conditions, multiple readings |
| Reproducibility | Robustness to change | Calibration drift, operator | Strict procedures, regular recalibration |
| Drift | Long-term change | Aging, temperature | Temperature compensation, periodic calibration |
| Dead Zone | Mechanical insensitivity | Bearing friction, threshold circuits | Reducing mechanical friction |
| Tolerance | Acceptable error band | Manufacturing variation | Tighter QA, calibration |
| Bias | Systematic offset | Misalignment, calibration offset | Re-zeroing, offset correction |

---

## 3.4 Important Relations in Static Measurement

### **3.4.1 Linear System — Combined Errors (Worst Case)**
If a system has several independent static error sources, the **worst-case combined error** is the **algebraic sum of magnitudes**:

$$e_{\text{total}} = \vert e_L \vert + \vert e_H \vert + \vert e_R \vert + \vert e_D \vert$$

A more realistic estimate uses the **root-sum-square (RSS)** method assuming independence:

$$e_{\text{total,RSS}} = \sqrt{e_L^2 + e_H^2 + e_R^2 + e_D^2}$$

### **3.4.2 Sensitivity in a Cascaded System**
If two instruments are cascaded with sensitivities $K_1$ and $K_2$, the overall sensitivity is:

$$K_{\text{total}} = K_1 \times K_2$$

### **3.4.3 Resolution of a Digital Display**
For an $n$-digit display on a range of $R$ (FS):

$$\text{Resolution} = \frac{R}{10^n - 1} \approx \frac{R}{10^n}$$

**Example:** 3.5-digit DMM on 200 V range: $\text{Resolution} = \frac{200}{1999} \approx 0.1$ V.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📙 Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Static Characteristics (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Static Characteristics of Instruments]:::root
    A --> B[Performance Indices]:::node1
    A --> C[Operational Quantities]:::node1
    A --> D[Reliability Quantities]:::node1

    B --> B1[Accuracy]:::leaf
    B --> B2[Precision]:::leaf
    B --> B3[Sensitivity]:::leaf
    B --> B4[Resolution]:::leaf
    B --> B5[Linearity]:::leaf
    B --> B6[Threshold]:::leaf

    C --> C1[Range and Span]:::leaf
    C --> C2[Dead Zone]:::leaf
    C --> C3[Bias / Offset]:::leaf
    C --> C4[Selectivity]:::leaf

    D --> D1[Hysteresis]:::leaf
    D --> D2[Repeatability]:::leaf
    D --> D3[Reproducibility]:::leaf
    D --> D4[Drift]:::leaf
    D --> D5[Stability]:::leaf
    D --> D6[Tolerance]:::leaf
    D --> D7[Backlash]:::leaf

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef node1 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff
    classDef leaf fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Static Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Measurand X true]:::nodeA --> B[Primary Sensor]:::nodeB
    B --> C[Signal Conditioner]:::nodeC
    C --> D[Display / Recorder]:::nodeD
    D --> E[Reading Y measured]:::nodeE

    F[Static Calibration]:::nodeF -.-> B
    F -.-> C
    F -.-> D

    G[Environmental Factors T, P, H]:::nodeG -.-> B
    G -.-> C

    classDef nodeA fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef nodeB fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef nodeC fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef nodeD fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef nodeE fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef nodeF fill:#f4b183,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef nodeG fill:#f4b183,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Interpretation of the Block Diagram:**
- The **measurand** (true value) enters the **primary sensor**, which converts it to a usable form.
- The **signal conditioner** amplifies, filters, and linearizes.
- The **display** shows the measured value $y$.
- The **Static Calibration** block (orange) overlays the entire chain — it determines $K$, $b$, linearity, hysteresis, etc.
- **Environmental factors** (temperature, pressure, humidity) feed into the sensor and conditioner, causing **drift** and **zero error** if not compensated.

---

## 4.3 The Static Calibration Curve — Visual Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[True Calibration Curve]:::node1 --> P2[Best-Fit Straight Line]:::node2
    P1 --> P3[End-Point Line]:::node3

    P1 -- deviation --> D1[Linearity Error eL]:::leaf
    P1 -- up-down split --> D2[Hysteresis Error eH]:::leaf
    P1 -- smallest step --> D3[Resolution]:::leaf
    P1 -- y-intercept --> D4[Bias b]:::leaf
    P1 -- slope --> D5[Sensitivity K]:::leaf
    P2 -- shift over time --> D6[Drift]:::leaf

    classDef node1 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef node2 fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef node3 fill:#ffe699,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#f8cbad,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The blue curve is the *true* response of the instrument. The green line is the ideal best-fit straight line. The yellow line connects the two end-points. The *deviation* between curve and best-fit line is the **linearity error**. The *difference* between up-scale and down-scale traces is the **hysteresis error**. The *y-intercept* is the **bias**. The *slope* is the **sensitivity**. All of these are static characteristics.

---

## 4.4 Concept Map: Cause-Effect of Static Errors

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sources of Static Error]:::root

    A --> S1[Instrument Design]:::node1
    A --> S2[Calibration Error]:::node1
    A --> S3[Environmental Drift]:::node1
    A --> S4[Loading Effect]:::node1
    A --> S5[Operator Error]:::node1

    S1 --> S2a[Non-linearity]:::leaf
    S1 --> S2b[Hysteresis]:::leaf
    S1 --> S2c[Dead Zone]:::leaf
    S1 --> S2d[Limited Resolution]:::leaf

    S2 --> S2e[Bias / Zero Error]:::leaf
    S2 --> S2f[Sensitivity Error]:::leaf

    S3 --> S2g[Zero Drift]:::leaf
    S3 --> S2h[Sensitivity Drift]:::leaf

    S4 --> S2i[Loading / Impedance Error]:::leaf
    S5 --> S2j[Parallax and Reading Error]:::leaf

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef node1 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef leaf fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
