topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT205 - BASIC OF INSTRUMENTATION ENGINEERING (INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Static Sensitivity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Static Sensitivity** is formally defined as the **ratio of the change in the output signal of a measuring instrument to the corresponding change in the input quantity (measurand)** under steady-state or static conditions (i.e., when the input is either constant or changing very slowly such that all transient/dynamic effects have died out).

Mathematically expressed as:

$$K = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \frac{dy}{dx}$$

where $K$ is the static sensitivity, $\Delta y$ is the change in output, and $\Delta x$ is the change in input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Static sensitivity is one of the most frequently tested static characteristics. The University Examiner's standard expects the answer to explicitly mention "the slope of the calibration curve at the operating point" along with the formal definition.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are using a **thermometer to measure the temperature of water**:
- A **highly sensitive** mercury thermometer will show a *large* rise in the mercury column for a *small* increase in water temperature (e.g., 1 cm rise per 1°C change).
- A **less sensitive** alcohol thermometer may show only 0.2 cm rise per 1°C change.

The **first instrument has higher static sensitivity** because its output (mercury height) changes more rapidly with respect to the input (temperature). The sensitivity here is essentially the **"responsiveness"** or **"amplification factor"** of the instrument.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Metrics:**
> - Standard unit of sensitivity: **output unit / input unit** (e.g., mm/°C, V/kPa, mV/°C)
> - For dimensionless instruments: **unitless (pure number)**
> - The **Inverse Sensitivity** (or Deflection Factor) $= \frac{1}{K} = \frac{\Delta x}{\Delta y}$ is the input change required per unit output change.

### 1.3 Classification by Linearity

| Type | Description | Sensitivity Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Constant Sensitivity** | Linear instruments | $K$ is constant over the entire range |
| **Variable Sensitivity** | Non-linear instruments | $K$ varies with the operating point |
| **Zero Sensitivity** | Dead zone region | No output change regardless of input |
| **Infinite Sensitivity** | Unstable/saturated region | Output jumps for any infinitesimal input |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundations

**Step 1: The Measurement Chain Concept**
Every measurement system follows a generic chain: *Measurand → Sensor → Signal Conditioner → Output Display*. Static sensitivity applies to the **end-to-end static gain** of this entire chain.

**Step 2: The Calibration Curve**
The static sensitivity is graphically represented as the **slope of the static calibration curve** — a plot of the instrument output (y-axis) versus the known input (x-axis), obtained under controlled laboratory conditions.

**Step 3: Why "Static"?**
The qualifier "static" implies:
- The input is either **constant** (DC measurement) or
- Varying so **slowly** that inertial, capacitive, and inductive dynamic effects are negligible.
- The system has reached **steady-state** (all transients have decayed to zero).

**Step 4: Sensitivity as a Derivative**
For a continuous, differentiable relationship $y = f(x)$, the sensitivity at any specific point $x_0$ is:

$$K(x_0) = \left.\frac{dy}{dx}\right|_{x = x_0}$$

**Step 5: Sensitivity vs. Range vs. Span vs. Resolution**
- **Sensitivity** → How much output per unit input (slope)
- **Range** → The minimum to maximum input the instrument can handle
- **Span** → (Max input − Min input) of the instrument
- **Resolution** → The smallest input change that produces a detectable output change

These four are **related but distinct** — high sensitivity does NOT automatically mean high resolution or wide range.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $K = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ | Static sensitivity (linear) | output/input |
| 2 | $K(x) = \dfrac{dy}{dx}$ | Static sensitivity (point-wise) | output/input |
| 3 | $y = K \cdot x + c$ | Linear instrument equation | output |
| 4 | $D = \dfrac{1}{K} = \dfrac{\Delta x}{\Delta y}$ | Inverse sensitivity / Deflection factor | input/output |
| 5 | $y = K_1 K_2 K_3 \cdot x$ | Overall sensitivity (cascaded system) | output/input |
| 6 | $\%\text{Error} = \dfrac{\Delta y_{\text{actual}} - \Delta y_{\text{ideal}}}{y_{\text{FS}}} \times 100$ | Sensitivity-based error | % |
| 7 | $K_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} K_i$ | Average sensitivity from n data points | output/input |
| 8 | $\text{Sensitivity Drift} = \dfrac{K_2 - K_1}{K_1} \times 100\%$ | Change in sensitivity over time | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In industrial process control, sensitivity is critical for **feedback loop design**. A sensor with too high sensitivity may cause **oscillations** in a closed-loop system, while too low sensitivity leads to **poor disturbance rejection**. Selecting the right sensitivity is a fundamental design tradeoff.

### 2.3 Loading Effect and Sensitivity

When a measuring instrument is connected to a source, the instrument's own input impedance **loads** the source and alters the true value being measured. The **measured sensitivity** in the loaded condition differs from the **true (open-circuit) sensitivity**. This is especially important in:
- **Voltage measurements** (voltmeter loading)
- **Pressure transducers** (mechanical loading)
- **Wheatstone bridge sensors** (impedance matching)

For a simple voltage divider source with source resistance $R_s$ and instrument input resistance $R_i$:

$$K_{\text{loaded}} = K_{\text{true}} \cdot \frac{R_i}{R_s + R_i}$$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Overall Sensitivity of Cascaded Instruments

When two or more instruments are connected in cascade (output of one feeds the input of the next), the overall static sensitivity is the **product of the individual sensitivities**.

**Derivation:**

Consider two cascaded stages with:
- Stage 1: input $x$, output $y_1$, sensitivity $K_1$
- Stage 2: input $y_1$, output $y_2$, sensitivity $K_2$

By definition:

$$K_1 = \frac{y_1}{x} \quad \Rightarrow \quad y_1 = K_1 \cdot x$$

$$K_2 = \frac{y_2}{y_1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad y_2 = K_2 \cdot y_1$$

Substituting $y_1$ into the second equation:

$$y_2 = K_2 \cdot (K_1 \cdot x)$$

$$y_2 = (K_1 \cdot K_2) \cdot x$$

By definition, the overall sensitivity $K_{\text{overall}} = \frac{y_2}{x}$:

$$K_{\text{overall}} = K_1 \cdot K_2$$

For $n$ cascaded stages:

$$K_{\text{overall}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} K_i = K_1 \cdot K_2 \cdot K_3 \cdots K_n$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Pattern

**Problem:** A temperature measurement system consists of a thermocouple (sensitivity = 0.05 mV/°C), an amplifier (gain = 200), and a voltmeter (sensitivity = 100 mm/V). Find:
1. The overall sensitivity of the system in mm/°C.
2. The deflection of the voltmeter pointer when the temperature changes by 20°C.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the individual sensitivities.**
- $K_1$ (Thermocouple) $= 0.05 \text{ mV/°C}$
- $K_2$ (Amplifier) $= 200$ (dimensionless)
- $K_3$ (Voltmeter) $= 100 \text{ mm/V} = 0.1 \text{ mm/mV}$

**Step 2: Convert all units to be consistent.**

Convert $K_3$ from mm/V to mm/mV:

$$K_3 = 100 \text{ mm/V} = \frac{100 \text{ mm}}{1000 \text{ mV}} = 0.1 \text{ mm/mV}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the overall sensitivity.**

$$K_{\text{overall}} = K_1 \cdot K_2 \cdot K_3$$

$$K_{\text{overall}} = 0.05 \text{ mV/°C} \times 200 \times 0.1 \text{ mm/mV}$$

$$K_{\text{overall}} = (0.05 \times 200 \times 0.1) \text{ mm/°C}$$

$$K_{\text{overall}} = 1.0 \text{ mm/°C}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the deflection for $\Delta T = 20°C$.**

$$\text{Deflection} = K_{\text{overall}} \times \Delta T$$

$$\text{Deflection} = 1.0 \text{ mm/°C} \times 20°C$$

$$\text{Deflection} = 20 \text{ mm}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Factors Affecting Static Sensitivity

| Factor | Effect on Sensitivity | Engineering Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Temperature** of surroundings | Drift in $K$ over time | Temperature compensation,恒温 |
| **Aging** of components | Gradual decrease in $K$ | Periodic recalibration |
| **Supply voltage** variation | Change in amplifier gain | Regulated power supply |
| **Mechanical wear** (contacts) | Erratic $K$ values | Use of solid-state sensors |
| **Hysteresis** | Different $K$ on rising vs. falling input | Material selection, dithering |
| **Linearity error** | $K$ varies across the range | Linearization circuits, software correction |
| **Loading effect** | $K_{\text{loaded}} < K_{\text{true}}$ | High input impedance buffers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** When a problem gives a **table of input-output data** (calibration data) and asks to find the sensitivity, compute $K = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ using the **two extreme points** for an "average" linear sensitivity, OR compute the slope between **consecutive points** to check for linearity.

### 3.4 Numerical Verification — Sensitivity from Calibration Data

Suppose the following calibration data is obtained for a pressure gauge:

| Input (kPa) | Output (V) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0.00 |
| 20 | 1.02 |
| 40 | 1.98 |
| 60 | 3.01 |
| 80 | 4.00 |
| 100 | 5.02 |

Calculate the sensitivity between successive points and verify linearity.

**Computation:**

For interval [0, 20]: $K_1 = \frac{1.02 - 0.00}{20 - 0} = 0.0510$ V/kPa

For interval [20, 40]: $K_2 = \frac{1.98 - 1.02}{40 - 20} = 0.0480$ V/kPa

For interval [40, 60]: $K_3 = \frac{3.01 - 1.98}{60 - 40} = 0.0515$ V/kPa

For interval [60, 80]: $K_4 = \frac{4.00 - 3.01}{80 - 60} = 0.0495$ V/kPa

For interval [80, 100]: $K_5 = \frac{5.02 - 4.00}{100 - 80} = 0.0510$ V/kPa

**Average sensitivity:**

$$K_{\text{avg}} = \frac{K_1 + K_2 + K_3 + K_4 + K_5}{5} = \frac{0.0510 + 0.0480 + 0.0515 + 0.0495 + 0.0510}{5} = 0.0502 \text{ V/kPa}$$

This shows the instrument is **reasonably linear** with a sensitivity of approximately **0.05 V/kPa**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Position of Static Sensitivity in a Measurement System

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Measurand X] --> B[Primary Sensor]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioner]
    C --> D[Output Indicator]
    D --> E[Output Y]
    A -- "Input Change ΔX" --> F[Static Sensitivity K = ΔY / ΔX]
    E -- "Output Change ΔY" --> F
    F --> G[Calibration Curve Slope]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Cascaded Sensitivity Calculation

```mermaid
graph LR
    P[Input X] --> Q[Stage 1 - Sensitivity K1]
    Q --> R[Stage 2 - Sensitivity K2]
    R --> S[Stage 3 - Sensitivity K3]
    S --> T[Output Y]
    Q --> U[K1 = Y1 / X]
    R --> V[K2 = Y2 / Y1]
    S --> W[K3 = Y3 / Y2]
    T --> X[Koverall = K1 x K2 x K3]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Calibration Curve (Static Sensitivity as Slope)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph CAL["Static Calibration Curve - Y vs X"]
        direction LR
        A1[Origin 0,0] --> A2[Point 1]
        A2 --> A3[Point 2]
        A3 --> A4[Point 3]
        A4 --> A5[Point n]
    end
    B[Slope of Straight Line = Static Sensitivity K] --> CAL
    C[Intercept c = Zero Error] --> CAL
    D[Equation: Y = KX + c] --> CAL
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Sensitivity Classification

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[Static Sensitivity] --> B[Constant K]
    A --> C[Variable K]
    A --> D[Zero K]
    A --> E[Infinite K]
    B --> B1[Linear Instruments]
    C --> C1[Non-linear Instruments]
    D --> D1[Dead Zone]
    E --> E1[Unstable / Saturated Region]
    B1 --> F1[Example: Ideal Voltmeter]
    C1 --> F2[Example: Thermistor]
    D1 --> F3[Example: Threshold Switch]
    E1 --> F4[Example: On-Off Controller]
```

### 4.5 Functional Architecture: Sensitivity Drift Over Time

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Time T1] --> B[K1 - Initial Sensitivity]
    C[Time T2] --> D[K2 - After Aging]
    E[Time T3] --> F[K3 - After Drift]
    B --> G[Sensitivity Drift Percentage = K2 - K1 / K1 x 100]
    D --> G
    F --> H[Recalibration Required]
    G --> H
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model QP]**
**Define static sensitivity. What are its units?**

**Model Answer:**

Static sensitivity is defined as the ratio of the change in output of a measuring instrument to the corresponding change in the input (measurand) under steady-state (static) conditions.

$$K = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$$

where $K$ is the static sensitivity, $\Delta y$ is the change in output, and $\Delta x$ is the change in input.

**Units:** The units of static sensitivity are the units of output divided by the units of input (e.g., mm/°C, V/kPa, mV/N). If the instrument is dimensionless, the sensitivity is a pure number (unitless). [3 marks]

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model QP]**
**Differentiate between sensitivity and inverse sensitivity.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Sensitivity | Inverse Sensitivity |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Ratio of change in output to change in input | Ratio of change in input to change in output |
| Formula | $K = \Delta y / \Delta x$ | $D = \Delta x / \Delta y$ |
| Also called | Forward gain / Deflection coefficient | Deflection factor / Reciprocal sensitivity |
| Numerical example | 5 mm/°C | 0.2 °C/mm |
| Use | Indicates how strongly the instrument responds | Indicates the input change needed for unit output change |

[3 marks]

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**a)** Define static sensitivity and explain its significance in the design of a measuring instrument. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A displacement measuring system uses a Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT) with a sensitivity of 0.5 V/mm, followed by an amplifier of gain 50, and a voltmeter that gives a full-scale deflection of 100 mm for an input of 10 V. Calculate:
   (i) The overall sensitivity of the system in mm/mm.
   (ii) The pointer deflection when the displacement is 4 mm. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

**Definition:** Static sensitivity $K$ of a measuring instrument is defined as the ratio of the small change in output $\Delta y$ to the corresponding small change in input $\Delta x$, under static (steady-state) conditions:

$$K = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \frac{dy}{dx}$$

**Significance in Instrument Design:**

1. **Determines Measurement Resolution:** Higher sensitivity enables detection of smaller input changes, improving the resolution of the measurement system.

2. **Defines Instrument Class:** Sensitivity classifies instruments into categories — null-type (low sensitivity but high accuracy) and deflection-type (high sensitivity, moderate accuracy).

3. **Influences Accuracy:** Sensitivity is necessary but not sufficient for accuracy. A highly sensitive instrument can still be inaccurate if linearity and calibration are poor.

4. **Sets the Operating Range:** Combined with span, sensitivity defines the working zone of the instrument. Excessive sensitivity outside the range causes saturation.

5. **Affects Control System Stability:** In feedback control, sensor sensitivity influences loop gain. Too high a sensitivity leads to instability; too low leads to sluggish response.

6. **Loading Effect Consideration:** The sensitivity is affected by the source impedance due to the loading effect, which designers must compensate using buffer stages.

7. **Engineering Trade-off:** Higher sensitivity often means narrower range, more susceptibility to noise, and higher cost — engineers must balance these factors.

[7 marks]

---

**Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

**Given:**
- LVDT sensitivity: $K_1 = 0.5$ V/mm
- Amplifier gain: $K_2 = 50$ (dimensionless)
- Voltmeter sensitivity: 100 mm full-scale for 10 V input

**Step 1: Convert voltmeter sensitivity to consistent units.**

$$K_3 = \frac{100 \text{ mm}}{10 \text{ V}} = 10 \text{ mm/V}$$

**Step 2: Convert $K_1$ to V/mm (already in V/mm).**

$$K_1 = 0.5 \text{ V/mm}$$

**Step 3: Calculate overall sensitivity in mm/mm.**

$$K_{\text{overall}} = K_1 \times K_2 \times K_3$$

$$K_{\text{overall}} = 0.5 \text{ V/mm} \times 50 \times 10 \text{ mm/V}$$

$$K_{\text{overall}} = 0.5 \times 50 \times 10 = 250 \text{ mm/mm}$$

**Answer (i):** $K_{\text{overall}} = 250$ mm/mm [3 marks]

**Step 4: Calculate pointer deflection for $\Delta x = 4$ mm.**

$$\text{Deflection} = K_{\text{overall}} \times \Delta x$$

$$\text{Deflection} = 250 \text{ mm/mm} \times 4 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{Deflection} = 1000 \text{ mm}$$

**Answer (ii):** Pointer deflection = 1000 mm (or 1 m) [4 marks]

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Expected Pattern]**

**a)** Explain the difference between static and dynamic sensitivity. Discuss how sensitivity is determined from a static calibration curve. **(7 marks)**

**b)** The following calibration data was obtained for a temperature sensor:

| Temperature (°C) | Output (mV) |
|---|---|
| 20 | 2.0 |
| 40 | 4.1 |
| 60 | 6.0 |
| 80 | 8.2 |
| 100 | 10.0 |

   (i) Calculate the average static sensitivity of the sensor.
   (ii) If the sensor output is 7.0 mV, estimate the temperature being measured.
   (iii) Determine the inverse sensitivity. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

**Static vs Dynamic Sensitivity:**

| Parameter | Static Sensitivity | Dynamic Sensitivity |
|---|---|---|
| Conditions | Input is constant or changing very slowly | Input is changing rapidly with time |
| Transient effects | All transients have died out | Transients are significant |
| Definition | $K_s = \Delta y / \Delta x$ | $K_d = \Delta y(t) / \Delta x(t)$ under sinusoidal or step input |
| Time domain | Steady-state value | Time-dependent function |
| Frequency dependence | None (ideally) | Function of input frequency |

**Determination from Static Calibration Curve:**

A **static calibration curve** is obtained by:
1. Applying a series of known, precise input values $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ to the instrument.
2. Recording the corresponding output values $y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_n$ after steady-state is reached.
3. Plotting $y$ versus $x$ on a graph.

**The static sensitivity is then determined as:**

- For a **linear** instrument: $K = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ (slope of the best-fit straight line).
- For a **non-linear** instrument: $K(x_0) = \left.\frac{dy}{dx}\right|_{x=x_0}$ (tangent slope at the operating point $x_0$).
- **Units:** Output units / Input units (e.g., mV/°C).
- A **high slope** = high sensitivity. A **flat (low slope)** curve = low sensitivity.

[7 marks]

---

**Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**Step 1: Calculate sensitivity between each pair of consecutive points.**

Between 20°C and 40°C:

$$K_1 = \frac{4.1 - 2.0}{40 - 20} = \frac{2.1}{20} = 0.105 \text{ mV/°C}$$

Between 40°C and 60°C:

$$K_2 = \frac{6.0 - 4.1}{60 - 40} = \frac{1.9}{20} = 0.095 \text{ mV/°C}$$

Between 60°C and 80°C:

$$K_3 = \frac{8.2 - 6.0}{80 - 60} = \frac{2.2}{20} = 0.110 \text{ mV/°C}$$

Between 80°C and 100°C:

$$K_4 = \frac{10.0 - 8.2}{100 - 80} = \frac{1.8}{20} = 0.090 \text{ mV/°C}$$

**Step 2: Calculate average sensitivity.**

$$K_{\text{avg}} = \frac{0.105 + 0.095 + 0.110 + 0.090}{4} = \frac{0.400}{4} = 0.100 \text{ mV/°C}$$

**Answer (i):** $K_{\text{avg}} = 0.1$ mV/°C [2 marks]

**Step 3: Estimate temperature for output = 7.0 mV.**

Using the linear relationship $y = Kx + c$:

From the first data point: $2.0 = 0.1 \times 20 + c \Rightarrow c = 2.0 - 2.0 = 0$ mV

So: $y = 0.1x$ → $x = y / 0.1 = 7.0 / 0.1 = 70°C$

**Answer (ii):** Estimated temperature = **70°C** [2 marks]

**Step 4: Calculate inverse sensitivity.**

$$D = \frac{1}{K_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{1}{0.1} = 10 \text{ °C/mV}$$

**Answer (iii):** Inverse sensitivity = **10 °C/mV** [3 marks]

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Students frequently mix units (e.g., V and mV, mm and m) when computing overall sensitivity. Always convert ALL sensitivities to a common base unit before multiplying.
> 2. **Forgetting the intercept:** When using calibration data, students compute the slope but ignore the **zero error** (intercept $c$). The full equation is $y = Kx + c$, not just $y = Kx$.
> 3. **Confusing Sensitivity with Accuracy:** A sensitive instrument is NOT necessarily accurate. Sensitivity only indicates responsiveness, not correctness.
> 4. **Static vs Dynamic mix-up:** Static sensitivity applies to steady-state only. Using it for time-varying inputs leads to incorrect results.
> 5. **Sign convention:** A negative sensitivity indicates the output decreases as the input increases (inverting instrument). Don't drop the sign.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Static Sensitivity:**

- **Definition:** $K = \Delta y / \Delta x$ — ratio of output change to input change under steady-state conditions.

- **Units:** Always **output unit / input unit** (e.g., mV/°C, V/kPa, mm/N).

- **Graphical Meaning:** $K$ = **slope of the static calibration curve**.

- **Inverse Sensitivity (Deflection Factor):** $D = 1/K = \Delta x / \Delta y$ — input change per unit output change.

- **Cascaded System Rule:** Overall sensitivity = **product** of individual stage sensitivities: $K_{\text{overall}} = K_1 \cdot K_2 \cdot K_3 \cdots K_n$.

- **Linear vs Non-linear:** Linear instruments have **constant K**; non-linear instruments have **variable K** (point-wise tangent slope).

- **Not the same as accuracy:** High sensitivity ≠ high accuracy. Sensitivity is a *necessary* but *not sufficient* condition for good measurement.

- **Loading Effect:** Connecting the instrument loads the source, reducing the effective (measured) sensitivity from its true value.

- **Drift:** Sensitivity changes over time due to aging, temperature, and component degradation → requires **periodic recalibration**.

- **Typical KTU 14-mark question structure:** (a) 7 marks = theory/definition with explanation; (b) 7 marks = numerical problem involving cascaded sensitivity OR sensitivity from calibration data.

- **Key formulas to memorize:** $K = \Delta y / \Delta x$, $D = 1/K$, $K_{\text{overall}} = \prod K_i$, $y = Kx + c$.

- **Most common error in exam:** Forgetting to convert units → **always normalize all units before multiplying sensitivities**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linearity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT205 - BASIC OF INSTRUMENTATION ENGINEERING (INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Linearity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of instrumentation engineering, **Linearity** is defined as the *static characteristic* of a measurement system that describes how closely the calibration curve (output versus input) of an instrument conforms to a **straight line** over its specified operating range.

Mathematically, a measurement system is considered **perfectly linear** if its output $y$ is related to its input $x$ by a constant-slope equation of the form:

$$y = a + b \cdot x$$

where $b$ is the constant **static sensitivity (gain)** of the instrument and $a$ is the **intercept** (often zero for ideal transducers).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – Static Characteristics):**
> Linearity is one of the *static (steady-state)* characteristics of a transducer, alongside **accuracy, precision, resolution, threshold, dead zone, hysteresis, repeatability, span, range, sensitivity, and drift.** Unlike dynamic characteristics (rise time, bandwidth, settling time), linearity is evaluated when the input is *held constant or varied very slowly.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **kitchen weighing scale** (a spring-type balance). You place apples on it one by one:
- 1 apple → pointer moves to position $A_1$
- 2 apples → pointer moves to position $A_2$
- 3 apples → pointer moves to position $A_3$

If the difference $A_2 - A_1 = A_3 - A_2$, the scale is **perfectly linear**. Every additional apple produces the *same incremental deflection*.

Now picture a *real* bathroom weighing scale that is slightly nonlinear: it under-reads at low weights and over-reads at high weights. The plot of *true weight* versus *displayed weight* does **not** form a straight line — this deviation from a straight line is called **non-linearity** or **linearity error**, and quantifying it is exactly the topic under study.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** Linearity measures *how straight* the instrument's input–output curve is. A perfectly linear instrument behaves like the equation of a straight line — output = (gain) × (input) + offset.

## 1.3 Why Linearity Matters in Engineering

- **Predictability**: A linear instrument allows engineers to use simple calibration formulas.
- **Interchangeability**: Linear instruments can replace each other without recalibrating downstream systems.
- **Signal Processing**: Most signal-conditioning electronics (amplifiers, ADC converters, controllers) assume linear behaviour.
- **Control System Stability**: A linear plant is *essential* for classical control theory (transfer functions, Bode plots, PID tuning all rely on linearity).

**Standard metrics highlighted by KTU:**

- **Static Sensitivity** $K = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ (units: output unit per input unit)
- **Span / Full-Scale Output (FSO)** = Maximum output − Minimum output
- **Linearity Error** typically expressed as **± % of Full-Scale Output (FSO)** or **± % of reading**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of Linearity — Decomposition

Linearity of a transducer/sensor can be analysed by decomposing the input–output relationship into three measurable components:

1. **Slope (Sensitivity) Component** — the *proportional* part: $b \cdot x$
2. **Offset (Zero-Drift) Component** — the *constant* part: $a$
3. **Higher-Order (Non-Linear) Component** — the residual: $\varepsilon(x)$

The general input–output equation of any static instrument is therefore:

$$y = a + b \cdot x + c_2 x^2 + c_3 x^3 + \dots + \varepsilon(x)$$

A system is **truly linear** if and only if all coefficients $c_2, c_3, \dots = 0$ and the residual $\varepsilon(x) = 0$ for the entire operating range.

## 2.2 Types of Linearity Used in Practice

In real measurement systems, the calibration curve is rarely a perfect straight line. Instrumentation engineers therefore define *how* a best-fit line should be drawn through the calibration data. KTU emphasises the following four types:

### 2.2.1 Independent Linearity (Best Straight Line Linearity)
- A straight line is drawn *as close as possible* to the actual calibration curve.
- The line is positioned so that the **maximum positive deviation equals the maximum negative deviation**.
- It is the *most accurate* measure because it minimises maximum error.
- **Typically the smallest numerical value of all linearity errors.**

### 2.2.2 Zero-Based Linearity
- The line is *forced through zero* (origin).
- Suitable for transducers whose output *should* be zero when the input is zero (e.g., strain gauge bridge).
- The slope is adjusted to give the best fit.

### 2.2.3 Terminal-Based Linearity (End-Point Linearity)
- A straight line connects the **first calibration point** (typically lower-range value) and the **last calibration point** (upper-range value).
- Simpler to determine, but produces a *larger* maximum deviation than independent linearity.

### 2.2.4 Theoretical Linearity (Least-Squares Linearity)
- Determined using a **least-squares regression** fit of the calibration data.
- Widely used in digital instrumentation and software calibration.

## 2.3 Linearity Error Definition

The **non-linearity (linearity error)** at any input $x_i$ is the difference between the *actual output* $y_{\text{actual}}(x_i)$ and the *output predicted by the chosen reference line* $y_{\text{ref}}(x_i)$:

$$\varepsilon_{\text{NL}}(x_i) = y_{\text{actual}}(x_i) - y_{\text{ref}}(x_i)$$

It is conventionally expressed as a **percentage of Full-Scale Output (FSO)** or as a percentage of the **reading**:

$$\text{Non-linearity (\% FSO)} = \frac{\max \left\vert y_{\text{actual}}(x_i) - y_{\text{ref}}(x_i) \right\vert}{y_{\text{FSO}}} \times 100\%$$

## 2.4 Linearisation Techniques

KTU frequently tests how engineers *correct* non-linear behaviour:

1. **Hardware Linearisation**:
   - Using *op-amp circuits* with diodes or nonlinear resistors that approximate the inverse characteristic.
   - Choosing operating regions where non-linearity is minimal.
2. **Software Linearisation**:
   - **Look-Up Table (LUT)** method: store correction values for each input.
   - **Polynomial curve fitting**: invert the calibration polynomial to compute correction factors.
   - **Piecewise linear interpolation**: divide the input range into small linear segments.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Linear system equation | $y = a + b x$ | $b$ = sensitivity, $a$ = offset |
| 2 | Static sensitivity / gain | $K = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ | output unit / input unit |
| 3 | Span (Full-Scale Span) | $\text{Span} = y_{\max} - y_{\min}$ | output units |
| 4 | Non-linearity (absolute) | $\varepsilon_{\text{NL}} = y_{\text{actual}} - y_{\text{ref}}$ | same as output units |
| 5 | Non-linearity (% FSO) | $\dfrac{\max \vert y_{\text{actual}} - y_{\text{ref}} \vert}{\text{Span}} \times 100\%$ | percentage |
| 6 | Non-linearity (% reading) | $\dfrac{\vert y_{\text{actual}} - y_{\text{ref}} \vert}{y_{\text{actual}}} \times 100\%$ | percentage |
| 7 | Calibration polynomial (general) | $y = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2 + a_3 x^3 + \dots$ | higher-order terms = non-linearity |
| 8 | Least-squares slope | $b = \dfrac{n \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}$ | regression fit |
| 9 | Least-squares intercept | $a = \dfrac{\sum y_i - b \sum x_i}{n}$ | regression fit |
| 10 | Linearisation inverse | $x = f^{-1}(y_{\text{measured}})$ | software correction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Linearity is fundamental in load cells (force measurement), thermocouples (temperature), LVDTs (displacement), and pressure transducers. Calibration certificates from National Physical Laboratory (NPL) and NABL-accredited labs always quote non-linearity as **% FSO** for instrument traceability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Linearity Error From a Calibration Curve

**Statement:** For an instrument whose calibration curve gives discrete $(x_i, y_i)$ data points, derive the expression for *end-point non-linearity* expressed as a percentage of full-scale output.

### Step 1 — Define the data set
Let the instrument be calibrated at $n$ points $(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \dots, (x_n, y_n)$.

### Step 2 — Identify the two terminal points
For end-point linearity, the reference straight line is drawn through:

$$(x_1, y_1) \quad \text{and} \quad (x_n, y_n)$$

### Step 3 — Write the equation of the reference line (point-slope form)
The slope of the end-point reference line is:

$$m_{\text{ep}} = \frac{y_n - y_1}{x_n - x_1}$$

Therefore, the line equation is:

$$y_{\text{ref}}(x) = y_1 + m_{\text{ep}} (x - x_1)$$

### Step 4 — Compute deviation at each calibration point
For every $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$:

$$d_i = y_i - y_{\text{ref}}(x_i) = y_i - \left[ y_1 + m_{\text{ep}} (x_i - x_1) \right]$$

### Step 5 — Identify maximum absolute deviation

$$\varepsilon_{\max} = \max_{i} \vert d_i \vert$$

### Step 6 — Express as % FSO

The full-scale span of the instrument is:

$$y_{\text{FSO}} = y_n - y_1 = m_{\text{ep}}(x_n - x_1)$$

Therefore the end-point non-linearity in % FSO is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NL}_{\text{end-point}}(\% \text{FSO})
&= \frac{\varepsilon_{\max}}{y_{\text{FSO}}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{\max_i \big\vert y_i - y_1 - m_{\text{ep}}(x_i - x_1) \big\vert}{y_n - y_1} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on sign convention:** Conventionally, **positive deviation** (actual output *above* reference line) is reported separately from **negative deviation** (actual output *below* reference line). The instrument manufacturer usually specifies non-linearity as **± % FSO**.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:**
A pressure transducer is calibrated against a standard. The following readings are obtained:

| Applied Pressure $x_i$ (bar) | Output $y_i$ (mV) |
|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0.5 |
| 2 | 10.3 |
| 4 | 20.6 |
| 6 | 30.2 |
| 8 | 40.8 |
| 10 | 50.5 |

Calculate the **end-point non-linearity in % FSO**.

### Step 1 — Identify terminal points
$(x_1, y_1) = (0, 0.5)$ and $(x_n, y_n) = (10, 50.5)$.

### Step 2 — Calculate slope of end-point line

$$m_{\text{ep}} = \frac{50.5 - 0.5}{10 - 0} = \frac{50.0}{10} = 5.0 \text{ mV/bar}$$

### Step 3 — Compute reference-line output at each input

$$y_{\text{ref}}(x_i) = 0.5 + 5.0 \cdot x_i$$

### Step 4 — Compute deviation $d_i = y_i - y_{\text{ref}}(x_i)$ at every point

| $x_i$ (bar) | $y_i$ (mV) | $y_{\text{ref}}$ (mV) | $d_i$ (mV) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0.5 | 0.5 | 0.0 |
| 2 | 10.3 | 10.5 | −0.2 |
| 4 | 20.6 | 20.5 | +0.1 |
| 6 | 30.2 | 30.5 | −0.3 |
| 8 | 40.8 | 40.5 | +0.3 |
| 10 | 50.5 | 50.5 | 0.0 |

### Step 5 — Find maximum absolute deviation

$$\varepsilon_{\max} = \max \{ 0.0,\; 0.2,\; 0.1,\; 0.3,\; 0.3,\; 0.0 \} = 0.3 \text{ mV}$$

### Step 6 — Calculate % FSO

$$y_{\text{FSO}} = 50.5 - 0.5 = 50.0 \text{ mV}$$

$$\text{NL}_{\text{end-point}} = \frac{0.3}{50.0} \times 100\% = 0.6\% \text{ FSO}$$

### Final Answer

$$\boxed{\text{Non-linearity (end-point)} = \pm 0.6\% \text{ FSO}}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Types of Linearity

| Criterion | Independent | Zero-Based | Terminal-Based | Least-Squares |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Reference line position | Free (minimises max error) | Forced through origin | Through two end points | Statistical best fit |
| Maximum non-linearity value | **Smallest** | Moderate | Largest | Moderate |
| Computational complexity | High | Low | Lowest | Medium |
| Recommended use | High-accuracy instruments | Null-deflection instruments | Field calibration | Digital systems |

## 3.4 Derivation of Linearisation Factor (Software Correction)

Suppose a transducer has the calibration equation:

$$y_{\text{raw}} = a_2 x^2 + a_1 x + a_0$$

To obtain a *linear* reading $y_{\text{linear}}$, we require $y_{\text{linear}} = k x$. Solving the quadratic in $x$:

$$x = \frac{-a_1 + \sqrt{a_1^2 - 4 a_2 (a_0 - y_{\text{raw}})}}{2 a_2}$$

Hence:

$$y_{\text{linear}} = k \cdot x = \frac{k \left( -a_1 + \sqrt{a_1^2 - 4 a_2 (a_0 - y_{\text{raw}})} \right)}{2 a_2}$$

This corrected output is linear with respect to the true input $x$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Concept Map: Position of "Linearity" Among Static Characteristics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[Static Characteristics of an Instrument] --> rootB[Static Characteristics]
    rootB --> node1[Accuracy]
    rootB --> node2[Precision]
    rootB --> node3[Resolution]
    rootB --> node4[Threshold]
    rootB --> node5[Dead Zone]
    rootB --> node6[Repeatability]
    rootB --> node7[Reproducibility]
    rootB --> node8[Hysteresis]
    rootB --> node9[Drift]
    rootB --> nodeA[Linearity]
    rootA --> dynamicA[Dynamic Characteristics]
    dynamicA --> node10[Rise Time]
    dynamicA --> node11[Settling Time]
    dynamicA --> node12[Bandwidth]
    dynamicA --> node13[Damping Ratio]
    nodeA --> linSub1[Independent Linearity]
    nodeA --> linSub2[Zero-Based Linearity]
    nodeA --> linSub3[Terminal-Based Linearity]
    nodeA --> linSub4[Least-Squares Linearity]
    nodeA --> errDef[Non-Linearity Error in % FSO]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Linearisation Process in a Smart Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inpA[Physical Input] --> inpB[Sensor Element Non-Linear]
    inpB --> inpC[Signal Conditioning Amplifier]
    inpC --> inpD[ADC Analog to Digital]
    inpD --> inpE[Microprocessor Linearisation LUT or Polynomial]
    inpE --> inpF[Corrected Linear Output]
    inpE -.uses inverse curve.-> inpB
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Calibration and Linearity Determination

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    srcA[Standard Reference Input Source] --> srcB[Apply Known Input Values x1 x2 ... xn]
    srcB --> srcC[Measure Transducer Outputs y1 y2 ... yn]
    srcC --> srcD[Plot Calibration Curve]
    srcD --> srcE[Choose Reference Straight Line]
    srcE --> srcF{Line Type}
    srcF --> srcG[Independent]
    srcF --> srcH[Zero Based]
    srcF --> srcI[Terminal End Point]
    srcF --> srcJ[Least Squares Regression]
    srcG --> srcK[Compute Deviations di]
    srcH --> srcK
    srcI --> srcK
    srcJ --> srcK
    srcK --> srcL[Calculate Maximum Absolute Deviation]
    srcL --> srcM[Express as Percentage of FSO]
    srcM --> srcN[Final Non Linearity Specification]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Visual: Calibration Curves for Different Linearity Types

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cA[Ideal Linear Curve Straight Line] --> cB[Actual Calibration Curve Slight Bow]
    cA --> cC[Independent Linearity Best Fit Free]
    cA --> cD[Zero Based Linearity Forced Through Origin]
    cA --> cE[Terminal Linearity Through End Points]
    cB --- cA
    cC --- cA
    cD --- cA
    cE --- cA
    cC --> fA[Smallest Maximum Deviation]
    cE --> fB[Largest Maximum Deviation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:**
> - In the **Independent Linearity** schematic, the reference line is positioned to *balance* positive and negative deviations.
> - In the **Zero-Based Linearity**, the line pivots about the origin to minimise deviation.
> - In the **Terminal Linearity**, the line *cannot tilt* — it is fixed by the two end points, producing a larger deviation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**
*Define the term "linearity" of a measuring instrument. List any two types of linearity.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Linearity is defined as the closeness with which the calibration curve of an instrument approaches a straight line over its specified operating range. Mathematically, an instrument is perfectly linear if its output $y$ is related to the input $x$ by the equation:

$$y = a + b \cdot x$$

where $b$ is the constant sensitivity and $a$ is the intercept.

**Two types of linearity (1 mark):**

1. Independent linearity (best straight line)
2. Terminal-based linearity (end-point linearity)

*(Valid alternatives: zero-based linearity, least-squares linearity — any two score full marks.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
*Define non-linearity and explain how it is expressed as a percentage of full-scale output.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Non-linearity (or linearity error) is the *deviation* of the actual calibration curve from a chosen straight reference line. Mathematically:

$$\varepsilon_{\text{NL}}(x_i) = y_{\text{actual}}(x_i) - y_{\text{ref}}(x_i)$$

It is expressed as a percentage of Full-Scale Output (FSO):

$$\text{Non-linearity (\% FSO)} = \frac{\max \vert y_{\text{actual}} - y_{\text{ref}} \vert}{y_{\text{FSO}}} \times 100\%$$

This gives a *normalised* measure independent of the absolute range, allowing comparison between different instruments.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students frequently forget to specify **which type of linearity** they are computing (end-point, independent, zero-based, least-squares). Examiners *require* explicit mention.
> 2. Confusing **% FSO** with **% of reading** — these are *different* and carry different numerical answers.
> 3. Failing to mention the **units of sensitivity** in the final answer (output units per input units).
> 4. In numerical problems, missing the **step of computing $y_{\text{ref}}(x_i)$** before evaluating deviation — a common 2-mark loss.
> 5. Drawing calibration curves without *labelled axes and units* — KTU deducts marks for unlabelled plots.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain in detail the different types of linearity used to characterise measuring instruments. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A temperature sensor produces the following calibration data:

| True Temperature $x_i$ (°C) | Sensor Output $y_i$ (mV) |
|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0.0 |
| 20 | 9.8 |
| 40 | 20.1 |
| 60 | 30.0 |
| 80 | 40.2 |
| 100 | 50.0 |

Calculate the **end-point non-linearity** expressed as a **percentage of full-scale output**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (a)

**Types of Linearity — 7 marks**

1. **Independent Linearity (Best Straight Line) — 2 marks**
   The reference line is drawn *through* the calibration curve such that the **maximum positive deviation equals the maximum negative deviation**. It yields the *lowest* reported non-linearity value among all types. Most accurate but most tedious to compute.

2. **Zero-Based Linearity — 1.5 marks**
   The straight line is *constrained* to pass through the origin $(0, 0)$. Only the slope is varied to minimise the maximum deviation. Suitable when the instrument output is *theoretically* zero at zero input (e.g., strain gauges in balanced bridge circuits).

3. **Terminal-Based Linearity (End-Point) — 2 marks**
   The reference line is drawn directly between the **first** and **last** calibration points. The slope is fixed as:

$$m_{\text{ep}} = \frac{y_n - y_1}{x_n - x_1}$$

This method is the *simplest* and is widely used in industrial datasheets.

4. **Theoretical / Least-Squares Linearity — 1.5 marks**
   The reference line is obtained by applying **linear regression** to the calibration data, minimising the sum of squared errors:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - y_{\text{ref}}(x_i))^2 = \text{minimum}$$

The slope and intercept are given by:

$$b = \frac{n \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}, \quad a = \frac{\sum y_i - b \sum x_i}{n}$$

Widely used in computer-based data acquisition systems.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (b)

**Step 1: Identify terminal points**
$(x_1, y_1) = (0, 0.0)$ and $(x_n, y_n) = (100, 50.0)$.

**Step 2: End-point slope**

$$m_{\text{ep}} = \frac{50.0 - 0.0}{100 - 0} = 0.5 \text{ mV/°C}$$

**Step 3: Reference line equation**

$$y_{\text{ref}}(x_i) = 0.0 + 0.5 \cdot x_i = 0.5 \, x_i$$

**Step 4: Deviations at each calibration point**

| $x_i$ (°C) | $y_i$ (mV) | $y_{\text{ref}}$ (mV) | $d_i = y_i - y_{\text{ref}}$ (mV) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0.0 | 0.0 | 0.0 |
| 20 | 9.8 | 10.0 | −0.2 |
| 40 | 20.1 | 20.0 | +0.1 |
| 60 | 30.0 | 30.0 | 0.0 |
| 80 | 40.2 | 40.0 | +0.2 |
| 100 | 50.0 | 50.0 | 0.0 |

**Step 5: Maximum absolute deviation**

$$\varepsilon_{\max} = 0.2 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 6: Calculate % FSO**

$$y_{\text{FSO}} = 50.0 - 0.0 = 50.0 \text{ mV}$$

$$\text{NL}_{\text{end-point}} = \frac{0.2}{50.0} \times 100\% = 0.4\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{End-point non-linearity} = \pm 0.4\% \text{ FSO}}$$

**Sensitivity of the sensor** $K = 0.5$ **mV / °C**.

---

### Question B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks)

**(a)** Discuss the static characteristics of a measuring system in detail. Highlight the importance of linearity among them. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the general expression for non-linearity expressed as a percentage of full-scale output. A load cell is calibrated and the data are as follows:

| Load $x_i$ (kg) | Output $y_i$ (V) |
|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0.00 |
| 50 | 1.02 |
| 100 | 1.99 |
| 150 | 3.05 |
| 200 | 4.00 |

Determine the **least-squares straight line** and calculate the **non-linearity as a percentage of FSO**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (a)

**Static Characteristics (Module 2 Core — 7 marks):**

Static characteristics describe the *steady-state* performance of an instrument when the input is held constant or varied slowly. They are:

1. **Accuracy** — closeness of measurement to true value.
2. **Precision** — repeatability of readings.
3. **Resolution** — smallest detectable change in input.
4. **Threshold** — minimum input required to produce detectable output.
5. **Dead Zone** — range of input over which there is no output change.
6. **Sensitivity** — ratio of output change to input change $\Delta y / \Delta x$.
7. **Span / Range** — difference between maximum and minimum measurable values.
8. **Linearity** — closeness of calibration curve to a straight line.
9. **Hysteresis** — difference in output when input is approached from increasing vs. decreasing direction.
10. **Repeatability / Reproducibility** — closeness of successive measurements.
11. **Drift** — slow change of output with time at constant input.

**Importance of Linearity (1.5 marks within the 7):**

- A linear instrument has a *predictable*, *simple* calibration relationship.
- It enables use of linear mathematical tools: superposition, transfer functions, frequency response.
- Simplifies signal conditioning, ADC conversion, and closed-loop control.
- Reduces non-linearity error and improves overall accuracy.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (b)

**Derivation (3 marks) and Numerical Computation (4 marks):**

**Derivation:** Already covered in Section 3.1.

**Step 1: Tabulate calibration data**

| $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $x_i y_i$ | $x_i^2$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0.00 | 0 | 0 |
| 50 | 1.02 | 51.0 | 2500 |
| 100 | 1.99 | 199.0 | 10000 |
| 150 | 3.05 | 457.5 | 22500 |
| 200 | 4.00 | 800.0 | 40000 |
| $\sum = 500$ | $\sum = 10.06$ | $\sum = 1507.5$ | $\sum = 75000$ |

Number of data points: $n = 5$.

**Step 2: Calculate least-squares slope**

$$b = \frac{n \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}$$

Substituting values:

$$b = \frac{5 \times 1507.5 - 500 \times 10.06}{5 \times 75000 - (500)^2} = \frac{7537.5 - 5030}{375000 - 250000} = \frac{2507.5}{125000}$$

$$\boxed{b = 0.02006 \text{ V/kg}}$$

**Step 3: Calculate least-squares intercept**

$$a = \frac{\sum y_i - b \sum x_i}{n} = \frac{10.06 - 0.02006 \times 500}{5} = \frac{10.06 - 10.03}{5} = \frac{0.03}{5}$$

$$\boxed{a = 0.006 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 4: Reference line equation**

$$y_{\text{ref}}(x) = 0.006 + 0.02006 \, x$$

**Step 5: Compute deviations**

| $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $y_{\text{ref}}$ | $d_i$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0.00 | 0.006 | −0.006 |
| 50 | 1.02 | 1.009 | +0.011 |
| 100 | 1.99 | 2.012 | −0.022 |
| 150 | 3.05 | 3.015 | +0.035 |
| 200 | 4.00 | 4.018 | −0.018 |

**Step 6: Maximum absolute deviation**

$$\varepsilon_{\max} = 0.035 \text{ V}$$

**Step 7: Full-scale span**

$$\text{FSO} = y_n - y_1 = 4.00 - 0.00 = 4.00 \text{ V}$$

**Step 8: Non-linearity in % FSO**

$$\text{NL} = \frac{0.035}{4.00} \times 100\% = 0.875\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{Non-linearity (least-squares)} \approx \pm 0.88\% \text{ FSO}}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Linearity** measures *straightness* of the input–output calibration curve.
- [x] Perfect linearity implies $y = a + bx$ for the entire operating range.
- [x] Four main types: **Independent, Zero-Based, Terminal-Based, Least-Squares**.
- [x] **Independent linearity** yields the *smallest* numerical non-linearity.
- [x] **Terminal-based linearity** is the *simplest* and most commonly quoted in datasheets.
- [x] Non-linearity is conventionally expressed as **± % FSO**.
- [x] Always specify the **type of reference line** when reporting non-linearity values.
- [x] **Static sensitivity** $K = \Delta y / \Delta x$ has units of output per input.
- [x] **Span** = maximum output − minimum output.
- [x] **Software linearisation** uses LUTs, polynomial inversion, or piecewise interpolation.
- [x] Linearity is a *static* characteristic — evaluate it at *steady state*, not during transients.
- [x] Real transducers almost always exhibit *some* non-linearity; the goal is to *minimise and quantify* it.
- [x] Calibration curves should *always* be plotted with **labelled axes and units** for full marks.
- [x] Non-linearity (% FSO) is *independent of the absolute range*, making it a fair metric for instrument comparison.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** In numerical problems, the *order of steps* (slope → reference equation → deviations → maximum → % FSO) is what examiners look for. Missing any step costs marks even if the final answer is correct. Always **state units at every stage**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hysteresis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT205 - BASIC OF INSTRUMENTATION ENGINEERING (INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hysteresis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hysteresis** is a phenomenon observed in measurement and control systems wherein the output of an instrument depends not only on the present value of the input but also on the **past history (previous values)** of that input. In instrumentation, it manifests as a **difference between the upscale (ascending) reading and the downscale (descending) reading** of an instrument when it is subjected to the same input value through opposite travel directions.

Mathematically, for a given input stimulus $x$, the instrument produces two distinct outputs:
$$y_{up} = f(x, x_{prev} \uparrow) \quad \text{and} \quad y_{down} = f(x, x_{prev} \downarrow)$$

where the arrows indicate the direction of travel of the input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Hysteresis is classified as one of the three primary **static characteristics errors** of an instrument, alongside **Dead Zone** and **Non-Linearity**. It is a *deterministic* (repeatable) error and can be predicted/corrected, unlike random errors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction from Dead Zone:** Hysteresis is a *gradual* lag across the full input range, whereas **Dead Zone** is a *specific* range of input over which the output remains unresponsive (e.g., due to friction, backlash in gears). Dead zone is often a *sub-form* of hysteresis in mechanical linkages.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine pushing a heavy **wooden door** that has rusty hinges:
- When you push it **open** (ascending force), you need to apply a *certain force* to overcome static friction.
- When the door swings back **closed** (descending force), it doesn't close all the way at the same force — it sticks, lags, and settles at a slightly different position.

If you plot door angle vs. applied force, you get a **loop** instead of a single line. The *width* of this loop is the **hysteresis**. The same physical input (force) gives two different outputs (angle) depending on whether you came from the "left" or the "right."

A more rigorous engineering example: a **Bourdon tube pressure gauge**. When pressure increases from 0 to 10 bar, the needle traces one path. When pressure decreases from 10 back to 0, the needle traces a *slightly lower* path. At 5 bar, the gauge reads differently in the two directions.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics

- **Hysteresis Error ($e_h$):** $e_h = y_{up} - y_{down}$ (at the same input value)
- **Hysteresis as % Full Scale (FS):** $\%e_h = \dfrac{(y_{up} - y_{down})_{max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100\%$
- **Hysteresis Loop Area (A):** Represents **energy dissipated per cycle** (in Joules/m³ for magnetic hysteresis, or energy loss per cycle for mechanical hysteresis).

### 1.4 Common Physical Origins in Instruments

| Origin | Affected Instruments |
|---|---|
| **Friction** (static > kinetic) | Moving coil meters, pivots, bearings |
| **Backlash** in gear trains | Rotary encoders, servo systems |
| **Elastic after-effect** | Springs, diaphragms, Bourdon tubes |
| **Magnetic domain lag** | Ferromagnetic cores in CTs, PTs, relays |
| **Thermal lag / creep** | Bimetallic strips, thermocouples |
| **Material memory** (stress-strain) | Strain gauges, load cells |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Hysteresis Phenomenon

Hysteresis is fundamentally a manifestation of **energy storage and dissipation asymmetry** within a physical system. The mechanism can be decomposed into the following logical steps:

1. **Energy Input Phase (Ascending):** As the input stimulus (force, pressure, magnetic field, temperature) increases, the system absorbs energy. Part of this energy is stored elastically, magnetically, or thermally; part is dissipated.

2. **Internal State Memory:** The system retains a "memory" of its prior state via:
   - Magnetic domain alignment (ferromagnetism)
   - Plastic deformation at micro-scale
   - Friction-induced stiction
   - Thermal inertia

3. **Energy Recovery Phase (Descending):** As the input decreases, the system attempts to release the stored energy. However, **not all stored energy is returned** — some is lost to friction, heat, or irreversible molecular rearrangement.

4. **Output Divergence:** Because the internal state during recovery differs from the input phase, the output for a given input value is different in the two directions, producing the characteristic **closed loop** (hysteresis loop) in the input–output plot.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind the loop:** Hysteresis is a hallmark of **irreversible thermodynamics**. The system never quite returns to its original microscopic configuration, even when macroscopic state variables match.

### 2.2 Types of Hysteresis in Instrumentation

#### 2.2.1 Mechanical / Elastic Hysteresis
Found in springs, diaphragms, Bourdon tubes, and bimetallic strips. Caused by **internal friction between molecules** (anelastic behavior) in the material. The stress–strain curve forms a loop, with area = energy lost as heat per cycle.

#### 2.2.2 Magnetic Hysteresis
Found in ferromagnetic cores of transformers, CTs, PTs, relays, inductors. Governed by the **B-H curve** (flux density vs. magnetizing force). The loop area equals **hysteresis loss** per unit volume per cycle:
$$W_h = \eta \cdot B_{max}^{x} \cdot f \cdot V \quad \text{(Steinmetz equation)}$$

#### 2.2.3 Thermal Hysteresis
Observed in bimetallic thermometers, thermistors, and some temperature sensors. The expansion–contraction cycle is not perfectly reversible due to microstructural changes.

#### 2.2.4 Electrical / Dielectric Hysteresis
Found in capacitors with ferroelectric dielectrics (e.g., barium titanate), where polarization lags the applied electric field.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $e_h = y_{up} - y_{down}$ | Absolute hysteresis error at a given input | Same as output |
| 2 | $\%e_h = \dfrac{(y_{up} - y_{down})_{max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100$ | Hysteresis as percentage of full scale | Dimensionless (%) |
| 3 | $W_h = \eta \cdot B_{max}^{x} \cdot f \cdot V$ | Steinmetz hysteresis loss (magnetic) | Joules (J) |
| 4 | $\eta$ (eta) | Steinmetz coefficient (material-dependent), typically $1.5 \le x \le 2.5$ | — |
| 5 | $W_h = V \cdot \oint H \, dB$ | Hysteresis energy loss per cycle per unit volume | J/m³ |
| 6 | $R_h = \dfrac{\Delta y_{hysteresis}}{y_{FS}}$ | Hysteresis resolution / repeatability ratio | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $P_h = \eta \cdot B_{max}^{x} \cdot f^{1} \cdot V$ (volumetric power) | Power loss due to hysteresis (per cycle: $f$ = 1) | Watts (W) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Steinmetz constant $x$** is typically **1.6** for silicon steel and **2.0** for soft iron. KTU questions often assume $x = 1.6$ to $2.0$ unless explicitly stated.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Instrument Design:** Knowing the hysteresis magnitude allows designers to add **compensation algorithms** (e.g., averaging up-scale and down-scale readings) or to **select low-hysteresis materials** (e.g., Mumetall, Permalloy for magnetic cores).
- **Calibration Standards:** Hysteresis error is reported on every instrument's **calibration certificate** as a separate, deterministic component of total error budget.
- **Control Systems:** A system with significant hysteresis can cause **limit cycles** in closed-loop control (e.g., thermostats overshooting/undershooting the setpoint). Anti-hysteresis techniques (dithering, dead-band compensation) are critical.
- **Energy Accounting:** In power transformers, hysteresis loss (along with eddy current loss) accounts for the **no-load loss**, which is always present whenever the transformer is energized — a key design parameter for energy-efficient transformers.

### 2.5 Methods to Reduce Hysteresis

1. **Material Selection:** Use low-hysteresis alloys (e.g., **Mumetal**, **Permalloy** for magnetic cores; **Invar** for elastic elements).
2. **Mechanical Refinement:** Use **knife-edge pivots**, **taut-band suspensions** instead of pivot-and-jewel bearings.
3. **Spring Design:** Use **torsion bars** or **pre-loaded springs** to avoid the non-linear region of the spring curve.
4. **Electronic Compensation:** Software-based look-up tables or model-based correction.
5. **Operating Point Selection:** Operate the sensor in the linear region of its characteristic curve, away from saturation.
6. **Heat Treatment / Annealing:** Reduces internal stresses in elastic elements, minimizing elastic hysteresis.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Magnetic Hysteresis Loss (Steinmetz Equation)

The Steinmetz equation is an **empirical** relation describing the energy lost per cycle in a ferromagnetic core due to hysteresis. Here we derive its form from first principles of the B-H loop.

#### 3.1.1 Energy Density in a Magnetic Field

The energy per unit volume stored in a magnetic field is given by:
$$w = \int_0^B H \, dB$$

This is the area under the magnetization curve from the origin to a point $(H, B)$.

#### 3.1.2 Net Energy Loss Per Cycle

For one complete cycle of the magnetizing force $H$ (from $0 \to +H_{max} \to 0 \to -H_{max} \to 0$), the energy absorbed by the core is:
$$W_{abs} = \oint_{+ \text{path}} H \, dB \quad \text{(integration along the upper branch)}$$

The energy released by the core on the return path is:
$$W_{rel} = \oint_{- \text{path}} H \, dB \quad \text{(integration along the lower branch)}$$

The **net energy lost per cycle per unit volume** equals the **enclosed area of the B-H loop**:
$$W_{h,\text{vol}} = \oint H \, dB = W_{abs} - W_{rel}$$

#### 3.1.3 Total Hysteresis Loss in the Core

For a core of volume $V$ and density of $f$ magnetization cycles per second (i.e., supply frequency), the **hysteresis power loss** is:
$$P_h = V \cdot f \cdot W_{h,\text{vol}}$$

#### 3.1.4 Empirical Steinmetz Approximation

Charles Proteus Steinmetz (1892) empirically observed that the loop area scales approximately as $B_{max}^{x}$ where $1.6 \le x \le 2.0$:
$$P_h = \eta \cdot B_{max}^{x} \cdot f \cdot V$$

where $\eta$ is the **Steinmetz hysteresis coefficient**, a material constant.

> [!NOTE]
> The "Steinmetz law" is empirical — it is not derived from first principles but fits measured data with reasonable accuracy for $0.1\,T \le B_{max} \le 1.5\,T$ and frequencies $50$–$60$ Hz.

#### 3.1.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A transformer core has a volume of $V = 1000\,\text{cm}^3$, operates at $B_{max} = 1.2\,\text{T}$, $f = 50\,\text{Hz}$. The Steinmetz coefficient for the core material is $\eta = 500\,\text{J/m}^3\cdot\text{T}^{1.6}$ and $x = 1.6$. Find the hysteresis power loss.

**Solution:**

Convert volume to SI: $V = 1000 \times 10^{-6} = 1 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^3$.

Apply the Steinmetz equation:
$$P_h = \eta \cdot B_{max}^{x} \cdot f \cdot V$$

Substitute values:
$$P_h = 500 \cdot (1.2)^{1.6} \cdot 50 \cdot (1 \times 10^{-3})$$

Compute $(1.2)^{1.6}$:
$$(1.2)^{1.6} = e^{1.6 \ln(1.2)} = e^{1.6 \times 0.1823} = e^{0.2917} \approx 1.3386$$

Now substitute:
$$P_h = 500 \cdot 1.3386 \cdot 50 \cdot 1 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$P_h = 500 \cdot 1.3386 \cdot 0.05$$

$$P_h = 500 \cdot 0.06693$$

$$P_h \approx 33.47\,\text{W}$$

**Hysteresis power loss ≈ 33.47 W.**

### 3.2 Derivation: Percentage Hysteresis Error

For a measuring instrument calibrated with full-scale reading $y_{FS}$, the **maximum hysteresis error** is:
$$\%e_h = \frac{(y_{up} - y_{down})_{\max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100\%$$

#### 3.2.1 Worked Example

A pressure gauge with full-scale range $0$–$10$ bar is subjected to a calibration cycle. The up-scale and down-scale readings at a calibration input of $5$ bar are:
- $y_{up}(5) = 4.92$ bar
- $y_{down}(5) = 5.08$ bar

Find the hysteresis error in bar and as a percentage of full scale.

**Solution (Step-by-Step):**

Step 1: Compute the absolute hysteresis at input = 5 bar:
$$e_h = y_{up} - y_{down} = 4.92 - 5.08 = -0.16\,\text{bar}$$

The magnitude is $\vert e_h \vert = 0.16$ bar.

Step 2: Express as percentage of full scale ($y_{FS} = 10$ bar):
$$\%e_h = \frac{0.16}{10} \times 100\% = 1.6\%$$

**Result: Hysteresis error magnitude = 0.16 bar, or 1.6 % of FS.**

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Hysteresis vs. Dead Zone vs. Non-Linearity

| Feature | Hysteresis | Dead Zone | Non-Linearity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Output depends on input history | Output unresponsive over a small input range | Output-input relationship is not a straight line |
| **Directional?** | Yes (up vs. down) | No (input direction irrelevant) | No (single-valued) |
| **Range** | Full input range | Small input range near origin | Full input range |
| **Repeatable?** | Yes (deterministic) | Yes | Yes |
| **Compensable?** | Yes (averaging) | Yes (offset) | Yes (linearization) |
| **Origin** | Friction, elasticity, magnetic lag | Backlash, stiction, threshold | Sensor physics (e.g., exponential) |

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Hysteresis in Different Transducers

| Transducer | Hysteresis Origin | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| Bourdon tube pressure gauge | Elastic after-effect in tube material | 0.1% to 0.5% FS |
| LVDT | Friction in core movement | < 0.1% FS (very low) |
| Strain gauge (bonded) | Bonding adhesive creep | 0.1% to 1% FS |
| Bimetallic thermometer | Differential expansion lag | 0.5% to 2% FS |
| Moving-iron meter | Magnetic domain friction | 1% to 3% FS |
| Iron-core CT/PT | B-H loop area | Dominant loss component |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

Define **hysteresis** in the context of instrumentation systems. How is it distinguished from **dead zone**?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Hysteresis** is the phenomenon in which the output of an instrument for a given input depends on the *direction* from which the input was approached. The up-scale and down-scale readings differ at the same input, producing a closed loop when output is plotted vs. input.
>
> **Distinction from Dead Zone:** Hysteresis is a *gradual* difference between up and down readings across the *entire* input range, whereas **dead zone** is a *specific narrow range of input* over which the output does not change at all (output remains constant). Dead zone is typically caused by static friction or backlash, while hysteresis arises from elastic, magnetic, or thermal memory effects.

*[Marking: 1.5 marks for definition + 1.5 marks for distinction]*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

State the **Steinmetz equation** for magnetic hysteresis loss and explain the significance of each term.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$P_h = \eta \cdot B_{max}^{x} \cdot f \cdot V$$

- $P_h$: Hysteresis power loss (Watts)
- $\eta$: Steinmetz hysteresis coefficient (material constant, J/m³·T$^x$)
- $B_{max}$: Peak flux density (Tesla)
- $x$: Steinmetz exponent (typically 1.6 to 2.0)
- $f$: Frequency of magnetization cycles (Hz)
- $V$: Volume of the core (m³)

The equation quantifies the energy dissipated per second in a ferromagnetic core due to the irreversible domain realignment during AC magnetization. The loss scales with frequency and core volume, and grows nonlinearly with flux density.

*[Marking: 1.5 marks for equation + 1.5 marks for term explanation]*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks - Module Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the phenomenon of **hysteresis** in measuring instruments. Discuss the various **causes** of hysteresis with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A transformer core has a volume of $1200\,\text{cm}^3$ and operates with a maximum flux density of $1.4\,\text{T}$ at a frequency of $50\,\text{Hz}$. The Steinmetz coefficient of the core material is $\eta = 600\,\text{J/m}^3\cdot\text{T}^{1.6}$ with Steinmetz exponent $x = 1.6$. Calculate the **hysteresis loss** in the core. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Hysteresis in Measuring Instruments (7 marks):**

Hysteresis in a measuring instrument is the phenomenon where the instrument reading for a given input value differs depending on whether the input is increasing or decreasing. It arises due to the energy storage and dissipation asymmetry in physical systems.

**Causes of Hysteresis:**

1. **Elastic Hysteresis** in springs, diaphragms, Bourdon tubes: Caused by internal friction between molecules during elastic deformation. The stress–strain cycle encloses an area representing energy lost as heat. Example: Bourdon tube pressure gauge reads differently on increasing and decreasing pressure.

2. **Magnetic Hysteresis** in ferromagnetic materials: Caused by irreversible domain wall motion. The B-H loop encloses an area representing energy loss per cycle. Example: Current Transformer (CT) cores, transformer cores.

3. **Friction** in moving parts: Static friction exceeds kinetic friction, so the instrument lags when the input reverses direction. Example: Moving-coil voltmeter pivots.

4. **Backlash** in gear trains: Gears do not immediately engage when motion reverses. Example: Rotary encoders with gear reducers.

5. **Thermal Hysteresis**: Bimetallic thermometers and some temperature sensors show lag between heating and cooling cycles due to thermal inertia and material memory.

**Effect on measurement:** Hysteresis produces a **deterministic but directional** error that contributes to the total measurement uncertainty. It is reported separately on calibration certificates.

**Minimization:** Use low-hysteresis materials (Mumetal, Invar), knife-edge pivots, electronic compensation, or operate in the linear region.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $V = 1200\,\text{cm}^3 = 1200 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^3 = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^3$
- $B_{max} = 1.4\,\text{T}$
- $f = 50\,\text{Hz}$
- $\eta = 600\,\text{J/m}^3\cdot\text{T}^{1.6}$
- $x = 1.6$

**Step 1:** Apply the Steinmetz equation:
$$P_h = \eta \cdot B_{max}^{x} \cdot f \cdot V$$

**Step 2:** Compute $B_{max}^{x} = (1.4)^{1.6}$:
$$(1.4)^{1.6} = e^{1.6 \cdot \ln(1.4)} = e^{1.6 \times 0.3365} = e^{0.5384} \approx 1.7135$$

**Step 3:** Substitute all values:
$$P_h = 600 \cdot 1.7135 \cdot 50 \cdot 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4:** Compute step by step:
$$P_h = 600 \cdot 1.7135 = 1028.1$$
$$P_h = 1028.1 \cdot 50 = 51405$$
$$P_h = 51405 \cdot 1.2 \times 10^{-3} = 61.686\,\text{W}$$

**Result:** Hysteresis loss $P_h \approx 61.69$ W.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 2 Alternative]**

**(a)** With the help of a **B-H curve**, explain **magnetic hysteresis**. Define the terms **retentivity** and **coercivity**. Discuss the differences between **soft magnetic materials** and **hard magnetic materials** in the context of hysteresis. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pressure gauge with a full-scale range of $0$–$200$ kPa is calibrated in both up-scale and down-scale directions. The readings at a nominal input of $100$ kPa are $98.5$ kPa (up-scale) and $101.2$ kPa (down-scale). Calculate the **absolute hysteresis error**, the **percentage hysteresis error (%FS)**, and the **corrected reading** (assuming the average is the true value). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Magnetic Hysteresis, B-H Curve, Retentivity, Coercivity (7 marks):**

**Magnetic Hysteresis:** When a ferromagnetic material is subjected to a cyclic magnetizing force $H$, the flux density $B$ does not retrace the same path on decreasing $H$ as it did on increasing $H$. This produces a closed loop called the **B-H curve** or **hysteresis loop**.

**Description of the B-H loop:**

- **Origin (0,0):** Unmagnetized state.
- **Path O → A:** As $H$ increases from 0 to $+H_{max}$, $B$ rises along the *initial magnetization curve* to point A at $(H_{max}, B_{sat})$.
- **Path A → B:** As $H$ decreases from $+H_{max}$ to 0, $B$ does not return to 0 but to a positive value $B_r$ at point B — the **Retentivity** (or remanent flux density).
- **Path B → C:** To reduce $B$ to 0, $H$ must be reversed to a negative value $-H_c$ at point C — the **Coercivity** (or coercive force).
- **Path C → D → E → F → A:** Continued cycling traces the closed loop A → B → C → D → E → F → A.

**Retentivity ($B_r$):** The flux density remaining in the material when the magnetizing force is reduced to zero. Materials with high $B_r$ retain magnetism well.

**Coercivity ($H_c$):** The reverse magnetizing force required to reduce the residual flux density to zero. Materials with high $H_c$ resist demagnetization.

**Soft vs. Hard Magnetic Materials:**

| Property | Soft Magnetic (e.g., iron, silicon steel) | Hard Magnetic (e.g., AlNiCo, NdFeB) |
|---|---|---|
| Hysteresis loop | Narrow (small area) | Wide (large area) |
| Retentivity $B_r$ | Low | High |
| Coercivity $H_c$ | Low | High |
| Hysteresis loss | Low | High |
| Applications | Transformer cores, CT/PT cores, relay yokes | Permanent magnets, motors, loudspeakers |

Soft materials are chosen for **AC applications** where low energy loss is critical; hard materials are chosen as **permanent magnets** where high residual flux is desired.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Full-scale range: $0$–$200$ kPa → $y_{FS} = 200$ kPa
- Up-scale reading at 100 kPa: $y_{up} = 98.5$ kPa
- Down-scale reading at 100 kPa: $y_{down} = 101.2$ kPa

**Step 1: Absolute Hysteresis Error:**
$$e_h = y_{up} - y_{down} = 98.5 - 101.2 = -2.7\,\text{kPa}$$

$$\vert e_h \vert = 2.7\,\text{kPa}$$

**Step 2: Percentage Hysteresis Error (%FS):**
$$\%e_h = \frac{\vert e_h \vert}{y_{FS}} \times 100 = \frac{2.7}{200} \times 100 = 1.35\%$$

**Step 3: Corrected (True) Reading:**
The best estimate of the true value at input = 100 kPa is the average of the up-scale and down-scale readings:
$$y_{true} = \frac{y_{up} + y_{down}}{2} = \frac{98.5 + 101.2}{2} = \frac{199.7}{2} = 99.85\,\text{kPa}$$

**Results:**
- Absolute hysteresis error = 2.7 kPa
- Percentage hysteresis error = 1.35 % FS
- Corrected reading ≈ 99.85 kPa (true value at 100 kPa nominal input)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign convention error:** Students often compute $e_h = y_{down} - y_{up}$ and end up with the wrong sign. Either sign is acceptable **if the formula is defined consistently**. Most textbooks use $e_h = y_{up} - y_{down}$; report magnitude for %FS.
> 2. **Forgetting unit conversion in Steinmetz:** Core volume is commonly given in **cm³** — convert to **m³** before substituting. A factor-of-$10^6$ error is the most common mistake.
> 3. **Confusing $B_r$ and $H_c$:** Retentivity has units of **Tesla** (B units), coercivity has units of **A/m** (H units). Examiners *will* check your units.
> 4. **Averaging up/down to "correct" hysteresis is only valid at one input value** — for a full calibration, a complete look-up table is needed.
> 5. **Forgetting to include the Steinmetz exponent $x$** in the calculation (some students write $B_{max}$ instead of $B_{max}^{x}$).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Hysteresis** = output depends on the *direction of input travel*, producing a **closed loop** in the input-output characteristic.
- It is a **deterministic, repeatable, and direction-dependent** static error — distinguishable from random noise and from non-linearity.
- **Mathematical form:** $e_h = y_{up} - y_{down}$ (at same input); $\%e_h = \dfrac{\vert e_h \vert_{max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100$.
- **Magnetic hysteresis** is governed by the **B-H loop**; the **loop area = energy lost per cycle per unit volume**.
- **Steinmetz equation** (must memorize): $P_h = \eta \cdot B_{max}^{x} \cdot f \cdot V$ — gives hysteresis **power** loss in Watts.
- **Steinmetz exponent** $x \in [1.6, 2.0]$; common default: $x = 1.6$ for silicon steel.
- **B-H curve key points:** $(H_{max}, B_{sat})$, $(0, B_r)$ = **Retentivity**, $(-H_c, 0)$ = **Coercivity**.
- **Soft magnetic materials** → narrow loop → low hysteresis loss → used in transformer cores, CT/PT.
- **Hard magnetic materials** → wide loop → high retentivity → used as permanent magnets.
- **Common causes in instruments:** friction, backlash, elastic after-effect, magnetic domain lag, thermal lag.
- **Minimization techniques:** low-hysteresis materials (Mumetal, Permalloy, Invar), knife-edge pivots, electronic compensation, software LUT correction, annealing, operating-point selection.
- **Distinction from dead zone:** Hysteresis is *gradual* over the *whole range*; dead zone is a *threshold* over a *narrow band*.
- **Compensation rule:** Best estimate of true reading = arithmetic mean of up-scale and down-scale readings.
- **Engineering impact:** In transformers, hysteresis loss is a *no-load loss* that persists whenever the transformer is energized; in control systems, hysteresis causes **limit cycles** and **dead-band** oscillations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Threshold
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCICT205 - BASIC OF INSTRUMENTATION ENGINEERING (INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Threshold # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Basic Instrumentation Engineering** (Module 2 — Measurement System Characteristics), the term **Threshold** refers to the **smallest magnitude or minimum value of the input stimulus (physical quantity) that an instrument, sensor, or measurement system can reliably detect and distinguish from noise or background conditions** to produce a measurable output response. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** > *Threshold is the minimum input value required to produce a detectable output change. It defines the lowest limit of operation of a measuring instrument and represents the boundary between "no measurement" and "valid measurement."* ### Mathematical Statement of Threshold If the input signal is denoted as $x(t)$ and the output response as $y(t)$, then mathematically, the threshold $T_h$ satisfies: $$y(x) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } x < T_h \\ f(x) & \text{if } x \geq T_h \end{cases}$$ Where: - $T_h$ = Threshold value (in SI units of the measured variable) - $f(x)$ = The instrument's transfer function - The transition occurs at exactly $x = T_h$ --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Whisper in a Noisy Room" > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Whisper Test** > > Imagine you are sitting in a very crowded, noisy restaurant. Your friend leans across the table and whispers a secret. > - If the whisper is **too quiet** (below the threshold), you simply cannot hear it — your ears (the "sensor") cannot distinguish it from the background chatter (noise). > - Once the whisper becomes **just loud enough**, you suddenly hear it clearly — this minimum audible whisper is the **threshold**. > - The "threshold" is therefore the *smallest meaningful input* the system can register. > > An instrument works exactly the same way. Below the threshold, the output is essentially **zero or buried in noise**; above the threshold, the output becomes a clean, usable signal. ### Three Real-World Engineering Examples | Domain | Application | Typical Threshold Value | |---|---|---| | **Acoustic Measurement** | Human hearing | **0 dB SPL** (Sound Pressure Level) | | **Optical Sensors** | Photodiode light detection | $\sim 10^{-9} \text{ W/m}^2$ | | **Pressure Gauges** | Minimum detectable pressure | **1 Pa** (for high-sensitivity gauges) | | **Strain Gauges** | Minimum detectable strain | $\sim 1\,\mu\varepsilon$ (microstrain) | | **Digital Voltmeters** | Resolution threshold | **1 µV** to **1 mV** depending on range | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** Threshold is **not** the same as accuracy or precision. It is purely a *minimum detectable input* — the floor below which the instrument is effectively "blind." --- ## 1.3 Distinguishing Threshold from Related Concepts | Concept | Definition | How It Differs from Threshold | |---|---|---| | **Threshold** | Minimum detectable input | The lower boundary of operation | | **Resolution** | Smallest change the instrument can display | Threshold is the *starting point*; resolution is the *step size* | | **Sensitivity** | Output change per unit input change | Threshold is a *static* property; sensitivity is a *dynamic* property | | **Range** | Min-to-max measurable input | Range has **two** limits; threshold is only the **lower** limit | | **Dead Zone** | Range of input over which output doesn't change | A *region* of insensitivity; threshold is a *single point* | | **Noise Floor** | Background signal level due to internal/external noise | Threshold is **set above** the noise floor to ensure reliable detection | > [!TIP] > **Memory Aid for KTU Exams:** *"Threshold = the floor; Sensitivity = the slope; Range = the corridor; Resolution = the step size."* --- ## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bold for Emphasis) The following are the **standard benchmark threshold values** used in instrumentation: - **Sound (Human Hearing Threshold):** $\mathbf{20\,\mu Pa}$ (RMS) at $\mathbf{1\,kHz}$ — corresponds to **0 dB SPL** - **Light (Visible Spectrum Threshold):** $\mathbf{10^{-6.5} \text{ lm/m}^2}$ for dark-adapted human eye - **Force (Touch Threshold, Fingertip):** $\mathbf{\sim 0.1 \, mN}$ - **Temperature (Tactile Threshold):** $\mathbf{0.1\,°C}$ variation detectable by skin - **Standard Reference Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) for reliable threshold detection:** **$\mathbf{SNR \geq 3 \text{ dB}}$** (industry convention) --- ## 1.5 Why Threshold Matters in Instrumentation — KTU Engineering Perspective > [!NOTE] > In any **measurement chain** (Primary Sensor → Signal Conditioner → Display/Recorder), threshold determines the **operational starting point**.
