## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Loading Mechanism

The loading effect unfolds in three universal stages:

1. **Connection Stage:** The instrument is physically connected (probes, leads, terminals) to the Device Under Test (DUT).
2. **Energy Transfer Stage:** The instrument draws a small current/voltage/power from the DUT to perform its sensing function. This current is non-zero because no instrument is ideal (no infinite resistance voltmeter, no zero-resistance ammeter exists).
3. **Perturbation Stage:** The withdrawn energy alters the original circuit's voltage/current/power distribution, so the value the instrument "sees" is no longer the value that existed *before* connection.

### 2.2 Case 1: Voltmeter Loading Effect (Most Common in KTU)

Consider a source with **EMF = E** and **internal/source resistance = $R_s$** connected to a load resistance $R_L$. The **true voltage** across $R_L$ is found by simple voltage division:

$$V_{true} = E \cdot \frac{R_L}{R_s + R_L}$$

When a voltmeter of internal resistance **$R_V$** is connected across $R_L$, it appears in **parallel** with $R_L$, giving an effective load:

$$R_{eff} = \frac{R_L \cdot R_V}{R_L + R_V}$$

Since $R_{eff} < R_L$, the voltage observed by the voltmeter is:

$$V_{measured} = E \cdot \frac{R_{eff}}{R_s + R_{eff}}$$

This $V_{measured}$ is always **less** than $V_{true}$, because the voltmeter "sucks" current through $R_s$, increasing the voltage drop across $R_s$.

### 2.3 Case 2: Ammeter Loading Effect

When an ammeter of internal resistance **$R_A$** is inserted in series with a load $R_L$ driven by source E with $R_s$:

- **True current** (no ammeter): $I_{true} = \dfrac{E}{R_s + R_L}$
- **Measured current** (with ammeter in series): $I_{measured} = \dfrac{E}{R_s + R_A + R_L}$

The ammeter always reads **less** than the true current because it adds its own resistance $R_A$ to the loop.

### 2.4 Case 3: Loading in Oscilloscopes and Bridges

- **Oscilloscope Loading:** Probes typically have 1 MΩ || 10–20 pF. At high frequencies, the capacitive loading becomes dominant, distorting waveforms and attenuating amplitudes.
- **Wheatstone Bridge Loading:** Connecting a galvanometer or detector across the bridge output draws current, unbalancing the bridge. The classic "no-load balance" condition $R_1 R_3 = R_2 R_4$ is altered when a finite-resistance detector is connected.

### 2.5 Case 4: Transducer Loading

A transducer's output is typically conditioned by an amplifier. If the amplifier's input impedance is not much greater than the transducer's output impedance (Thévenin equivalent), significant signal loss occurs via the voltage divider effect.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Table — Pin This for the Exam**

| **Scenario** | **Key Formula** | **Variables** | **Design Rule of Thumb** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Voltmeter loading error | $e_V = \dfrac{R_s}{R_s + R_V} \times 100\%$ | $R_s$ = source resistance, $R_V$ = voltmeter resistance | $R_V \geq 10 \cdot R_s$ for error $\leq 9.1\%$ |
| Ammeter loading error | $e_A = \dfrac{R_A}{R_s + R_L + R_A} \times 100\%$ | $R_A$ = ammeter resistance | $R_A \leq 0.01 \cdot (R_s + R_L)$ |
| Measured voltage | $V_m = V_{true} \cdot \dfrac{R_V}{R_s + R_V}$ (when $R_L \gg R_V$) | | |
| Loading in cascaded stages | $V_{out} = V_{source} \cdot \dfrac{Z_{in2}}{Z_{out1} + Z_{in2}}$ | $Z_{in2}$ = next stage input, $Z_{out1}$ = current stage output | $Z_{in2} \geq 10 \cdot Z_{out1}$ |
| Insertion loss (dB) | $IL = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{V_{loaded}}{V_{unloaded}}\right)$ | | Lower is better (less loading) |
| True value from measured | $V_{true} = V_m \left(1 + \dfrac{R_s}{R_V}\right)$ | | Used to correct instrument readings |
| Effective load (parallel) | $R_{eff} = \dfrac{R_L \cdot R_V}{R_L + R_V}$ | | Always less than the smaller of $R_L, R_V$ |

**Critical Rule of Thumb (Memorize for KTU):**
- For voltmeters: **$R_V$ must be at least 10× greater than the Thevenin resistance of the source being measured.**
- For ammeters: **$R_A$ must be at least 10× smaller than the Thevenin resistance of the source being measured.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of Loading Analysis:**
> - **Sensor selection:** Choose transducers with output impedance at least 100× lower than the readout device's input impedance.
> - **PCB design:** Trace resistance, probe capacitance, and IC input impedance all contribute to loading at high frequencies.
> - **Medical instrumentation:** ECG/EEG electrodes loading the skin-electrode interface can distort biopotential signals.
> - **Power systems:** Voltage sag when a heavy load is connected to a weak source (e.g., diesel generator) is a form of electrical loading.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Voltmeter Loading Error Percentage

**Problem Setup (Canonical KTU form):**
A voltmeter of internal resistance **$R_V = 50\text{ k}\Omega$** is used to measure the voltage across a resistor **$R_L = 10\text{ k}\Omega$** that is fed from a source with EMF **E = 20 V** and internal resistance **$R_s = 5\text{ k}\Omega$**. Find:
(a) The true voltage across $R_L$
(b) The voltage indicated by the voltmeter
(c) The loading error in percent
(d) The correction factor

**Step 1 — Identify the true circuit (no voltmeter connected):**

The circuit is simply: EMF source E in series with $R_s$ and $R_L$. The true voltage across $R_L$ is given by the voltage divider rule.

$$V_{true} = E \cdot \frac{R_L}{R_s + R_L}$$

$$V_{true} = 20 \cdot \frac{10 \times 10^3}{5 \times 10^3 + 10 \times 10^3}$$

**Step 2 — Plug in values and compute:**

$$V_{true} = 20 \cdot \frac{10{,}000}{15{,}000}$$

$$V_{true} = 20 \cdot \frac{2}{3}$$

$$V_{true} = 13.33 \text{ V}$$

So the **true voltage** is **13.33 V** (the value that existed *before* the voltmeter was connected).

**Step 3 — Now connect the voltmeter. The voltmeter $R_V$ appears in parallel with $R_L$:**

$$R_{eff} = \frac{R_L \cdot R_V}{R_L + R_V}$$

$$R_{eff} = \frac{(10 \times 10^3)(50 \times 10^3)}{10 \times 10^3 + 50 \times 10^3}$$

$$R_{eff} = \frac{500 \times 10^6}{60 \times 10^3}$$

$$R_{eff} = 8.333 \text{ k}\Omega$$

Note that $R_{eff} = 8.333 \text{ k}\Omega < R_L = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$. The parallel combination always **reduces** the effective resistance.

**Step 4 — Apply the voltage divider again using $R_{eff}$:**

$$V_{measured} = E \cdot \frac{R_{eff}}{R_s + R_{eff}}$$

$$V_{measured} = 20 \cdot \frac{8.333 \times 10^3}{5 \times 10^3 + 8.333 \times 10^3}$$

$$V_{measured} = 20 \cdot \frac{8.333}{13.333}$$

$$V_{measured} = 20 \cdot 0.625$$

$$V_{measured} = 12.50 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the absolute error:**

$$\Delta V = V_{true} - V_{measured} = 13.33 - 12.50 = 0.83 \text{ V}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the percentage loading error:**

$$e_V = \frac{V_{true} - V_{measured}}{V_{true}} \times 100\%$$

$$e_V = \frac{0.83}{13.33} \times 100\%$$

$$e_V = 6.25\%$$

**Step 7 — Cross-check with the short formula:**

$$e_V = \frac{R_s}{R_s + R_V} \times 100\%$$

Wait — this short formula applies **only** when the voltmeter is directly across an ideal source (no $R_L$ in parallel) OR when $R_V \gg R_L$. In our general case the proper derivation above gives 6.25%. If instead $R_L \to \infty$ (open circuit), the voltmeter would read:

$$V_{measured,\text{open}} = E \cdot \frac{R_V}{R_s + R_V} = 20 \cdot \frac{50}{55} = 18.18 \text{ V}$$

and the error would be:

$$e_{V,\text{open}} = \frac{R_s}{R_s + R_V} = \frac{5}{55} = 9.09\%$$

**Step 8 — Compute the correction factor:**

$$k = \frac{V_{true}}{V_{measured}} = \frac{13.33}{12.50} = 1.0667$$

To obtain the true voltage, multiply the voltmeter reading by 1.0667.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Ammeter Loading Error Percentage

**Problem Setup:**
A circuit has EMF **E = 12 V**, source resistance **$R_s = 1 \Omega$**, and load resistance **$R_L = 11 \Omega$**. An ammeter of internal resistance **$R_A = 0.5 \Omega$** is inserted to measure the current.

**Step 1 — True current (no ammeter):**

$$I_{true} = \frac{E}{R_s + R_L} = \frac{12}{1 + 11} = \frac{12}{12} = 1.000 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 — Measured current (ammeter adds $R_A$ in series):**

$$I_{measured} = \frac{E}{R_s + R_A + R_L} = \frac{12}{1 + 0.5 + 11} = \frac{12}{12.5} = 0.960 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 — Absolute error:**

$$\Delta I = I_{true} - I_{measured} = 1.000 - 0.960 = 0.040 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4 — Percentage error:**

$$e_A = \frac{\Delta I}{I_{true}} \times 100\% = \frac{0.040}{1.000} \times 100\% = 4.0\%$$

**Step 5 — Verification with the short formula:**

$$e_A = \frac{R_A}{R_s + R_L + R_A} \times 100\% = \frac{0.5}{12.5} \times 100\% = 4.0\% \checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Correction factor:**

$$k = \frac{I_{true}}{I_{measured}} = \frac{1.000}{0.960} = 1.0417$$

To recover the true current, multiply the ammeter reading by 1.0417.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: How to Choose Instruments

> [!TIP]
> **Use this matrix to justify your instrument choice in KTU 14-mark problems.**

| **Measurand Type** | **Source Impedance** | **Required Instrument Impedance** | **Justification (Rule of Thumb)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| DC Voltage | Low ($R_s < 1 \text{ k}\Omega$) | $R_V \geq 10 R_s$ | Loading error $\leq 9.1\%$ |
| DC Voltage | High ($R_s > 1 \text{ M}\Omega$) | $R_V \geq 100 R_s$ (use FET/EMF meter) | Loading error $\leq 1\%$ |
| DC Current | High-power ($R_L \gg R_A$) | $R_A \leq 0.01 R_L$ | Loading error $\leq 1\%$ |
| DC Current | Low-power (small $R_L$) | Use external shunt to bypass $R_A$ | Reduces effective $R_A$ by $m+1$ |
| AC High-Frequency | $50 \Omega$ transmission line | Use 10× active probe ($10 \text{ M}\Omega, < 10 \text{ pF}$) | Reduces capacitive loading 10× |
| Bridge Output | Detector resistance $R_D$ | $R_D \geq 100 \times R_{bridge,\text{eq}}$ | Maintains bridge balance |
| Transducer Output | $Z_{out,\text{sensor}}$ | $Z_{in,\text{amp}} \geq 100 \times Z_{out,\text{sensor}}$ | Signal loss $\leq 1\%$ |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Loading Effect Mechanism (Generalized)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[True Source Signal] --> B[Source Internal Impedance Zs]
    B --> C[Original Circuit Node with True Value]
    C --> D[Instrument Connection Point]
    D --> E[Instrument Input Impedance Zin]
    E --> F[Internal Sensing and Display]
    F --> G[Measured Value Output]
    D --> H[Slight Current Diverted into Instrument]
    H --> I[Perturbation of Original Node]
    I --> C
    C --> J[Difference between True and Measured]
    J --> K[Loading Error Percentage]
```

### 4.2 Schematic Flow: Voltmeter Loading Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[EMF Source E volts] --> B[Internal Resistance Rs]
    B --> C[Node A in Original Circuit]
    C --> D[True Voltage Vtrue across RL]
    C --> E[Voltmeter Connected with Resistance Rv]
    E --> F[New Effective Resistance Reff Parallel of RL and Rv]
    F --> G[Reff less than RL]
    G --> H[Voltage Divider Reconfigured]
    H --> I[Voltmeter Reads Vmeasured which is less than Vtrue]
    I --> J[Loading Error Calculated as Vtrue minus Vmeasured over Vtrue times 100]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Ammeter Loading Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[EMF Source E] --> B[Source Resistance Rs]
    B --> C[Load Resistance RL Original Loop]
    C --> D[True Current Itrue equals E over Rs plus RL]
    B --> E[Ammeter Inserted with Resistance Ra]
    E --> F[New Loop Resistance Rs plus Ra plus RL]
    F --> G[Ammeter Reads Imeasured less than Itrue]
    G --> H[Loading Error equals Ra over Total Loop Resistance times 100]
```

### 4.4 Comparative Architecture: Loaded vs Unloaded Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Unloaded Reference State"
        U1[Source E and Rs] --> U2[True Load RL]
        U2 --> U3[True Value Vtrue or Itrue]
    end
    subgraph "Loaded Measurement State"
        L1[Source E and Rs] --> L2[Effective Load Reff with Instrument]
        L2 --> L3[Measured Value Vm or Im]
        L3 --> L4[Loading Error equals True minus Measured over True times 100]
    end
    U3 -. comparison .-> L4
```

### 4.5 Mitigation Strategy Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Loading Error Detected] --> B{Is Error less than 1 percent}
    B -->|Yes| C[Accept Reading as is]
    B -->|No| D{Type of Measurement}
    D -->|Voltage| E[Use Voltmeter with Rv greater than 100 Rs]
    D -->|Current| F[Use Ammeter with Ra less than 0.01 RL]
    D -->|High Frequency| G[Use 10x Active Probe with Low Capacitance]
    D -->|Bridge Detector| H[Use High Impedance Detector greater than 100 times Bridge Impedance]
    E --> I[Recheck Loading Error]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{Error now less than 1 percent}
    J -->|No| K[Apply Mathematical Correction Factor]
    J -->|Yes| C
    K --> C
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "Loading Effect" in instrumentation. Why is it important to minimize it in precision measurements?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Loading effect is the phenomenon wherein the connection of a measuring instrument to a circuit under test disturbs the original operating conditions of the circuit, causing the measured value to deviate from the true value. This occurs because every practical instrument draws some energy (current or power) from the circuit being measured, thereby altering the very quantity it is intended to measure.

**Importance of minimization (1.5 marks):** Minimizing loading effect is critical in precision measurements because it directly determines measurement accuracy. Excessive loading introduces systematic errors that cannot be eliminated by repeated measurements. In sensitive applications such as biomedical signal acquisition (ECG, EEG), high-impedance transducer readouts, and high-frequency RF measurements, loading effects can render measurements completely meaningless. Proper design ensures the measurement process is non-invasive and faithful to the true signal.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**A voltmeter has an internal resistance of 200 kΩ. It is used to measure the voltage across a 50 kΩ resistor connected to a source of 30 V with 10 kΩ internal resistance. Calculate the percentage loading error.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Effective load resistance when voltmeter is connected:**

$$R_{eff} = \frac{R_L \cdot R_V}{R_L + R_V} = \frac{50 \times 200}{50 + 200} = \frac{10{,}000}{250} = 40 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — True and measured voltages:**

$$V_{true} = 30 \cdot \frac{50}{10 + 50} = 25.00 \text{ V}$$

$$V_{measured} = 30 \cdot \frac{40}{10 + 40} = 24.00 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Percentage loading error:**

$$e_V = \frac{25.00 - 24.00}{25.00} \times 100\% = 4.00\%$$

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Format)

---

## **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the **loading effect of a voltmeter** when measuring voltage across a high-resistance source. Derive the expression for the percentage loading error. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **Wheatstone bridge** is balanced with all four arms of 120 Ω. A detector of resistance 100 Ω is connected across the bridge output. Calculate: (i) the balance condition (assume infinite detector resistance), (ii) the actual bridge output voltage when a 5 V DC excitation is applied and a 10 Ω resistor is added to one arm, and (iii) the percentage loading error introduced by the finite detector resistance. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Voltmeter Loading Effect (7 marks)

**Circuit Diagram (1 mark):**

```
        Rs          RL
   +---/\/\---+---/\/\---+
   |          |          |
  (E)         |          |
   |          |          |
   +----------+----------+
              |
              |
             ===  (Voltmeter Rv)
              |
              +
```

**Explanation (3 marks):** When a voltmeter of internal resistance $R_V$ is connected across a load resistor $R_L$ that is fed by a source with EMF E and internal resistance $R_s$, the voltmeter appears in parallel with $R_L$. Since the voltmeter is not ideal (it has finite resistance), it draws a small current from the circuit. This current flows through $R_s$, creating an extra voltage drop across $R_s$ and reducing the voltage across $R_L$ from its true value. The result is that the voltmeter reads a value **less** than the actual voltage that existed when no instrument was connected.

**Derivation of percentage error (3 marks):**

**True voltage** (no voltmeter):
$$V_{true} = E \cdot \frac{R_L}{R_s + R_L}$$

**Effective load** (with voltmeter):
$$R_{eff} = \frac{R_L \cdot R_V}{R_L + R_V}$$

**Measured voltage:**
$$V_{measured} = E \cdot \frac{R_{eff}}{R_s + R_{eff}}$$

**Substituting and simplifying**:
$$V_{measured} = E \cdot \frac{R_L R_V}{R_s R_L + R_s R_V + R_L R_V}$$

**Loading error percentage:**
$$e_V = \frac{V_{true} - V_{measured}}{V_{true}} \times 100\%$$

After algebraic simplification (assuming $R_L \gg R_s$ for clarity), this reduces to:
$$e_V \approx \frac{R_s}{R_s + R_V} \times 100\%$$

This shows that the error depends only on the source resistance and voltmeter resistance, and can be minimized by making $R_V$ as large as possible (ideally infinite).

---

#### Part (b) — Wheatstone Bridge Loading (7 marks)

**Given:** All four arms $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = 120 \Omega$, detector $R_D = 100 \Omega$, excitation $E = 5\text{ V DC}$, one arm increased by $\Delta R = 10 \Omega$.

**(i) Balance condition with infinite detector resistance (2 marks):**

The Wheatstone bridge is balanced when no current flows through the detector, which occurs when the ratio of resistances in adjacent arms is equal:

$$\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{R_3}{R_4}$$

For our values:
$$\frac{120}{120} = \frac{120}{120} = 1.000 \checkmark$$

The bridge is balanced when $R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3$ (or $R_1 R_3 = R_2 R_4$ depending on configuration). With all arms equal at 120 Ω, the bridge is in nominal balance, and the detector reads **0 V** (assuming infinite detector resistance).

**(ii) Bridge output with 10 Ω added to one arm and finite detector (3 marks):**

New configuration: $R_1 = 120 + 10 = 130 \Omega$, $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = 120 \Omega$, $R_D = 100 \Omega$.

Node voltages (using Thévenin equivalent at detector terminals):

$$V_{th} = E \left(\frac{R_3}{R_1 + R_3} - \frac{R_4}{R_2 + R_4}\right) = 5 \left(\frac{120}{130 + 120} - \frac{120}{120 + 120}\right)$$

$$V_{th} = 5 \left(\frac{120}{250} - \frac{120}{240}\right) = 5 \left(0.4800 - 0.5000\right) = 5 \cdot (-0.0200) = -0.1000 \text{ V}$$

$$R_{th} = (R_1 \parallel R_3) + (R_2 \parallel R_4) = \frac{130 \cdot 120}{130 + 120} + \frac{120 \cdot 120}{120 + 120}$$

$$R_{th} = \frac{15{,}600}{250} + \frac{14{,}400}{240} = 62.4 + 60.0 = 122.4 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Detector voltage (with loading):**
$$V_D = V_{th} \cdot \frac{R_D}{R_{th} + R_D} = -0.1000 \cdot \frac{100}{122.4 + 100} = -0.1000 \cdot \frac{100}{222.4}$$

$$V_D = -0.1000 \cdot 0.4496 = -0.04496 \text{ V} \approx -44.96 \text{ mV}$$

**Detector voltage (without loading, $R_D = \infty$):**
$$V_{D,\text{unloaded}} = V_{th} = -0.1000 \text{ V} = -100.00 \text{ mV}$$

**(iii) Percentage loading error (2 marks):**

$$e = \frac{|V_{D,\text{unloaded}}| - |V_D|}{|V_{D,\text{unloaded}}|} \times 100\% = \frac{100.00 - 44.96}{100.00} \times 100\% = 55.04\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Loading Effect Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to compute $R_{eff}$:** Many students directly write the voltmeter reading as $E \cdot \frac{R_V}{R_s + R_V}$ without first computing the effective parallel resistance with $R_L$. This loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Sign confusion in bridge problems:** Always retain the sign of $V_{th}$ and $V_D$; do not assume a magnitude and write a positive number when the detector polarity is reversed.
> 3. **Confusing "loading error" with "measurement error":** Loading error is the *change* in indicated value due to the instrument; the loading error percentage is computed with the **ideal (no-load) value as the denominator**, not the measured value.
> 4. **Unit mismatches in cascaded stages:** When output impedance and input impedance are in different units (e.g., one in Ω, another in kΩ), normalize first; the KTU evaluator will mark this as careless.
> 5. **Forgetting the rule of thumb in justification:** When asked to "suggest a remedy," do not just state it — also justify with the $10\times$ or $100\times$ rule, since this is what the answer key looks for.

---

## **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **loading effect of an ammeter** with a circuit diagram. Derive the expression for the percentage loading error and state the design rule of thumb for selecting an ammeter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ammeter with internal resistance 0.2 Ω is used in a circuit with a 12 V battery, source resistance 0.3 Ω, and load resistance 5.5 Ω. Compute: (i) the true current, (ii) the measured current, (iii) the percentage loading error, and (iv) the corrected true current from the measured value. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Ammeter Loading Effect (7 marks)

**Circuit Diagram (1 mark):**

```
    +---/\/\---+---/\/\---+---/\/\---+
    |    Rs    |   RA     |    RL    |
   (E)         | (Ammeter)|          |
    |          |          |          |
    +----------+----------+----------+
```

**Explanation (3 marks):** An ammeter must be connected in series with the load to measure current. However, every practical ammeter has a small but non-zero internal resistance $R_A$. When this ammeter is inserted into the circuit, it adds $R_A$ in series with the existing loop resistance $R_s + R_L$. The total circuit resistance increases, and the current that flows (and hence the current the ammeter reads) becomes **less** than the true current that would flow in the absence of the ammeter. This is the **ammeter loading effect**, and it causes a systematic under-reading of the true current.

**Derivation of percentage error (2 marks):**

**True current:**
$$I_{true} = \frac{E}{R_s + R_L}$$

**Measured current:**
$$I_{measured} = \frac{E}{R_s + R_A + R_L}$$

**Loading error percentage:**
$$e_A = \frac{I_{true} - I_{measured}}{I_{true}} \times 100\%$$

$$e_A = \frac{R_A}{R_s + R_L + R_A} \times 100\%$$

**Design rule of thumb (1 mark):** For loading error to be less than 1%, the ammeter's internal resistance must be less than or equal to **1/100th** of the total circuit resistance (excluding the ammeter). Equivalently, $R_A \leq 0.01 \cdot (R_s + R_L)$.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:** $E = 12\text{ V}$, $R_s = 0.3 \text{ }\Omega$, $R_L = 5.5 \text{ }\Omega$, $R_A = 0.2 \text{ }\Omega$.

**(i) True current (1.5 marks):**

$$I_{true} = \frac{E}{R_s + R_L} = \frac{12}{0.3 + 5.5} = \frac{12}{5.8} = 2.0690 \text{ A}$$

**(ii) Measured current (1.5 marks):**

$$I_{measured} = \frac{E}{R_s + R_A + R_L} = \frac{12}{0.3 + 0.2 + 5.5} = \frac{12}{6.0} = 2.0000 \text{ A}$$

**(iii) Percentage loading error (2 marks):**

$$e_A = \frac{I_{true} - I_{measured}}{I_{true}} \times 100\% = \frac{2.0690 - 2.0000}{2.0690} \times 100\%$$

$$e_A = \frac{0.0690}{2.0690} \times 100\% = 3.335\%$$

**Cross-check using short formula:**

$$e_A = \frac{R_A}{R_s + R_L + R_A} \times 100\% = \frac{0.2}{6.0} \times 100\% = 3.333\% \checkmark$$

**(iv) Correction factor and corrected true current (2 marks):**

$$k = \frac{I_{true}}{I_{measured}} = \frac{2.0690}{2.0000} = 1.0345$$

$$I_{true,\text{corrected}} = I_{measured} \times k = 2.0000 \times 1.0345 = 2.0690 \text{ A} \checkmark$$

The corrected value matches the true current, confirming the correction factor is valid.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**
>
> ✅ **Core Concept:** Every instrument perturbs the system it measures; the perturbation is the **Loading Effect**.
>
> ✅ **Voltmeter Rule:** Voltmeter reads **LOW** because its finite $R_V$ parallels the load and diverts current through $R_s$. Fix: use $R_V \geq 10\times$ to $100\times$ the source resistance.
>
> ✅ **Ammeter Rule:** Ammeter reads **LOW** because its $R_A$ adds in series, increasing total loop resistance. Fix: use $R_A \leq 0.01 \times$ total circuit resistance.
>
> ✅ **Master Equations to Memorize:**
> - $V_m = E \cdot \dfrac{R_{eff}}{R_s + R_{eff}}$ where $R_{eff} = \dfrac{R_L R_V}{R_L + R_V}$
> - $I_m = \dfrac{E}{R_s + R_A + R_L}$
> - $e_V = \dfrac{R_s}{R_s + R_V} \times 100\%$ (simplified case)
> - $e_A = \dfrac{R_A}{R_s + R_L + R_A} \times 100\%$
>
> ✅ **Cascaded Stage Rule:** $V_{out} = V_{src} \cdot \dfrac{Z_{in,next}}{Z_{out,prev} + Z_{in,next}}$ — always verify the loading is acceptable.
>
> ✅ **Bridge Loading:** Detector with finite $R_D$ loads the bridge output, reducing the measured imbalance voltage. Use $R_D \geq 100 \times R_{th,\text{bridge}}$.
>
> ✅ **High-Frequency Loading:** Capacitive loading from oscilloscope probes dominates; use **10× passive** or **active FET probes** to minimize this.
>
> ✅ **Transducer Loading:** Match by ensuring $Z_{in,\text{amplifier}} \geq 100 \times Z_{out,\text{transducer}}$.
>
> ✅ **Correction Factor:** To recover true value from measured: $V_{true} = V_m \cdot (1 + R_s/R_V)$ and $I_{true} = I_m \cdot (1 + R_A/(R_s + R_L))$.
>
> ✅ **Common KTU Mistake to Avoid:** Never assume the voltmeter reads the open-circuit voltage of the source unless $R_V \to \infty$.
>
> ✅ **Always draw the equivalent circuit** (with the instrument's internal resistance shown explicitly) when solving loading effect problems. This is what earns full marks.
>
> ✅ **Always state the assumption** that the instrument is ideal except for its internal resistance — KTU evaluators check for this explicit statement.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Input and Output Impedances
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCICT205 - BASIC OF INSTRUMENTATION ENGINEERING (INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Input and Output Impedances # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Input Impedance ($Z_{in}$)? The **Input Impedance** of any electronic device, instrument, or two-port network is defined as the equivalent impedance *presented by the device to whatever is driving it*. It is the ratio of the voltage appearing across the input terminals to the current flowing into those terminals, measured looking **into** the input port. Mathematically expressed as: $$Z_{in} = \frac{V_{in}}{I_{in}}\bigg\vert_{V_{out} = \text{test condition}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition:** Input impedance is the impedance measured at the input terminals of a circuit when the output is connected to its normal load (or open-circuited, depending on the convention taught in your course textbook). For an **ideal voltmeter or amplifier input**, $Z_{in} \rightarrow \infty$ (open circuit, draws no current). ## 1.2 What is Output Impedance ($Z_{out}$)? The **Output Impedance** of a device is the equivalent internal impedance *looking back into the output terminals* of the source. It represents the "resistance" that the source itself possesses internally before delivering current to a load. Mathematically: $$Z_{out} = \frac{V_{out,\text{open}} - V_{out,\text{loaded}}}{I_{out,\text{loaded}}}$$ Or equivalently, the Thévenin equivalent resistance seen at the output port when all independent sources inside the device are deactivated (voltage sources shorted, current sources opened). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Concept:** For an **ideal voltage source or ideal amplifier output**, $Z_{out} = 0\ \Omega$ (a perfect voltage source has zero internal resistance). For an **ideal current source output**, $Z_{out} \rightarrow \infty$. ## 1.3 Source Impedance ($Z_s$) and Load Impedance ($Z_L$) — The Supporting Cast | Term | Symbol | What it represents | |------|--------|--------------------| | Source Impedance | $Z_s$ | The internal impedance of the **signal source** driving the circuit (e.g., the output resistance of a function generator, a sensor's Thevenin resistance). | | Load Impedance | $Z_L$ | The impedance of the **device being driven** (e.g., a speaker, an oscilloscope probe, the next stage of amplification). | ## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Hose and Nozzle" System Imagine the following real-world scenario: 🚿 **A water tap connected to a hose with a nozzle at the end.** - The **tap + hose combination** has a fixed internal restriction. Even when the nozzle is removed, the tap cannot deliver infinite flow. This internal restriction is exactly like **$Z_s$** (source impedance) or **$Z_{out}$** of the previous stage. - The **nozzle** has a certain opening width that restricts water flow even more. This is exactly like **$Z_{in}$** (input impedance) of the next stage. - When the nozzle is too narrow (low $Z_{in}$), the pressure at the hose end **collapses** — you barely get any water. This is the **loading effect** in action. > [!NOTE] > **The Golden Rule of Instrumentation:** A high-fidelity measurement system should have **very high input impedance** (to not "steal" signal from the source) and **very low output impedance** (to drive the next stage without losing signal strength). This is the "**10× Rule**" — a voltmeter's input impedance should be at least **10 times** the source impedance it measures. ## 1.5 Why Are These Concepts Non-Negotiable in Instrumentation? In instrumentation, you routinely **cascade** devices: **Sensor → Amplifier → Filter → ADC → Display** Each "cascaded junction" is a potential source of signal degradation due to impedance mismatch. Without understanding $Z_{in}$ and $Z_{out}$, an engineer cannot: 1. Select the correct measuring instrument (DMM, oscilloscope, spectrum analyzer). 2. Design an amplifier-to-transducer interface. 3. Calculate loading errors in sensor readings. 4. Implement proper **impedance matching** for maximum power transfer (e.g., in communication systems). 5. Diagnose why a "perfectly working" circuit gives wrong readings in the field. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 How Input Impedance Behaves — The Physics The input impedance is a **frequency-dependent, complex quantity** in general, having both real (resistive) and imaginary (reactive) components. $$Z_{in}(j\omega) = R_{in}(\omega) + jX_{in}(\omega)$$ Where: - $R_{in}(\omega)$ = real part (resistive loading) - $X_{in}(\omega)$ = imaginary part (capacitive or inductive reactance) For most instrumentation amplifiers (e.g., op-amp based), the input is dominantly **resistive + capacitive**, with typical values: - $R_{in}$ ranges from **$10^6\ \Omega$** (general op-amp) to **$10^{12}\ \Omega$** (FET/CMOS input op-amp). - $C_{in}$ is typically a few **pF** (picofarads). ## 2.2 How Output Impedance Behaves — The Physics The output impedance represents how much the output voltage sags when a load demands current. A real source with $Z_{out}$ behaves as: $$V_{out,\text{loaded}} = V_{out,\text{open}} \cdot \frac{Z_L}{Z_L + Z_{out}}$$ This is precisely the **voltage divider rule** applied to a non-ideal source. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Insight:** If $Z_{out} \ll Z_L$ (output impedance is much smaller than load impedance), the loaded voltage is essentially equal to the open-circuit voltage — this is the **"stiff" voltage source** condition. Most good voltage amplifiers are designed to operate in this regime. ## 2.3 The Loading Effect — A Cascaded System Analysis Consider a generic cascade: ``` [Source Zs] → [Device 1: Zin1, Zout1] → [Device 2: Zin2, Zout2] → [Load ZL] ``` At every junction, the actual signal transferred is reduced by the voltage divider formed between the upstream $Z_{out}$ and the downstream $Z_{in}$. The general **loaded voltage gain** is: $$A_{v,\text{loaded}} = A_{v,\text{intrinsic}} \cdot \frac{Z_{in,\text{next stage}}}{Z_{in,\text{next stage}} + Z_{out,\text{this stage}}}$$ ## 2.4 Maximum Power Transfer Theorem (KTU Hot Topic) When the goal is to deliver **maximum power** (not maximum voltage) to a load — typical in RF, communication, and transducer design — the condition is: $$\boxed{Z_L = Z_s^{*}}$$ That is, the load impedance must be the **complex conjugate** of the source impedance. For purely resistive sources: $R_L = R_s$, and the maximum power delivered is: $$P_{\max} = \frac{V_{s}^{2}}{4 R_s}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** When maximum power is transferred, only **50% of the source power** reaches the load — the other 50% is dissipated in the source. This is why power transfer ≠ efficiency. In instrumentation, we usually prioritize **voltage transfer fidelity**, not power transfer, so $Z_L \gg Z_s$ is preferred. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Typical Unit / Condition | |---|---------|-------------|---------------------------| | 1 | $Z_{in} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{I_{in}}$ | Definition of input impedance | Ohms ($\Omega$) | | 2 | $Z_{out} = \left.\dfrac{V_{th}}{I_{sc}}\right\vert_{\text{sources off}}$ | Thévenin equivalent output impedance | Ohms ($\Omega$) | | 3 | $V_{L} = V_{oc} \cdot \dfrac{Z_L}{Z_L + Z_{out}}$ | Loaded output voltage (voltage divider) | Volts (V) | | 4 | $\% \text{Loading Error} = \dfrac{Z_{out}}{Z_L + Z_{out}} \times 100$ | Percentage drop in signal due to loading | Dimensionless (%) | | 5 | $A_{v,\text{loaded}} = A_{v} \cdot \dfrac{Z_{in}}{Z_{in} + Z_s}$ | Voltage gain with source loading | Dimensionless | | 6 | $Z_L = Z_s^{*}$ | Maximum power transfer condition | Ohms ($\Omega$) | | 7 | $P_{\max} = \dfrac{V_{s}^{2}}{4 R_s}$ | Maximum power transfer (resistive case) | Watts (W) | | 8 | $Z_{in,\text{ideal voltmeter}} \to \infty$ | Ideal voltmeter draws no current | $\Omega$ | | 9 | $Z_{out,\text{ideal voltage source}} = 0$ | Ideal voltage source has no internal drop | $\Omega$ | | 10 | $Z_{out,\text{ideal current source}} \to \infty$ | Ideal current source delivers constant current | $\Omega$ | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Required Impedance Behavior | |-------------|----------------------------| | Oscilloscope probe ($10\times$) | High $Z_{in}$ to minimally load the circuit under test | | Function generator output | Low $Z_{out}$ (typically $50\ \Omega$) for predictable drive | | Microphone to pre-amp interface | High $Z_{in}$ pre-amp; low $Z_{out}$ microphone | | Speaker to amplifier | Impedance matching for power transfer | | ECG / EEG electrodes | Very high $Z_{in}$ amplifier ($>10^9\ \Omega$) for biological signals | | RF transmission line | $50\ \Omega$ matched system to prevent reflections | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Loaded Output Voltage of a Non-Ideal Source ### Setup Consider a real (non-ideal) voltage source modelled as an **ideal source $V_s$ in series with an internal (output) impedance $Z_{out}$**, driving a **load impedance $Z_L$**. ### Step 1 — Write Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the loop $$V_s = I \cdot Z_{out} + I \cdot Z_L$$ Where $I$ is the current flowing in the loop. ### Step 2 — Solve for the loop current $$I = \frac{V_s}{Z_{out} + Z_L}$$ ### Step 3 — Express the loaded voltage across the load The voltage across the load is the current times the load impedance: $$V_L = I \cdot Z_L = \frac{V_s \cdot Z_L}{Z_{out} + Z_L}$$ ### Step 4 — Define the transfer ratio $$\frac{V_L}{V_s} = \frac{Z_L}{Z_{out} + Z_L}$$ > **Interpretation:** This is the **voltage divider ratio**. When $Z_L \gg Z_{out}$, the ratio tends to 1 (no loss). When $Z_L \ll Z_{out}$, the ratio tends to 0 (catastrophic loss). ## 3.2 Derivation: Loading Error Percentage The loading error is defined as the fractional difference between the loaded voltage and the ideal (unloaded) voltage. ### Step 1 — Ideal voltage = source voltage $$V_{\text{ideal}} = V_s$$ ### Step 2 — Actual voltage across load (from §3.1) $$V_{\text{actual}} = \frac{V_s \cdot Z_L}{Z_{out} + Z_L}$$ ### Step 3 — Compute the fractional error $$\text{Error} = \frac{V_{\text{ideal}} - V_{\text{actual}}}{V_{\text{ideal}}} = 1 - \frac{Z_L}{Z_{out} + Z_L}$$ ### Step 4 — Simplify using common denominator $$\text{Error} = \frac{Z_{out} + Z_L - Z_L}{Z_{out} + Z_L} = \frac{Z_{out}}{Z_{out} + Z_L}$$ ### Step 5 — Convert to percentage $$\boxed{\%\text{Loading Error} = \frac{Z_{out}}{Z_{out} + Z_L} \times 100\%}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation: Effect of Source Impedance on Amplifier Input ### Setup A signal source with open-circuit voltage $V_s$ and source resistance $R_s$ drives an amplifier of input resistance $R_{in}$. ### Step 1 — The amplifier "looks like" a load to the source $$V_{in} = V_s \cdot \frac{R_{in}}{R_{in} + R_s}$$ ### Step 2 — Define the input attenuation factor $$\alpha = \frac{V_{in}}{V_s} = \frac{R_{in}}{R_{in} + R_s}$$ ### Step 3 — Special cases | Condition | Result | Engineering meaning | |-----------|--------|--------------------| | $R_{in} \gg R_s$ | $\alpha \to 1$ | Amplifier captures the full source voltage. **DESIRABLE.** | | $R_{in} = R_s$ | $\alpha = 0.5$ | Half the signal is lost across the source resistance. | | $R_{in} \ll R_s$ | $\alpha \to 0$ | Amplifier "kills" the signal. **AVOID AT ALL COSTS.** | ## 3.4 Derivation: Cascaded Two-Port Loading ### Setup Two amplifier stages with intrinsic (no-load) voltage gains $A_{v1}$ and $A_{v2}$, output resistances $R_{o1}$ and $R_{o2}$, and input resistances $R_{i1}$ and $R_{i2}$ are cascaded. ### Step 1 — Voltage delivered to Stage 1 input $$V_{i1} = V_s \cdot \frac{R_{i1}}{R_{i1} + R_s}$$ ### Step 2 — Stage 1 output voltage (open-circuit, before Stage 2 loads it) $$V_{o1,\text{open}} = A_{v1} \cdot V_{i1}$$ ### Step 3 — Stage 1 output voltage AFTER Stage 2 loads it $$V_{i2} = V_{o1,\text{open}} \cdot \frac{R_{i2}}{R_{i2} + R_{o1}} = A_{v1} \cdot V_{i1} \cdot \frac{R_{i2}}{R_{i2} + R_{o1}}$$ ### Step 4 — Overall voltage gain from source to Stage 2 input $$A_{v,\text{total}} = \frac{V_{i2}}{V_s} = A_{v1} \cdot \frac{R_{i1}}{R_{i1} + R_s} \cdot \frac{R_{i2}}{R_{i2} + R_{o1}}$$ > **Conclusion:** The overall gain is degraded by **two loading factors** — the source loading on Stage 1, and the Stage 2 loading on Stage 1's output. This is why impedance ratios are designed to be very large. ## 3.5 Derivation: Maximum Power Transfer (Resistive Case) ### Step 1 — Power delivered to a resistive load $R_L$ from source $V_s$ with source resistance $R_s$ $$P_L = I^2 \cdot R_L = \left(\frac{V_s}{R_s + R_L}\right)^2 \cdot R_L$$ ### Step 2 — Differentiate $P_L$ with respect to $R_L$ to find the maximum $$\frac{dP_L}{dR_L} = V_s^2 \cdot \frac{(R_s + R_L)^2 - R_L \cdot 2(R_s + R_L)}{(R_s + R_L)^4}$$ ### Step 3 — Simplify the numerator $$(R_s + R_L) - 2 R_L = R_s - R_L$$ ### Step 4 — Set the derivative to zero $$R_s - R_L = 0 \implies R_L = R_s$$ ### Step 5 — Substitute back to find $P_{\max}$ $$P_{\max} = \left(\frac{V_s}{R_s + R_s}\right)^2 \cdot R_s = \frac{V_s^2}{4 R_s}$$ ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Examples ### Example A: Loading Error in a Sensor Readout **Given:** A sensor has output impedance $Z_{out} = 600\ \Omega$. A voltmeter with $Z_{in} = 1\ \text{M}\Omega$ is connected to it. The true open-circuit sensor voltage is $V_{oc} = 5.000\ \text{V}$. **Find:** (a) The actual voltmeter reading. (b) The percentage loading error. #### Solution **Part (a):** Apply the loaded voltage formula: $$V_L = V_{oc} \cdot \frac{Z_{in}}{Z_{in} + Z_{out}} = 5.000 \cdot \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000 + 600}$$ $$V_L = 5.000 \cdot \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{1{,}000{,}600} = 5.000 \cdot 0.99940 = 4.9970\ \text{V}$$ **Part (b):** Percentage error: $$\%\text{Error} = \frac{600}{1{,}000{,}600} \times 100\% = 0.05999\% \approx 0.06\%$$ > **Engineering verdict:** This loading error is acceptable for general measurements. If higher accuracy were required, a voltmeter with $Z_{in} > 10\ \text{M}\Omega$ would be used. ### Example B: Two-Stage Amplifier Cascade **Given:** Stage 1: $A_{v1} = 20$, $R_{o1} = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_{i1} = 100\ \text{k}\Omega$. Stage 2: $R_{i2} = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$. Source: $V_s = 10\ \text{mV}$, $R_s = 600\ \Omega$. **Find:** Voltage at Stage 2 input. #### Solution **Step 1:** Source loading factor: $$\alpha_s = \frac{R_{i1}}{R_{i1} + R_s} = \frac{100{,}000}{100{,}000 + 600} = 0.99403$$ **Step 2:** Inter-stage loading factor: $$\alpha_{12} = \frac{R_{i2}}{R_{i2} + R_{o1}} = \frac{10{,}000}{10{,}000 + 1{,}000} = 0.90909$$ **Step 3:** Voltage at Stage 1 input: $$V_{i1} = V_s \cdot \alpha_s = 10\ \text{mV} \cdot 0.99403 = 9.9403\ \text{mV}$$ **Step 4:** Voltage at Stage 2 input: $$V_{i2} = A_{v1} \cdot V_{i1} \cdot \alpha_{12} = 20 \cdot 9.9403\ \text{mV} \cdot 0.90909 = 180.73\ \text{mV}$$ > **Engineering verdict:** Without loading effects, the expected voltage would have been $20 \cdot 10\ \text{mV} = 200\ \text{mV}$. The combined loading losses have reduced the signal by approximately **9.6%**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Canonical Two-Port Network Model The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how every electronic subsystem can be abstracted as a **two-port network** with explicit input and output impedances, connected between a real source and a real load. ```mermaid graph LR VS[Voltage Source Vs]:::sourceNode ZS[Source Impedance Zs]:::impNode ZIN[Input Impedance Zin]:::impNode AMP[Two Port Network
Intrinsic Gain Av]:::coreNode ZOUT[Output Impedance Zout]:::impNode ZL[Load Impedance ZL]:::impNode VS -- Vsource --> ZS ZS -- Vdelivered to network --> ZIN ZIN -- Vin into network --> AMP AMP -- Vout_open from network --> ZOUT ZOUT -- Vout_loaded to load --> ZL classDef sourceNode fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B8770E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef coreNode fill:#9CC2E5,stroke:#1F4E79,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000 classDef impNode fill:#C6E0B4,stroke:#548235,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.2 How to Measure $Z_{in}$ — The Test Source Method The following Mermaid flowchart shows the *de facto* procedure to experimentally determine the input impedance of any device under test (DUT). ```mermaid graph TD AC[Apply AC Test Source
Vtest and ftest]:::inputNode MEASV[Measure Voltage Vin
across DUT input]:::meterNode MEASI[Measure Current Iin
into DUT input]:::meterNode CALC[Calculate Zin = Vin / Iin]:::processNode RESULT[Output Zin magnitude
and phase angle]:::outputNode AC --> MEASV AC --> MEASI MEASV --> CALC MEASI --> CALC CALC --> RESULT classDef inputNode fill:#F4B084,stroke:#A52A2A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef meterNode fill:#FFE699,stroke:#BF8F00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef processNode fill:#B4C7E7,stroke:#1F4E79,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef outputNode fill:#A9D18E,stroke:#548235,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.3 How to Measure $Z_{out}$ — The Half-Open-Circuit Test The classical technique to determine output impedance is the **half-voltage loading test** — drive the input with a fixed signal, measure the open-circuit output voltage, then attach a known load and measure the loaded output voltage. ```mermaid graph TD APPLY[Apply fixed input signal]:::inputNode VOC[Measure open circuit output voltage Voc]:::meterNode VLOAD[Connect known load RL and measure VL]:::meterNode SOLVE[Solve voltage divider:
VL = Voc times RL over RL plus Zout]:::processNode ZOUT[Zout equals RL times Voc minus VL divided by VL]:::outputNode APPLY --> VOC APPLY --> VLOAD VOC --> SOLVE VLOAD --> SOLVE SOLVE --> ZOUT classDef inputNode fill:#F4B084,stroke:#A52A2A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef meterNode fill:#FFE699,stroke:#BF8F00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef processNode fill:#B4C7E7,stroke:#1F4E79,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef outputNode fill:#A9D18E,stroke:#548235,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.4 Cascaded Instrumentation Chain — Loading Accumulation The diagram below maps out a typical real-world instrumentation chain, marking each junction where loading effects can degrade the signal. ```mermaid graph LR SENSOR[Sensor
Zout_sense]:::sourceNode COND[Signal Conditioner
Zin_cond, Zout_cond]:::stageNode AMP[Amplifier Stage
Zin_amp, Zout_amp]:::stageNode FILT[Filter Stage
Zin_filt, Zout_filt]:::stageNode ADC[ADC Input
Zin_adc]:::loadNode SENSOR -- junction 1 --> COND COND -- junction 2 --> AMP AMP -- junction 3 --> FILT FILT -- junction 4 --> ADC classDef sourceNode fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B8770E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef stageNode fill:#9CC2E5,stroke:#1F4E79,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef loadNode fill:#C6E0B4,stroke:#548235,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 ``` > **Key takeaway:** At every numbered junction above, the **output impedance of the upstream stage** forms a voltage divider with the **input impedance of the downstream stage**. The cumulative loss across all four junctions determines the final signal integrity at the ADC. ## 4.5 The Impedance Trade-Off Summary (Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph TD IDEAL[Ideal Instrument Design Goals]:::goalNode HIGHZIN[High Input Impedance
Zin approaches infinity]:::goodNode LOWZOUT[Low Output Impedance
Zout approaches zero]:::goodNode MATCHED[Matched for Power Transfer
Zin equals Zsource conjugate]:::goodNode IDEAL --> HIGHZIN IDEAL --> LOWZOUT IDEAL --> MATCHED classDef goalNode fill:#FFCCCC,stroke:#C00000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000 classDef goodNode fill:#A9D18E,stroke:#548235,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define input impedance and output impedance of an electronic instrument. What are their ideal values for a measuring voltmeter and a voltage source respectively?** **Model Answer:** The **input impedance** of an instrument is the impedance presented by the instrument to the signal source, defined as the ratio of voltage across the input terminals to the current drawn into those terminals. $$Z_{in} = \frac{V_{in}}{I_{in}}$$ The **output impedance** is the internal impedance of the instrument as seen from the output terminals, representing its ability to deliver signal to a load without voltage drop. **Ideal values:** - For a measuring voltmeter: $Z_{in} \to \infty$ (open circuit, so it draws zero current from the circuit being measured and does not disturb it). - For a voltage source: $Z_{out} = 0\ \Omega$ (so the output voltage remains constant regardless of the load current drawn). ### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is the loading effect? Why does it occur, and how can it be minimized?** **Model Answer:** The **loading effect** is the reduction in signal amplitude (or distortion) that occurs when a load is connected to a real (non-ideal) source, due to the voltage divider formed between the source's output impedance and the load's input impedance. It occurs because **no real source has zero output impedance** and **no real measuring instrument has infinite input impedance**. The finite impedances share the signal voltage. **How to minimize it:** 1. Use measuring instruments with very high input impedance (e.g., FET-input op-amps, modern DMMs with $Z_{in} > 10\ \text{M}\Omega$). 2. Use drivers and amplifiers with very low output impedance. 3. Insert a **buffer stage** (e.g., voltage follower using an op-amp) between the source and the load. 4. Ensure $Z_{in,\text{measuring}} \geq 10 \times Z_{out,\text{source}}$ as a design rule of thumb. --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question B — Option A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]` **(a) Derive the expression for the loaded output voltage of a non-ideal voltage source having source voltage $V_s$ and internal (output) impedance $Z_{out}$ when connected to a load $Z_L$.** (7 Marks) **(b) A sensor with output impedance $Z_{out} = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$ produces an open-circuit voltage of $V_{oc} = 100\ \text{mV}$. It is connected to a measuring instrument with input impedance $Z_{in} = 50\ \text{k}\Omega$. Calculate: (i) the actual voltage measured by the instrument, (ii) the percentage loading error, (iii) the voltage that would be measured if a unity-gain buffer (with $Z_{in} = 1\ \text{M}\Omega$ and $Z_{out} = 10\ \Omega$) were inserted between the sensor and the instrument.** (7 Marks) #### Model Solution for Option A — Part (a) **Step 1:** Model the real voltage source as an ideal source $V_s$ in series with $Z_{out}$. **Step 2:** Apply KVL around the source–load loop: $$V_s = I Z_{out} + I Z_L$$ **Step 3:** Solve for current: $$I = \frac{V_s}{Z_{out} + Z_L}$$ **Step 4:** Voltage across the load is: $$V_L = I Z_L = \frac{V_s \cdot Z_L}{Z_{out} + Z_L}$$ **Step 5:** This is the **loaded output voltage** expression. As $Z_L \to \infty$, $V_L \to V_s$ (no loading). As $Z_L \to 0$, $V_L \to 0$ (short-circuit). #### Model Solution for Option A — Part (b) **(i) Actual voltage measured by the instrument (direct connection):** $$V_{\text{measured}} = V_{oc} \cdot \frac{Z_{in}}{Z_{in} + Z_{out}} = 100\ \text{mV} \cdot \frac{50{,}000}{50{,}000 + 1{,}000}$$ $$V_{\text{measured}} = 100\ \text{mV} \cdot \frac{50{,}000}{51{,}000} = 100\ \text{mV} \cdot 0.98039 = 98.04\ \text{mV}$$ **(ii) Percentage loading error:** $$\%\text{Error} = \frac{Z_{out}}{Z_{in} + Z_{out}} \times 100 = \frac{1{,}000}{51{,}000} \times 100 = 1.96\%$$ **(iii) Voltage measured with unity-gain buffer inserted:** With a buffer: $Z_{in,\text{buffer}} = 1\ \text{M}\Omega$ (so the buffer sees virtually the full sensor voltage), and $Z_{out,\text{buffer}} = 10\ \Omega$ (so the instrument sees virtually the buffer output voltage). Voltage at buffer input: $$V_{\text{buf,in}} = V_{oc} \cdot \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000 + 1{,}000} = 100\ \text{mV} \cdot 0.99900 = 99.90\ \text{mV}$$ Voltage at instrument input (buffer output is unity gain): $$V_{\text{meas}} = 99.90\ \text{mV} \cdot \frac{50{,}000}{50{,}000 + 10} = 99.90\ \text{mV} \cdot 0.99980 = 99.88\ \text{mV}$$ **Loading error after buffering:** $$\%\text{Error}_{\text{buffered}} \approx 0.12\%$$ The buffer has reduced the loading error from **1.96% to 0.12%** — a 16× improvement. --- ### Question B — Option B (Internal Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]` **(a) State and explain the Maximum Power Transfer Theorem. Derive the condition for maximum power transfer in a purely resistive source–load system.** (7 Marks) **(b) A signal source with open-circuit voltage $V_s = 10\ \text{V}$ (rms) and internal resistance $R_s = 50\ \Omega$ is connected to a load $R_L$. Calculate: (i) the value of $R_L$ for maximum power transfer, (ii) the maximum power delivered to the load, (iii) the total power supplied by the source, and (iv) the efficiency of power transfer at this condition.** (7 Marks) #### Model Solution for Option B — Part (a) **Statement:** The Maximum Power Transfer Theorem states that *a load receives maximum power from a real source when the load impedance is equal to the complex conjugate of the source impedance.* For purely resistive systems, this reduces to $R_L = R_s$. **Derivation:** **Step 1:** Power delivered to the load: $$P_L = I^2 R_L = \left(\frac{V_s}{R_s + R_L}\right)^2 R_L$$ **Step 2:** Differentiate $P_L$ with respect to $R_L$: $$\frac{dP_L}{dR_L} = V_s^2 \cdot \frac{(R_s + R_L)^2 - R_L \cdot 2(R_s + R_L)}{(R_s + R_L)^4}$$ **Step 3:** Simplify numerator: $$(R_s + R_L) - 2 R_L = R_s - R_L$$ **Step 4:** Set derivative to zero: $$R_s - R_L = 0 \implies R_L = R_s$$ **Step 5:** Second derivative test confirms this is a maximum (not minimum). #### Model Solution for Option B — Part (b) **(i) Value of $R_L$ for maximum power transfer:** $$R_L = R_s = 50\ \Omega$$ **(ii) Maximum power delivered to the load:** $$P_{\max} = \frac{V_s^2}{4 R_s} = \frac{(10)^2}{4 \times 50} = \frac{100}{200} = 0.5\ \text{W}$$ **(iii) Total power supplied by the source:** $$P_{\text{total}} = V_s \cdot I = V_s \cdot \frac{V_s}{R_s + R_L} = \frac{V_s^2}{R_s + R_L}$$ $$P_{\text{total}} = \frac{100}{50 + 50} = 1\ \text{W}$$ **(iv) Efficiency of power transfer:** $$\eta = \frac{P_{\max}}{P_{\text{total}}} \times 100 = \frac{0.5}{1.0} \times 100 = 50\%$$ > **Conclusion:** At maximum power transfer, only 50% efficiency is achieved — confirming the KTU pitfall that maximum power transfer does not imply efficient operation. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Mixing up $Z_{in}$ and $Z_{out}$:** Students often write $V_L = V_{oc} \cdot Z_{out}/(Z_{in}+Z_{out})$ — the formula is the **opposite**. The numerator must always be the **load** (downstream) impedance. > 2. **Forgetting the percentage sign:** When asked for "loading error," students give a decimal value instead of multiplying by 100. Marks are deducted for missing units. > 3. **Conjugate notation confusion:** Maximum power transfer requires $Z_L = Z_s^{*}$ (complex conjugate), not simply $Z_L = Z_s$. This distinction matters for AC systems. > 4. **Forgetting the buffer's $Z_{out}$:** In cascaded problems, students often treat a buffer as ideal (zero $Z_{out}$ and infinite $Z_{in}$) without considering its finite values. Even unity-gain buffers have small but non-zero $Z_{out}$. > 5. **Not stating the ideal values:** Whenever defining input or output impedance, KTU examiners expect the ideal-case values ($Z_{in} = \infty$, $Z_{out} = 0$) to be explicitly stated. Omitting these forfeits 1 mark easily. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Input Impedance ($Z_{in}$):** Impedance looking **into** the input port of a device. $Z_{in} = V_{in}/I_{in}$. **Ideal: $\infty$** (no current drawn). - **Output Impedance ($Z_{out}$):** Thévenin equivalent impedance looking **back into** the output port. **Ideal: $0\ \Omega$** for voltage sources, $\infty$ for current sources. - **Source Impedance ($Z_s$):** The internal impedance of the source driving the system. **Load Impedance ($Z_L$):** The impedance of the device being driven. - **Loaded Voltage Formula (MUST MEMORIZE):** $V_L = V_{oc} \cdot Z_L / (Z_L + Z_{out})$. - **Loading Error Percentage:** $\% \text{Error} = Z_{out} / (Z_{out} + Z_L) \times 100$. - **Cascaded Gain Formula:** $A_{v,\text{total}} = A_{v1} \cdot \alpha_s \cdot \alpha_{12}$, where each $\alpha$ is a loading factor $< 1$. - **Maximum Power Transfer:** $Z_L = Z_s^{*}$ (resistive case: $R_L = R_s$), giving $P_{\max} = V_s^2 / 4R_s$ and only **50% efficiency**. - **Impedance Matching Hierarchy:** In instrumentation, **voltage transfer fidelity** (use $Z_L \gg Z_s$) is usually more important than power transfer; in communication/RF, **power transfer** (use $Z_L = Z_s^{*}$) is the goal. - **The 10× Rule of Thumb:** Voltmeter $Z_{in}$ should be at least **10× greater** than the source $Z_{out}$ to keep loading error below ~9%. - **Buffers Save the Day:** Inserting a unity-gain op-amp buffer between a high-$Z_{out}$ source and a low-$Z_{in}$ load drastically reduces loading error. - **Ideal Op-Amp Assumptions (revise here):** $Z_{in} \to \infty$, $Z_{out} = 0$ — these two assumptions alone let you solve most amplifier-stage problems in your exam.

topics.title: Input Impedances
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT205 - BASIC OF INSTRUMENTATION ENGINEERING (INSTRUMENT AND CONTROL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Input Impedances

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Input Impedance ($Z_{in}$)** is defined as the equivalent impedance presented by a device, circuit, or instrument at its input terminals, as "seen" by the source driving it. It is the ratio of the input voltage to the input current measured at the input port.

$$Z_{in} = \frac{V_{in}}{I_{in}}$$

Where:
- $V_{in}$ = RMS (or phasor) voltage at the input terminals (**Volts, V**)
- $I_{in}$ = RMS (or phasor) current flowing into the input terminals (**Amperes, A**)
- $Z_{in}$ = complex input impedance (**Ohms, $\Omega$**)

In polar/complex form:

$$Z_{in} = R_{in} + jX_{in} = \vert Z_{in} \vert \angle \theta$$

Where $R_{in}$ is the resistive component and $X_{in}$ is the reactive component (positive for inductive, negative for capacitive).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Input impedance is a *frequency-dependent* parameter in AC systems. At DC, only the resistive component $R_{in}$ matters. Always specify the frequency when stating input impedance values.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Tap and the Bucket"

Imagine a water tap (the source) pouring water into a bucket (the instrument). The **input impedance** is like the *size of the bucket's opening*:

- **Large opening (High $Z_{in}$):** The bucket accepts water easily without slowing the tap. The tap sees almost no load — the source voltage remains undisturbed. This is the ideal for a **voltmeter** or any **voltage-measuring device**.
- **Small opening (Low $Z_{in}$):** The bucket restricts flow, slowing the tap and "loading" it. The source is disturbed — measurement becomes inaccurate. This is the ideal for an **ammeter** (which needs current to flow through it).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The cardinal rule of instrumentation — *A voltage-measuring instrument should have infinite input impedance, and a current-measuring instrument should have zero input impedance*. Real instruments only approximate this.

## 1.3 Why Input Impedance Matters — The Loading Effect

When an instrument is connected to a circuit under test, the input impedance of the instrument forms a **voltage divider** with the source (Thévenin) impedance of the circuit. This perturbs the circuit and causes a **loading error**.

$$V_{measured} = V_{source} \cdot \frac{Z_{in}}{Z_{in} + Z_{source}}$$

The **loading error** in percent is:

$$\text{Loading Error (\%)} = \frac{Z_{source}}{Z_{in} + Z_{source}} \times 100$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU Lab Standard):** For a loading error $< 1\%$, the input impedance of the measuring instrument must be at least **100 times** the source impedance ($Z_{in} \geq 100 \cdot Z_{source}$).

## 1.4 Classification by Magnitude

| Category | Typical Range | Application |
|----------|---------------|-------------|
| **High $Z_{in}$** | $> 1\ \text{M}\Omega$ | Voltmeters, oscilloscopes, op-amp non-inverting inputs |
| **Medium $Z_{in}$** | $1\ \text{k}\Omega - 1\ \text{M}\Omega$ | General purpose instruments, audio line inputs |
| **Low $Z_{in}$** | $50\ \Omega - 600\ \Omega$ | Transmission lines, RF systems, ammeters (shunts) |
| **Ultra-low $Z_{in}$** | $< 1\ \Omega$ | Current shunts, high-current ammeters |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

### Step 1: Source-Instrument Interface Model
Every measurement scenario can be modeled as a **Thévenin equivalent source** ($V_s$, $Z_s$) connected to the **input port** of an instrument with input impedance $Z_{in}$.

- The instrument does **not** "see" the true source voltage $V_s$.
- It "sees" $V_{in} = V_s \cdot \dfrac{Z_{in}}{Z_{in} + Z_s}$.
- This is the **loaded** voltage.

### Step 2: The Loading Error Mechanism
Loading error arises from the unintended current drawn by the instrument. The error magnitude is inversely proportional to the ratio $Z_{in}/Z_s$.

**Why does this happen?**
Because the instrument, by design, *must* absorb some energy to perform measurement. This energy absorption is represented by a non-infinite $Z_{in}$.

### Step 3: The "Why" Behind High $Z_{in}$ for Voltmeters
A voltmeter is connected in **parallel** with the component whose voltage is being measured. If $Z_{in}$ were low, it would "short out" the component and disturb the circuit. A high $Z_{in}$ ensures the parallel combination approximates the original circuit impedance.

### Step 4: The "Why" Behind Low $Z_{in}$ for Ammeters
An ammeter is connected in **series** with the circuit. A low $Z_{in}$ ensures minimal voltage drop across the meter (since $V = I \cdot Z_{amMeter}$), and thus minimal disturbance to the circuit current.

### Step 5: Frequency Dependence
For capacitive input (typical of op-amps and oscilloscopes):

$$Z_{in}(f) = R_{in} \parallel \dfrac{1}{j2\pi f C_{in}}$$

This means $Z_{in}$ **decreases** as frequency increases — a critical issue in high-frequency measurements.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas for KTU ESE 2024 — these appear almost every year in Part A (3-mark) questions and frequently in Part B (14-mark) derivations.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Input Impedance (definition) | $Z_{in} = V_{in}/I_{in}$ | $\Omega$ | Phasor ratio in AC |
| 2 | Loaded voltage | $V_L = V_s \cdot \dfrac{Z_{in}}{Z_{in} + Z_s}$ | V | Voltage divider rule |
| 3 | Loading error (fraction) | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{Z_s}{Z_{in} + Z_s}$ | dimensionless | True value minus measured, divided by true |
| 4 | Loading error (%) | $\varepsilon_{\%} = \dfrac{Z_s}{Z_{in} + Z_s} \times 100$ | % | Multiply by 100 |
| 5 | Rule of thumb (1% error) | $Z_{in} \geq 100 \cdot Z_s$ | $\Omega$ | Standard design criterion |
| 6 | Rule of thumb (0.1% error) | $Z_{in} \geq 1000 \cdot Z_s$ | $\Omega$ | Precision measurements |
| 7 | RC parallel input impedance | $Z_{in} = \dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}$ | $\Omega$ | Magnitude: $\dfrac{R}{\sqrt{1+(\omega RC)^2}}$ |
| 8 | Op-amp inverting input $Z_{in}$ | $Z_{in} = R_1$ | $\Omega$ | Equal to input resistor |
| 9 | Op-amp non-inverting $Z_{in}$ | $Z_{in} \to \infty$ (ideal) | $\Omega$ | Practically $\geq 10^{12}\ \Omega$ |
| 10 | dB conversion of impedance | $Z_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(Z/Z_{ref})$ | dB | $Z_{ref} = 600\ \Omega$ (audio) |
| 11 | Capacitive reactance | $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$ | $\Omega$ | Falls with frequency |
| 12 | Power absorbed by instrument | $P = \dfrac{V_{in}^2}{Z_{in}}$ | W | For purely resistive $Z_{in}$ |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oscilloscope probes ($10\times$):** The $10\times$ probe has an input impedance of **$10\ \text{M}\Omega$** vs. the standard $1\ \text{M}\Omega$ of the scope — this reduces circuit loading by a factor of 10.
- **Biomedical instruments (ECG, EEG):** Skin-electrode impedance is high ($\sim 50\ \text{k}\Omega$); instruments use input impedances of **$> 10\ \text{M}\Omega$** to avoid distorting the tiny bioelectric signals.
- **Audio mixing consoles:** Line inputs are designed for **$10\ \text{k}\Omega$** or higher to prevent "loading down" preceding stages.
- **RF / Transmission lines:** Standard $50\ \Omega$ input impedance for impedance matching to prevent signal reflections (VSWR concerns).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1: Loading Error in a Voltage Measurement

**Given:** A source with Thévenin voltage $V_s$ and Thévenin impedance $Z_s$ is connected to a voltmeter with input impedance $Z_{in}$.

**Find:** The loading error in percent.

### Derivation:

Step 1: Draw the equivalent circuit. The source $V_s$ with internal impedance $Z_s$ in series drives the voltmeter impedance $Z_{in}$ in a closed loop.

Step 2: Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the loop:

$$V_s = I \cdot Z_s + I \cdot Z_{in}$$

Step 3: Solve for the loop current $I$:

$$I = \dfrac{V_s}{Z_s + Z_{in}}$$

Step 4: The voltage actually measured by the voltmeter is the voltage across $Z_{in}$:

$$V_{measured} = I \cdot Z_{in} = \dfrac{V_s \cdot Z_{in}}{Z_s + Z_{in}}$$

Step 5: Recognize this as the classical voltage divider:

$$V_{measured} = V_s \cdot \dfrac{Z_{in}}{Z_{in} + Z_s}$$

Step 6: Define the absolute error as the difference between the true voltage and the measured voltage:

$$\Delta V = V_s - V_{measured} = V_s - V_s \cdot \dfrac{Z_{in}}{Z_{in} + Z_s}$$

Step 7: Factor out $V_s$:

$$\Delta V = V_s \left[1 - \dfrac{Z_{in}}{Z_{in} + Z_s}\right] = V_s \cdot \dfrac{Z_s}{Z_{in} + Z_s}$$

Step 8: Divide by the true value $V_s$ to get the fractional error:

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta V}{V_s} = \dfrac{Z_s}{Z_{in} + Z_s}$$

Step 9: Multiply by 100 to convert to percentage:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{\%} = \dfrac{Z_s}{Z_{in} + Z_s} \times 100\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key observation:** As $Z_{in} \to \infty$, $\varepsilon \to 0$ (no loading). As $Z_{in} \to Z_s$, $\varepsilon \to 50\%$ (catastrophic loading).

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2: Input Impedance of an RC Parallel Network

**Given:** A resistor $R$ in parallel with a capacitor $C$.

**Find:** The frequency-dependent input impedance.

### Derivation:

Step 1: Recall the impedance of a resistor is $Z_R = R$ and of a capacitor is $Z_C = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}$.

Step 2: For two impedances in parallel, the equivalent impedance is the product divided by the sum:

$$Z_{in} = \dfrac{Z_R \cdot Z_C}{Z_R + Z_C}$$

Step 3: Substitute the expressions:

$$Z_{in} = \dfrac{R \cdot \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}}{R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}}$$

Step 4: Multiply numerator and denominator by $j\omega C$ to clear the fraction:

$$Z_{in} = \dfrac{R}{j\omega C \cdot R + 1} = \dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega R C}$$

Step 5: To find the magnitude, multiply numerator and denominator by the complex conjugate of the denominator $(1 - j\omega RC)$:

$$Z_{in} = \dfrac{R(1 - j\omega RC)}{(1 + j\omega RC)(1 - j\omega RC)} = \dfrac{R(1 - j\omega RC)}{1 + (\omega RC)^2}$$

Step 6: Separate real and imaginary parts:

$$Z_{in} = \dfrac{R}{1 + (\omega RC)^2} - j \cdot \dfrac{\omega R^2 C}{1 + (\omega RC)^2}$$

Step 7: The magnitude is:

$$\vert Z_{in} \vert = \dfrac{R}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

Step 8: At DC ($\omega = 0$): $\vert Z_{in} \vert = R$ (full resistor value).

Step 9: At high frequency ($\omega \to \infty$): $\vert Z_{in} \vert \to 0$ (capacitor shorts the resistor).

$$\boxed{Z_{in}(\omega) = \dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}, \quad \vert Z_{in} \vert = \dfrac{R}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Pattern:** Such frequency-response problems are common in ESE. Note the **$-3\text{ dB}$ cutoff** occurs at $\omega = 1/(RC)$, where $\vert Z_{in} \vert = R/\sqrt{2}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3: Input Impedance of Inverting Op-Amp Configuration

**Given:** Standard inverting amplifier with input resistor $R_1$ and feedback resistor $R_f$, driven by an ideal op-amp (open-loop gain $A \to \infty$).

**Find:** The input impedance seen at the inverting input terminal.

### Derivation:

Step 1: Let the source apply a voltage $v_s$ at the input node. The current entering the inverting terminal is:

$$i_{in} = \dfrac{v_s - v_{-}}{R_1}$$

Step 2: For an ideal op-amp, the two input terminals are at the same potential (virtual short): $v_{-} = v_{+} = 0$ (since $v_+$ is grounded).

Step 3: Substitute $v_{-} = 0$:

$$i_{in} = \dfrac{v_s - 0}{R_1} = \dfrac{v_s}{R_1}$$

Step 4: By definition, input impedance is:

$$Z_{in} = \dfrac{v_s}{i_{in}} = \dfrac{v_s}{v_s / R_1}$$

$$\boxed{Z_{in} = R_1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** For an inverting op-amp, the input impedance is *finite* and equal to $R_1$. For a non-inverting op-amp, the input impedance is the op-amp's own (extremely high) input impedance ($\sim 10^{12}\ \Omega$). This is why **buffer (voltage follower) stages** are inserted when interfacing high-impedance sources to inverting amplifiers.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A voltmeter with input impedance $Z_{in} = 1\ \text{M}\Omega$ is used to measure the open-circuit voltage of a source having Thévenin impedance $Z_s = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ and $V_s = 5\ \text{V}$. Calculate (a) the measured voltage, and (b) the loading error in percent.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Apply the voltage divider formula:

$$V_{measured} = V_s \cdot \dfrac{Z_{in}}{Z_{in} + Z_s}$$

Substituting values:

$$V_{measured} = 5 \cdot \dfrac{1 \times 10^6}{1 \times 10^6 + 10 \times 10^3}$$

$$V_{measured} = 5 \cdot \dfrac{10^6}{1.01 \times 10^6} = 5 \cdot 0.990099$$

$$V_{measured} = 4.9505\ \text{V}$$

**Part (b):** Loading error:

$$\varepsilon_{\%} = \dfrac{Z_s}{Z_{in} + Z_s} \times 100 = \dfrac{10^4}{1.01 \times 10^6} \times 100$$

$$\varepsilon_{\%} = 0.9901\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical check:** Ratio $Z_{in}/Z_s = 100$, so error $\approx 1\%$ as expected by the rule of thumb. If a $0.1\%$ error were required, $Z_{in} \geq 10\ \text{M}\Omega$ would be needed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Source–Instrument Interface Model

```mermaid
graph LR
    SRC[VOLTAGE SOURCE VS] --> ZS[THVENIN IMPEDANCE ZS]
    ZS --> NODE[INPUT TERMINAL A]
    NODE --> ZIN[INSTRUMENT INPUT IMPEDANCE ZIN]
    ZIN --> GND[GROUND TERMINAL B]
    NODE -. MEASURED VOLTAGE .-> VM[VOLTMETER READING]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The voltmeter reading equals the voltage developed across $Z_{in}$, which is always less than the true open-circuit $V_s$ because of the voltage divider formed by $Z_s$ and $Z_{in}$.

## 4.2 Flowchart: Choosing the Right Input Impedance

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[MEASUREMENT TASK] --> B{WHAT QUANTITY}
    B --> C[VOLTAGE]
    B --> D[CURRENT]
    C --> E[USE HIGH ZIN INSTRUMENT]
    E --> F[VOLTMETER OR SCOPE]
    E --> G[ADD BUFFER IF NEEDED]
    D --> H[USE LOW ZIN INSTRUMENT]
    H --> I[AMMETER OR SHUNT]
    H --> J[NEAR ZERO ZIN PREFERRED]
    G --> K{ERROR LT 1 PERCENT}
    J --> K
    K --> L[YES INSTRUMENT SUITABLE]
    K --> M[NO UPGRADE INSTRUMENT]
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture: Buffer Stage for Impedance Transformation

```mermaid
graph LR
    SRC[HI Z SOURCE ZS] --> ZIN[BUFFER INPUT ZIN VERY HIGH]
    ZIN --> OA[UNITY GAIN OPAMP FOLLOWER]
    OA --> ZOUT[BUFFER OUTPUT ZOUT VERY LOW]
    ZOUT --> LOAD[LOW Z LOAD OR CABLE]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical:** A unity-gain buffer (voltage follower using op-amp TL081 / LM358) presents $\sim 10^{12}\ \Omega$ at its input and a few ohms at its output — solving virtually all loading problems in instrumentation chains.

## 4.4 Frequency Response of RC Input Impedance

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[DC OMEGA 0] --> B[ZIN EQUALS R MAXIMUM]
    B --> C[FREQUENCY RISES]
    C --> D[ZIN FALLS WITH 1 OVER ROOT 1 PLUS OMEGA RC SQUARED]
    D --> E[CUTOFF AT OMEGA 1 OVER RC]
    E --> F[HIGH FREQ OMEGA LARGE]
    F --> G[ZIN APPROACHES ZERO]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define input impedance. Why must a voltmeter have a very high input impedance?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Input impedance $Z_{in}$ is the impedance presented by an instrument at its input terminals, defined as the ratio of input voltage to input current: $Z_{in} = V_{in}/I_{in}$.
- **Reason 1 (1 mark):** A voltmeter is connected in parallel across the component under test. If $Z_{in}$ were low, it would draw significant current and disturb the circuit under measurement (loading effect).
- **Reason 2 (1 mark):** A high $Z_{in}$ ensures that the voltmeter draws negligible current, so the measured voltage accurately equals the true circuit voltage (loading error $< 1\%$ when $Z_{in} \geq 100 Z_s$).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**A voltmeter of input impedance $2\ \text{M}\Omega$ is used to measure a voltage across a source of internal resistance $20\ \text{k}\Omega$. Calculate the loading error.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Using the loading error formula:

$$\varepsilon_{\%} = \dfrac{Z_s}{Z_{in} + Z_s} \times 100$$

Substituting:

$$\varepsilon_{\%} = \dfrac{20 \times 10^3}{2 \times 10^6 + 20 \times 10^3} \times 100 = \dfrac{20{,}000}{2{,}020{,}000} \times 100$$

$$\varepsilon_{\%} = 0.9901\% \approx 0.99\%$$

**Answer: Loading error $\approx 0.99\%$, which is within the standard $1\%$ tolerance.**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** Derive the expression for the loading error when a voltmeter of input impedance $Z_{in}$ is used to measure the voltage of a source with Thévenin impedance $Z_s$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A $10\ \text{V}$ source with internal resistance $R_s = 50\ \text{k}\Omega$ is measured by (i) a $1\ \text{M}\Omega$ voltmeter, and (ii) a $10\ \text{M}\Omega$ voltmeter. Compute the reading and loading error in each case. Comment on the results. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
Step 1: State the source-instrument model — $V_s$ in series with $Z_s$, driving $Z_{in}$.
Step 2: Apply KVL: $V_s = I Z_s + I Z_{in}$, so $I = V_s/(Z_s + Z_{in})$.
Step 3: The measured voltage is across $Z_{in}$:

$$V_m = I \cdot Z_{in} = V_s \cdot \dfrac{Z_{in}}{Z_{in} + Z_s}$$

Step 4: Absolute error: $\Delta V = V_s - V_m = V_s \cdot \dfrac{Z_s}{Z_{in} + Z_s}$.

Step 5: Fractional/percentage error:

$$\varepsilon_{\%} = \dfrac{Z_s}{Z_{in} + Z_s} \times 100\%$$

**Full marks awarded for clearly stating the assumption (linear, single-frequency), KVL application, and the final boxed expression.**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Case (i):** $Z_{in} = 1\ \text{M}\Omega = 10^6\ \Omega$, $R_s = 5 \times 10^4\ \Omega$, $V_s = 10\ \text{V}$.

$$V_m = 10 \cdot \dfrac{10^6}{10^6 + 5 \times 10^4} = 10 \cdot \dfrac{10^6}{1.05 \times 10^6} = 9.5238\ \text{V}$$

$$\varepsilon_{\%} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^4}{1.05 \times 10^6} \times 100 = 4.762\%$$

**Case (ii):** $Z_{in} = 10\ \text{M}\Omega = 10^7\ \Omega$.

$$V_m = 10 \cdot \dfrac{10^7}{10^7 + 5 \times 10^4} = 10 \cdot \dfrac{10^7}{1.005 \times 10^7} = 9.9502\ \text{V}$$

$$\varepsilon_{\%} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^4}{1.005 \times 10^7} \times 100 = 0.4975\%$$

**Comment (1 mark):** Increasing $Z_{in}$ from $1\ \text{M}\Omega$ to $10\ \text{M}\Omega$ (10×) reduces the loading error by 10× (from 4.76% to 0.50%). This confirms the inverse proportionality between $Z_{in}$ and error, validating the design rule $Z_{in} \geq 100 R_s$ for $<1\%$ accuracy.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the concept of input impedance. Discuss the frequency dependence of input impedance for an RC network. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An oscilloscope with input impedance $1\ \text{M}\Omega$ in parallel with $20\ \text{pF}$ is used to observe a $1\ \text{MHz}$ square wave from a $50\ \Omega$ source. Calculate: (i) the input impedance magnitude at $1\ \text{MHz}$, (ii) the loading error. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Concept + Frequency Dependence (7 marks):**
- Input impedance definition with block diagram ($V_{in}/I_{in}$).
- For RC parallel network: $Z_{in}(\omega) = R / (1 + j\omega RC)$.
- Magnitude: $\vert Z_{in} \vert = R / \sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}$.
- Bode plot description: flat at $R$ for low $\omega$, rolls off at $-20$ dB/decade beyond $\omega = 1/RC$.
- Practical implication: input impedance decreases with frequency — critical in high-frequency and pulse measurements.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

Given: $R = 10^6\ \Omega$, $C = 20\ \text{pF} = 20 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F}$, $f = 10^6\ \text{Hz}$, $Z_s = 50\ \Omega$.

Step 1: Calculate $\omega RC$:

$$\omega RC = 2\pi f \cdot R \cdot C = 2\pi \times 10^6 \times 10^6 \times 20 \times 10^{-12}$$

$$\omega RC = 2\pi \times 0.02 = 0.1257$$

Step 2: Input impedance magnitude:

$$\vert Z_{in} \vert = \dfrac{10^6}{\sqrt{1 + (0.1257)^2}} = \dfrac{10^6}{\sqrt{1.0158}} = \dfrac{10^6}{1.0079}$$

$$\vert Z_{in} \vert = 992{,}150\ \Omega \approx 992\ \text{k}\Omega$$

Step 3: Loading error:

$$\varepsilon_{\%} = \dfrac{50}{992{,}150 + 50} \times 100 = 0.00504\%$$

**Result:** Loading error is negligible (0.005%), but at higher frequencies ($\sim 100\ \text{MHz}$), the capacitive component would dominate and the input impedance would drop to a few kΩ, causing severe loading and signal distortion.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU examiners penalize (loss of 2–3 marks per error):**
> 1. **Confusing input and output impedance** — Input impedance is at the input port; output impedance is at the output. Don't mix them in derivations.
> 2. **Forgetting the reactive part** — In AC, students often treat input impedance as purely resistive, missing the $jX$ component. Always write $Z_{in} = R_{in} + jX_{in}$.
> 3. **Wrong unit in loading error** — Loading error is dimensionless (or %); never write it in volts or ohms.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions** — In derivations, always state: "assuming ideal source, single frequency, linear operation."
> 5. **Sign errors in voltage divider** — Remember $V_{measured} < V_s$ always (the instrument cannot increase the source voltage). If your answer exceeds $V_s$, you've made an error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Input Impedances**

- ⚡ **Definition:** $Z_{in} = V_{in} / I_{in}$ — impedance "seen" by the source at the input.
- ⚡ **Voltmeter Rule:** $Z_{in} \to \infty$ (ideally); practically $Z_{in} \geq 100 Z_s$ for $<1\%$ error.
- ⚡ **Ammeter Rule:** $Z_{in} \to 0$ (ideally); minimizes voltage drop and circuit disturbance.
- ⚡ **Loading Error Formula:** $\varepsilon_{\%} = \dfrac{Z_s}{Z_{in} + Z_s} \times 100$.
- ⚡ **Voltage Divider:** $V_m = V_s \cdot \dfrac{Z_{in}}{Z_{in} + Z_s}$.
- ⚡ **RC Input Impedance:** $Z_{in}(\omega) = \dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}$, magnitude $\dfrac{R}{\sqrt{1+(\omega RC)^2}}$.
- ⚡ **Cutoff Frequency:** $\omega_c = 1/(RC)$ where $\vert Z_{in} \vert$ drops to $R/\sqrt{2}$ ($-3$ dB).
- ⚡ **Inverting Op-Amp:** $Z_{in} = R_1$ (finite, often low — a disadvantage).
- ⚡ **Non-Inverting Op-Amp / Buffer:** $Z_{in} \to \infty$ (ideal) — used as an impedance transformer.
- ⚡ **Frequency Dependence:** $Z_{in}$ decreases with increasing frequency in capacitive systems.
- ⚡ **Units:** $Z$ in $\Omega$, $V$ in V, $I$ in A, $f$ in Hz, $C$ in F.
- ⚡ **Practical Examples:** Oscilloscope $1\ \text{M}\Omega \parallel 20\ \text{pF}$; ECG $\sim 10\ \text{M}\Omega$; RF $50\ \Omega$; Audio line $\sim 10\ \text{k}\Omega$.
- ⚡ **Buffer solves loading:** Insert a unity-gain op-amp follower when $Z_s$ is comparable to $Z_{in}$.
- ⚡ **KTU Exam Cue:** Numerical problems on loading error and frequency response of $Z_{in}$ appear every year — practice at least 3 each.
- ⚡ **Examiner's Pet Question:** "Why is a $10\times$ probe preferred over a $1\times$ probe?" — Answer: because it raises $Z_{in}$ from $1\ \text{M}\Omega$ to $10\ \text{M}\Omega$, reducing circuit loading by 10×.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Input Admittance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCICT205 - BASIC OF INSTRUMENTATION ENGINEERING (INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Input Admittance ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme Instrumentation Engineering (Module 3 — Electronic Instruments and Operational Amplifiers), **Input Admittance ($Y_{in}$)** is formally defined as the **complex ratio of the input current ($I_{in}$) flowing into a device or network to the input voltage ($V_{in}$) applied across its input terminals**, measured under small-signal AC conditions.
