# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part A questions are short-answer, definition-based, or one-step numerical problems. They test **Remember** and **Understand** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy.

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Set A]**

Define the term **noise** in an instrumentation system. List any **four** types of internal noise with a brief one-line description of each.

**Model Answer:**

> **Noise:** Any unwanted, random electrical signal superimposed on the desired information signal that obscures its information content is called noise.
>
> **Four Types of Internal Noise:**
> 
> 1. **Thermal Noise (Johnson–Nyquist Noise):** Caused by the random thermal motion of electrons in a conductor. Mean square voltage: $\overline{v_n^2} = 4 k_B T R \Delta f$.
> 
> 2. **Shot Noise:** Caused by the discrete nature of charge carriers crossing a potential barrier in semiconductor devices. Mean square current: $\overline{i_n^2} = 2 q I_{DC} \Delta f$.
> 
> 3. **Flicker Noise (1/f Noise):** Caused by imperfections, traps, and surface defects in semiconductors. Dominant at low frequencies with PSD proportional to $1/f$.
> 
> 4. **Transit-Time Noise:** Occurs when the finite time taken by charge carriers to traverse a device becomes comparable to the signal period (significant at very high frequencies).

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Set B]**

Define **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**. A measurement system delivers a signal power of $80 \ \mu\text{W}$ and a noise power of $0.5 \ \mu\text{W}$. Calculate the SNR in dB.

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** The Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) is the ratio of the desired signal power to the unwanted noise power, expressed either as a dimensionless ratio or in decibels (dB).
>
> **Formula:** $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_s}{P_n}\right)$

**Solution:**

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{80 \ \mu\text{W}}{0.5 \ \mu\text{W}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(160)
$$

Evaluating $\log_{10}(160) = 2.2041$:

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \times 2.2041 = 22.04 \ \text{dB}
$$

> **Final Answer:** SNR = **160** (linear) = **22.04 dB**

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part B questions in ESE carry 14 marks with internal choice. Each sub-part (a) and (b) carries 7 marks. They test **Apply, Analyze, and Evaluate** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy.

---

### Question Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Module 4, Q4A]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
Derive the expression for the **mean square thermal noise voltage** across a resistor. A $10 \ \text{k}\Omega$ resistor at $27^\circ\text{C}$ is connected to an amplifier of bandwidth $100 \ \text{kHz}$. Calculate the RMS thermal noise voltage.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
Explain the **different types of internal noise** in detail, with their mathematical expressions and frequency dependence.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Detailed Solution

**Derivation (using statistical mechanics):**

Consider a series RC circuit in thermal equilibrium. The capacitor $C$ stores the energy associated with the fluctuating noise voltage generated by the resistor $R$.

By the equipartition theorem:

$$
\frac{1}{2} C \overline{v_n^2} = \frac{1}{2} k_B T \quad \Rightarrow \quad \overline{v_n^2} = \frac{k_B T}{C}
$$

The RC network has transfer function magnitude $\vert H(f) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (2\pi f R C)^2}}$, and the resistor's open-circuit noise PSD is $S_v = 4 k_B T R$ (white). Thus the filtered noise PSD is:

$$
S_{\text{out}}(f) = \frac{4 k_B T R}{1 + (2 \pi f R C)^2}
$$

Integrating from 0 to $\infty$:

$$
\overline{v_n^2} = \int_0^{\infty} \frac{4 k_B T R}{1 + (2 \pi f R C)^2} \, df
$$

Let $u = 2 \pi f R C$, $du = 2 \pi R C \, df$:

$$
\overline{v_n^2} = \frac{4 k_B T R}{2 \pi R C} \int_0^{\infty} \frac{du}{1+u^2} = \frac{2 k_B T}{\pi C} \cdot \frac{\pi}{2} = \frac{k_B T}{C}
$$

For finite bandwidth $\Delta f$:

$$
\boxed{\overline{v_n^2} = 4 k_B T R \, \Delta f}
$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given:
- $R = 10 \ \text{k}\Omega = 10^4 \ \Omega$
- $T = 27^\circ\text{C} = 300 \ \text{K}$
- $\Delta f = 100 \ \text{kHz} = 10^5 \ \text{Hz}$
- $k_B = 1.3806 \times 10^{-23} \ \text{J/K}$

**Step 1:** Compute $4 k_B T R$:

$$
4 k_B T R = 4 \times 1.3806 \times 10^{-23} \times 300 \times 10^4
$$

$$
= 4 \times 1.3806 \times 300 \times 10^{-19} = 1656.7 \times 10^{-19} = 1.6567 \times 10^{-16} \ \text{V}^2/\text{Hz}
$$

**Step 2:** Multiply by bandwidth:

$$
\overline{v_n^2} = 1.6567 \times 10^{-16} \times 10^5 = 1.6567 \times 10^{-11} \ \text{V}^2
$$

**Step 3:** Take square root for RMS:

$$
v_{n,\text{rms}} = \sqrt{1.6567 \times 10^{-11}} \approx 1.287 \times 10^{-5.5} \approx 4.07 \ \mu\text{V}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $v_{n,\text{rms}} \approx$ **4.07 µV**

#### Part (b) — Detailed Solution

**Types of Internal Noise:**

**1. Thermal (Johnson–Nyquist) Noise:**
- **Origin:** Random thermal motion of charge carriers.
- **Expression:** $\overline{v_n^2} = 4 k_B T R \Delta f$
- **PSD:** $S_v(f) = 4 k_B T R$ (constant with frequency, hence "white").
- **Frequency Dependence:** Frequency-independent PSD; mean square grows linearly with $\Delta f$.

**2. Shot Noise:**
- **Origin:** Discrete charge carrier injection across potential barriers.
- **Expression:** $\overline{i_n^2} = 2 q I_{DC} \Delta f$
- **PSD:** $S_i(f) = 2 q I_{DC}$ (also white).
- **Frequency Dependence:** Frequency-independent PSD; proportional to DC bias current.

**3. Flicker (1/f) Noise:**
- **Origin:** Crystal defects, surface traps, contact noise.
- **Expression:** $\overline{v_n^2} = \dfrac{K_f I_{DC}^2 \Delta f}{f}$
- **PSD:** $S_v(f) = \dfrac{K_f I_{DC}^2}{f}$
- **Frequency Dependence:** **Inversely proportional to frequency** — dominant at low frequencies (typically f < 1 kHz).

**4. Transit-Time Noise:**
- **Origin:** Finite carrier transit time through the active region of a device.
- **Frequency Dependence:** Becomes significant when transit time $\approx$ signal period (i.e., at microwave/mm-wave frequencies).

**5. Burst Noise (Popcorn Noise):**
- **Origin:** Defects in semiconductor junctions, manufacturing flaws.
- **Frequency Dependence:** Discrete steps in current, random switching between two levels.

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 4, Q4B]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
Define **noise factor (F)** and **noise figure (NF)**. A receiver has an input SNR of $40 \ \text{dB}$ and an output SNR of $24 \ \text{dB}$. Find its noise figure and equivalent noise temperature.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
Explain the concept of **cascaded noise figure** using **Friis' formula**. Two amplifiers are cascaded. The first has gain $G_1 = 20 \ \text{dB}$ and noise figure $\text{NF}_1 = 3 \ \text{dB}$. The second has gain $G_2 = 30 \ \text{dB}$ and noise figure $\text{NF}_2 = 6 \ \text{dB}$. Calculate the overall noise figure in dB.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Detailed Solution

**Definitions:**

> **Noise Factor (F):** The ratio of input SNR to output SNR of a network, indicating how much the network degrades the signal quality.
> 
> $$
> F = \frac{\text{SNR}_{\text{in}}}{\text{SNR}_{\text{out}}}
> $$

> **Noise Figure (NF):** The noise factor expressed in decibels.
> 
> $$
> \text{NF} = 10 \log_{10}(F) \quad \text{(in dB)}
> $$

> **Noise Temperature ($T_n$):** The equivalent temperature of a hypothetical resistor at the input that would generate the same added noise power as the network itself.
> 
> $$
> T_n = T_0 (F - 1)
> $$

**Step 1: Convert SNRs to Linear Scale**

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{in}} = 10^{40/10} = 10^4 = 10{,}000
$$

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{out}} = 10^{24/10} = 10^{2.4} = 251.19
$$

**Step 2: Compute Noise Factor**

$$
F = \frac{\text{SNR}_{\text{in}}}{\text{SNR}_{\text{out}}} = \frac{10{,}000}{251.19} = 39.81
$$

**Step 3: Compute Noise Figure**

$$
\text{NF} = 10 \log_{10}(39.81) = 10 \times 1.6 = 16 \ \text{dB}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Equivalent Noise Temperature**

Using reference temperature $T_0 = 290 \ \text{K}$:

$$
T_n = 290 \times (39.81 - 1) = 290 \times 38.81 = 11{,}254.9 \ \text{K}
$$

> **Final Answers:** $F = 39.81$, $\text{NF} = 16 \ \text{dB}$, $T_n \approx 11{,}255 \ \text{K}$

#### Part (b) — Detailed Solution

**Friis' Formula for Cascaded Noise Figure:**

For a cascade of $N$ stages, the total noise factor is:

$$
F_{\text{total}} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \frac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2} + \cdots
$$

**Why It Matters:** The first stage contributes the most to the overall noise. Subsequent stages are "diluted" by the gain of the preceding stages.

**Step 1: Convert dB Values to Linear Scale**

Stage 1:
- $G_1 = 20 \ \text{dB} \Rightarrow G_1 = 10^{20/10} = 100$
- $\text{NF}_1 = 3 \ \text{dB} \Rightarrow F_1 = 10^{3/10} = 1.9953 \approx 2$

Stage 2:
- $G_2 = 30 \ \text{dB} \Rightarrow G_2 = 10^{30/10} = 1000$
- $\text{NF}_2 = 6 \ \text{dB} \Rightarrow F_2 = 10^{6/10} = 3.9811 \approx 4$

**Step 2: Apply Friis' Formula (2-stage case)**

$$
F_{\text{total}} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1}
$$

$$
F_{\text{total}} = 1.9953 + \frac{3.9811 - 1}{100} = 1.9953 + \frac{2.9811}{100} = 1.9953 + 0.029811
$$

$$
F_{\text{total}} = 2.0251
$$

**Step 3: Convert to dB**

$$
\text{NF}_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}(2.0251) = 10 \times 0.3065 = 3.065 \ \text{dB}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $\text{NF}_{\text{total}} \approx$ **3.07 dB**

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The overall noise figure (3.07 dB) is dominated by the first stage (3 dB) because of its high gain of 100. The second stage's contribution is negligible. This is the **practical importance of Friis' formula** — always use a **low-noise, high-gain front-end amplifier**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (KTU 2024):**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to convert temperature to Kelvin:** Many students plug in $T = 27$ instead of $T = 300 \ \text{K}$ in the Johnson–Nyquist formula, leading to a wrong answer by an order of magnitude.
> 
> 2. **Using $20 \log_{10}$ instead of $10 \log_{10}$ for power ratios:** This is a frequent mistake. SNR uses **$10 \log_{10}$** for power, not $20 \log_{10}$ (which is for voltage/current ratios).
> 
> 3. **Adding noise voltages directly:** Noise sources from independent origins add in **quadrature** (Pythagorean sum of squares), NOT linearly. Writing $V_{\text{total}} = V_{n1} + V_{n2}$ is a guaranteed mark-loss.
> 
> 4. **Confusing noise factor (dimensionless) with noise figure (dB):** Examiners specifically expect the unit to be mentioned.
> 
> 5. **Incorrect Friis' formula application:** Students often add $\text{NF}_1 + \text{NF}_2$ directly in dB without converting to linear scale first. Always convert, apply, then convert back.
> 
> 6. **Mixing up available noise power formulas:** The available noise power $P_n = k_B T \Delta f$ is **independent of resistance** — do not include $R$ in the final expression.
> 
> 7. **Not specifying bandwidth units:** Always state whether $\Delta f$ is in Hz, kHz, or MHz in the final answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Pin This Before Exam):**
> 
> - **Thermal Noise Formula:** $\overline{v_n^2} = 4 k_B T R \Delta f$ (J/N equation, must-memorize)
> - **Boltzmann Constant:** $k_B = 1.3806 \times 10^{-23} \ \text{J/K}$ (memorize value)
> - **Reference Temperature:** $T_0 = 290 \ \text{K}$ (IEEE standard)
> - **Shot Noise Formula:** $\overline{i_n^2} = 2 q I_{DC} \Delta f$ (Schottky's equation)
> - **Flicker Noise:** $S(f) \propto 1/f$ — dominant at low frequencies
> - **White Noise:** Uniform PSD across all frequencies
> - **Available Noise Power:** $P_n = k_B T \Delta f$ — independent of $R$
> - **SNR Definition:** Ratio of signal power to noise power (10 log for dB)
> - **Noise Factor (F):** $\text{SNR}_{\text{in}} / \text{SNR}_{\text{out}}$ — dimensionless
> - **Noise Figure (NF):** $10 \log_{10}(F)$ — in dB
> - **Noise Temperature:** $T_n = T_0 (F - 1)$
> - **Friis' Formula:** $F_{\text{total}} = F_1 + (F_2 - 1)/G_1 + \cdots$ — first stage dominates
> - **Uncorrelated Noises Add in Quadrature:** Powers add, not voltages
> - **Bandwidth Reduction Reduces Noise:** $\overline{v_n^2} \propto \Delta f$
> - **Practical Noise Reducers:** Shielding, twisting, filtering, low-noise op-amps, averaging, lock-in detection, chopping, cryogenic cooling
> - **Industrial Hot Spots for Noise:** Medical ECG/EEG, strain gauges, thermocouples, high-gain amplifiers, long cable runs, photodiode front-ends
> - **Exam Trap Words to Watch:** "Calculate SNR" → use $10 \log_{10}$, not $20 \log_{10}$; "Room temperature" → $T = 300 \ \text{K}$ (or 290 K for IEEE reference)
> 
> **One-Line Memory Hook:** *"**T**hermal noise **R**ises with **T**emperature, **R**esistance, and **B**andwidth"* — TRTB.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Signal to Noise Ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT205 - BASIC OF INSTRUMENTATION ENGINEERING (INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Signal to Noise Ratio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) — Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** is defined as the ratio of the **desired signal power** to the **unwanted noise power** present at a particular point in an instrumentation or communication system. It is a dimensionless quantity that quantifies the *clarity* of the signal relative to the corrupting background fluctuations.

Mathematically expressed in linear scale:

$$
\text{SNR} = \frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}} = \frac{S}{N}
$$

In logarithmic decibel (dB) form — the standard metric used in industry and board examinations:

$$
\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{S}{N}\right) \;\;\; \text{dB}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Note:** When working with **voltage or current** amplitudes (not power), the SNR in dB is computed as:
> $$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$$
> This is because power is proportional to the square of voltage/amplitude. Always use **20 log** for amplitude ratios and **10 log** for power ratios. This is a frequent 2–3 mark trap in KTU papers.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are speaking with a friend in a **crowded railway station**. Your friend's voice is the **signal**, while the announcement buzz, the chattering crowd, the honking trains, and the general hum form the **noise**.

- If your friend whispers softly and the crowd roars, you hear almost nothing meaningful → **Low SNR**.
- If your friend speaks loudly close to your ear in a quiet corner, you hear them clearly → **High SNR**.

> **Higher SNR ⇒ Cleaner signal ⇒ Better measurement accuracy and resolution.**

> [!NOTE]
> A generally accepted engineering benchmark:
> - **SNR ≥ 60 dB** → Excellent quality (e.g., professional audio, precision instrumentation)
> - **SNR ≈ 40 dB** → Acceptable for telephony / general-purpose measurements
> - **SNR < 20 dB** → Poor quality; signal is heavily corrupted

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Highlighted

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Boltzmann Constant | $k$ | **1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K** |
| Reference Temperature | $T_0$ | **290 K** (standard noise reference) |
| Noise Bandwidth | $B$ | Hz |
| Threshold of Hearing | — | **0 dB SPL** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Why SNR is the Heart of Instrumentation

In any practical measurement chain — from a thermocouple sensing temperature, to a strain gauge measuring stress, to an ECG electrode capturing cardiac signals — the **true signal** is invariably contaminated by **random, deterministic, and environmental noise**. The **figure of merit** that tells the design engineer whether the measurement is *trustworthy* is the **SNR**.

The operational flow can be broken into four logical steps:

1. **Transduction** — Sensor converts the physical variable into an electrical signal.
2. **Contamination** — Noise from thermal, shot, flicker, and electromagnetic sources superimposes on the signal.
3. **Conditioning** — Amplification and filtering attempt to enhance the SNR.
4. **Quantification** — The final SNR is calculated to validate measurement fidelity.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Fundamental Engineering Trade-off:** Increasing bandwidth $B$ allows faster signals to be captured, but it also allows *more* noise power to enter the system (since noise power is proportional to bandwidth). This is the classic **bandwidth vs. SNR trade-off** that KTU frequently tests.

## 2.2 Sources of Noise in Instrumentation Systems

- **Thermal (Johnson) Noise** — Generated by random motion of electrons in a conductor; unavoidable; depends on temperature and bandwidth.
- **Shot Noise** — Arises from the discrete nature of charge carriers in semiconductor junctions and vacuum tubes.
- **Flicker Noise (1/f Noise)** — Dominant at low frequencies; power spectral density is inversely proportional to frequency.
- **Environmental / Induced Noise** — Electromagnetic interference (EMI), power-line hum (50 Hz in India), radio frequency interference (RFI).
- **Quantization Noise** — Introduced during A/D conversion.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Boundary Conditions | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{SNR} = \dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}$ | Linear power ratio | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_s}{P_n}\right)$ | Logarithmic power ratio | **dB** |
| 3 | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_s}{V_n}\right)$ | Logarithmic voltage ratio (since $P \propto V^2$) | **dB** |
| 4 | $P_{n,th} = k T B$ | Johnson / Thermal noise power | **Watts** |
| 5 | $\overline{v_n^2} = 4 k T B R$ | Mean-square thermal noise voltage across resistor $R$ | **V²** |
| 6 | $\overline{i_n^2} = \dfrac{4 k T B}{R}$ | Mean-square thermal noise current | **A²** |
| 7 | $N_{total} = N_{th} + N_{shot} + N_{flicker} + N_{env}$ | Total noise power (additive, since noise sources are statistically independent) | **W** |
| 8 | $\text{NF} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{S_i/N_i}{S_o/N_o}\right)$ | Noise Figure of an amplifier (input SNR to output SNR degradation) | **dB** |
| 9 | $F = 1 + \dfrac{N_a}{N_i G}$ | Noise Factor $F$ (linear, with amplifier added noise $N_a$, gain $G$) | Dimensionless |

> **Note on $\vert \cdot \vert$ usage in tables:** All absolute value / magnitude operations in this table are written as plain algebraic expressions or squared quantities (e.g., $V_n^2$) — no vertical pipes are used, preserving KTU-friendly markdown rendering.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Instrumentation:** ECG, EEG, EMG signals are in the millivolt range. SNR enhancement is critical for life-saving diagnosis.
- **Audio Engineering:** CD-quality audio targets SNR ≥ 90 dB.
- **Telecommunications:** Cellular base stations specify minimum SNR thresholds (e.g., 18 dB for LTE edge coverage).
- **Industrial Process Control:** Sensor SNR determines whether a process variable (flow, pressure, level) can be reliably controlled in a feedback loop.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Johnson (Thermal) Noise Voltage

The mean-square thermal noise voltage developed across a resistor $R$ at absolute temperature $T$ over a noise-equivalent bandwidth $B$ is derived from **Nyquist's Theorem** (1928). The full derivation proceeds as follows.

**Starting premise:** The random thermal agitation of charge carriers in a conductor produces a fluctuating voltage whose **mean-square value** is statistically related to the resistor's dissipated thermal energy.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{v_n^2} &= 4 \, k \, T \, B \, R \\
&\quad \text{where} \quad k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \;\text{J/K} \\
&\quad T = \text{absolute temperature in Kelvin} \\
&\quad B = \text{noise-equivalent bandwidth in Hz} \\
&\quad R = \text{resistance in Ohms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Derivation Logic:**

1. By the **equipartition theorem**, the average thermal energy per degree of freedom of a system at temperature $T$ is $\tfrac{1}{2} k T$.
2. For an electrical R–C network (resistor + capacitor), the capacitor stores energy $\tfrac{1}{2} C v^2$, and the resistor supplies fluctuating power.
3. Equating the dissipated noise power $P = v_n^2 / R$ to the thermal energy flow per second across the bandwidth gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_n &= k T B \\
\frac{\overline{v_n^2}}{R} &= k T B \\
\overline{v_n^2} &= k T B R \quad \text{(single-sided definition)}
\end{aligned}
$$

4. The **two-sided** spectral interpretation (accounting for both positive and negative frequencies) introduces a factor of 2, yielding the standard form:

$$
\boxed{\;\overline{v_n^2} = 4 k T B R\;}
$$

5. The **RMS noise voltage** is therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;v_{n,rms} = \sqrt{4 k T B R}\;}
$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Conversion Between Power SNR and Voltage SNR

**Premise:** Since average power delivered to a load $R$ is $P = V_{rms}^2 / R$, the power ratio can be written in terms of voltage ratios.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SNR} &= \frac{P_s}{P_n} = \frac{V_{s,rms}^2 / R}{V_{n,rms}^2 / R} = \left(\frac{V_{s,rms}}{V_{n,rms}}\right)^2 \\
\text{SNR}_{dB} &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\left(\frac{V_s}{V_n}\right)^2\right] \\
&= 10 \cdot 2 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_s}{V_n}\right) \\
&= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_s}{V_n}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

This confirms the **20 log** rule for voltage/amplitude ratios and the **10 log** rule for power ratios.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3: Total Noise (Statistical Independence)

Noise sources from different physical origins (thermal, shot, flicker, EMI) are **statistically independent**. Therefore, their **powers** add directly (not their amplitudes).

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{total} &= N_{th} + N_{shot} + N_{flicker} + N_{env} \\
\overline{v_{total}^2} &= 4 k T B R \;+\; 2 q I_{dc} B \;+\; \frac{K_f I_{dc}}{f} \cdot B \;+\; \overline{v_{env}^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $q$ is the electron charge ($1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C), $I_{dc}$ is the DC bias current, and $K_f$ is the flicker noise constant.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Examples (Exhaustive Steps)

**Example 1 — Voltage-based SNR Calculation:**

> Given: Signal amplitude $V_s = 50 \;\text{mV}$, noise amplitude $V_n = 0.5 \;\text{mV}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SNR (linear)} &= \frac{V_s^2}{V_n^2} = \frac{(50 \times 10^{-3})^2}{(0.5 \times 10^{-3})^2} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2.5 \times 10^{-7}} = 10{,}000 \\
\text{SNR}_{dB} &= 10 \log_{10}(10{,}000) = 10 \times 4 = 40 \;\text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Verification (using voltage formula):** $\;20 \log_{10}(50 / 0.5) = 20 \log_{10}(100) = 20 \times 2 = 40 \;\text{dB}$ ✓

**Example 2 — Thermal Noise RMS Voltage:**

> Given: $R = 10 \;\text{k}\Omega$, $T = 300 \;\text{K}$, $B = 10 \;\text{kHz}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{n,rms} &= \sqrt{4 k T B R} \\
&= \sqrt{4 \times (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 300 \times (10 \times 10^{3}) \times (10 \times 10^{3})} \\
&= \sqrt{4 \times 1.38 \times 3 \times 10^{5} \times 10^{-23+3+3}} \\
&= \sqrt{16.56 \times 10^{-12}} \\
&= 1.287 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{V} \\
&\approx 1.29 \;\mu\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Notice the noise voltage is only ~1.29 µV. This is precisely why low-level instrumentation amplifiers (e.g., INA128, AD620) with very low noise floors are mandatory when measuring sensor signals in the microvolt range.

**Example 3 — Bandwidth vs SNR Trade-off:**

> Given: $V_s = 2 \;\text{V rms}$, $V_n$ dominated by thermal noise, $B = 10 \;\text{kHz}$ initially, then reduced to $1 \;\text{kHz}$.

Thermal noise scales as $\sqrt{B}$. So if bandwidth reduces by factor of 10, noise voltage reduces by $\sqrt{10} \approx 3.162$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{n,1} &= V_{n,0} / \sqrt{10} \\
\text{SNR}_{1, dB} &= \text{SNR}_{0, dB} + 10 \log_{10}(10) = \text{SNR}_{0, dB} + 10 \;\text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **A 10× bandwidth reduction yields exactly a 10 dB SNR improvement.** This is a classic KTU problem and a foundational result in communication theory.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Noise Flow in an Instrumentation Chain

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcA[Physical Quantity] --> trA[Transducer / Sensor]
    trA --> sigA[Desired Signal Vsig]
    sigA --> node1[Signal Conditioning Stage]
    node1 --> ampA[Preamplifier]
    ampA --> fltA[Bandpass Filter]
    fltA --> adcA[ADC and Processor]
    adcA --> outA[Displayed Output]

    nsrc1[Thermal Noise Source] -.->|adds noise| node1
    nsrc2[Shot Noise Source] -.->|adds noise| ampA
    nsrc3[Flicker 1/f Noise] -.->|adds noise| trA
    nsrc4[EMI and Powerline Hum] -.->|couples inductively| fltA
    nsrc5[Quantization Noise] -.->|digital error| adcA

    node1 --> sumA[SNR Computation Point]
    sumA --> outB[Final SNR in dB]
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- The **solid arrows** trace the **signal path** from physical variable to displayed output.
- The **dashed arrows** represent **noise injection points** at each stage of the measurement chain.
- The **SNR Computation Point** aggregates the signal amplitude and the total accumulated noise from all sources to yield the final SNR in dB.

## 4.2 Hierarchical Noise Source Classification

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Total Noise in Instrumentation System]

    rootA --> intA[Internal Noise]
    rootA --> extA[External Noise]

    intA --> thA[Thermal Johnson Noise]
    intA --> shA[Shot Noise]
    intA --> flA[Flicker 1 over f Noise]
    intA --> qnA[Quantization Noise]

    extA --> emA[EMI Electromagnetic Interference]
    extA --> rfA[Radio Frequency Interference]
    extA --> pwA[Powerline 50 Hz Hum]
    extA --> gpA[Ground Loop Noise]
```

**Interpretation:** The diagram classifies noise into **internal (generated by components themselves)** and **external (injected from the environment)**. The sum of all such powers gives the total system noise used in the SNR denominator.

## 4.3 Functional Flow — From Physical Phenomenon to SNR Output

```mermaid
graph LR
    pA[Step 1 Physical Variable] --> pB[Step 2 Transduction to Voltage]
    pB --> pC[Step 3 Superposition of Noise Sources]
    pC --> pD[Step 4 Amplification]
    pD --> pE[Step 5 Filtering]
    pE --> pF[Step 6 Compute Psignal and Pnoise]
    pF --> pG[Step 7 Calculate SNR dB]
    pG --> pH[Step 8 Compare with Threshold for Validity]
```

> This step-by-step flow mirrors the **operational methodology** an instrumentation engineer follows when validating a measurement system against a specified SNR requirement.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`

**Define Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR). Mention its significance in instrumentation systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Signal-to-Noise Ratio is defined as the ratio of the desired signal power to the unwanted noise power present in a measurement system, expressed as:

$$
\text{SNR} = \frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}} \quad \text{or in dB:} \quad \text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_s}{P_n}\right)
$$

**Significance:** SNR quantifies the *clarity* and *reliability* of a measurement. A high SNR ensures accurate detection, faithful reproduction, and trustworthy decision-making in control loops. In medical and industrial instrumentation, low SNR can lead to **false readings**, misdiagnosis, and unsafe control actions.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for significance.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**List any four sources of noise in a measurement system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Thermal (Johnson) noise** — due to random electron motion in conductors.
2. **Shot noise** — due to discrete charge carrier flow across junctions.
3. **Flicker (1/f) noise** — dominant at low frequencies in semiconductors.
4. **Electromagnetic interference (EMI)** — from nearby power lines, motors, RF sources.

*(Any four with one-line descriptions = 3 marks; bare list = 2 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) Derive the expression for the RMS value of thermal (Johnson) noise voltage developed across a resistor. State the assumptions used.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**

1. The resistor is at absolute temperature $T$ Kelvin.
2. The system is in thermal equilibrium.
3. The noise is random (Gaussian) and statistically stationary.
4. The noise is measured over an equivalent noise bandwidth $B$.

**Derivation:**

By Nyquist's theorem, the mean-square noise voltage across a resistor $R$ over bandwidth $B$ at temperature $T$ is given by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{v_n^2} &= 4 k T B R \\
\text{where} \quad k &= 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \;\text{J/K} \quad \text{(Boltzmann constant)} \\
T &= \text{absolute temperature in K} \\
B &= \text{noise-equivalent bandwidth in Hz} \\
R &= \text{resistance in } \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

The **RMS noise voltage** is therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;v_{n,rms} = \sqrt{4 k T B R}\;}
$$

**Physical Interpretation (for full marks):**
- Noise voltage is **proportional to** $\sqrt{T}$, $\sqrt{B}$, and $\sqrt{R}$.
- Doubling the temperature or bandwidth increases the RMS noise by a factor of $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$.
- This noise is **unavoidable** — it is a fundamental physical limit set by thermodynamics (often called the *Johnson-Nyquist limit*).

**(b) A transducer delivers an output of 2 V RMS with a noise level of 10 μV RMS, measured over a 10 kHz bandwidth. Calculate: (i) the SNR in dB, and (ii) the new SNR if the bandwidth is reduced to 1 kHz (assuming thermal noise dominates).** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) SNR in dB at original bandwidth:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SNR}_{linear} &= \frac{V_s^2}{V_n^2} = \frac{(2)^2}{(10 \times 10^{-6})^2} = \frac{4}{10^{-10}} = 4 \times 10^{10} \\
\text{SNR}_{dB} &= 10 \log_{10}(4 \times 10^{10}) \\
&= 10 \left[\log_{10}(4) + 10\right] \\
&= 10 \left[0.602 + 10\right] \\
&= 10 \times 10.602 \\
&= 106.02 \;\text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) New SNR after bandwidth reduction to 1 kHz:**

Since thermal noise dominates, noise voltage scales as $\sqrt{B}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{V_{n,new}}{V_{n,old}} &= \sqrt{\frac{B_{new}}{B_{old}}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{10}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{10}} \\
\text{SNR improvement in dB} &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{B_{old}}{B_{new}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(10) = 10 \;\text{dB} \\
\text{New SNR}_{dB} &= 106.02 + 10 = 116.02 \;\text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Key Result:** Reducing bandwidth by a factor of 10 improves the SNR by exactly 10 dB.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the different sources of noise in an instrumentation system. How are they mitigated?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.2):**

The total noise in any instrumentation chain can be classified as:

| Source | Origin | Mitigation Technique |
|---|---|---|
| **Thermal noise** | Random electron motion in conductors | Reduce $R$, cool the system, minimize bandwidth |
| **Shot noise** | Discrete charge flow in semiconductor junctions | Use low-bias devices, FETs at low current |
| **Flicker noise** | Surface traps, impurities in semiconductors | Use chopper stabilization, AC coupling |
| **EMI / RFI** | External electromagnetic fields | Shielding, twisted pairs, proper grounding |
| **Powerline hum** | 50 Hz mains coupling | Use 50 Hz notch filter, differential amplification |
| **Ground loops** | Multiple ground paths | Single-point grounding, isolation amplifiers |
| **Quantization noise** | ADC finite resolution | Increase ADC bits, oversampling, dithering |

**Mitigation Hierarchy (for full marks):**

1. **Source elimination** — Shield cables, isolate grounds.
2. **Bandwidth limitation** — Use narrow bandpass filters to admit only the signal band.
3. **Modulation and synchronous detection** — Chopper-stabilized amplifiers.
4. **Averaging** — Multiple readings reduce random noise by $\sqrt{N}$.
5. **Cooling** — Lower $T$ reduces thermal noise (used in sensitive radio astronomy).

**(b) An instrumentation amplifier has an input signal of 25 mV peak-to-peak and an input noise of 5 μV RMS. If the amplifier has a voltage gain of 100 and adds 20 μV RMS of noise, calculate: (i) the input SNR in dB, and (ii) the output SNR in dB.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Converting peak-to-peak to RMS** (assuming sinusoidal: $V_{rms} = V_{p-p} / (2\sqrt{2})$):

$$
V_{s,rms} = \frac{25 \times 10^{-3}}{2\sqrt{2}} = \frac{25 \times 10^{-3}}{2.828} = 8.84 \;\text{mV}
$$

**(i) Input SNR in dB:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SNR}_{in,linear} &= \frac{V_{s,rms}^2}{V_{n,in}^2} = \frac{(8.84 \times 10^{-3})^2}{(5 \times 10^{-6})^2} = \frac{7.815 \times 10^{-5}}{2.5 \times 10^{-11}} = 3.126 \times 10^{6} \\
\text{SNR}_{in,dB} &= 10 \log_{10}(3.126 \times 10^{6}) = 10 \times 6.495 = 64.95 \;\text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Output SNR in dB:**

- Output signal: $V_{s,out} = 100 \times 8.84 \;\text{mV} = 884 \;\text{mV RMS}$
- Output noise: $V_{n,out} = \sqrt{(100 \times 5 \times 10^{-6})^2 + (20 \times 10^{-6})^2}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{n,out} &= \sqrt{(500 \times 10^{-6})^2 + (20 \times 10^{-6})^2} \\
&= \sqrt{2.5 \times 10^{-7} + 4 \times 10^{-10}} \\
&= \sqrt{2.504 \times 10^{-7}} \\
&= 5.004 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{V} = 500.4 \;\mu\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SNR}_{out,linear} &= \frac{(884 \times 10^{-3})^2}{(500.4 \times 10^{-6})^2} = \frac{0.7815}{2.504 \times 10^{-7}} = 3.121 \times 10^{6} \\
\text{SNR}_{out,dB} &= 10 \log_{10}(3.121 \times 10^{6}) = 64.94 \;\text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Key Result:** The output SNR is *marginally lower* than the input SNR by approximately 0.01 dB — this small degradation quantifies the **Noise Figure** of the amplifier. The amplifier's *own* added noise (20 μV) is negligible compared to the amplified input noise (~500 μV) because the input signal-to-noise ratio is high.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Points:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the Johnson noise formula and writing $v_n^2 = 2 k T B R$ instead of $4 k T B R$ → loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Using $10 \log$ for voltage ratios** instead of $20 \log$ → yields wrong dB values; loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Adding noise amplitudes** instead of noise *powers* (a voltage-level error) → the noise voltages must be added in quadrature: $V_{n,total}^2 = V_{n1}^2 + V_{n2}^2$, not $V_{n,total} = V_{n1} + V_{n2}$.
> 4. **Skipping unit conversions** — mixing mV with μV, or p-p with RMS without stating the conversion factor.
> 5. **Not stating the Boltzmann constant value** explicitly in derivation problems — KTU examiners often allocate 0.5 mark for this.
> 6. **Confusing Noise Figure with SNR** — Noise Figure is the *degradation* of SNR through an amplifier; it is not the same as SNR itself.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **SNR Definition:** $S/N$ (linear) or $10 \log_{10}(S/N)$ in dB. Use $20 \log$ for voltage/amplitude ratios.
- **Johnson Thermal Noise:** $v_{n,rms} = \sqrt{4 k T B R}$ — the most-tested formula in this module.
- **Boltzmann Constant:** $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K — memorize this.
- **Reference Temperature:** Standard is 290 K (sometimes 300 K is used in numerical problems; check the question).
- **Bandwidth-SNR Trade-off:** Halving $B$ → noise voltage drops by $\sqrt{2}$ → SNR improves by 3 dB.
- **Noise Sources (in order of importance for KTU):** Thermal, Shot, Flicker, EMI, Quantization.
- **Independent Noise Sources:** Their **powers add**, not their amplitudes.
- **Noise Figure (NF):** Measures SNR degradation by an amplifier; lower NF ⇒ better amplifier.
- **High SNR** ⇒ High precision, low measurement uncertainty.
- **Typical SNR Targets:** Medical (≥ 40 dB), Hi-Fi audio (≥ 60 dB), CD quality (≥ 90 dB), precision lab (≥ 100 dB).
- **Common Conversion:** Peak-to-peak to RMS for a sinusoid: $V_{rms} = V_{p-p} / (2\sqrt{2})$.
- **Remember the 3 dB Rule:** A 3 dB increase in SNR corresponds to a doubling of signal power (or halving of noise power).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sources of Noise
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT205 - BASIC OF INSTRUMENTATION ENGINEERING (INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sources of Noise

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Instrumentation Engineering** and the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus, **Noise** is defined as any unwanted, random, and uncorrelated electrical signal that interferes with, contaminates, or obscures the desired information-carrying signal in a measurement or communication system. Noise is an inherent property of all electronic components, materials, and transmission channels, and it sets the fundamental lower limit on the smallest signal amplitude that can be reliably measured or detected.

**Sources of Noise** refer to the physical, electrical, and environmental origins from which these unwanted signals are generated. In an instrumentation chain, noise can be introduced at every stage: from the transducer, through the signal conditioning circuits (amplifiers, filters), to the data acquisition and display systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Students must be able to classify noise into intrinsic (internal) and extrinsic (external) categories, derive the governing noise equations, and calculate the effective signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) at the output of a measurement system.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to have a quiet, intimate conversation with a friend inside a large, crowded train station. Your friend's voice is the **desired signal** — clear, meaningful, and carrying the information you need. However, your ears also pick up:
- The constant low **hiss** of the air-conditioning system (this is like **thermal noise**).
- Random **clatter** of footsteps and dragging luggage (this is like **shot noise**).
- A low **rumble** that gets louder as a heavy train passes by (this is like **flicker/1/f noise**).
- Occasional **loud pops** from a public announcement system (this is like **burst/popcorn noise**).

Even if you cup your hands around your ears (the equivalent of using a **filter** or a **low-noise amplifier**), you can never completely eliminate these unwanted sounds because some of them are physically generated by the very air and surfaces around you. Similarly, in electronics, noise is generated by the **resistors, semiconductors, and interconnections** themselves due to the discrete, random motion of electrons and charge carriers.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants (Must Memorize for KTU Exams)

| Symbol | Constant | Value | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $k$ | Boltzmann Constant | $1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ | J/K |
| $q$ | Electron Charge | $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ | Coulombs (C) |
| $T$ | Absolute Temperature | $290$ (Standard Reference) | Kelvin (K) |
| $B$ | Bandwidth (Noise Equivalent) | Variable | Hertz (Hz) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** The value $4kT$ at standard room temperature ($T = 290\,\text{K}$) evaluates to approximately $1.6 \times 10^{-20}\,\text{W/Hz}$. This numerical value appears frequently in KTU numerical problems and is a high-yield shortcut.

### 1.4 Broad Classification of Noise Sources

Sources of noise in instrumentation systems are broadly classified into two main families:

1. **Intrinsic Noise (Internal Noise):** Generated by the components and devices within the electronic system itself.
   - Thermal Noise (Johnson-Nyquist Noise)
   - Shot Noise
   - Flicker Noise ($1/f$ Noise)
   - Burst Noise (Popcorn Noise)
   - Avalanche Noise

2. **Extrinsic Noise (External Noise):** Originating from outside the system, typically coupled via electromagnetic induction, radiation, or power line interference.
   - Environmental / Atmospheric Noise
   - Industrial Noise
   - Electromagnetic Interference (EMI) / Radio Frequency Interference (RFI)
   - Crosstalk
   - Power Line Hum (50 Hz / 60 Hz)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Analysis of Each Noise Source

#### 2.1.1 Thermal Noise (Johnson-Nyquist Noise)

**Origin:** The random, thermal agitation of charge carriers (electrons) inside a conductor or resistor at any temperature above absolute zero ($0\,\text{K}$). Even with no applied voltage, electrons move in a chaotic manner, producing a fluctuating voltage across the terminals.

**Why it matters:** Thermal noise is **ubiquitous** — it exists in *every* resistive element, in the channel resistance of FETs, in the series resistance of capacitors and inductors, and even in the source resistance of sensors. It is the ultimate physical limit on measurement sensitivity.

**How it behaves:**
- The mean-square noise voltage is **proportional to absolute temperature**.
- It is **proportional to the measurement bandwidth**.
- It is **uniformly distributed** across all frequencies (a "white noise" property).
- It is **independent of the DC current** flowing through the resistor.

#### 2.1.2 Shot Noise

**Origin:** The discrete, quantized nature of electric charge. Current is not a smooth fluid but a stream of individual electrons. In devices like diodes, BJTs, and photodiodes, the random emission or arrival of charge carriers across a potential barrier produces fluctuations.

**Why it matters:** Shot noise becomes the dominant noise source in **low-impedance, high-current** semiconductor devices. It is the theoretical floor for the noise performance of photodetectors and PIN diodes used in fiber-optic and radiation instrumentation.

**How it behaves:**
- Mean-square noise current is **proportional to the DC bias current**.
- It is **proportional to bandwidth**.
- It has a **flat (white) frequency spectrum** up to very high frequencies.
- It is absent in pure ohmic conductors (where conduction is by drift, not barrier emission).

#### 2.1.3 Flicker Noise ($1/f$ Noise)

**Origin:** Associated with imperfections in the semiconductor crystal lattice — trapping and de-trapping of charge carriers at oxide interfaces, contamination sites, and dislocations. It is also observed in carbon resistors and certain sensors.

**Why it matters:** At **low frequencies** (below approximately $1\,\text{kHz}$), $1/f$ noise often dominates over thermal and shot noise. It is the primary reason that precision DC amplifiers, strain gauge signal conditioners, and biomedical instrumentation (ECG, EEG) require **chopper stabilization** or **auto-zeroing** techniques.

**How it behaves:**
- Power spectral density is **inversely proportional to frequency** ($1/f$).
- Energy is concentrated at low frequencies.
- Strongly **device-dependent** and varies with fabrication technology.

#### 2.1.4 Burst Noise (Popcorn Noise)

**Origin:** Sudden, random switching between two or more discrete current levels in semiconductor junctions, typically caused by heavy-ion contamination or lattice defects in integrated circuits.

**Why it matters:** It appears as random "pops" of audio frequency in audio amplifiers and DC offsets in op-amps. Although less common in modern ICs, it is a known failure mechanism.

#### 2.1.5 Avalanche Noise

**Origin:** Occurs in PN junctions operated in **reverse breakdown (avalanche) mode**, such as Zener diodes and avalanche photodiodes. A carrier gains enough kinetic energy to create new electron-hole pairs by impact ionization, producing a random multiplication chain.

**Why it matters:** It is the dominant noise in Zener-diode-based voltage references and avalanche photodiodes used for low-light detection.

#### 2.1.6 Environmental / External Noise

- **Atmospheric Noise:** Lightning discharges, static electricity.
- **Industrial Noise:** Motors, relays, switching power supplies, fluorescent lamps.
- **EMI/RFI:** Broadcast transmitters, mobile phones, Wi-Fi routers.
- **Crosstalk:** Capacitive or inductive coupling between adjacent signal traces in a PCB or between cables.
- **Power Line Hum:** 50 Hz interference from mains supply and its harmonics (100 Hz, 150 Hz, ...).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **exam-critical**. Every formula here has appeared in KTU 2024 scheme question papers and is highly likely to recur. Memorize the symbols, units, and boundary conditions.

| # | Noise Type | Governing Equation (Mean-Square Value) | Spectral Density Form | Unit | Key Proportionality |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Thermal (Voltage) | $\overline{V_n^2} = 4kTBR$ | $S_V(f) = 4kTR$ | $\text{V}^2$ | $\propto T \cdot B \cdot R$ |
| 2 | Thermal (Current) | $\overline{I_n^2} = \dfrac{4kTB}{R}$ | $S_I(f) = \dfrac{4kT}{R}$ | $\text{A}^2$ | $\propto T \cdot B \cdot R^{-1}$ |
| 3 | Shot Noise | $\overline{I_n^2} = 2qI_{dc}B$ | $S_I(f) = 2qI_{dc}$ | $\text{A}^2$ | $\propto I_{dc} \cdot B$ |
| 4 | Flicker ($1/f$) Voltage | $\overline{V_n^2} = \dfrac{KI_{dc} B}{f}$ | $S_V(f) = \dfrac{KI_{dc}}{f}$ | $\text{V}^2$ | $\propto I_{dc} \cdot B \cdot f^{-1}$ |
| 5 | Flicker ($1/f$) Current | $\overline{I_n^2} = \dfrac{K I_n^{\alpha} B}{f}$ | $S_I(f) = \dfrac{K I_n^{\alpha}}{f}$ | $\text{A}^2$ | $\propto f^{-1}$ |
| 6 | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $\text{SNR} = \dfrac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}$ | — | dimensionless (dB) | — |
| 7 | SNR in Decibels | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_s}{P_n}\right)$ | — | dB | — |
| 8 | Noise Figure | $F = \dfrac{\text{SNR}_{\text{in}}}{\text{SNR}_{\text{out}}}$ | — | dimensionless (dB) | $F \geq 1$ |
| 9 | Noise Temperature | $T_n = T_0 (F - 1)$ | — | Kelvin | $T_0 = 290\,\text{K}$ |
| 10 | Total RMS Noise | $V_{n,\text{tot}} = \sqrt{\int_{f_1}^{f_2} S_V(f)\,df}$ | — | V (RMS) | Integration over band |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, all absolute value bars are written using the LaTeX `\vert` macro to prevent breaking the Markdown table syntax. In your examination answer sheets, use the standard vertical bar $\vert x \vert$ notation.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding noise sources is not an academic exercise — it is the foundation of **every precision measurement**:

- **Medical Instrumentation (ECG/EEG):** Electrode-skin contact noise (flicker-type) limits the detection of microvolt-level biopotentials.
- **Aerospace Telemetry:** Cosmic radiation produces shot-like noise in high-altitude sensors.
- **Process Control:** Thermocouple lead wires contribute thermal noise that is indistinguishable from the true temperature reading.
- **Optical Communication:** Avalanche photodiodes (APDs) amplify signal via impact ionization but simultaneously amplify the noise.
- **Audio Engineering:** Flicker noise in op-amps dictates the choice of chopper-stabilized or zero-drift amplifiers for studio-quality preamps.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Thermal Noise Across a Resistor

We derive the **Nyquist Theorem** (one of the foundational results in noise theory) from first principles using classical statistical mechanics.

**Step 1 — Energy Equipartition:**
For a system in thermal equilibrium at temperature $T$, each degree of freedom carries an average energy of $\dfrac{1}{2}kT$. A lossless LC resonator (tank circuit) at resonance has two energy-storing elements (L and C), giving total mean energy $E = kT$.

**Step 2 — Power Radiated by a Resonator:**
A series RLC circuit of resistance $R$ has a quality factor $Q = \dfrac{\omega_0 L}{R}$. The energy decays with time constant $\tau = \dfrac{2L}{R}$, and the average power dissipated is $P = \dfrac{E}{\tau}$.

**Step 3 — Spectral Energy Density:**
The mean-square noise voltage across the resistor, measured over a narrow bandwidth $\Delta f$ centered at $f_0$, can be written as:

$$
S_V(f) = \lim_{\Delta f \to 0} \frac{\overline{V_n^2}}{\Delta f}
$$

**Step 4 — Combining via Equipartition:**
Equating the energy delivered by the noise source to the energy dissipated in R, the spectral density becomes:

$$
S_V(f) = 4kTR \quad \left[\frac{\text{V}^2}{\text{Hz}}\right]
$$

**Step 5 — Mean-Square Value Over a Bandwidth B:**
Integrating the flat (white) spectrum from $f_1$ to $f_2$ where $B = f_2 - f_1$:

$$
\overline{V_n^2} = \int_{0}^{B} S_V(f)\,df = \int_{0}^{B} 4kTR\,df
$$

$$
\overline{V_n^2} = 4kTRB
$$

This is the celebrated **Johnson-Nyquist formula**. The corresponding RMS noise voltage is:

$$
V_{n,\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{4kTRB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The integration of a constant (white noise) over a frequency band simply multiplies it by the bandwidth. This is why reducing the measurement bandwidth $B$ (via filtering) directly reduces the thermal noise — a key design principle in lock-in amplifiers and spectrum analyzers.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Shot Noise Mean-Square Current

**Step 1 — Model the Current as a Poisson Process:**
Consider $N$ independent charge carriers, each carrying charge $q$, crossing a potential barrier in time $t$. The average current is $I_{dc} = \dfrac{Nq}{t}$.

**Step 2 — Variance of a Poisson Distribution:**
For a Poisson process, the variance of the count $N$ equals its mean: $\sigma_N^2 = \langle N \rangle$.

**Step 3 — Variance of the Current:**
The instantaneous current is $i(t) = \dfrac{n(t) q}{t}$ where $n(t)$ is the number of carriers in interval $t$. The mean-square fluctuation is:

$$
\overline{(i - I_{dc})^2} = \frac{q^2 \sigma_N^2}{t^2} = \frac{q^2 \langle N \rangle}{t^2} = \frac{q I_{dc}}{t}
$$

**Step 4 — Introduce the Bandwidth:**
The observation time $t$ is related to the measurement bandwidth by the Fourier uncertainty relation $B = \dfrac{1}{2t}$, giving $t = \dfrac{1}{2B}$. Substituting:

$$
\overline{I_n^2} = 2qI_{dc}B
$$

This is the **Schottky Shot Noise Formula**. The RMS shot noise current is:

$$
I_{n,\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{2qI_{dc}B}
$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU Board Pattern)

#### Example 3.3.1 — Thermal Noise Calculation

**Problem:** A $10\,\text{k}\Omega$ resistor is measured by an instrument with a noise bandwidth of $100\,\text{kHz}$ at room temperature $T = 300\,\text{K}$. Calculate the RMS thermal noise voltage.

**Step 1 — Identify the values:**
$R = 10 \times 10^3 = 10^4\,\Omega$, $B = 100 \times 10^3 = 10^5\,\text{Hz}$, $T = 300\,\text{K}$, $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\,\text{J/K}$.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the Johnson formula:**

$$
V_{n,\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{4kTRB}
$$

$$
V_{n,\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{4 \times (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 300 \times 10^4 \times 10^5}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerical product inside the square root:**

$$
4 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300 \times 10^4 \times 10^5
$$

$$
= 4 \times 1.38 \times 300 \times 10^{-23+4+5}
$$

$$
= 4 \times 1.38 \times 300 \times 10^{-14}
$$

$$
= 1656 \times 10^{-14} = 1.656 \times 10^{-11}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the square root:**

$$
V_{n,\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{1.656 \times 10^{-11}} \approx 1.287 \times 10^{-5.5}
$$

$$
V_{n,\text{RMS}} \approx 12.87\,\mu\text{V}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** $V_{n,\text{RMS}} \approx 12.87\,\mu\text{V}$. This is approximately **40 times smaller** than the thermal noise of a $1\,\text{M}\Omega$ resistor in the same bandwidth — illustrating why low-impedance signal chains are preferred for low-noise design.

#### Example 3.3.2 — Shot Noise in a Photodiode

**Problem:** A photodiode produces a DC photocurrent of $I_{dc} = 5\,\mu\text{A}$. The measurement bandwidth is $B = 1\,\text{MHz}$. Find the RMS shot noise current.

**Step 1 — Substitute values into the Schottky formula:**

$$
I_{n,\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{2qI_{dc}B}
$$

$$
I_{n,\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{2 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^6}
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the product inside the radical:**

$$
2 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^6
$$

$$
= 2 \times 1.602 \times 5 \times 10^{-19-6+6}
$$

$$
= 16.02 \times 10^{-19} = 1.602 \times 10^{-18}
$$

**Step 3 — Take the square root:**

$$
I_{n,\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{1.602 \times 10^{-18}} \approx 1.266 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{A}
$$

$$
I_{n,\text{RMS}} \approx 1.27\,\text{nA}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** $I_{n,\text{RMS}} \approx 1.27\,\text{nA}$. This noise floor determines the minimum detectable optical power in a photodiode-based instrumentation system.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Thermal Noise | Shot Noise | Flicker Noise | Burst Noise | Avalanche Noise |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Origin** | Charge carrier thermal motion | Discrete charge emission | Lattice defects, traps | Heavy-ion contamination | Impact ionization in breakdown |
| **Devices** | All resistors, FETs | Diodes, BJTs, photodiodes | Semiconductors, carbon R | ICs (defective) | Zeners, APDs |
| **Spectrum** | White (flat) | White (flat) | $1/f$ (pink) | Multi-level random | White (approximately) |
| **Depends on** | $T, B, R$ | $I_{dc}, B$ | $I_{dc}, f, B$ | Junction quality | $I_{dc}, B$ |
| **Dominant at** | All frequencies | High frequencies | Low frequencies ($<1\,\text{kHz}$) | Audio band | Reverse-biased junctions |
| **Reducible by** | Cooling, lowering R, narrow B | Reducing bias current | Chopper stabilization | Better IC fabrication | Avoid breakdown region |
| **Statistical Type** | Gaussian | Poisson | Non-Gaussian | Random telegraph | Multiplicative Gaussian |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Noise in an Instrumentation System

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates where each major noise source enters a typical transducer-based measurement system, and the corresponding mitigation strategy applied at each stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sensorA[Physical Quantity] --> transB[Transducer Stage]
    transB --> transC[Sensor Output Signal]
    transC --> condD[Signal Conditioner]
    condD --> condE[Amplifier Stage]
    condE --> procF[Filter and ADC]
    procF --> procG[Digital Output]

    noise1[Thermal Noise from Source R] --> condE
    noise2[Shot Noise from Diode Junctions] --> condE
    noise3[Flicker Noise at Low Frequency] --> condE
    noise4[Burst Noise from Defects] --> condE
    noise5[Avalanche Noise from APD] --> transB
    noise6[EMI from Environment] --> condD
    noise7[Power Line Hum 50Hz] --> condD
    noise8[Crosstalk from PCB Traces] --> condD

    condE --> mit1[Use Low-Noise Op-Amp]
    condE --> mit2[Bandwidth Limiting Filter]
    condD --> mit3[Shielding and Grounding]
    condD --> mit4[Differential Amplifier]
    procF --> mit5[Digital Averaging]

    mit1 --> procF
    mit2 --> procF
    mit3 --> condD
    mit4 --> condD
    mit5 --> procG
```

### 4.2 Noise Frequency Spectrum — Conceptual Schematic

The following block diagram shows the **combined noise power spectral density** of a typical electronic system as a function of frequency, with each region labeled according to the dominant noise mechanism.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    regionA[Region 1: 1/f Dominant] -->|Low Frequency| regionB[Region 2: Thermal and Shot Plateau]
    regionB -->|High Frequency| regionC[Region 3: Gain Roll-Off Decay]

    axisX[Frequency Axis Log Scale] --> regionA
    axisY[Noise Power Spectral Density] --> regionA

    regionA --> label1[Flicker Noise Falls as 1/f]
    regionB --> label2[White Noise: Thermal plus Shot]
    regionC --> label3[Capacitive Cutoff Reduces PSD]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** In **Region 1** (typically $f < 1\,\text{kHz}$), flicker noise dominates and the PSD decreases as $1/f$. In **Region 2** (the mid-band plateau), white noise sources (thermal + shot) dominate and the PSD is flat. In **Region 3** (high frequencies), the system's finite bandwidth causes the PSD to roll off. The optimal operating point for a low-noise amplifier is chosen at the **corner frequency** $f_c$ where $1/f$ noise equals the white-noise floor.

### 4.3 Noise Source Decision Tree

A quick-reference flowchart to identify which noise source is dominant in a given scenario.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Identify Noise Source] --> q1{Is the source a passive resistor or conductor?}
    q1 -- Yes --> out1[Thermal Johnson Noise]
    q1 -- No --> q2{Is there a PN junction with DC bias current?}
    q2 -- Yes --> q3{Is the junction in reverse breakdown?}
    q3 -- Yes --> out2[Avalanche Noise]
    q3 -- No --> q4{Is the operating frequency below 1 kHz?}
    q4 -- Yes --> out3[Flicker 1/f Noise]
    q4 -- No --> out4[Shot Noise]
    q2 -- No --> q5{Is the noise from external radiation or coupling?}
    q5 -- Yes --> out5[EMI, RFI, or Crosstalk]
    q5 -- No --> q6{Is the interference at 50 Hz or 60 Hz?}
    q6 -- Yes --> out6[Power Line Hum]
    q6 -- No --> out7[Atmospheric or Industrial Noise]
```
