## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define thermal noise. Derive the expression for the thermal noise voltage generated by a resistor $R$ at temperature $T$ over a bandwidth $B$.*

**Model Answer:**
Thermal noise (also called Johnson-Nyquist noise) is the random fluctuating voltage that appears across the terminals of a resistor or any passive conductor due to the thermal agitation of charge carriers (electrons) at a temperature above absolute zero. It is a fundamental, unavoidable property of all resistive elements.

The mean-square thermal noise voltage is given by the **Nyquist Formula**:

$$
\overline{V_n^2} = 4kTBR
$$

where $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\,\text{J/K}$ is the Boltzmann constant, $T$ is the absolute temperature in Kelvin, $B$ is the noise equivalent bandwidth in Hertz, and $R$ is the resistance in Ohms. The corresponding RMS noise voltage is $V_{n,\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{4kTBR}$.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Differentiate between shot noise and thermal noise. Mention the devices in which each is dominant.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Thermal Noise | Shot Noise |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Origin** | Random thermal motion of charge carriers | Discrete quantized emission of charge carriers across a junction |
| **Exists in** | All resistors and conductors | Devices with PN junctions (diodes, BJTs, FETs) |
| **Depends on** | Temperature $T$, resistance $R$, bandwidth $B$ | DC current $I_{dc}$, bandwidth $B$ |
| **Independent of** | Applied voltage or current | Temperature (at first order) |
| **Spectrum** | White (flat in $f$) | White (flat in $f$) |
| **Formula** | $\overline{V_n^2} = 4kTBR$ | $\overline{I_n^2} = 2qI_{dc}B$ |
| **Dominant in** | High-impedance passive circuits | Low-impedance semiconductor devices with DC bias |

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, classify the different sources of noise in an instrumentation system. Discuss in detail any two intrinsic noise sources. (7 Marks)

**Q. (b)** A $50\,\text{k}\Omega$ resistor is connected across the input of an amplifier with a noise bandwidth of $200\,\text{kHz}$ at a temperature of $300\,\text{K}$. Calculate:
(i) The RMS thermal noise voltage.
(ii) The corresponding RMS thermal noise current.
(iii) The mean-square noise voltage. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Classification of Noise Sources:**

**A. Intrinsic (Internal) Noise Sources:**
1. Thermal Noise (Johnson-Nyquist Noise)
2. Shot Noise (Schottky Noise)
3. Flicker Noise ($1/f$ Noise)
4. Burst Noise (Popcorn Noise)
5. Avalanche Noise

**B. Extrinsic (External) Noise Sources:**
1. Electromagnetic Interference (EMI) / Radio Frequency Interference (RFI)
2. Power Line Hum (50 Hz / 60 Hz)
3. Atmospheric Noise (lightning, static)
4. Industrial Noise (motors, switching devices)
5. Crosstalk (capacitive/inductive coupling)

**Detailed Discussion of Two Intrinsic Sources:**

**1. Thermal Noise:**
- Originates from the random thermal agitation of electrons in a conductor.
- Formula: $\overline{V_n^2} = 4kTBR$
- Uniform (white) spectrum across all frequencies.
- Proportional to $T$, $B$, and $R$.
- Independent of DC current or applied voltage.

**2. Shot Noise:**
- Originates from the discrete nature of charge carriers crossing a potential barrier.
- Formula: $\overline{I_n^2} = 2qI_{dc}B$
- Predominant in semiconductor devices like BJTs, diodes, and photodiodes.
- Proportional to the DC current $I_{dc}$.
- White spectrum up to very high frequencies.

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given: $R = 50 \times 10^3\,\Omega$, $B = 200 \times 10^3\,\text{Hz}$, $T = 300\,\text{K}$, $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\,\text{J/K}$.

**(i) RMS Thermal Noise Voltage:**

$$
V_{n,\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{4kTBR}
$$

$$
V_{n,\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{4 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300 \times 50 \times 10^3 \times 200 \times 10^3}
$$

**Step-by-step simplification:**

$$
= \sqrt{4 \times 1.38 \times 300 \times 50 \times 200 \times 10^{-23+3+3}}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{4 \times 1.38 \times 300 \times 10000 \times 10^{-17}}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{4 \times 1.38 \times 3 \times 10^6 \times 10^{-17}}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{4 \times 4.14 \times 10^{-11}} = \sqrt{16.56 \times 10^{-11}}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{1.656 \times 10^{-10}} \approx 1.287 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{V}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{n,\text{RMS}} \approx 12.87\,\mu\text{V}}
$$

**(ii) RMS Thermal Noise Current:**

$$
I_{n,\text{RMS}} = \frac{V_{n,\text{RMS}}}{R} = \frac{12.87 \times 10^{-6}}{50 \times 10^3}
$$

$$
\boxed{I_{n,\text{RMS}} \approx 0.257\,\text{nA}}
$$

**(iii) Mean-Square Noise Voltage:**

$$
\overline{V_n^2} = V_{n,\text{RMS}}^2 = (12.87 \times 10^{-6})^2
$$

$$
\boxed{\overline{V_n^2} \approx 1.656 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{V}^2}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]

**Q. (a)** Explain flicker noise and shot noise. Derive the expression for the mean-square shot noise current in terms of DC current and bandwidth. (7 Marks)

**Q. (b)** A photodiode generates a DC photocurrent of $10\,\mu\text{A}$ and is measured with a transimpedance amplifier having a noise bandwidth of $5\,\text{MHz}$ at $T = 290\,\text{K}$. Calculate:
(i) The RMS shot noise current.
(ii) The signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) in dB if the signal current is the full DC value.
(iii) Comment on how the SNR will change if the bandwidth is reduced to $1\,\text{MHz}$. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Flicker Noise ($1/f$ Noise):**
Flicker noise is a low-frequency noise present in all semiconductor devices and some resistors. It arises due to crystal lattice defects, surface states, and contamination that cause random trapping and de-trapping of charge carriers. Its power spectral density is inversely proportional to frequency:

$$
S_V(f) = \frac{K I_{dc}}{f} \quad \left[\frac{\text{V}^2}{\text{Hz}}\right]
$$

The mean-square noise voltage in a band $f_1$ to $f_2$ is:

$$
\overline{V_n^2} = K I_{dc} \ln\left(\frac{f_2}{f_1}\right)
$$

Flicker noise dominates at low frequencies (typically below $1\,\text{kHz}$) and is reduced by chopper stabilization or auto-zero techniques.

**Shot Noise:**
Shot noise arises from the discrete quantized nature of charge carriers crossing a potential barrier. The **Schottky derivation** treats the arrival of carriers as a Poisson process. Let $N$ be the number of carriers crossing in time $t$, with mean $\langle N \rangle$ and variance $\sigma_N^2 = \langle N \rangle$ (Poisson statistics). The instantaneous current is $i = Nq/t$ and the average current is $I_{dc} = \langle N \rangle q/t$. The mean-square fluctuation in current is:

$$
\overline{I_n^2} = \frac{q^2 \sigma_N^2}{t^2} = \frac{q^2 \langle N \rangle}{t^2} = \frac{q I_{dc}}{t}
$$

Using the Fourier bandwidth relation $B = 1/(2t)$:

$$
\overline{I_n^2} = 2qI_{dc}B
$$

This is the **Schottky Shot Noise Formula**, applicable to BJTs, diodes, photodiodes, and any device with barrier-junction conduction.

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given: $I_{dc} = 10 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{A}$, $B = 5 \times 10^6\,\text{Hz}$, $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{C}$.

**(i) RMS Shot Noise Current:**

$$
I_{n,\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{2qI_{dc}B}
$$

$$
I_{n,\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{2 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 10 \times 10^{-6} \times 5 \times 10^6}
$$

**Step-by-step simplification:**

$$
= \sqrt{2 \times 1.602 \times 10 \times 5 \times 10^{-19-6+6}}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{2 \times 1.602 \times 50 \times 10^{-19}}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{160.2 \times 10^{-19}} = \sqrt{1.602 \times 10^{-17}}
$$

$$
I_{n,\text{RMS}} \approx 1.266 \times 10^{-8.5}
$$

$$
\boxed{I_{n,\text{RMS}} \approx 4.00 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{A} = 4.00\,\text{nA}}
$$

**(ii) Signal-to-Noise Ratio:**

$$
\text{SNR} = \frac{I_{\text{signal}}^2}{I_{n,\text{RMS}}^2} = \frac{(10 \times 10^{-6})^2}{(4.00 \times 10^{-9})^2} = \left(\frac{10 \times 10^{-6}}{4.00 \times 10^{-9}}\right)^2
$$

$$
= (2500)^2 = 6.25 \times 10^6
$$

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(6.25 \times 10^6) = 10 \times (6 + \log_{10}6.25) = 10 \times (6 + 0.796)
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} \approx 67.96\,\text{dB}}
$$

**(iii) Effect of Bandwidth Reduction:**

If the bandwidth is reduced from $5\,\text{MHz}$ to $1\,\text{MHz}$ (a factor of 5 reduction), the shot noise RMS reduces by a factor of $\sqrt{5} \approx 2.236$. The new SNR becomes:

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{new}} = 5 \times \text{SNR}_{\text{old}} = 5 \times 6.25 \times 10^6 = 3.125 \times 10^7
$$

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{new,dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(3.125 \times 10^7) \approx 74.95\,\text{dB}
$$

**Comment:** Reducing the bandwidth by a factor of 5 improves the SNR by approximately $7\,\text{dB}$, which is the equivalent of $\dfrac{1}{2} \times 10 \log_{10}(5) \approx 3.5\,\text{dB}$ of additional headroom per decade of bandwidth reduction. This confirms that **bandwidth limiting is the most effective way to improve SNR in shot-noise-limited photodiode systems**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Many students write $B$ in kHz but forget to convert to Hz. Always convert $B$ to **Hertz** before substituting into any noise formula. This single error costs 2-3 marks.
> 2. **RMS vs Mean-Square:** The Johnson and Schottky formulas give **mean-square** values, not RMS. You must apply the square root to obtain the RMS quantity, and the question will explicitly ask for one or the other.
> 3. **Temperature must be in Kelvin:** If the problem gives Celsius, add $273.15$ to obtain $T$ in K. Using Celsius directly is a common mistake and results in physically meaningless answers.
> 4. **SNR formula selection:** Use $10 \log_{10}$ for power/voltage-squared ratios, and $20 \log_{10}$ for voltage or current amplitude ratios. KTU answers that mix these are penalized.
> 5. **Shot noise is absent in pure resistors:** If a question asks "which noise is present in a pure resistor," the answer is **only thermal noise**. Shot noise requires a junction with barrier-emission.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Summary — Sources of Noise (Module 4)**

- **Thermal Noise (Johnson-Nyquist):** $\overline{V_n^2} = 4kTBR$, $\overline{I_n^2} = \dfrac{4kTB}{R}$. White, present in all resistors, dependent on $T$.
- **Shot Noise (Schottky):** $\overline{I_n^2} = 2qI_{dc}B$. White, present in PN junctions, dependent on $I_{dc}$.
- **Flicker Noise ($1/f$):** $S_V(f) = \dfrac{KI_{dc}}{f}$. Dominant at low frequencies, mitigated by chopper stabilization.
- **Burst Noise (Popcorn):** Random telegraph signal due to IC defects, appears as sudden jumps in DC level.
- **Avalanche Noise:** Occurs in reverse-biased junctions under breakdown (Zeners, APDs), large noise due to carrier multiplication.
- **Standard Temperature:** $T_0 = 290\,\text{K}$ (KTU reference value for noise figure calculations).
- **Boltzmann Constant:** $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\,\text{J/K}$.
- **Electron Charge:** $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{C}$.
- **SNR formula:** $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_s}{P_n}\right)$.
- **Noise Figure:** $F = \dfrac{\text{SNR}_{\text{in}}}{\text{SNR}_{\text{out}}} \geq 1$.
- **Noise Temperature:** $T_n = T_0(F - 1)$.
- **Bandwidth Limitation:** Reducing $B$ reduces both thermal and shot noise by $\sqrt{B}$.
- **Intrinsic vs Extrinsic:** Intrinsic = generated by the device itself; Extrinsic = picked up from the environment (EMI, RFI, power line).
- **Most Common Mistake:** Using $T$ in Celsius instead of Kelvin. Always convert.
- **Most Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting the square root when the question asks for RMS.
- **Effective Mitigation Hierarchy:** (1) Reduce bandwidth $\to$ (2) Lower temperature $\to$ (3) Use low-noise components $\to$ (4) Shield and ground properly $\to$ (5) Apply differential measurement.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Johnson Noise
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCICT205 - BASIC OF INSTRUMENTATION ENGINEERING (INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Johnson Noise ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition **Johnson Noise** (also called **Thermal Noise** or **Johnson–Nyquist Noise**) is the random, fluctuation-type electrical noise generated by the **thermal agitation of charge carriers** (electrons) inside a passive conductor or resistor, present even in the complete absence of any applied voltage or current. It sets a fundamental, unavoidable lower limit on the noise floor of any electronic measurement system. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Johnson Noise is a *white noise* process with a **flat (uniform) Power Spectral Density (PSD)** over an extremely wide frequency range, and its instantaneous amplitude follows a **zero-mean Gaussian probability distribution**. It is named after **John B. Johnson** (who first observed it in 1926 at Bell Labs) and **Harry Nyquist** (who provided its thermodynamic theory in 1928). ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a room full of blindfolded people (electrons) standing shoulder-to-shoulder in a narrow corridor (the resistor). Even when nobody is pushing (no applied voltage), every person is constantly jittering, shifting, bumping into neighbours because they all have **thermal energy** (heat). This continuous, random, microscopic bumping creates tiny, fluctuating pushes on the walls of the corridor — measurable as tiny fluctuating voltages across the conductor's terminals. The **hotter the room** (higher temperature), the more violent the jittering and the larger the noise. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Physical Constants (must be memorized):** > - **Boltzmann Constant ($k_B$): $1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K** > - **Absolute Temperature ($T$): in Kelvin** ($T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$) > - **Charge of an electron ($e$): $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C** (relevant for shot noise comparison) ### Engineering Significance Johnson noise determines the **smallest measurable signal** in any high-gain, low-frequency, or precision DC measurement (e.g., strain gauges, thermocouple amplifiers, biomedical ECG/EEG front-ends, radio astronomy). Without understanding thermal noise, instrument designers cannot specify the **minimum detectable signal (MDS)** or the **signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)** of their systems. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### Operational / Physical Logic (Why & How) - **Why does it exist?** The free electrons in a conductor are in constant random motion (Brownian motion) because they possess thermal kinetic energy. This motion, when summed over all electrons, produces a net fluctuating current even with **zero net current flow** at the terminals. - **How is it characterized?** Since it is a *stationary*, *ergodic*, *zero-mean* random process, it is fully described by its **mean square value** $\overline{V_n^2}$ and its **Power Spectral Density (PSD)**. - **Three engineering levers** to reduce Johnson noise in a circuit: 1. **Cool the resistor** (lower $T$) — used in cryogenic amplifiers and radio astronomy. 2. **Lower the source resistance $R$** — but this conflicts with sensor impedance matching. 3. **Restrict the measurement bandwidth $B$** — using band-pass filters. - **Universality:** Johnson noise is *resistance-dependent only* and is independent of the resistor's construction, material, or composition (carbon, metal film, wire-wound all behave identically at a given $R$ and $T$). > [!TIP] > **White vs. Coloured Noise:** Johnson noise is called **white noise** because the PSD is **constant (flat) up to frequencies of $\sim 10^{12}$ Hz**. Beyond this, quantum effects (Planck's distribution) cause the PSD to roll off, giving a $\text{sinc}$-shaped spectrum. ### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Key Variable Meaning | |---|----------|---------|------|---------------------| | 1 | **RMS Johnson Noise Voltage** | $V_n = \sqrt{4\,k_B\,T\,R\,B}$ | V (volts) | $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant; $T$ = temperature (K); $R$ = resistance ($\Omega$); $B$ = bandwidth (Hz) | | 2 | **Mean-Square Noise Voltage** | $\overline{V_n^2} = 4\,k_B\,T\,R\,B$ | $V^2$ | Used in SNR calculations | | 3 | **RMS Noise Current** | $I_n = \sqrt{4\,k_B\,T\,B / R}$ | A (amps) | Useful for current-mode sensors | | 4 | **Voltage PSD** | $S_V(f) = 4\,k_B\,T\,R$ | $V^2/\text{Hz}$ | Constant (white) in classical regime | | 5 | **Current PSD** | $S_I(f) = 4\,k_B\,T / R$ | $A^2/\text{Hz}$ | Constant (white) in classical regime | | 6 | **Available Noise Power** | $P_n = k_B\,T\,B$ | W (watts) | Maximum power transferable to a matched load | | 7 | **Noise Power per Hz** | $P_n/B = k_B\,T$ | W/Hz | A fundamental constant at temp $T$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > The factor of **4** (and not 2) in the Johnson formula arises from Nyquist's derivation using a transmission-line model with two propagating wave modes. This factor is a *very common KTU examiner trap* — students often write "2" instead of "4". ### Real-World Engineering Utility - **Noise Thermometry:** Measuring Johnson noise of a known resistor yields temperature (used in cryogenics and primary thermometers). - **True Random Number Generators (TRNGs):** Amplified Johnson noise is digitized to produce cryptographically secure randomness. - **Radio Astronomy (e.g., SKA, Arecibo):** Receiver front-ends are cooled to ~20 K to suppress Johnson noise of the first-stage LNA. - **Precision Weighing Scales & Strain Gauges:** Bridge-excitation voltage and amplifier noise floor are chosen so that Johnson noise of the gauge resistance is below the resolution. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### Derivation 1: Nyquist's Transmission-Line Model (The Main KTU Derivation) **Setup:** Consider a lossless transmission line of length $L$, characteristic impedance $Z_0 = R$, with both ends terminated in matched resistors $R$ to prevent reflections. The line is in thermal equilibrium at temperature $T$. **Step 1 — Counting electromagnetic modes:** Standing waves in the line of length $L$ have wavelengths $\lambda_n = 2L/n$ for mode $n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$ The corresponding frequencies are: $$f_n = \frac{v}{2L} n$$ **Step 2 — Degrees of freedom in a band $df$:** The number of modes in a frequency interval $df$ is: $$N(f)\,df = \frac{2L}{v}\,df$$ > The factor of 2 is included because waves can travel in **both directions** along the line (forward and backward), giving two independent modes per wavelength. **Step 3 — Equipartition Theorem:** Each electromagnetic mode behaves like a harmonic oscillator with quadratic energy $\tfrac{1}{2}k_BT$ per degree of freedom (one for the electric field, one for the magnetic field, giving $k_BT$ per mode). The total energy in a band $df$ at each end of the line is therefore: $$dE = k_BT \cdot N(f)\,df = k_BT \cdot \frac{2L}{v}\,df$$ **Step 4 — Power delivered to the matched load:** Energy $dE$ travels from the line into one matched resistor in a time: $$\Delta t = \frac{L}{v}$$ So the power delivered to one resistor in band $df$ is: $$dP = \frac{dE}{\Delta t} = k_BT \cdot \frac{2L}{v}\,df \cdot \frac{v}{L} = k_BT\,df$$ **Step 5 — Available noise power and the PSD:** For a matched load, the available power per unit bandwidth is constant: $$\frac{dP}{df} = k_BT \quad [\text{W/Hz}]$$ By Thevenin's theorem, the resistor $R$ can be modelled as a noiseless resistor in series with a noise voltage source. Maximum power is delivered when source and load are matched ($R = R$): $$P_n = k_BT \cdot B \quad [\text{W}]$$ **Step 6 — Converting power to RMS voltage:** Using $P = V_n^2 / (4R)$ (matched-load power formula): $$\frac{V_n^2}{4R} = k_BT \cdot B$$ Solving for $V_n^2$: $$V_n^2 = 4\,k_B T R B$$ Therefore the RMS Johnson noise voltage is: $$\boxed{\,V_n = \sqrt{4\,k_B T R B}\,}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Logic check (dimensional analysis):** $k_B T$ has units of Joules; $R$ has units of V/A; $B$ has units of 1/s. Their product has units of $V^2$, and the square root yields volts. ✔ Consistent. --- ### Derivation 2: Johnson Noise Current Derivation **Step 1:** Start from the mean-square noise voltage obtained above: $$\overline{V_n^2} = 4\,k_B T R B$$ **Step 2:** By Ohm's law, $I_n = V_n / R$, so: $$\overline{I_n^2} = \frac{\overline{V_n^2}}{R^2} = \frac{4\,k_B T R B}{R^2}$$ **Step 3:** Simplify: $$\boxed{\,I_n = \sqrt{\frac{4\,k_B T B}{R}}\,}$$ --- ### Derivation 3: Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard 3-Mark Style) **Problem:** A $10\,\text{k}\Omega$ resistor is measured using an oscilloscope with a bandwidth of $20\,\text{MHz}$ at room temperature ($T = 300\,\text{K}$). Calculate the RMS Johnson noise voltage and the noise power delivered to a matched load. **Given:** - $R = 10 \times 10^3 \,\Omega$ - $B = 20 \times 10^6 \,\text{Hz}$ - $T = 300\,\text{K}$ - $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\,\text{J/K}$ **Step 1 — Substitute into the voltage formula:** $$V_n = \sqrt{4 \times (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 300 \times (10 \times 10^3) \times (20 \times 10^6)}$$ **Step 2 — Multiply term by term:** $$4 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} = 5.52 \times 10^{-23}$$ $$5.52 \times 10^{-23} \times 300 = 1.656 \times 10^{-20}$$ $$1.656 \times 10^{-20} \times 10^{4} = 1.656 \times 10^{-16}$$ $$1.656 \times 10^{-16} \times (2 \times 10^{7}) = 3.312 \times 10^{-9}$$ **Step 3 — Take the square root:** $$V_n = \sqrt{3.312 \times 10^{-9}} = 5.755 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{V}$$ **Result:** $$\boxed{\,V_n \approx 57.55\,\mu\text{V (RMS)}\,}$$ **Step 4 — Available noise power:** $$P_n = k_B T B = (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 300 \times (20 \times 10^6)$$ $$\boxed{\,P_n = 8.28 \times 10^{-14}\,\text{W} = 82.8\,\text{fW}\,}$$ --- ### Derivation 4: Effect of Cascaded Resistors (Useful for KTU Part B Problems) When two resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$ are in **series** (and uncorrelated): $$V_{n,\text{total}}^2 = V_{n1}^2 + V_{n2}^2 = 4 k_B T B (R_1 + R_2)$$ When two resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$ are in **parallel**: $$\frac{1}{R_{\text{eq}}} = \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2}$$ $$V_{n,\text{total}}^2 = 4 k_B T B R_{\text{eq}}$$ > [!TIP] > In *all* these derivations, note that Johnson noise voltages are *uncorrelated random variables* — they add in **quadrature (Pythagorean sum)**, **never linearly**. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### Figure 4.1 — Thevenin Equivalent Model of a Noisy Resistor ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["Resistor R
at Temperature T
(Physical Device)"] nodeB["Noise Voltage Source Vn
Vn = sqrt(4 kB T R B)"] nodeC["Ideal Noiseless Resistor R"] nodeD["Open-Circuit Terminals
across which Vn is measured"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD -.feeds back.-> nodeA ``` > **Reading the schematic:** Any real resistor $R$ at temperature $T$ is electrically equivalent to an *ideal* noiseless resistor $R$ in **series** with a noise voltage source $V_n$ whose RMS value follows the Johnson–Nyquist formula. --- ### Figure 4.2 — Block-Level Functional Architecture: Johnson Noise Generation & Measurement ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph THERMO["Thermodynamic Source"] dirA["Thermal Energy kB T
in Conductor Lattice"] dirB["Random Electron Motion
Brownian Jitter"] end subgraph DEVN["Equivalent Noise Source"] dirC["Thevenin Voltage Vn
Gaussian White Noise"] end subgraph MEAS["Measurement Path"] dirD["Band-Limited Amplifier
Bandwidth B"] dirE["RMS Meter / Oscilloscope"] end dirA --> dirB --> dirC --> dirD --> dirE dirE -.feeds back.-> MEAS ``` > **Reading the architecture:** Thermal energy drives random electron motion, which manifests as a Gaussian white-noise voltage. The instrument chain (amplifier + RMS meter) sees this noise only over a finite bandwidth $B$, which directly scales the integrated noise power observed. --- ### Figure 4.3 — Power Spectral Density of Johnson Noise ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeX["Frequency f (Hz)"] nodeY["PSD S_v f (V squared per Hz)"] nodeZ["Classical Plateau
4 kB T R (constant)
valid for hf less than kB T"] nodeW["Quantum Roll-off
Planck Correction
near 10 to power 12 Hz"] nodeX --> nodeY nodeY --> nodeZ nodeZ --> nodeW ``` > **Reading the PSD plot:** Up to frequencies of $\sim k_B T / h$ (where $h$ is Planck's constant), the PSD is **flat and equal to $4 k_B T R$**, hence the label *white noise*. At higher frequencies, quantum mechanical effects cause the spectrum to fall off. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** **Define Johnson noise. State the Johnson–Nyquist formula for RMS noise voltage and explain the significance of each term.** **Model Answer:** Johnson noise (or thermal noise) is the random fluctuation in voltage observed across a resistor due to the thermal agitation of charge carriers, even in the absence of any applied voltage. The Johnson–Nyquist formula for RMS noise voltage is: $$V_n = \sqrt{4\,k_B T R B}$$ Significance of each term: - $k_B$ ($1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K): Boltzmann constant — links temperature to energy. - $T$ (K): Absolute temperature of the conductor. - $R$ ($\Omega$): Resistance of the source. - $B$ (Hz): Measurement bandwidth over which the noise is integrated. - The factor **4** (not 2) accounts for the two directions of wave propagation in Nyquist's transmission-line model. **[3 Marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for term explanation.]** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **Why is Johnson noise called "white noise"? Mention two ways to reduce it in a measurement system.** **Model Answer:** Johnson noise is called **white noise** because, like white light, its **Power Spectral Density (PSD) is uniform (flat)** across a very wide frequency range (up to $\sim 10^{12}$ Hz). This means equal noise power per unit bandwidth is present at all frequencies. Two ways to reduce it: 1. **Reduce the operating temperature $T$** of the resistor (e.g., cryogenic cooling of front-end amplifiers). 2. **Limit the measurement bandwidth $B$** using a band-pass filter or a narrower-band amplifier. **(Reducing $R$ is the third method, but the question asks for two.)** --- ### Part B — 14-Mark Module-Internal-Choice Question --- #### ✅ CHOICE — Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU ESE Typical Pattern]` **(a) Derive the Johnson–Nyquist expression for RMS noise voltage starting from Nyquist's transmission-line model. Clearly state the equipartition theorem's role in your derivation. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Set up the model:** Consider a lossless transmission line of length $L$ and characteristic impedance $Z_0 = R$, terminated at both ends by matched resistors $R$ in thermal equilibrium at temperature $T$. **Step 2 — Quantize the modes:** Standing-wave modes have frequencies $f_n = v n / (2L)$, where $n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$ **Step 3 — Count modes in $df$:** The number of modes in bandwidth $df$ is $N(f)\,df = (2L/v)\,df$, with the factor 2 accounting for forward and backward wave directions. **Step 4 — Apply equipartition:** Each mode carries average energy $k_B T$ (since it has both electric and magnetic quadratic degrees of freedom). **Step 5 — Compute energy in $df$:** $dE = k_B T \cdot (2L/v)\,df$ **Step 6 — Power to matched load:** $dP = dE / (L/v) = k_B T\,df$ **Step 7 — Convert to voltage:** Using matched-load power formula $P = V_n^2 / (4R)$: $$V_n^2 / (4R) = k_B T B \;\Rightarrow\; V_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R B}$$ **[Awarding marks: 1 for model setup, 1 for mode counting, 1 for equipartition statement, 1 for energy-power conversion, 2 for the final algebra, 1 for the boxed final answer.]** --- **(b) A $50\,\text{k}\Omega$ resistor is connected to a measurement amplifier of bandwidth $100\,\text{kHz}$ at a temperature of $27\,^{\circ}\text{C}$. Compute the (i) RMS noise voltage, (ii) RMS noise current, and (iii) available noise power. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** $R = 50 \times 10^3\,\Omega$; $B = 100 \times 10^3\,\text{Hz}$; $T = 27 + 273.15 = 300.15 \approx 300\,\text{K}$; $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\,\text{J/K}$. **(i) RMS Noise Voltage:** $$V_n = \sqrt{4 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300 \times 5 \times 10^{4} \times 10^{5}}$$ $$= \sqrt{8.28 \times 10^{-11}} \approx 9.1 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{V}$$ $$\boxed{V_n \approx 9.1\,\mu\text{V (RMS)}}$$ **(ii) RMS Noise Current:** $$I_n = V_n / R = 9.1 \times 10^{-6} / (5 \times 10^4)$$ $$\boxed{I_n \approx 1.82 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{A} = 0.182\,\text{nA (RMS)}}$$ **(iii) Available Noise Power:** $$P_n = k_B T B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300 \times 10^{5}$$ $$\boxed{P_n = 4.14 \times 10^{-16}\,\text{W} = 0.414\,\text{fW}}$$ --- #### ✅ CHOICE — Question B (14 Marks) `[Alternative Internal Choice]` **(a) Explain the physical origin of Johnson noise. Discuss the dependence of Johnson noise on temperature, resistance, and bandwidth, and justify why it is classified as a "white" Gaussian noise. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Physical origin:** Electrons in a conductor are never at rest — they possess thermal kinetic energy $\tfrac{1}{2} m v^2 \sim k_B T$. This produces random Brownian-like motion, summing to a fluctuating net current (or voltage across the terminals) with zero mean but non-zero mean square. **Dependence:** - **Temperature $T$:** $V_n \propto \sqrt{T}$. Halving temperature reduces $V_n$ by factor $\sqrt{2}$. - **Resistance $R$:** $V_n \propto \sqrt{R}$. A noisier source has higher resistance. - **Bandwidth $B$:** $V_n \propto \sqrt{B}$. Doubling the bandwidth adds $\sqrt{2}$ times more noise. **Why "White":** The Power Spectral Density $S_V = 4 k_B T R$ is independent of frequency in the classical regime, hence flat — analogous to white light having equal intensity at all visible frequencies. **Why "Gaussian":** The net voltage is the *sum* of an enormous number of independent microscopic electron-jitter contributions. By the Central Limit Theorem, the resulting amplitude distribution is Gaussian (Normal) with zero mean. --- **(b) Two resistors $R_1 = 20\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $R_2 = 30\,\text{k}\Omega$ are connected in series at $T = 290\,\text{K}$. The measurement bandwidth is $10\,\text{kHz}$. Find the total RMS noise voltage. Compare this with the case when they are connected in parallel. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Series Case:** Total resistance: $$R_{\text{eq}} = R_1 + R_2 = 50 \times 10^{3}\,\Omega$$ $$V_{n,\text{series}}^2 = 4 k_B T R_{\text{eq}} B = 4 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 290 \times 5 \times 10^{4} \times 10^{4}$$ $$= 8.004 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{V}^2$$ $$\boxed{V_{n,\text{series}} \approx 2.83\,\mu\text{V (RMS)}}$$ **Parallel Case:** $$R_{\text{eq}} = \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{20 \times 30}{50} \times 10^{3} = 12 \times 10^{3}\,\Omega$$ $$V_{n,\text{parallel}}^2 = 4 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 290 \times 1.2 \times 10^{4} \times 10^{4}$$ $$= 1.92 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{V}^2$$ $$\boxed{V_{n,\text{parallel}} \approx 1.386\,\mu\text{V (RMS)}}$$ **Comparison:** Series connection produces a *larger* noise voltage because $R_{\text{eq}}$ is higher ($\propto \sqrt{R}$). Paralleling resistors reduces Johnson noise — a useful trick when noise must be minimized without cooling. --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common student mistakes that cost marks:** > 1. **Writing the factor as "2" instead of "4"** in the formula $V_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R B}$. This is the **#1 most-deducted error** in KTU valuation. Memorize the factor of 4! > 2. **Forgetting to convert °C to Kelvin.** A temperature of "27 °C" in the formula gives a wrong answer if used directly. Always add 273.15. > 3. **Adding noise voltages linearly** instead of in quadrature. Multiple noise sources add as $\sqrt{V_1^2 + V_2^2 + \dots}$, **never** as $V_1 + V_2$. > 4. **Confusing PSD with RMS.** $S_V = 4 k_B T R$ has units of $\text{V}^2/\text{Hz}$ (a density). You must multiply by $B$ and take $\sqrt{\cdot}$ to get an RMS voltage. > 5. **Forgetting the unit of $B$.** Bandwidth in KTU problems is **almost always in Hz**, not kHz or MHz. Convert before substituting. > 6. **Omitting the matched-load condition** when using the available-power formula $P_n = k_B T B$ — this formula strictly holds *only* for $R_{\text{source}} = R_{\text{load}}$. --- ### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Johnson Noise = Thermal Noise = Johnson–Nyquist Noise** — caused by random thermal motion of electrons. - ✅ **Master Formula (RMSC voltage):** $V_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R B}$ — factor of **4**, not 2. - ✅ **Boltzmann constant** $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K — must be in J/K (use Kelvin, never °C). - ✅ **PSD is flat (white)** up to $\sim 10^{12}$ Hz: $S_V = 4 k_B T R$ in V²/Hz. - ✅ **Amplitude is Gaussian-distributed** (Central Limit Theorem) with **zero mean**. - ✅ **Three control knobs:** $T \downarrow$, $R \downarrow$, $B \downarrow$ all reduce noise. - ✅ **Available noise power:** $P_n = k_B T B$ (matched load condition). - ✅ **Noise combines in quadrature** — never linearly. - ✅ **Series** $R$ increases noise; **Parallel** $R$ decreases noise. - ✅ **Independent of resistor material** — depends only on $R$, $T$, and $B$. - ✅ **Fundamental physical limit** — cannot be eliminated, only minimized. - ✅ **Applications:** noise thermometry, TRNG, radio astronomy, sensor front-end design.

topics.title: Power Spectrum Density
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT205 - BASIC OF INSTRUMENTATION ENGINEERING (INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Power Spectrum Density

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** of a deterministic power signal $x(t)$ is formally defined as the **Fourier Transform of its autocorrelation function** $R_x(\tau)$, as stated by the **Wiener–Khinchin Theorem** (also called the Wiener–Khinchin–Einstein theorem).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Approved):**
> "The Power Spectral Density $S_x(f)$ of a signal $x(t)$ is the Fourier Transform of its time-averaged autocorrelation function $R_x(\tau)$. It quantifies how the *average power* of the signal is distributed across the frequency spectrum, expressed in units of **watts per Hertz (W/Hz)**."

Mathematically:

$$S_x(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_x(\tau)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} R_x(\tau)\, e^{-j2\pi f \tau}\, d\tau$$

And the inverse relation:

$$R_x(\tau) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{S_x(f)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} S_x(f)\, e^{+j2\pi f \tau}\, df$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why only for Power Signals?** PSD applies strictly to *power signals* (signals with **finite, non-zero average power** but **infinite energy**), such as periodic signals, stationary random processes, and persistent noise. For *energy signals* (finite energy, zero average power), the correct tool is the **Energy Spectral Density (ESD)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a **white LED light bulb** that is always on. If you pass this light through a **prism**, the light splits into a continuous band of seven colours (red → violet). The intensity of each colour is different — red may be strong, yellow moderate, violet weak.

Now map this to a signal:
- The **light bulb** → a time-varying electrical signal $x(t)$ that persists forever (a **power signal**).
- The **prism** → a **spectral analyser** (or mathematically, the Fourier Transform).
- The **colour intensities** → the **Power Spectral Density** $S_x(f)$ at each frequency $f$.

So the PSD is essentially a "**prism for signals**" — it tells you, "For every frequency $f$ present in the signal, how much *electrical power* is concentrated there?"

> [!TIP]
> **Plain English Summary for First-Time Learners:**
> PSD = "Where does the power of my signal *live* in the frequency domain?" If your signal is a pure 50 Hz sine wave from the mains, the PSD will show a tall spike at $f = 50$ Hz and almost nothing elsewhere.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Power Spectral Density | $S_x(f)$ | **W/Hz** or **V²/Hz** | Power per unit bandwidth |
| Frequency | $f$ | **Hz** | Cycles per second |
| Angular frequency | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | **rad/s** | Radian frequency |
| Autocorrelation | $R_x(\tau)$ | **W** or **V²** | Average power at time-lag $\tau$ |
| Bandwidth | $B$ | **Hz** | Range of significant frequency content |
| Total Average Power | $P_x$ | **W** | Area under the PSD curve |

> [!NOTE]
> The unit **W/Hz** is NOT power — it is power *density*. You must integrate it over a frequency band to obtain actual power.

---

## 1.4 Why PSD Matters in Instrumentation Engineering

In the **KTU PCICT205 syllabus** and real-world instrumentation:

- **Sensor Noise Analysis:** Every transducer (thermocouple, strain gauge, LVDT) has *intrinsic noise*. PSD tells you whether the noise is **white** (uniform across $f$) or **1/f (pink/flicker) noise** (dominant at low frequencies). This directly determines your **filter design**.
- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):** The SNR in a bandwidth $B$ is calculated as:
$$\text{SNR} = \frac{\int_{B} S_{\text{signal}}(f)\, df}{\int_{B} S_{\text{noise}}(f)\, df}$$
- **System Identification:** By injecting known noise and measuring output PSD, you can identify transfer functions of unknown systems.
- **Vibration & Acoustic Measurement:** Accelerometers and microphones produce PSD plots to characterise machinery health (bearing faults, resonances).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pre-Requisite: Average Power of a Signal

Before defining PSD, the **average power** of a signal $x(t)$ over the interval $[-T, +T]$ is:

$$P_x = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{+T} \vert x(t) \vert^2\, dt$$

For a signal to be a **power signal**, $P_x$ must be **finite and non-zero**, and the **total energy** $E = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2\, dt$ must be **infinite**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Parseval's Theorem (Power Form):** The total average power of a power signal can be computed by integrating its PSD over **all frequencies**:
$$P_x = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} S_x(f)\, df = R_x(0)$$
> This is one of the most commonly tested 3-mark questions in KTU exams.

---

## 2.2 Autocorrelation Function $R_x(\tau)$ — The Time-Domain Twin of PSD

The **time-averaged autocorrelation function** of a power signal $x(t)$ is:

$$R_x(\tau) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{+T} x(t)\, x(t+\tau)\, dt$$

**Step-by-step physical meaning:**

1. Take the signal $x(t)$ and a **time-shifted copy** $x(t+\tau)$.
2. Multiply them point-by-point — this measures how much they "resemble" each other at lag $\tau$.
3. Average over all time — gives a clean, statistical similarity measure.
4. If $x(t)$ and $x(t+\tau)$ look similar (e.g., periodic signal at exact period), the product is large and positive.
5. If they are uncorrelated (random noise), the product averages to zero.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three golden properties** (frequently asked in KTU):
> 1. $R_x(0) = P_x$ — the average power of the signal.
> 2. $R_x(\tau) = R_x(-\tau)$ — **even function** of $\tau$.
> 3. $\vert R_x(\tau) \vert \leq R_x(0)$ — the maximum occurs at zero lag.

---

## 2.3 The Wiener–Khinchin Theorem (Bridge Between Time and Frequency)

This theorem is the **theoretical cornerstone** of PSD and the **single most important concept** for KTU Module 4.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wiener–Khinchin Theorem Statement:**
> For a wide-sense stationary (WSS) random process or any power signal, the **Power Spectral Density** and the **Autocorrelation Function** form a **Fourier Transform pair**:
$$\boxed{S_x(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} R_x(\tau)\, e^{-j2\pi f \tau}\, d\tau \quad \longleftrightarrow \quad R_x(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} S_x(f)\, e^{+j2\pi f \tau}\, df}$$

**Why this theorem is revolutionary:**

- Direct Fourier transform of $x(t)$ may not exist (random noise is non-square-integrable).
- BUT the autocorrelation $R_x(\tau)$ is square-integrable for stationary processes, so its Fourier transform **always exists**.
- This makes PSD a **physically measurable and mathematically rigorous** quantity.

---

## 2.4 Cross-Spectral Density (Brief Overview)

For two jointly stationary signals $x(t)$ and $y(t)$, the **Cross-Spectral Density (CSD)** is:

$$S_{xy}(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_{xy}(\tau)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} R_{xy}(\tau)\, e^{-j2\pi f \tau}\, d\tau$$

where the cross-correlation is:

$$R_{xy}(\tau) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{+T} x(t)\, y(t+\tau)\, dt$$

> [!TIP]
> In KTU exams, if they ask about $S_{xy}(f)$, remember it is a **complex-valued** function, and $S_{xy}(f) = S_{yx}^{*}(f)$ (conjugate symmetry). It is the basis of **frequency-response measurement** using random noise excitation.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_x = \lim_{T\to\infty}\frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T}\vert x(t)\vert^2 dt$ | Average power | W |
| 2 | $R_x(\tau) = \lim_{T\to\infty}\frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T} x(t)\,x(t+\tau)\,dt$ | Autocorrelation | W or V² |
| 3 | $S_x(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} R_x(\tau)\, e^{-j2\pi f\tau}\,d\tau$ | PSD (Wiener–Khinchin) | W/Hz |
| 4 | $R_x(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} S_x(f)\, e^{+j2\pi f\tau}\,df$ | Inverse Wiener–Khinchin | W or V² |
| 5 | $P_x = R_x(0) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} S_x(f)\,df$ | Parseval's theorem | W |
| 6 | $S_{xy}(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_{xy}(\tau)\}$ | Cross-spectral density | W/Hz |
| 7 | $H(f) = \frac{S_{xy}(f)}{S_{xx}(f)}$ | Transfer function via CSD | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\gamma^2(f) = \frac{\vert S_{xy}(f)\vert^2}{S_{xx}(f)\,S_{yy}(f)}$ | Coherence function | 0 to 1 |
| 9 | $S_x(f) = \vert X(f)\vert^2 / T$ (for $x(t)$ truncated to $T$) | Periodogram estimate | W/Hz |
| 10 | $S_{\text{white}} = \frac{N_0}{2}$ (two-sided) | White noise PSD | W/Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite Trap:** The PSD is **always real, non-negative, and even**: $S_x(f) = S_x(-f) \geq 0$. The CSD, however, is generally **complex**. Mixing these two up costs marks.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of PSD |
|---|---|
| **Biomedical Instrumentation** | EEG/ECG frequency-band analysis (alpha, beta, delta, theta waves) |
| **Structural Health Monitoring** | Identifying natural frequencies and damping of bridges/buildings |
| **Audio Engineering** | Equaliser design, noise-cancelling headphones |
| **Communication Systems** | Channel noise characterisation, Shannon capacity |
| **Process Control** | Disturbance spectrum shaping in PID tuning |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Parseval's Theorem for Power Signals

**Starting point:** Average power definition

$$P_x = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{+T} \vert x(t) \vert^2\, dt$$

**Step 1:** Truncate the signal to a finite interval $[-T, +T]$ to make it an energy signal:

$$x_T(t) = \begin{cases} x(t) & \vert t \vert \leq T \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the standard Parseval's theorem (for energy signals):

$$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x_T(t) \vert^2\, dt = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert X_T(f) \vert^2\, df$$

**Step 3:** Divide both sides by $2T$ to obtain average power over the interval:

$$\frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{+T} \vert x(t) \vert^2\, dt = \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert X_T(f) \vert^2\, df$$

**Step 4:** Take the limit as $T \to \infty$:

$$P_x = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert X_T(f) \vert^2\, df$$

**Step 5:** Define the quantity inside the limit as the **Power Spectral Density**:

$$\boxed{S_x(f) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{\vert X_T(f) \vert^2}{2T}}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute back to get the master equation:

$$P_x = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} S_x(f)\, df$$

This completes the derivation of Parseval's theorem for power signals from first principles.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Wiener–Khinchin Theorem (PSD ↔ Autocorrelation)

**Starting point:** Definition of autocorrelation

$$R_x(\tau) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{+T} x(t)\, x(t+\tau)\, dt$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the inverse Fourier representation for $x(t+\tau)$:

$$x(t+\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} X(f)\, e^{j2\pi f (t+\tau)}\, df$$

**Step 2:** Plug into the autocorrelation integral:

$$R_x(\tau) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{+T} x(t) \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} X(f')\, e^{j2\pi f'(t+\tau)}\, df'\, dt$$

**Step 3:** Interchange the order of integration (Fubini's theorem, valid for well-behaved signals):

$$R_x(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} X(f') \left[ \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{+T} x(t)\, e^{j2\pi f' t}\, dt \right] e^{j2\pi f' \tau}\, df'$$

**Step 4:** Recognise the bracketed term as the **limiting spectral density**:

$$S_x(f') \approx \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{\vert X_T(f') \vert^2}{2T}$$

**Step 5:** For a stationary process, the bracketed term equals the spectral density, leading to:

$$R_x(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} S_x(f)\, e^{j2\pi f \tau}\, df$$

**Step 6:** Take the Fourier transform of both sides, using the duality property:

$$S_x(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} R_x(\tau)\, e^{-j2\pi f \tau}\, d\tau$$

**Conclusion:** The PSD and autocorrelation form a **Fourier Transform pair** — this is the **Wiener–Khinchin Theorem**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: PSD of a Pure Sinusoid $x(t) = A\cos(2\pi f_0 t)$

**Step 1:** Compute the autocorrelation of $x(t)$:

$$R_x(\tau) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{+T} A\cos(2\pi f_0 t) \cdot A\cos(2\pi f_0 (t+\tau))\, dt$$

**Step 2:** Apply the trigonometric identity $\cos\alpha \cos\beta = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(\alpha-\beta) + \cos(\alpha+\beta)]$:

$$R_x(\tau) = \frac{A^2}{2} \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{+T} \left[ \cos(2\pi f_0 \tau) + \cos(2\pi f_0(2t+\tau)) \right] dt$$

**Step 3:** The second term oscillates symmetrically and its integral is zero as $T \to \infty$:

$$R_x(\tau) = \frac{A^2}{2} \cos(2\pi f_0 \tau)$$

**Step 4:** Take the Fourier transform of $R_x(\tau)$ to get the PSD:

$$S_x(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{A^2}{2}\cos(2\pi f_0 \tau)\, e^{-j2\pi f \tau}\, d\tau$$

**Step 5:** Use the standard Fourier pair $\cos(2\pi f_0 \tau) \leftrightarrow \frac{1}{2}[\delta(f-f_0) + \delta(f+f_0)]$:

$$\boxed{S_x(f) = \frac{A^2}{4}\,\bigl[\delta(f-f_0) + \delta(f+f_0)\bigr]}$$

**Physical interpretation:** A pure sinusoid has its entire power concentrated at exactly two frequencies $\pm f_0$, each carrying a power of $A^2/4$. The PSD is a pair of **Dirac delta impulses** — a classic KTU exam result.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: PSD of White Noise

**Step 1:** White noise $n(t)$ is defined by the property that its samples at any two distinct times are **uncorrelated**:

$$R_n(\tau) = \frac{N_0}{2}\,\delta(\tau)$$

where $N_0/2$ is the constant PSD level (two-sided form).

**Step 2:** Take the Fourier transform of $R_n(\tau)$:

$$S_n(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{N_0}{2}\,\delta(\tau)\, e^{-j2\pi f \tau}\, d\tau$$

**Step 3:** Apply the sifting property of the Dirac delta $\int \delta(\tau) g(\tau) d\tau = g(0)$:

$$\boxed{S_n(f) = \frac{N_0}{2} \quad \text{for all } f}$$

**Conclusion:** White noise has a **flat (constant) PSD across all frequencies** — hence the name "white" (analogous to white light containing all colours equally).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Summary of Key Results

| Signal $x(t)$ | Autocorrelation $R_x(\tau)$ | PSD $S_x(f)$ | Shape |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A\cos(2\pi f_0 t)$ | $\frac{A^2}{2}\cos(2\pi f_0 \tau)$ | $\frac{A^2}{4}[\delta(f-f_0)+\delta(f+f_0)]$ | Two impulses |
| DC signal $A$ | $A^2$ | $A^2 \delta(f)$ | Single impulse at $f=0$ |
| White noise | $\frac{N_0}{2}\delta(\tau)$ | $\frac{N_0}{2}$ (constant) | Flat line |
| Band-limited white ($B$ Hz) | $N_0 B \cdot \text{sinc}(2B\tau)$ | $\frac{N_0}{2}$ for $\vert f\vert < B$, 0 otherwise | Rectangular |
| Square wave (period $T_0$, amplitude $A$) | Triangle wave of period $T_0$ | Discrete line spectrum at $f = n/T_0$ | Line spectrum |
| 1/f (pink) noise | — | $\frac{K}{\vert f\vert^{\alpha}}$ | Diverges at $f=0$ |
