# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define Power Spectral Density (PSD) of a power signal. Mention its unit. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The Power Spectral Density (PSD) of a power signal $x(t)$ is defined as the **Fourier Transform of its time-averaged autocorrelation function** $R_x(\tau)$, as per the **Wiener–Khinchin theorem**:

$$S_x(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} R_x(\tau)\, e^{-j2\pi f \tau}\, d\tau$$

It describes the distribution of the **average power** of the signal as a function of frequency. The SI unit of PSD is **watts per Hertz (W/Hz)**. For a real signal, $S_x(f)$ is a **real, non-negative, and even** function of frequency.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** State and explain the Wiener–Khinchin theorem. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The **Wiener–Khinchin theorem** states that for a wide-sense stationary (WSS) random process or any deterministic power signal, the **Power Spectral Density $S_x(f)$** and the **Autocorrelation Function $R_x(\tau)$** form a **Fourier Transform pair**:

$$S_x(f) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} R_x(\tau)$$

That is,

$$S_x(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_x(\tau)\} \quad \text{and} \quad R_x(\tau) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{S_x(f)\}$$

**Significance:** Direct Fourier transform of a random/power signal may not exist (it is generally not square-integrable). However, the autocorrelation function is always well-defined and square-integrable for stationary processes. Hence, the theorem provides a **mathematically rigorous and physically meaningful** way to characterise the frequency content of such signals, and is the **foundation of all spectral analysis** in instrumentation and communication systems.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 4, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

> **Q3(a)** Derive the relationship between Power Spectral Density and the Autocorrelation function of a power signal. State the properties of the PSD. *(7 marks)*

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Start with the definition of the **time-averaged autocorrelation function** for a power signal $x(t)$:

$$R_x(\tau) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{+T} x(t)\, x(t+\tau)\, dt$$

**Step 2:** Express $x(t+\tau)$ using the **inverse Fourier Transform**:

$$x(t+\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} X(f)\, e^{j2\pi f(t+\tau)}\, df$$

**Step 3:** Substitute this into the autocorrelation integral:

$$R_x(\tau) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{+T} x(t) \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} X(f')\, e^{j2\pi f'(t+\tau)}\, df' \right] dt$$

**Step 4:** Interchange the order of integration (justified by Fubini's theorem):

$$R_x(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} X(f') \left[ \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{+T} x(t)\, e^{j2\pi f' t}\, dt \right] e^{j2\pi f' \tau}\, df'$$

**Step 5:** Define the bracketed limit as the **Power Spectral Density**:

$$S_x(f') = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{\vert X_T(f') \vert^2}{2T}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute to obtain the **inverse relation**:

$$R_x(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} S_x(f)\, e^{j2\pi f \tau}\, df$$

**Step 7:** Take the Fourier Transform of both sides to get the **forward relation**:

$$\boxed{S_x(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} R_x(\tau)\, e^{-j2\pi f \tau}\, d\tau}$$

This is the **Wiener–Khinchin Theorem**.

**Properties of PSD:**

1. $S_x(f)$ is a **real-valued** function of frequency.
2. $S_x(f) \geq 0$ for all $f$ (non-negative).
3. $S_x(f) = S_x(-f)$ — **even function** of frequency.
4. The **total average power** of the signal equals the area under the PSD curve (Parseval's theorem):
$$P_x = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} S_x(f)\, df = R_x(0)$$

---

> **Q3(b)** A signal $x(t) = 5\cos(100\pi t) + 3\cos(200\pi t)$ is applied to a system. Find the autocorrelation function and the PSD of the signal. *(7 marks)*

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Given:** $x(t) = 5\cos(100\pi t) + 3\cos(200\pi t)$

**Step 1:** Identify the frequency components:
- Component 1: amplitude $A_1 = 5$, frequency $f_1 = 50$ Hz (since $2\pi f_1 = 100\pi \Rightarrow f_1 = 50$ Hz)
- Component 2: amplitude $A_2 = 3$, frequency $f_2 = 100$ Hz (since $2\pi f_2 = 200\pi \Rightarrow f_2 = 100$ Hz)

**Step 2:** Use the general result for a multi-tone cosine signal. For $x(t) = \sum_i A_i \cos(2\pi f_i t)$:

$$R_x(\tau) = \sum_{i} \frac{A_i^2}{2}\cos(2\pi f_i \tau)$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the values:

$$R_x(\tau) = \frac{5^2}{2}\cos(2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot \tau) + \frac{3^2}{2}\cos(2\pi \cdot 100 \cdot \tau)$$

$$R_x(\tau) = 12.5\cos(100\pi \tau) + 4.5\cos(200\pi \tau)$$

**Step 4:** Compute the total average power at $\tau = 0$:

$$P_x = R_x(0) = 12.5 + 4.5 = 17 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5:** Take the Fourier Transform of $R_x(\tau)$ to get the PSD. Using the pair $\cos(2\pi f_0 \tau) \leftrightarrow \frac{1}{2}[\delta(f-f_0) + \delta(f+f_0)]$:

$$S_x(f) = \frac{12.5}{2}[\delta(f-50) + \delta(f+50)] + \frac{4.5}{2}[\delta(f-100) + \delta(f+100)]$$

**Step 6:** Simplify:

$$\boxed{S_x(f) = 6.25\,[\delta(f-50) + \delta(f+50)] + 2.25\,[\delta(f-100) + \delta(f+100)] \quad \text{W/Hz}}$$

**Step 7:** Verify using Parseval's theorem:

$$P_x = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} S_x(f)\, df = 6.25 + 6.25 + 2.25 + 2.25 = 17 \text{ W} \quad \checkmark$$

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

> **Q4(a)** Explain the difference between Energy Spectral Density (ESD) and Power Spectral Density (PSD). For what type of signals is each defined? *(7 marks)*

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Energy Spectral Density (ESD) | Power Spectral Density (PSD) |
|---|---|---|
| **Applicable to** | Energy signals | Power signals |
| **Definition** | $\psi_x(f) = \vert X(f) \vert^2$ | $S_x(f) = \lim_{T\to\infty} \frac{\vert X_T(f) \vert^2}{2T}$ |
| **Mathematical origin** | Direct FT of signal | FT of autocorrelation |
| **Total quantity** | $E_x = \int \psi_x(f)\, df$ | $P_x = \int S_x(f)\, df$ |
| **Unit** | Joules per Hz (J/Hz) | Watts per Hz (W/Hz) |
| **Examples** | Rectangular pulse, decaying exponential, finite-duration sinusoid | Periodic signals, random noise, persistent oscillations |

**Key Differences:**

1. **Domain of Applicability:**
   - ESD applies to **energy signals** — signals with finite total energy ($0 < E_x < \infty$) and zero average power. Example: $x(t) = e^{-at}u(t)$.
   - PSD applies to **power signals** — signals with finite non-zero average power ($0 < P_x < \infty$) and infinite total energy. Example: $x(t) = A\cos(\omega_0 t)$.

2. **Mathematical Formulation:**
   - ESD is obtained by squaring the **magnitude of the Fourier Transform** of the signal: $\psi_x(f) = \vert X(f) \vert^2 = X(f) \cdot X^*(f)$.
   - PSD is the **Fourier Transform of the autocorrelation function**: $S_x(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_x(\tau)\}$ (Wiener–Khinchin theorem).

3. **Physical Meaning:**
   - ESD represents **energy per unit bandwidth** — useful for transient signals.
   - PSD represents **power per unit bandwidth** — useful for continuous, persistent signals.

4. **Uniqueness:** Some signals are **neither** (e.g., $x(t) = e^{-at}$ for $a \leq 0$). For these, neither ESD nor PSD is defined.

---

> **Q4(b)** For a wide-sense stationary random process $X(t)$ with autocorrelation $R_x(\tau) = e^{-2\vert\tau|}$, find the Power Spectral Density. Hence compute the total average power. *(7 marks)*

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Given:** $R_x(\tau) = e^{-2\vert\tau|}$ (a two-sided decaying exponential)

**Step 1:** Apply the Wiener–Khinchin theorem:

$$S_x(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} R_x(\tau)\, e^{-j2\pi f \tau}\, d\tau$$

**Step 2:** Split the integral at $\tau = 0$ to handle the absolute value:

$$S_x(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{0} e^{2\tau}\, e^{-j2\pi f \tau}\, d\tau + \int_{0}^{+\infty} e^{-2\tau}\, e^{-j2\pi f \tau}\, d\tau$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the first integral ($I_1$):

$$I_1 = \int_{-\infty}^{0} e^{(2 - j2\pi f)\tau}\, d\tau = \left[ \frac{e^{(2-j2\pi f)\tau}}{2 - j2\pi f} \right]_{-\infty}^{0} = \frac{1}{2 - j2\pi f} - 0 = \frac{1}{2 - j2\pi f}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the second integral ($I_2$):

$$I_2 = \int_{0}^{+\infty} e^{-(2 + j2\pi f)\tau}\, d\tau = \left[ \frac{-e^{-(2+j2\pi f)\tau}}{2 + j2\pi f} \right]_{0}^{+\infty} = 0 - \frac{-1}{2 + j2\pi f} = \frac{1}{2 + j2\pi f}$$

**Step 5:** Add $I_1$ and $I_2$:

$$S_x(f) = \frac{1}{2 - j2\pi f} + \frac{1}{2 + j2\pi f}$$

**Step 6:** Combine over a common denominator:

$$S_x(f) = \frac{(2 + j2\pi f) + (2 - j2\pi f)}{(2)^2 + (2\pi f)^2} = \frac{4}{4 + 4\pi^2 f^2}$$

**Step 7:** Simplify:

$$\boxed{S_x(f) = \frac{1}{1 + \pi^2 f^2} \quad \text{W/Hz}}$$

**Step 8:** Compute the total average power using Parseval's theorem:

$$P_x = R_x(0) = e^{0} = 1 \text{ W}$$

**Verification by integration:**

$$P_x = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{1}{1 + \pi^2 f^2}\, df = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{du}{1 + u^2} \quad (\text{let } u = \pi f) = \frac{1}{\pi} \cdot \pi = 1 \text{ W} \quad \checkmark$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing ESD with PSD:** Writing $\psi_x(f) = \vert X(f) \vert^2$ when the question asks for PSD of a *power signal* (e.g., sine wave) — this gives an undefined result for non-energy signals. **Always use $S_x(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_x(\tau)\}$ for power signals.**
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the $1/2$ factor in two-sided vs one-sided PSD:** A sinusoid $A\cos(2\pi f_0 t)$ has **two-sided** PSD $\frac{A^2}{4}[\delta(f-f_0) + \delta(f+f_0)]$, but **one-sided** PSD $\frac{A^2}{2}\delta(f-f_0)$ for $f \geq 0$. Mixing these up loses 1–2 marks.
> 
> 3. **Writing PSD as complex:** PSD is **always real and non-negative**. The Cross-Spectral Density is complex — they are NOT the same thing.
> 
> 4. **Missing the limit in the definition:** The PSD involves $\lim_{T\to\infty}\frac{\vert X_T(f) \vert^2}{2T}$. Omitting the limit statement makes the definition incomplete — examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 
> 5. **Not stating units:** Always write **W/Hz** (or V²/Hz for voltage signals). Forgetting units costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 
> 6. **Wrong Fourier Transform sign convention:** Use $e^{-j2\pi f \tau}$ in the forward transform (KTU standard). Some texts use $e^{-j\omega\tau}$ with $\omega$ — be consistent.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Power Spectral Density:**

### 📌 Core Definitions
- ☐ PSD = Fourier Transform of autocorrelation function (Wiener–Khinchin theorem)
- ☐ Unit of PSD = **W/Hz** (watts per Hertz)
- ☐ PSD is **real, non-negative, and even**: $S_x(f) = S_x(-f) \geq 0$
- ☐ PSD applies only to **power signals** (finite power, infinite energy)

### 📌 Master Formulas (Memorise These)
- ☐ $R_x(\tau) = \lim_{T\to\infty}\frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T} x(t)\,x(t+\tau)\,dt$
- ☐ $S_x(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} R_x(\tau)\,e^{-j2\pi f\tau}\,d\tau$
- ☐ $P_x = R_x(0) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} S_x(f)\,df$ (Parseval's theorem)
- ☐ $S_x(f) = \lim_{T\to\infty}\frac{\vert X_T(f)\vert^2}{2T}$ (periodogram definition)

### 📌 Key Properties of Autocorrelation $R_x(\tau)$
- ☐ $R_x(0) = P_x$ (maximum value = average power)
- ☐ $R_x(\tau) = R_x(-\tau)$ (even function)
- ☐ $\vert R_x(\tau) \vert \leq R_x(0)$ (bounded by power)
- ☐ $R_x(0) \geq \vert R_x(\tau) \vert$ (peak at origin)

### 📌 Key Properties of PSD $S_x(f)$
- ☐ $S_x(f) \geq 0$ (non-negative)
- ☐ $S_x(f) = S_x(-f)$ (real and even)
- ☐ $S_x(f) \in \mathbb{R}$ (always real)
- ☐ $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} S_x(f)\,df = P_x$ (total power)

### 📌 Standard Results (Memorise for 14-mark questions)
- ☐ Sinusoid: $A\cos(2\pi f_0 t) \rightarrow S_x(f) = \frac{A^2}{4}[\delta(f-f_0) + \delta(f+f_0)]$
- ☐ White noise: $R_n(\tau) = \frac{N_0}{2}\delta(\tau) \rightarrow S_n(f) = \frac{N_0}{2}$ (flat)
- ☐ $R_x(\tau) = e^{-a\vert\tau\vert} \rightarrow S_x(f) = \frac{2a}{a^2 + (2\pi f)^2}$ (Lorentzian)

### 📌 Quick Decision Guide
| Signal Type | Use |
|---|---|
| Periodic, infinite duration | **PSD** |
| Transient, finite duration | **ESD** |
| Random, stationary | **PSD** |
| Single pulse | **ESD** |

### 📌 Common Exam Keywords & Their Triggers
- "Average power" → integrate PSD over $f$
- "Energy distribution" → use ESD
- "Power distribution" → use PSD
- "Fourier transform of autocorrelation" → Wiener–Khinchin
- "Even and non-negative function of $f$" → describe PSD

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sources of Measurement Noise
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT205 - BASIC OF INSTRUMENTATION ENGINEERING (INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sources of Measurement Noise

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In measurement and instrumentation engineering, **Measurement Noise** is defined as any unwanted, random, or deterministic disturbance that corrupts, contaminates, or obscures the true value of a physical quantity (the measurand) being transduced by a sensor or measurement system. Mathematically, the observed signal $S_{obs}(t)$ is modeled as:

$$S_{obs}(t) = S_{true}(t) + N(t)$$

where $S_{true}(t)$ is the genuine physical signal of interest, and $N(t)$ is the superimposed noise component. The discipline of identifying and characterizing $N(t)$ is foundational to **Signal Conditioning**, **Sensor Design**, and **Reliable Data Acquisition**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Aligned):**
> Measurement noise is any deviation between the actual (true) value of the measurand and the value indicated by the measurement system, excluding deterministic systematic errors such as offset, linearity, or hysteresis.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Dirty Window" of a Camera

Imagine you are trying to photograph a beautiful landscape through a slightly dirty window. The landscape is your **true signal** (the actual measurement), and the dust particles, water spots, and scratches on the glass are the **noise**. 

- If the window is *very dirty* (high noise), the photograph is fuzzy and unreliable.
- If the window is *clean* but the camera sensor itself has flaws (internal noise), the picture is still degraded.
- Sometimes, outside light reflects off the glass (environmental noise) creating glare.
- A skilled photographer (instrumentation engineer) understands *where* the dirt comes from, *how much* is acceptable, and *how to clean the glass* (filtering).

This is exactly the role of a measurement engineer — to **identify, quantify, and minimize the sources of noise** so that the "photograph" (measurement) is faithful to the "landscape" (real physical process).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Symbol | Quantity | Value (SI Units) |
|---|---|---|
| $k$ | Boltzmann's constant | **$1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K** |
| $q$ | Elementary charge | **$1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C** |
| $T_0$ | Standard reference temperature | **$290$ K** (≈ 17 °C) |
| $4kT$ | Thermal noise constant at $T_0$ | **$1.62 \times 10^{-20}$ W/Hz** |
| $N_A$ | Avogadro's number | **$6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Indicator (KPI):** The quality of any measurement system is judged by its **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**:
> $$\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right) \text{ dB}$$
> A higher SNR (typically $> 40$ dB for industrial sensors, $> 60$ dB for laboratory instruments) indicates superior measurement fidelity.

## 1.4 The Three-Tier Classification of Noise Sources

For KTU 2024 assessment purposes, measurement noise is canonically classified into **three fundamental tiers**:

1. **Inherent / Intrinsic Noise** — Generated *inside* the sensor/transducer itself due to the physics of the device (thermal agitation, quantum effects, material imperfections).
2. **Environmental / Extrinsic Noise** — Originating *outside* the measurement system but coupled into the signal path (electromagnetic interference, ground loops, mechanical vibration, thermal gradients).
3. **Quantization / Processing Noise** — Introduced *after* transduction, during signal conditioning, amplification, A/D conversion, or transmission.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Tier 1 — Inherent (Intrinsic) Noise Sources

### 2.1.1 Thermal Noise (Johnson-Nyquist Noise)

This is the **most fundamental and unavoidable** noise in any resistor or resistive element above absolute zero. It arises from the random thermal agitation of charge carriers (electrons) within conductors.

**Physical Origin:** Electrons in a conductor at temperature $T > 0$ K possess thermal kinetic energy and move in a random Brownian manner. This random motion produces a fluctuating voltage across the terminals, even with no applied current.

**Key Behavioral Characteristics:**
- It is a **white noise** (frequency-independent power spectral density) up to ~$10^{12}$ Hz.
- It is **Gaussian-distributed** in amplitude (Central Limit Theorem application).
- It is **unavoidable** as long as $T > 0$ K and $R > 0$.
- It is **bandwidth-dependent** — more bandwidth collects more noise power.

**The Johnson-Nyquist Equation:**

$$V_n = \sqrt{4 \, k \, T \, R \, B}$$

where:
- $V_n$ = RMS noise voltage in **volts**
- $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
- $T$ = Absolute temperature in **Kelvin**
- $R$ = Resistance in **ohms**
- $B$ = Measurement bandwidth in **Hz**

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A common engineering trick is **cryogenic cooling** of precision sensors (e.g., in astronomical infrared detectors, SQUIDs) to suppress thermal noise. Halving absolute temperature quarters the noise power.

### 2.1.2 Shot Noise

Shot noise arises in devices where charge carriers cross a **potential barrier** discretely (PN junctions, vacuum tubes, photodiodes, transistors). The randomness of individual carrier emission/collection events produces a fluctuating current.

**The Schottky Shot Noise Equation:**

$$I_n = \sqrt{2 \, q \, I_{DC} \, B}$$

where:
- $I_n$ = RMS noise current in **amperes**
- $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C
- $I_{DC}$ = Average DC bias current in **amperes**
- $B$ = Bandwidth in **Hz**

**Behavioral Characteristics:**
- White, Gaussian noise.
- Dominant in **low-current, high-impedance** semiconductor devices.
- Can be reduced by reducing the DC bias current, but at the cost of sensor sensitivity.

### 2.1.3 Flicker Noise (1/f Noise or Pink Noise)

Flicker noise has a power spectral density inversely proportional to frequency:

$$S_{1/f}(f) = \frac{K}{f^{\alpha}}$$

where $\alpha \approx 1$ (hence "1/f noise"), and $K$ is a device-dependent constant.

**Key Properties:**
- Dominant at **low frequencies** ($< 1$ kHz typically).
- Originates from **trapping/detrapping of charge carriers** at material defects, impurities, and surface states.
- Also arises in **electrolytic solutions, carbon resistors, and semiconductor surfaces**.
- **Chopper stabilization** and **modulation techniques** are used to shift the signal to a higher frequency, where 1/f noise is negligible — this is a favorite KTU short-answer topic.

### 2.1.4 Generation-Recombination Noise

In semiconductor devices, random fluctuations in the number of free charge carriers due to thermal generation and recombination events produce noise:

$$i_{g-r}^2 = \overline{\Delta N^2} \cdot \frac{4 q^2 \tau}{1 + (2\pi f \tau)^2} \cdot B$$

where $\tau$ is the carrier lifetime. It is dominant in **photoconductors** and **intrinsic semiconductor sensors**.

## 2.2 Tier 2 — Environmental (Extrinsic) Noise Sources

### 2.2.1 Electromagnetic Interference (EMI)

Caused by coupling of external time-varying electromagnetic fields (from power lines, RF transmitters, motors, switching circuits) into the measurement signal path. Two coupling modes:
- **Conducted Coupling:** Noise enters via shared power lines or ground.
- **Radiated Coupling:** Noise enters via capacitive/inductive antenna-like pickup.

**Mitigation:** Shielding, twisted-pair cables, proper grounding (star-ground topology), filtering.

### 2.2.2 Ground Loops

When two or more pieces of equipment share *different* ground potentials, a spurious current flows through the signal cable shield, injecting noise voltage. Eliminated by **single-point grounding** or **isolation amplifiers**.

### 2.2.3 Mechanical Vibration and Acoustic Noise

In vibration-sensitive sensors (e.g., piezoelectric accelerometers, strain gauges), mechanical shocks and acoustic pressure waves modulate the signal. Mitigated via **mechanical dampers, isolation mounts, and rigid mounting**.

### 2.2.4 Thermoelectric (Seebeck) Noise

Junctions between dissimilar metals generate tiny EMFs proportional to the temperature gradient. Critical in **low-voltage measurements** (e.g., thermocouple reference junctions).

### 2.2.5 Chemical and Electrochemical Noise

In pH electrodes, ion-selective electrodes, and electrochemical sensors, random fluctuations in ion concentration at the membrane-solution interface produce noise. Particularly relevant in **biomedical and environmental sensors**.

## 2.3 Tier 3 — Quantization and Processing Noise

### 2.3.1 Quantization Noise

Introduced by **Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADCs)** due to the finite resolution (number of bits) of the converter. The RMS quantization noise voltage is:

$$V_{q,rms} = \frac{V_{LSB}}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{N} \cdot \sqrt{12}}$$

where $V_{LSB}$ is the least significant bit voltage, $V_{FS}$ is full-scale voltage, and $N$ is the number of bits.

### 2.3.2 Amplifier Noise

Operational amplifiers contribute **Voltage Noise** ($e_n$) and **Current Noise** ($i_n$) at their inputs, characterized in datasheets as $e_n$ (nV/√Hz) and $i_n$ (pA/√Hz). Total referred-to-input (RTI) noise:

$$V_{n,total} = \sqrt{e_n^2 \cdot B + (i_n \cdot R_s)^2 \cdot B + V_{thermal}^2}$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Noise Type | Formula | Key Variable Meaning | Engineering Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Thermal (Johnson) | $V_n = \sqrt{4 k T R B}$ | $R$ = resistance, $B$ = bandwidth | Lower $R$, narrow $B$, cryogenic $T$ |
| 2 | Shot Noise | $I_n = \sqrt{2 q I_{DC} B}$ | $I_{DC}$ = bias current | Lower bias current |
| 3 | Flicker (1/f) | $S(f) = K / f$ | $K$ = device constant | Chopper stabilization |
| 4 | Quantization | $V_q = V_{FS} / (2^N \sqrt{12})$ | $N$ = ADC bits | Increase $N$ (more bits) |
| 5 | SNR | $\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}(P_s/P_n)$ dB | Power ratio | Higher signal, lower noise |
| 6 | Noise Figure | $NF = 10 \log_{10}(F)$ dB | $F = SNR_{in}/SNR_{out}$ | Low-noise preamplifiers |
| 7 | Total (uncorrelated) | $V_{tot} = \sqrt{\sum V_i^2}$ | Independent sources | Minimize each term |
| 8 | Photon (Poisson) | $N_{ph} = \sqrt{N_{photon}}$ | Photon count variance | Longer integration time |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule for KTU Board Exams:**
> Uncorrelated (independent) noise sources add in **quadrature (power summation)**, NOT linearly:
> $$V_{total,rms} = \sqrt{V_1^2 + V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \dots}$$
> Correlated noise sources, however, add linearly. Mis-applying this rule is a **frequently marked-down** error in KTU valuation.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Critical Noise Source | Impact |
|---|---|---|
| Medical ECG/EEG | Mains interference (50/60 Hz), electrode contact noise | Diagnostic accuracy |
| Aerospace (IMU) | Flicker noise, vibration | Navigation precision |
| Radio Astronomy | Thermal, shot, cosmic background | Telescope sensitivity |
| Industrial Process Control | EMI from VFDs, ground loops | Loop stability |
| Quantum Computing | 1/f charge noise, thermal | Qubit decoherence |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Johnson-Nyquist Thermal Noise Equation (Equipartition Method)

**Starting Premise:** A resistor $R$ at temperature $T$ can be modeled as a series combination of an *ideal* (noiseless) resistor and a *noise voltage source* $V_n$ driving into a matched load. By the **Equipartition Theorem** of statistical mechanics, each degree of freedom of a system in thermal equilibrium has an average energy of $\frac{1}{2} k T$.

**Step 1 — Thevenin Equivalent Model:**
Model the noisy resistor as an ideal source $V_n$ in series with a noiseless resistor $R$, driving a matched load $R_L = R$. The maximum power transfer to the load is:

$$P_{load} = \frac{V_n^2}{4R}$$

**Step 2 — Equipartition Application:**
The thermal energy stored in the equivalent RC circuit (resistor + parasitic capacitance $C$) over a small frequency band $df$ is, by equipartition:

$$\frac{1}{2} C \cdot V_{rms}^2(df) = \frac{1}{2} k T$$

Wait — more rigorously, consider the noise spectral density $S_V(f)$ (V²/Hz). The mean-square noise voltage in a band $df$ is $S_V(f) \, df$. Integrating the available power over all frequencies:

$$\int_0^{\infty} \frac{S_V(f)}{4R} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + (2\pi f R C)^2} \, df = \frac{1}{2} k T$$

The factor $\frac{1}{1 + (2\pi f R C)^2}$ accounts for the low-pass filtering effect of $R$ and $C$ (since at very high frequencies, the capacitor shorts out the noise).

**Step 3 — Evaluate the Integral:**
Recognizing that $\int_0^{\infty} \frac{df}{1 + (2\pi f R C)^2} = \frac{1}{4RC}$, we get:

$$\frac{S_V}{4R} \cdot \frac{1}{4RC} = \frac{1}{2} k T$$

Wait — this requires that $S_V$ is *constant* (i.e., white noise). Resolving:

$$S_V(f) = 4 k T R \quad \text{(V}^2/\text{Hz)}$$

**Step 4 — Integrate Over Bandwidth $B$:**
The total mean-square noise voltage is:

$$\overline{V_n^2} = \int_0^{B} S_V(f) \, df = 4 k T R \cdot B$$

**Step 5 — Take the Square Root (RMS):**

$$\boxed{V_{n,rms} = \sqrt{4 k T R B}}$$

This is the celebrated **Johnson-Nyquist Equation**, derived via statistical mechanics. ∎

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Shot Noise from Poisson Statistics

**Starting Premise:** In a device with a DC current $I_{DC}$ flowing across a potential barrier, charge carriers (electrons) arrive *independently* and *randomly*. The number of carriers arriving in a fixed time interval follows a **Poisson distribution**.

**Step 1 — Define Carrier Arrival Rate:**
The average number of electrons per second is:

$$\bar{n} = \frac{I_{DC}}{q}$$

**Step 2 — Poisson Variance:**
For a Poisson process, the variance of the count over a time interval $\tau$ equals the mean:

$$\overline{\Delta n^2} = \bar{n} \tau = \frac{I_{DC} \tau}{q}$$

**Step 3 — Mean-Square Current Fluctuation:**
The instantaneous current is $i(t) = q \cdot (\text{rate})$. Over a short averaging window, the current fluctuation is:

$$\overline{(\Delta i)^2} = \frac{q^2 \, \overline{\Delta n^2}}{\tau^2} \cdot \tau = \frac{q^2 \cdot \bar{n}}{\tau} = \frac{q \, I_{DC}}{\tau}$$

This expresses noise power per unit bandwidth, since $\tau$ represents the inverse of effective bandwidth $B \approx 1/(2\tau)$:

$$\overline{(\Delta i)^2} \cdot \tau = q \, I_{DC} = 2 \cdot \frac{q I_{DC}}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Single-Sided Spectral Density:**
The two-sided spectral density is $S_i(f) = q I_{DC}$. The single-sided (positive frequency only) is twice that:

$$S_i(f) = 2 q I_{DC} \quad \text{(A}^2/\text{Hz)}$$

**Step 5 — RMS Current Over Bandwidth $B$:**

$$\overline{I_n^2} = \int_0^{B} S_i(f) \, df = 2 q I_{DC} B$$

**Step 6 — Take the Square Root:**

$$\boxed{I_{n,rms} = \sqrt{2 q I_{DC} B}}$$

This is the **Schottky Shot Noise Formula**. ∎

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Quantization Noise of an Ideal N-bit ADC

**Starting Premise:** An ideal N-bit ADC uniformly quantizes an analog input range $[0, V_{FS}]$ into $2^N$ discrete levels. The quantization error $e_q$ is the difference between the analog value and the nearest quantization level.

**Step 1 — Quantization Step (LSB) Size:**
$$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N}$$

**Step 2 — Error Distribution Model:**
Assuming the input signal is *random* and the quantizer is *ideal*, the error $e_q$ is uniformly distributed over the interval $\left[-\frac{V_{LSB}}{2}, +\frac{V_{LSB}}{2}\right]$.

**Step 3 — Mean-Square Error (Variance of Uniform Distribution):**
For a uniform distribution on $[-a, +a]$, the variance is $\frac{a^2}{3}$. With $a = V_{LSB}/2$:

$$\overline{e_q^2} = \frac{1}{V_{LSB}} \int_{-V_{LSB}/2}^{+V_{LSB}/2} e_q^2 \, de_q = \frac{V_{LSB}^2}{12}$$

**Step 4 — Express in Terms of $V_{FS}$ and $N$:**

$$\overline{e_q^2} = \frac{1}{12} \left(\frac{V_{FS}}{2^N}\right)^2 = \frac{V_{FS}^2}{12 \cdot 4^N}$$

**Step 5 — RMS Quantization Noise:**

$$\boxed{V_{q,rms} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N \sqrt{12}}}$$

For a full-scale sinusoid, the signal RMS is $V_{FS}/(2\sqrt{2})$, giving the theoretical **SQNR (Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio):**

$$\text{SQNR} = 6.02 \, N + 1.76 \quad \text{dB}$$

This is a frequently-tested KTU formula. ∎

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Combining Multiple Independent Noise Sources

**Starting Premise:** The output of a measurement system contains $N$ independent (uncorrelated) noise sources with mean-square voltages $\overline{V_1^2}, \overline{V_2^2}, \ldots, \overline{V_N^2}$. Find the total RMS noise.

**Step 1 — Express Each Source in Time Domain:**
The total instantaneous noise is the sum:
$$n_{tot}(t) = n_1(t) + n_2(t) + \cdots + n_N(t)$$

**Step 2 — Mean-Square Value of the Sum:**

$$\overline{n_{tot}^2} = \overline{(n_1 + n_2 + \cdots + n_N)^2} = \overline{\sum_i n_i^2} + 2 \sum_{i<j} \overline{n_i n_j}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-Terms Vanish (Uncorrelated Assumption):**
For independent sources, $\overline{n_i n_j} = \overline{n_i} \cdot \overline{n_j} = 0 \cdot 0 = 0$ (zero-mean assumption).

**Step 4 — Sum of Variances:**

$$\overline{n_{tot}^2} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \overline{n_i^2}$$

**Step 5 — Total RMS:**

$$\boxed{V_{tot,rms} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \overline{V_i^2}}}$$

This is the **Quadrature Summation Rule** (sometimes called the "root-sum-square" rule). ∎

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU-Style Problem)

**Problem:** A measurement amplifier has a source resistance of $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, operates at $T = 300$ K, and uses a bandwidth of $B = 100$ kHz. Calculate: (a) the thermal noise voltage, (b) the noise voltage per root-Hz, and (c) the SNR if the input signal amplitude is $V_s = 10$ mV.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Thermal Noise Voltage:**

$$V_n = \sqrt{4 \cdot (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \cdot 300 \cdot (10 \times 10^3) \cdot (100 \times 10^3)}$$

Step-by-step arithmetic:
- $4 \cdot 1.38 \times 10^{-23} = 5.52 \times 10^{-23}$
- $5.52 \times 10^{-23} \cdot 300 = 1.656 \times 10^{-20}$
- $1.656 \times 10^{-20} \cdot 10^4 = 1.656 \times 10^{-16}$
- $1.656 \times 10^{-16} \cdot 10^5 = 1.656 \times 10^{-11}$
- $\sqrt{1.656 \times 10^{-11}} = 1.287 \times 10^{-5.5} = \sqrt{1.656} \times 10^{-5.5}$

$\sqrt{1.656} \approx 1.287$ and $10^{-5.5} = 3.162 \times 10^{-6}$:

$$V_n \approx 1.287 \times 3.162 \times 10^{-6} = 4.07 \times 10^{-6} \text{ V} = 4.07 \text{ μV}$$

**Part (b) — Noise Spectral Density (per √Hz):**

$$\frac{V_n}{\sqrt{B}} = \frac{4.07 \times 10^{-6}}{\sqrt{10^5}} = \frac{4.07 \times 10^{-6}}{316.23} \approx 12.87 \text{ nV}/\sqrt{\text{Hz}}$$

**Part (c) — Signal-to-Noise Ratio:**

$$\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_s}{V_n}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{4.07 \times 10^{-6}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(2457)$$

$$20 \log_{10}(2457) \approx 20 \times 3.390 = 67.8 \text{ dB}$$

This is an excellent SNR, indicating a high-quality measurement setup. ∎

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define Johnson-Nyquist thermal noise. Derive the expression for the RMS thermal noise voltage developed across a resistor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Johnson-Nyquist thermal noise is the random fluctuating voltage that appears across the terminals of a resistor (or any resistive element) due to the random thermal agitation of charge carriers (electrons) within the conductor at temperatures above absolute zero.

**Key Characteristics (1 mark):**
- It is white noise — its power spectral density is independent of frequency (up to ~$10^{12}$ Hz).
- It has a Gaussian amplitude distribution.
- It is unavoidable at finite temperatures.
- It depends on resistance, temperature, and measurement bandwidth.

**Formula (1 mark):**

$$V_{n,rms} = \sqrt{4 k T R B}$$

where $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K is Boltzmann's constant, $T$ is the absolute temperature, $R$ is the resistance, and $B$ is the measurement bandwidth.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between thermal noise and shot noise. Give the formula for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Thermal Noise (Johnson) | Shot Noise (Schottky) |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Random thermal motion of electrons in a conductor | Discrete random emission of charge carriers across a potential barrier |
| Occurs In | All resistors, conductors | PN junctions, diodes, transistors, vacuum tubes |
| Formula | $V_n = \sqrt{4 k T R B}$ | $I_n = \sqrt{2 q I_{DC} B}$ |
| Reduction | Lower R, lower T, narrower B | Lower DC bias current |
| Frequency | White (frequency independent) | White (frequency independent) |

**Key Difference (1 mark):** Thermal noise depends on **resistance and temperature** and appears as a voltage; shot noise depends on **DC current** and appears as a current. Shot noise is absent in pure ohmic resistors without potential barriers.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question Choice A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Define measurement noise. Classify the various sources of measurement noise in instrumentation systems with a neat block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the RMS thermal noise voltage across a resistor using the equipartition theorem. A measurement system uses a sensor with internal resistance $R = 50 \text{ k}\Omega$ at $T = 300$ K. If the system bandwidth is $B = 10 \text{ kHz}$, calculate the RMS thermal noise voltage. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Measurement noise is any unwanted disturbance superimposed on the true value of a physical quantity being measured, causing the indicated value to deviate from the actual measurand. It may be random, periodic, or transient, and can be internal or external to the measurement system.

**Classification (4 marks):** The three main categories are:

**1. Inherent (Intrinsic) Noise** — generated within the sensor itself:
- Thermal (Johnson-Nyquist) noise: $V_n = \sqrt{4kTRB}$
- Shot noise: $I_n = \sqrt{2qI_{DC}B}$
- Flicker (1/f) noise: $S(f) \propto 1/f$
- Generation-recombination noise

**2. Environmental (Extrinsic) Noise** — originates outside the system:
- Electromagnetic Interference (EMI)
- Ground loops
- Mechanical vibration and acoustic noise
- Thermoelectric (Seebeck) effects
- Chemical/electrochemical noise at sensor interfaces

**3. Processing Noise** — introduced during signal conditioning:
- Quantization noise in ADCs: $V_q = V_{FS}/(2^N \sqrt{12})$
- Amplifier voltage and current noise
- Switching transients in multiplexers

**Block Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the comprehensive Mermaid classification diagram. A textual version:

```
                    [Measurement Noise Sources]
                              |
        ┌─────────────────────┼─────────────────────┐
        |                     |                     |
  [Inherent Noise]     [Environmental Noise]  [Processing Noise]
        |                     |                     |
  - Thermal           - EMI                  - Quantization
  - Shot              - Ground Loop          - Amplifier
  - 1/f Flicker       - Vibration            - Switching
  - Gen-Recomb        - Thermoelectric
```

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Derivation (4 marks):** [See Section 3.1 for the full step-by-step derivation using equipartition theorem.]

Result:

$$V_{n,rms} = \sqrt{4 k T R B}$$

**Calculation (3 marks):**

Given: $R = 50 \times 10^3 \text{ }\Omega$, $T = 300$ K, $B = 10 \times 10^3$ Hz, $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.

$$V_n = \sqrt{4 \times (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 300 \times (50 \times 10^3) \times (10 \times 10^3)}$$

$$V_n = \sqrt{4 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300 \times 50 \times 10^3 \times 10^4}$$

Step-by-step:
- $4 \times 1.38 = 5.52$, so $5.52 \times 10^{-23}$
- $\times 300 = 1.656 \times 10^{-20}$
- $\times 50 \times 10^3 = 8.28 \times 10^{-16}$
- $\times 10^4 = 8.28 \times 10^{-12}$

$$V_n = \sqrt{8.28 \times 10^{-12}} = 2.877 \times 10^{-6} \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{n,rms} \approx 2.88 \text{ μV}}$$

---

### **Question Choice B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain shot noise and flicker (1/f) noise in detail. Compare their frequency dependence and suggest suitable mitigation techniques for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 12-bit ADC is used to digitize a signal with a full-scale range of $\pm 5$ V. Calculate: (i) the quantization step size, (ii) the RMS quantization noise voltage, and (iii) the theoretical Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) in dB. If two such ADCs are cascaded, what is the total RMS quantization noise? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Shot Noise (3 marks):** Shot noise arises in devices where charge carriers cross a potential barrier, such as in PN-junction diodes, BJTs, FETs, photodiodes, and vacuum tubes. The discrete and random nature of carrier emission produces current fluctuations. Its formula is:

$$I_{n,rms} = \sqrt{2 q I_{DC} B}$$

Properties:
- **White noise** — power spectral density is constant with frequency.
- **Gaussian amplitude distribution**.
- Dominant in low-current, high-impedance semiconductor devices.
- Proportional to the square root of bias current and bandwidth.
- **Mitigation:** Reduce DC bias current, narrow the system bandwidth, use low-noise preamplifiers with high input impedance.

**Flicker (1/f) Noise (3 marks):** Flicker noise, also called pink noise or 1/f noise, is a low-frequency noise with power spectral density:

$$S(f) = \frac{K}{f^{\alpha}}, \quad \alpha \approx 1$$

Origin: Trapping and detrapping of charge carriers at material defects, crystal dislocations, surface states, and contamination sites. Common in carbon resistors, semiconductor devices, and thin-film sensors.

Properties:
- **Inverse frequency dependence** — PSD increases as frequency decreases.
- Dominant below ~$1$ kHz in most electronic devices.
- Device-specific — varies considerably between samples.
- **Mitigation:** Chopper stabilization (modulating the signal to higher frequency where 1/f noise is negligible), AC coupling, and using premium low-noise components.

**Comparison (1 mark):**

| Feature | Shot Noise | Flicker Noise |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency Dependence | White (constant) | $1/f$ (decreases with $f$) |
| Origin | Discrete carrier emission | Trapping at material defects |
| Dominant At | All frequencies (above 1/f) | Low frequencies ($< 1$ kHz) |
| Mitigation | Lower bias, narrower BW | Chopper stabilization |

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:** $N = 12$ bits, $V_{FS} = 10$ V (range $\pm 5$ V means peak-to-peak span = 10 V).

**(i) Quantization Step Size (LSB) (2 marks):**

$$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N} = \frac{10}{2^{12}} = \frac{10}{4096} = 2.441 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}$$

$$V_{LSB} \approx 2.44 \text{ mV}$$

**(ii) RMS Quantization Noise (2 marks):**

$$V_{q,rms} = \frac{V_{LSB}}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{2.441 \times 10^{-3}}{3.464} = 7.047 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V}$$

$$V_{q,rms} \approx 0.705 \text{ mV}$$

**(iii) Theoretical SQNR (1 mark):**

Using the standard formula for a full-scale sinusoid:

$$\text{SQNR} = 6.02 \, N + 1.76 = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.0 \text{ dB}$$

**(iv) Cascaded ADCs (2 marks):**
If two ADCs are cascaded, the total RMS quantization noise combines in quadrature (assuming uncorrelated noise):

$$V_{q,total} = \sqrt{V_{q1}^2 + V_{q2}^2} = \sqrt{2 \cdot V_{q1}^2} = V_{q1} \cdot \sqrt{2}$$

$$V_{q,total} = 0.705 \times \sqrt{2} = 0.705 \times 1.414 = 0.997 \text{ mV}$$

$$\boxed{V_{q,total} \approx 0.997 \text{ mV}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Quadrature Summation:** Many students incorrectly *linearly add* noise voltages ($V_1 + V_2$). For uncorrelated noise, always use $V_{total} = \sqrt{V_1^2 + V_2^2}$. *(-2 marks typical penalty)*
> 2. **Bits vs. Levels:** $2^N$ gives the number of *levels*, not $2^{N-1}$. For a 12-bit ADC, it is $4096$ levels, not $2048$. *(-1 mark penalty)*
> 3. **Units:** Always state units explicitly (V, mV, μV, dB). KTU examiners mark off for missing units. *(-0.5 mark penalty)*
> 4. **SNR vs. SQNR:** SQNR specifically refers to quantization noise; SNR includes *all* noise sources. Do not use them interchangeably.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Memorize These for KTU:**

- [x] **Thermal noise** depends on $T$, $R$, and $B$ — formula: $V_n = \sqrt{4kTRB}$. Unavoidable in all resistors.
- [x] **Shot noise** depends on $I_{DC}$ and $B$ — formula: $I_n = \sqrt{2qI_{DC}B}$. Occurs across potential barriers.
- [x] **Flicker (1/f) noise** dominates at **low frequencies** ($< 1$ kHz). Suppressed via **chopper stabilization**.
- [x] **Thermal and shot noise** are **white** (frequency-independent PSD). Flicker is **pink** (PSD $\propto 1/f$).
- [x] **Boltzmann constant** $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K; **electron charge** $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C; **room temp** $T_0 = 290$ K (often approximated as 300 K in problems).
- [x] **SNR** = $10 \log_{10}(P_s/P_n)$ dB = $20 \log_{10}(V_s/V_n)$ dB (for equal impedances).
- [x] **Quantization noise** of N-bit ADC: $V_q = V_{FS}/(2^N \sqrt{12})$. **SQNR** = $6.02N + 1.76$ dB.
- [x] **Independent noise sources add in quadrature:** $V_{tot} = \sqrt{\sum V_i^2}$. NEVER add linearly.
- [x] **EMI** is suppressed by **shielding + twisted pair cables + proper grounding**.
- [x] **Ground loops** are eliminated by **single-point (star) grounding** or **isolation amplifiers**.
- [x] **Mechanical vibration** is mitigated by **rigid mounting + damping + vibration isolation mounts**.
- [x] **Thermoelectric (Seebeck) noise** arises at dissimilar metal junctions — critical in low-voltage measurements.
- [x] **Cryogenic cooling** reduces thermal noise (used in radio astronomy, IR detectors, SQUIDs).
- [x] **Common-mode noise** is rejected using **differential amplifiers with high CMRR** (Common-Mode Rejection Ratio).
- [x] **Bandwidth reduction** is the simplest way to reduce white noise — narrowband filtering trades speed for SNR.
- [x] **Noise Figure (NF)** = $10 \log_{10}(F)$ where $F = SNR_{in}/SNR_{out}$ — quantifies amplifier-induced SNR degradation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Quick Recall — "TS-FE-Q" for noise types:**
> **T**hermal, **S**hot, **F**licker, **E**nvironmental, **Q**uantization. These are the five categories most frequently tested.

---

topics.title: Inductive Coupling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT205 - BASIC OF INSTRUMENTATION ENGINEERING (INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Inductive Coupling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inductive Coupling** is the phenomenon of energy transfer between two electrical circuits (or coils) through a time-varying magnetic field, governed by the principle of **mutual inductance**. It forms the foundational operating principle of a wide class of passive transducers used in industrial instrumentation, including **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)**, **RVDT**, **eddy-current proximity probes**, and **electromagnetic flowmeters**.

Mathematically, when an alternating current $i_1(t)$ flows in the *primary* coil, it produces a time-varying magnetic flux $\Phi_{12}$ that links the *secondary* coil. By **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, this linking flux induces an electromotive force (EMF) $e_2$ in the secondary coil even though there is **no physical (galvanic) connection** between the two coils.

$$e_2 = -M \frac{di_1}{dt}$$

where the proportionality constant $M$ is the **mutual inductance**, measured in **henries (H)**, with **SI base units of $\text{V}{\cdot}\text{s/A}$**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Two Coils Whispering Through Magnetism"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine two people standing in two separate rooms, but they can still talk to each other by passing notes tied to magnets. When one person shakes a magnet (creates a changing magnetic field), the field travels through the wall and pulls the other magnet, which in turn moves the second person's note. They never physically touch, yet information (or energy) is transferred.

In the same way:
- The **primary coil** is the first person — it carries a changing current and creates a changing magnetic field.
- The **secondary coil** is the second person — it "feels" that magnetic field and produces an EMF.
- The **magnetic field** is the invisible messenger between them.
- The **core** (air, ferrite, or iron) is the "wall" that guides the magnetic flux efficiently from one coil to the other.

The efficiency of this magnetic "conversation" is captured by a number between **0 and 1** called the **coefficient of coupling (k)**. A value of $k=1$ means a perfect magnetic conversation (every line of flux from coil 1 links coil 2), while $k=0$ means the two coils are magnetically deaf to each other.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constants and Units to Memorize**
> - **Permeability of free space:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m}$
> - **Relative permeability of ferritic cores:** typically $\mu_r$ ranges from **2000 to 50000**
> - **Inductance unit:** $1\ \text{Henry (H)} = 1\ \text{V}{\cdot}\text{s/A}$
> - **Standard excitation frequency for LVDTs:** typically **$1\ \text{kHz}$ to $10\ \text{kHz}$** (audio range)
> - **Coefficient of coupling range:** $0 \le k \le 1$ (dimensionless)
> - **Operating temperature range for industrial LVDTs:** $-55^\circ\text{C}$ to $+150^\circ\text{C}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 4):** The expected depth includes understanding of mutual inductance, coefficient of coupling, working principles of LVDT/RVDT, and the ability to perform calculations involving flux linkage, induced EMF, and displacement sensitivity. Numerical problems based on coupled coils and LVDT output voltage form a regular part of the End Semester Examination (ESE).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Inductive Coupling — Step by Step

The operation of any inductively coupled system can be broken down into four sequential logic steps. Understanding these steps is the key to solving any KTU-level problem on this topic.

- **Step 1 — Excitation of the Primary Coil:** An alternating current source (typically a sine-wave oscillator at $1\text{–}10\ \text{kHz}$) drives the primary coil. The instantaneous primary current is:
  $$i_1(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$$

- **Step 2 — Generation of Time-Varying Magnetic Flux:** By Ampere's Law, this current produces a magnetic flux $\Phi_1$ whose magnitude is proportional to both the current and the self-inductance of the coil:
  $$\Phi_1 = \frac{L_1\, i_1}{N_1}$$
  where $N_1$ is the number of turns in the primary coil.

- **Step 3 — Flux Linkage to the Secondary Coil:** Only a fraction of $\Phi_1$ links the secondary coil. The *mutual flux* is:
  $$\Phi_{12} = k \cdot \Phi_1$$
  and the *total flux linkages* in the secondary coil are:
  $$\Psi_2 = N_2 \cdot \Phi_{12} = k \cdot N_2 \cdot \Phi_1$$

- **Step 4 — Induction of EMF in the Secondary Coil:** By Faraday's Law, a changing current in the primary induces a voltage in the secondary. Using the chain rule:
  $$e_2 = -\frac{d\Psi_2}{dt} = -N_2 k \frac{d\Phi_1}{dt} = -k N_2 \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{L_1 i_1}{N_1}\right)$$
  This simplifies to the central relationship:
  $$e_2 = -M \frac{di_1}{dt}, \quad \text{where}\ M = k\sqrt{L_1 L_2}$$

---

## 2.2 Coefficient of Coupling — The "Magnetic Intimacy" Factor

The **coefficient of coupling (k)** is the most fundamental parameter of inductive coupling. It measures the *fraction of flux produced by one coil that links the other coil*.

$$k = \frac{M}{\sqrt{L_1 L_2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation of $k$:**
> - $k = 0$ → Coils are magnetically *isolated* (no flux linking the other). Common when coils are far apart or shielded.
> - $k = 1$ → Coils are *perfectly coupled* (every line of flux from coil 1 links coil 2). Approached in tightly wound toroidal transformers.
> - $0 < k < 1$ → The realistic case for most transducers. Typical values: $k \approx 0.6$ to $0.95$ for industrial LVDTs.

---

## 2.3 The KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below consolidates every high-yield formula on Inductive Coupling that you are expected to know for the KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize the units, the limiting cases of $k$, and the sign convention for aiding/opposing coils.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Self-inductance of a coil | $L = \dfrac{N\Phi}{i}$ | H (henry) | $N$ = turns, $\Phi$ = Wb, $i$ = A |
| 2 | Mutual inductance (definition) | $M = \dfrac{N_2\,\Phi_{12}}{i_1}$ | H | Flux from coil 1 linking coil 2 |
| 3 | Mutual inductance (Neumann) | $M = \dfrac{\mu_0\, N_1 N_2\, A}{\ell}$ | H | For two coaxial solenoids |
| 4 | Coefficient of coupling | $k = \dfrac{M}{\sqrt{L_1 L_2}}$ | dimensionless | $0 \le k \le 1$ |
| 5 | Mutual EMF in secondary | $e_2 = -M\,\dfrac{di_1}{dt}$ | V | Faraday's law applied |
| 6 | Mutual EMF in primary | $e_1 = -M\,\dfrac{di_2}{dt}$ | V | By reciprocity |
| 7 | Series-aiding total inductance | $L_T = L_1 + L_2 + 2M$ | H | Fluxes add constructively |
| 8 | Series-opposing total inductance | $L_T = L_1 + L_2 - 2M$ | H | Fluxes subtract (used in LVDT) |
| 9 | Energy stored in coupled coils | $W = \dfrac{1}{2} L_1 i_1^2 + \dfrac{1}{2} L_2 i_2^2 \pm M i_1 i_2$ | J | +ve for aiding, -ve for opposing |
| 10 | LVDT output (linear region) | $e_o = K \cdot x$ | V | $K$ = sensitivity (V/mm) |
| 11 | LVDT output (general) | $e_o = e_{s1} - e_{s2}$ | V | $e_{s1}, e_{s2}$ = secondary EMFs |
| 12 | LVDT sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{e_o}{x}$ | V/mm | Higher $S$ = better transducer |
| 13 | Mutual flux linkage | $\Psi_{12} = M\, i_1$ | Wb-turns (Wb) | Alternative definition of $M$ |
| 14 | Inductance of a long solenoid | $L = \dfrac{\mu_0 \mu_r N^2 A}{\ell}$ | H | Used to compute $M$ for coaxial coils |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Inductive coupling is the **workhorse principle** of modern industrial measurement. It is chosen over resistive or capacitive sensing because it offers:
> 1. **Non-contact operation** — no friction, no wear, infinite mechanical life.
> 2. **High resolution** — sub-micrometre displacement detection in precision LVDTs.
> 3. **Robustness** — survives harsh environments (vibration, dust, oil, mild radiation).
> 4. **Intrinsic safety** — passive transducers with no electronics at the sensing point (especially useful in explosive atmospheres).
> 5. **Bidirectional operation** — works for static and dynamic measurements.
>
> **Industrial Applications:** Aircraft control-surface position feedback, hydraulic ram displacement, machine-tool axis feedback, weigh scales (load cells with LVDT cores), geotechnical borehole extensometers, jet-engine vibration probes (eddy-current type), and blood-pressure catheter-tip transducers in biomedical instrumentation.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section is the **examination-preparation core**. KTU 2024 ESE questions on Module 4 frequently test the derivations below for **3 to 7 marks**. The complete algebraic path is shown so that you can reproduce every line in the answer script.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mutual Inductance of Two Coaxial Solenoids (Neumann-Type Result)

**Problem Setup:** Two solenoids of lengths $\ell_1$ and $\ell_2$, radii $r_1$ and $r_2$, and turns $N_1$ and $N_2$ are placed coaxially such that one is *inside* the other (a common LVDT/transformer configuration). Derive the mutual inductance $M$.

### Step 1 — Compute the magnetic field inside the inner solenoid
When a current $i_1$ flows in solenoid 1 (the inner one), the magnetic field along its axis is uniform and given by:
$$B_1 = \mu_0\, \frac{N_1}{\ell_1}\, i_1$$

### Step 2 — Compute the magnetic flux through one turn of the outer solenoid
The flux through a single turn of the outer (secondary) solenoid of cross-sectional area $A_2 = \pi r_2^{\,2}$ is:
$$\Phi_{12,\text{per turn}} = B_1 \cdot A_2 = \mu_0\, \frac{N_1}{\ell_1}\, i_1 \cdot \pi r_2^{\,2}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the total flux linkage with the secondary coil
The total flux linkage is the per-turn flux multiplied by the number of turns of the secondary:
$$\Psi_{12} = N_2 \cdot \Phi_{12,\text{per turn}} = \mu_0\, \frac{N_1 N_2 \pi r_2^{\,2}}{\ell_1}\, i_1$$

### Step 4 — Apply the definition of mutual inductance
By definition, $M = \Psi_{12}/i_1$. Dividing both sides by $i_1$:
$$\boxed{\,M = \frac{\mu_0\, N_1 N_2\, \pi r_2^{\,2}}{\ell_1}\,} \quad \text{(henries)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Textual Explanation:** The mutual inductance is proportional to the *product of turns* of both coils, the cross-sectional area of the *secondary* coil, and inversely proportional to the *length of the primary*. This is why LVDTs are designed as short, fat coils wound on a common bobbin — to maximize $M$ and hence the output sensitivity.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Coefficient of Coupling $k$ From First Principles

**Starting Point:** Two coils with self-inductances $L_1$ and $L_2$ and mutual inductance $M$ between them.

### Step 1 — Define self-inductance of coil 1
$$L_1 = \frac{N_1 \Phi_{11}}{i_1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Phi_{11} = \frac{L_1 i_1}{N_1}$$
where $\Phi_{11}$ is the flux produced by coil 1 that links *itself*.

### Step 2 — Define mutual inductance from coil 1 to coil 2
$$M = \frac{N_2 \Phi_{12}}{i_1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Phi_{12} = \frac{M i_1}{N_2}$$
where $\Phi_{12}$ is the flux produced by coil 1 that links coil 2.

### Step 3 — Form the ratio of mutual to self-flux linkage
The fraction of flux from coil 1 that links coil 2 is, by definition, the *coefficient of coupling*:
$$k = \frac{\Phi_{12}}{\Phi_{11}} = \frac{M i_1 / N_2}{L_1 i_1 / N_1} = \frac{M N_1}{L_1 N_2}$$

### Step 4 — Eliminate $N_1/N_2$ by symmetry
By repeating the same argument with roles reversed (current in coil 2 inducing flux in coil 1), one also obtains:
$$k = \frac{M N_2}{L_2 N_1}$$

### Step 5 — Multiply the two expressions
$$k^2 = \frac{M^2 N_1 N_2}{L_1 L_2 N_1 N_2} = \frac{M^2}{L_1 L_2}$$

### Step 6 — Take the square root
$$\boxed{\,k = \frac{M}{\sqrt{L_1 L_2}}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Textual Explanation:** This result confirms that $k$ is a pure dimensionless number bounded by 0 and 1. If $M^2 > L_1 L_2$, the system would be unphysical (it would violate energy conservation), hence the bound $k \le 1$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Induced EMF in the Secondary Coil (Faraday's Law)

**Starting Point:** Coil 1 has $N_1$ turns carrying current $i_1(t)$, producing total flux $\Phi_1$ that links coil 2 with mutual flux $\Phi_{12} = k \Phi_1$.

### Step 1 — Write the flux linkage in coil 2
The total flux linkage in coil 2 is the mutual flux multiplied by its number of turns:
$$\Psi_2 = N_2 \cdot \Phi_{12} = N_2 k \Phi_1$$

### Step 2 — Apply Faraday's Law of Induction
The induced EMF in coil 2 is the negative time derivative of flux linkage:
$$e_2 = -\frac{d\Psi_2}{dt} = -N_2 k \frac{d\Phi_1}{dt}$$

### Step 3 — Express $\Phi_1$ in terms of $i_1$
Since $\Phi_1 = L_1 i_1 / N_1$, its time derivative is:
$$\frac{d\Phi_1}{dt} = \frac{L_1}{N_1} \cdot \frac{di_1}{dt}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute back
$$e_2 = -N_2 k \cdot \frac{L_1}{N_1} \cdot \frac{di_1}{dt} = -\left(\frac{k N_2 L_1}{N_1}\right) \frac{di_1}{dt}$$

### Step 5 — Recognise the bracket as $M$
From Derivation 3.1, the term in brackets is exactly the mutual inductance $M$:
$$M = \frac{\mu_0 N_1 N_2 A_2}{\ell_1} \quad \text{and}\quad L_1 = \frac{\mu_0 N_1^{\,2} A_1}{\ell_1}$$

Substituting these into the bracket and simplifying gives the identity:
$$\frac{k N_2 L_1}{N_1} = \frac{k N_2}{N_1} \cdot \frac{\mu_0 N_1^{\,2} A_1}{\ell_1} = \frac{\mu_0 k N_1 N_2 A_1}{\ell_1} \equiv M$$

### Step 6 — Final compact form
$$\boxed{\,e_2 = -M \frac{di_1}{dt}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Textual Explanation:** The negative sign embodies **Lenz's Law** — the induced EMF opposes the change that produced it. In sinusoidal steady state, with $i_1 = I_m \sin(\omega t)$, this becomes $e_2 = -j\omega M I_m e^{j\omega t} = j\omega M I_m e^{j(\omega t - \pi/2)}$, showing the secondary voltage *lags* the primary current by 90°.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: LVDT Output Voltage in the Linear Region

**Starting Point:** An LVDT has one primary coil (P) and two identical secondary coils (S1 and S2) connected in *series opposition*. A movable ferromagnetic core is positioned at a displacement $x$ from the null point.

### Step 1 — Define the secondary EMFs
Let the primary be excited by $v_p = V_p \sin(\omega t)$. The mutual inductances from primary to each secondary depend on core position:
$$M_1(x) = M_0 + Kx \quad \text{(S1, increases as core moves toward S1)}$$
$$M_2(x) = M_0 - Kx \quad \text{(S2, decreases as core moves toward S2)}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the individual secondary EMFs
$$e_{s1} = -M_1(x) \frac{di_p}{dt}, \qquad e_{s2} = -M_2(x) \frac{di_p}{dt}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the differential output (series-opposing connection)
$$e_o = e_{s1} - e_{s2} = -\left[M_1(x) - M_2(x)\right] \frac{di_p}{dt} = -\left[(M_0 + Kx) - (M_0 - Kx)\right] \frac{di_p}{dt}$$

### Step 4 — Simplify
$$e_o = -(2Kx) \frac{di_p}{dt} = -2Kx \frac{di_p}{dt}$$

### Step 5 — Replace $di_p/dt$ for sinusoidal excitation
With $i_p = I_p \sin(\omega t)$, $\dfrac{di_p}{dt} = \omega I_p \cos(\omega t)$. The peak output voltage is therefore:
$$E_{o,\text{peak}} = 2Kx \cdot \omega I_p$$

### Step 6 — Define the LVDT sensitivity
The sensitivity of the LVDT is defined as the output voltage per unit displacement:
$$\boxed{\,S = \frac{E_{o,\text{peak}}}{x} = 2K\omega I_p \quad \text{(V/mm or V/m)}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Textual Explanation:** The LVDT's brilliance is that the *common-mode* components (the $M_0$ term) cancel out, leaving only the *differential-mode* term proportional to displacement. This gives excellent linearity, low thermal drift, and inherent rejection of supply-voltage variations.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The diagrams below follow the **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards** — node IDs are purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters; labels contain only clean uppercase alphanumeric text (no bold, no italics, no embedded tables).

## 4.1 Figure 1: Functional Block Diagram of Inductive Coupling Between Two Coils

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[AC VOLTAGE SOURCE] -->|EXCITATION i1 t| A2[PRIMARY COIL N1]
    A2 -->|TIME VARYING FLUX PHI1| A3[FERROMAGNETIC CORE OR AIR GAP]
    A3 -->|MUTUAL FLUX PHI12| A4[SECONDARY COIL N2]
    A4 -->|INDUCED EMF e2| A5[OUTPUT MEASURING INSTRUMENT]
    A6[MUTUAL INDUCTANCE M] -.-> A2
    A6 -.-> A4
    A7[COEFFICIENT OF COUPLING K] -.-> A3
```

**Reading the Diagram:** The solid arrows represent physical signal flow (current, flux, voltage). The dashed arrows represent the *mathematical parameters* that govern the coupling: $M$ is the property of the coil pair, and $k$ is the property of the magnetic circuit that channels the flux between them.

---

## 4.2 Figure 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of an LVDT Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    B1[OSCILLATOR 1 KHZ TO 10 KHZ SINEWAVE] -->|AC EXCITATION Vp| B2[PRIMARY COIL P]
    B2 -->|MAGNETIC FIELD B| B3[MOVABLE FERROMAGNETIC CORE]
    B3 -->|COUPLED FLUX INTO S1| B4[SECONDARY COIL S1]
    B3 -->|COUPLED FLUX INTO S2| B5[SECONDARY COIL S2]
    B4 -->|es1| B6[SERIES OPPOSING JUNCTION]
    B5 -->|es2 MINUS| B6
    B6 -->|DIFFERENTIAL OUTPUT eo| B7[PHASE SENSITIVE DEMODULATOR]
    B7 -->|RECTIFIED DC SIGNAL| B8[LOW PASS FILTER]
    B8 -->|SMOOTH DC OUTPUT| B9[INDICATOR OR CONTROLLER]
    B10[DISPLACEMENT X APPLIED] -.-> B3
    B11[CALIBRATION S Eo X] -.-> B9
```

**Reading the Diagram:** The core's mechanical displacement $x$ modulates the *relative* flux linking the two secondaries. The series-opposing junction cancels the common-mode component (which is large when $x=0$ and the core is centred), leaving only the differential signal proportional to $x$. The demodulator converts the AC output to a DC signal whose polarity indicates the *direction* of displacement.

---

## 4.3 Figure 3: Classification of Inductively-Coupled Transducers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph C1[INDUCTIVELY COUPLED TRANSDUCERS]
        C2[SINGLE COIL VARIABLE INDUCTANCE]
        C3[LVDT LINEAR VARIABLE DIFFERENTIAL TRANSFORMER]
        C4[RVDT ROTARY VARIABLE DIFFERENTIAL TRANSFORMER]
        C5[EDDY CURRENT PROXIMITY PROBE]
        C6[ELECTROMAGNETIC FLOWMETER]
    end
    C2 --> C7[DISPLACEMENT AND PRESSURE]
    C3 --> C8[LINEAR DISPLACEMENT 1 MM TO 500 MM]
    C4 --> C9[ANGULAR DISPLACEMENT 0 TO 360 DEG]
    C5 --> C10[NON CONTACT POSITION VIBRATION]
    C6 --> C11[CONDUCTIVE FLUID FLOW RATE]
```

**Reading the Diagram:** All five transducer types share the same physical root — *a time-varying magnetic field interacting with a sensing element* — but exploit different aspects of the coupling (mutual inductance variation, eddy-current loss, or motional EMF).

---

## 4.4 Figure 4: Working Principle of LVDT at Different Core Positions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    D1[PRIMARY COIL EXCITED BY AC] --> D2
    D2{CORE POSITION}
    D2 -->|CORE AT NULL CENTRE| D3[EQUAL FLUX IN S1 AND S2]
    D3 --> D4[OUTPUT eo EQUALS e1 MINUS e2 EQUALS 0 VOLTS]
    D2 -->|CORE SHIFTED TOWARDS S1| D5[FLUX IN S1 INCREASES]
    D5 --> D6[OUTPUT eo POSITIVE PROPORTIONAL TO PLUS X]
    D2 -->|CORE SHIFTED TOWARDS S2| D7[FLUX IN S2 INCREASES]
    D7 --> D8[OUTPUT eo NEGATIVE PROPORTIONAL TO MINUS X]
```

**Reading the Diagram:** This decision-flow representation captures the three operational states of an LVDT. The *phase* (or polarity, after demodulation) of the output voltage tells the observer the *direction* of displacement, while the *magnitude* tells the *amount*. This is the basis for the LVDT's use as a bidirectional position sensor.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** *[KTU University Exam — May 2023]* **Define mutual inductance and the coefficient of coupling. State the relationship between them and explain the physical significance of a coupling coefficient of $k=0$ and $k=1$.**

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Definition of Mutual Inductance (1 mark):**
> Mutual inductance $M$ between two coils is defined as the flux linkage of one coil per unit current in the other coil.
> $$\boxed{\,M = \frac{N_2 \Phi_{12}}{i_1} = \frac{N_1 \Phi_{21}}{i_2}\,}\quad \text{(unit: henry)}$$

**Definition of Coefficient of Coupling (1 mark):**
> The coefficient of coupling $k$ is the fraction of the total flux produced by one coil that links the other coil.
> $$\boxed{\,k = \frac{M}{\sqrt{L_1 L_2}}\,}\quad \text{where}\ 0 \le k \le 1$$

**Physical Significance (1 mark):**
> - $k = 0$ → The coils are magnetically isolated; no flux from coil 1 links coil 2 (e.g., coils far apart or magnetically shielded).
> - $k = 1$ → Perfect coupling; every line of flux from coil 1 links coil 2 (approached in closely wound toroidal transformers).

---

> **Q2.** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]* **List the three main parts of an LVDT and state the function of each. What is the role of the demodulator circuit in an LVDT measurement system?**

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Three Main Parts of an LVDT (2 marks):**

| # | Part | Function |
|---|------|----------|
| 1 | **Primary coil (P)** | Energized by an AC excitation source (1–10 kHz); produces the time-varying magnetic flux. |
| 2 | **Two identical secondary coils (S1 and S2)** | Wound on either side of the primary; connected in *series opposition* to produce a differential output. |
| 3 | **Movable ferromagnetic core (armature)** | Soft-iron or nickel-iron slug; its axial position determines the relative flux linking S1 and S2. |

**Role of the Demodulator (1 mark):**
> The phase-sensitive demodulator converts the AC differential output of the LVDT into a rectified DC signal whose **magnitude** is proportional to the core displacement and whose **polarity** indicates the **direction** of displacement. This is necessary because the LVDT output is inherently AC and cannot directly drive a DC indicator or controller.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Note on KTU 2024 Pattern:** The ESE Part B questions in Module 4 typically allow an *internal choice* — students choose between two alternatives. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are designed as a complete internal-choice pair. Each question carries 14 marks and is split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. **Both alternatives are fully solved.**

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **A(a).** *[KTU University Exam — June 2024]* **Derive the expression for the mutual inductance of two magnetically coupled coils in terms of their self-inductances and the coefficient of coupling. Also derive the expression for the total inductance when the coils are connected in (i) series-aiding and (ii) series-opposing modes. (7 marks)**

> **A(b).** **With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT). Derive the expression for its output voltage and sensitivity. (7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to A(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Definition of Mutual Inductance in terms of self-inductances (2 marks):**

Consider two coils with self-inductances $L_1$ and $L_2$ and mutual inductance $M$. The self-flux produced by coil 1 is $\Phi_{11} = L_1 i_1 / N_1$, and the flux from coil 1 linking coil 2 is $\Phi_{12} = M i_1 / N_2$. The coefficient of coupling is:

$$k = \frac{\Phi_{12}}{\Phi_{11}} = \frac{M i_1 / N_2}{L_1 i_1 / N_1} = \frac{M N_1}{L_1 N_2}$$

By symmetry (reversing the roles of the two coils), we also obtain $k = M N_2 / (L_2 N_1)$. Multiplying these two expressions:

$$k^2 = \frac{M^2 N_1 N_2}{L_1 L_2 N_1 N_2} = \frac{M^2}{L_1 L_2}$$

Taking the positive square root (since $k \ge 0$ and $M \ge 0$):

$$\boxed{\,M = k \sqrt{L_1 L_2}\,}$$

**Step 2 — Series-Aiding Connection (2.5 marks):**

When currents $i_1$ and $i_2$ flow through the two coils *in the same direction* (aiding), the total flux linking either coil is the sum of its self-flux and the mutual flux. The total flux linkage is:

$$\Psi_T = (L_1 i_1 + M i_2) + (L_2 i_2 + M i_1)$$

Defining the total inductance $L_T = \Psi_T / i$ where $i = i_1 = i_2$ (series connection implies same current):

$$L_T i = L_1 i + L_2 i + 2M i$$

$$\boxed{\,L_T = L_1 + L_2 + 2M\,}$$

**Step 3 — Series-Opposing Connection (2.5 marks):**

When the coils are connected so that the currents are in *opposite directions* with respect to their dot-markings, the mutual flux *opposes* the self-flux. The total flux linkage becomes:

$$\Psi_T = (L_1 i_1 - M i_2) + (L_2 i_2 - M i_1)$$

With $i_1 = i_2 = i$ (series):

$$L_T i = L_1 i + L_2 i - 2M i$$

$$\boxed{\,L_T = L_1 + L_2 - 2M\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Textual Wrap-up:** These two results are the foundation for analysing coupled circuits and for the working of the LVDT (where the secondaries are in series-opposing connection).

---

#### Solution to A(b) — 7 Marks

**Construction (2 marks):**

An LVDT consists of:
- A **cylindrical non-magnetic former** (typically a ceramic or phenolic bobbin).
- **One primary coil (P)** wound centrally on the former, energized by an AC source of frequency $1\text{–}10\ \text{kHz}$.
- **Two identical secondary coils (S1 and S2)** wound on either side of the primary, connected in *series opposition*.
- A **movable soft-iron core (armature)** attached to the displacement-sensing rod. The core slides axially inside the former.

**Working (3 marks):**

When the core is at the *null (central) position*, the flux linking S1 equals that linking S2. The induced EMFs are equal in magnitude and 180° out of phase (due to series-opposing connection). The differential output is:

$$e_o = e_{s1} - e_{s2} = 0$$

When the core moves *toward S1*, the flux linking S1 increases while that linking S2 decreases. The net output is a sine wave at the excitation frequency, with amplitude proportional to displacement $x$ and phase indicating the direction:

$$e_o = e_{s1} - e_{s2} = -2Kx \frac{di_p}{dt}$$

**Output and Sensitivity Derivation (2 marks):**

With $i_p = I_p \sin(\omega t)$, the peak output is:

$$E_{o,\text{peak}} = 2Kx \cdot \omega I_p$$

The sensitivity of the LVDT is defined as:

$$\boxed{\,S = \frac{E_{o,\text{peak}}}{x} = 2K \omega I_p \quad \text{(V/mm)}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Textual Wrap-up:** The LVDT offers high linearity (within the central region), infinite resolution, and excellent reliability. It is widely used for measuring linear displacement in machine-tool slides, hydraulic actuators, and pressure transducers.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

> **B(a).** **Explain the principle of inductive coupling with a clear block-diagram description. Discuss FOUR practical transducer applications that exploit this principle. (7 marks)**

> **B(b).** **A coil A with 500 turns is wound on a magnetic core of mean length $40\ \text{cm}$ and cross-sectional area $20\ \text{cm}^2$. A second coil B with 2000 turns is wound over coil A such that 40% of the flux from coil A links coil B. The relative permeability of the core is $1200$. If the current in coil A changes uniformly from $0$ to $6\ \text{A}$ in $3\ \text{ms}$, find (i) the self-inductance of coil A, (ii) the mutual inductance, and (iii) the EMF induced in coil B. (7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to B(a) — 7 Marks

**Principle of Inductive Coupling (2 marks):**

Inductive coupling is the transfer of energy between two coils through a *time-varying magnetic field*, with no physical electrical connection. The coupling efficiency is described by the coefficient of coupling $k = M / \sqrt{L_1 L_2}$.

**Block-Diagram Description (1.5 marks):**

> AC Source → Primary Coil → Magnetic Field (guided by core) → Secondary Coil → Induced EMF → Load/Instrument

**Four Practical Applications (3.5 marks — distribute 0.5–1 mark each):**

1. **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer):** Measures linear displacement. Core position modulates the differential flux linking two secondaries.

2. **RVDT (Rotary Variable Differential Transformer):** Measures angular displacement of a shaft. The rotating cam-shaped core changes the coupling to the secondary windings.

3. **Eddy-Current Proximity Probe:** A high-frequency coil generates an alternating magnetic field. When a conductive target approaches, eddy currents induced in the target absorb energy and reduce the coil's effective inductance. Used for vibration and position measurement in turbomachinery.

4. **Electromagnetic Flowmeter:** A magnetic field across a conductive fluid (e.g., blood, slurry, or industrial liquor) induces an EMF proportional to the flow velocity (Faraday's law). The EMF is picked up by two electrodes in the pipe wall.

> [!NOTE]
> **Textual Wrap-up:** All these transducers share the common feature that a *change in the measurand* produces a *change in magnetic flux linkage*, which is converted into a measurable electrical signal.

---

#### Solution to B(b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**

$$N_1 = 500,\quad N_2 = 2000,\quad \ell = 40\ \text{cm} = 0.4\ \text{m}$$
$$A = 20\ \text{cm}^2 = 20 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2 = 2 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2$$
$$\mu_r = 1200,\quad k = 0.40,\quad \Delta i = 6\ \text{A},\quad \Delta t = 3\ \text{ms} = 3 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s}$$

**Step 1 — Self-Inductance of Coil A (2 marks):**

Using $L_1 = \dfrac{\mu_0 \mu_r N_1^{\,2} A}{\ell}$:

$$L_1 = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times 1200 \times (500)^2 \times (2 \times 10^{-3})}{0.4}$$

$$L_1 = \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1200 \times 250000 \times 2 \times 10^{-3}}{0.4}$$

Computing the numerator step by step:
- $4\pi \times 10^{-7} \approx 12.566 \times 10^{-7}$
- $\times 1200 = 15080 \times 10^{-7} = 1.508 \times 10^{-3}$
- $\times 250000 = 377.0$
- $\times 2 \times 10^{-3} = 0.754$

Divide by 0.4:

$$L_1 = \frac{0.754}{0.4} = 1.885\ \text{H}$$

$$\boxed{\,L_1 \approx 1.885\ \text{H}\,}$$

**Step 2 — Mutual Inductance (2.5 marks):**

First compute $L_2$:

$$L_2 = \frac{\mu_0 \mu_r N_2^{\,2} A}{\ell} = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times 1200 \times (2000)^2 \times (2 \times 10^{-3})}{0.4}$$

- $2000^2 = 4 \times 10^6$
- $1.508 \times 10^{-3} \times 4 \times 10^6 = 6032$
- $\times 2 \times 10^{-3} = 12.064$
- $\div 0.4 = 30.16\ \text{H}$

Now apply $M = k \sqrt{L_1 L_2}$:

$$L_1 \cdot L_2 = 1.885 \times 30.16 \approx 56.85$$

$$\sqrt{L_1 L_2} = \sqrt{56.85} \approx 7.540\ \text{H}$$

$$M = 0.40 \times 7.540 = 3.016\ \text{H}$$

$$\boxed{\,M \approx 3.016\ \text{H}\,}$$

**Step 3 — Induced EMF in Coil B (2.5 marks):**

Using $e_2 = -M \dfrac{di_1}{dt}$ (magnitude only):

$$\left\vert e_2 \right\vert = M \cdot \frac{\Delta i}{\Delta t} = 3.016 \times \frac{6}{3 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$= 3.016 \times 2000 = 6032\ \text{V}$$

$$\boxed{\,\left\vert e_2 \right\vert \approx 6032\ \text{V} = 6.032\ \text{kV}\,}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversion trap (1 mark lost here regularly):** Students often forget to convert cm to m and cm² to m² before substituting. The answer becomes off by a factor of $10^4$ to $10^6$.
> 2. **Square-root of product error:** $M = k\sqrt{L_1 L_2}$ — *not* $k(L_1 + L_2)/2$. This is a common mistake that yields wrong $M$ and propagates the error to the final EMF.
> 3. **Sign convention:** $e_2$ is negative per Lenz's law. The numerical answer should report the *magnitude* unless the question explicitly asks for polarity.
> 4. **Boundary check on $k$:** Always verify $0 \le k \le 1$. If your calculation gives $k > 1$, you have a units error or a wrong assumption.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Inductive Coupling**

- **Mutual Inductance** $M = N_2 \Phi_{12} / i_1$, measured in **henries (H)**, with SI base $\text{V}{\cdot}\text{s/A}$.
- **Coefficient of Coupling** $k = M / \sqrt{L_1 L_2}$, **dimensionless**, bounded by $0 \le k \le 1$.
- **Mutual EMF** $e_2 = -M \dfrac{di_1}{dt}$ — Faraday's law in coupled-coil form.
- **Series-Aiding Total Inductance** $L_T = L_1 + L_2 + 2M$.
- **Series-Opposing Total Inductance** $L_T = L_1 + L_2 - 2M$ — the configuration used inside an LVDT.
- **LVDT Output** $e_o = e_{s1} - e_{s2} = 0$ at the **null position** (core centred).
- **LVDT Sensitivity** $S = E_{o,\text{peak}} / x = 2K\omega I_p$ (V/mm).
- **LVDT Excitation Frequency** is typically **$1\ \text{kHz}$ to $10\ \text{kHz}$** (audio range).
- **Three parts of an LVDT** — Primary coil, two secondaries, and a movable ferromagnetic core.
- **Demodulator** converts the AC differential output to a DC signal whose **polarity** indicates displacement *direction*.
- **LVDT Advantages** — Non-contact, infinite resolution, robust, bidirectional, intrinsically safe.
- **LVDT Disadvantages** — Requires AC excitation and signal conditioning; relatively large in size for sub-millimetre measurements.
- **Other inductively-coupled transducers** — RVDT, eddy-current probe, electromagnetic flowmeter.
- **Energy stored in coupled coils:** $W = \frac{1}{2} L_1 i_1^2 + \frac{1}{2} L_2 i_2^2 \pm M i_1 i_2$ (use **+** for aiding, **−** for opposing).
- **Permeability of free space** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m}$ — always use SI units in calculations.
- **Common exam trap** — confusing *self*-inductance with *mutual* inductance in numerical problems. Always identify which coil is the source and which is the receptor before writing $M$.
- **Reciprocity** — $M_{12} = M_{21} = M$. The mutual inductance is the same regardless of which coil is energised.
- **In-phase / out-of-phase** — Aiding connection → secondary voltage in phase with primary; Opposing connection → 180° phase shift.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capacitive (Electrostatic) Coupling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT205 - BASIC OF INSTRUMENTATION ENGINEERING (INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Capacitive (Electrostatic) Coupling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Capacitive (Electrostatic) Coupling** is a form of *unintended electrical interference* in which an alternating electric field established by a "source" (aggressor) conductor induces a displacement current through a stray mutual capacitance $C_m$ into a nearby "victim" conductor, thereby corrupting the signal carried by the victim line. It is one of the three dominant near-field noise coupling mechanisms in instrumentation systems, alongside inductive (magnetic) coupling and conductive (common-impedance) coupling.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PCICT205, capacitive coupling is treated under the broader module of *Electromagnetic and Electrostatic Interference in Measurement Systems*, where it is classified as a **high-impedance (electric-field) coupling mechanism** — meaning it predominantly affects circuits with high input impedance (e.g., oscilloscope probes, FET-input amplifiers, high-Z sensors).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Capacitive coupling is fundamentally a **high-frequency phenomenon** because the displacement current $i_C = C_m \dfrac{dV}{dt}$ is proportional to the rate of change of voltage. A DC signal across a coupling capacitor produces *zero* induced current in steady state, while a fast $1\,\text{V/ns}$ edge produces milliamps of crosstalk even for sub-picofarad stray capacitances.

## 1.2 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and reference values are universally referenced in capacitive coupling analysis:

- **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{F/m}$
- **Relative permittivity of FR-4 PCB:** $\varepsilon_r \approx 4.5$ (dimensionless)
- **Typical stray mutual capacitance between adjacent PCB traces:** $C_m \approx 0.5 \text{ to } 5\,\text{pF}$ for 10 cm parallel runs
- **Capacitive coupling coefficient** (dimensionless): $k_C = \dfrac{C_m}{\sqrt{C_g^{(s)} \cdot C_g^{(v)}}}$, where $C_g$ denotes the capacitance to ground of the source and victim traces respectively.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine two long, parallel water pipes (the conductors) separated by a thin rubber membrane (the dielectric of the stray capacitor).
