When you suddenly pulse high-pressure water through pipe A, the membrane flexes and pushes a small amount of water into pipe B — even though the pipes are not physically connected. The faster you pulse (higher $dV/dt$), the more water sloshes into pipe B.

In electrical terms:
- **Pipe A** = aggressor / source conductor carrying signal $V_s(t)$.
- **Rubber membrane** = stray mutual capacitance $C_m$ between the two conductors.
- **Pipe B** = victim conductor, where an unwanted noise voltage $V_n$ appears.
- **Water slosh** = displacement current $i_C$ that flows into the victim circuit's impedance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** The reason capacitive coupling troubles instrumentation is that *capacitive current must complete a loop* — it returns to its source via the **common ground** or **shield**. Without a low-impedance return path, the coupling becomes unpredictable and severe. This is the single most important practical takeaway for KTU lab and design questions.

## 1.4 Where Capacitive Coupling Appears in Instrumentation

1. **Adjacent signal traces on a PCB** carrying switching waveforms (clock, PWM).
2. **Coaxial cable shields** acting as the "second plate" of a long distributed capacitor when measurement currents return via earth ground.
3. **High-impedance transducer cables** (e.g., pH probes, piezoelectric accelerometers) picking up mains $50\,\text{Hz}$ fields.
4. **Oscilloscope probe ground leads** forming a loop that picks up capacitive noise from nearby HV lines.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Coupling — Step-by-Step Logic

The coupling process can be decomposed into four sequential physical events:

- **Step 1 — Electric Field Establishment:** The source conductor, held at potential $V_s$ with respect to a reference ground, sets up an electric field $\vec{E}$ in the surrounding dielectric medium.
- **Step 2 — Displacement Current Generation:** The time-varying $\vec{E}$ field produces a displacement current density $\vec{J_d} = \varepsilon \dfrac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t}$ through the dielectric separating the two conductors.
- **Step 3 — Current Injection into Victim:** This displacement current is collected by the victim conductor and forced to flow through the victim circuit's impedance to ground $Z_v$.
- **Step 4 — Noise Voltage Development:** By Ohm's law $V_n = I_C \cdot Z_v$, a noise voltage appears across the victim load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the coupling depends on victim impedance:** A floating victim ($Z_v \to \infty$) charges up until $V_n \to V_s$ (worst case — full transfer). A perfectly grounded victim ($Z_v = 0$) shunts the current harmlessly. This is why instrumentation inputs must be designed with *controlled, low-impedance* terminations.

## 2.2 Equivalent Circuit Model

The classic lumped-element model of capacitive coupling consists of:
- Source: ideal voltage $V_s$ in series with source impedance $R_s$.
- Mutual (stray) capacitor: $C_m$ between the two nodes.
- Victim: load impedance $Z_v$ to ground.

When $R_s \ll \dfrac{1}{\omega C_m}$ (low-impedance driver, the common case), the source end acts as a stiff voltage source, and the noise voltage simplifies to a voltage-divider expression in the victim branch.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | Displacement current through $C_m$ | $i_C(t) = C_m \dfrac{dV_s}{dt}$ | Amperes; $C_m$ in Farads |
| 2 | Induced noise voltage (general) | $V_n = \dfrac{Z_v}{Z_v + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_m}} \cdot V_s$ | Frequency domain |
| 3 | Low-frequency approximation $\left(\omega C_m Z_v \ll 1\right)$ | $V_n \approx j\omega C_m Z_v \cdot V_s$ | Linear in $\omega$ |
| 4 | High-frequency approximation $\left(\omega C_m Z_v \gg 1\right)$ | $V_n \approx V_s$ | Saturates to full source |
| 5 | Magnitude (resistive victim $Z_v = R_v$) | $\vert V_n \vert = \dfrac{\omega R_v C_m V_s}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega R_v C_m)^2}}$ | Volts RMS |
| 6 | Coupling coefficient | $k_C = \dfrac{C_m}{\sqrt{C_1 C_2}}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le k_C \le 1$ |
| 7 | Parallel-wire mutual capacitance (in air) | $C_m \approx \dfrac{\pi \varepsilon_0 \ell}{\cosh^{-1}(D/r)}$ | $\ell$ = length, $D$ = separation, $r$ = radius |
| 8 | dB coupling loss | $C_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\vert V_n \vert}{\vert V_s \vert}\right)$ | Negative dB = attenuation |
| 9 | -3 dB corner of single-pole transfer | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_v C_m}$ | Hz; onset of full coupling |
| 10 | Rise-time-limited peak noise | $V_{n,\text{peak}} \approx C_m \cdot R_v \cdot \dfrac{\Delta V_s}{\Delta t}$ | Step response approximation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Quick Rule of Thumb:** For digital edges with $10\text{–}90\%$ rise time $t_r$, the peak capacitive crosstalk on a victim line of characteristic impedance $Z_0$ is approximately $V_{n,\text{peak}} \approx \dfrac{Z_0 C_m}{2 t_r} \cdot V_s$. This single expression is the most-tested formula in KTU university exam questions on EMC.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Capacitive coupling analysis is the foundation of:
- **EMC compliance design** (CISPR-11, IEC 61000-4) for medical, automotive, and industrial instrumentation.
- **PCB layout rules** (3W rule, guard traces, ground stitching vias) to keep $C_m$ below acceptable bounds.
- **Cable shielding design** — a coaxial braid reduces $C_m$ to a few pF per metre versus several hundred pF for an unshielded twisted pair.
- **Probe selection in oscilloscopes** — $10\times$ passive probes have roughly 10× lower $C_m$ than $1\times$ probes, reducing capacitive loading.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Induced Noise Voltage for a Resistive Victim

We begin with the canonical two-conductor coupling network. Let:

- $V_s(t)$ = source (aggressor) voltage, phasor $\tilde{V}_s$.
- $R_s$ = source impedance (assumed $R_s \to 0$ for a stiff driver).
- $C_m$ = mutual stray capacitance between source and victim conductors.
- $R_v$ = victim load resistance to ground (resistive for clarity).
- $\tilde{V}_n$ = phasor of the noise voltage appearing across $R_v$.

**Step 1 — Write KCL at the victim node.**

The current flowing through the coupling capacitor $C_m$ must equal the current flowing through the victim load $R_v$ (Kirchhoff's Current Law — every current entering the node must leave it).

$$
i_{C_m} = i_{R_v}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the capacitor current in phasor form.**

For a sinusoidal excitation $V_s(t) = V_m \cos(\omega t)$, the phasor of the current flowing from source to victim through $C_m$ is:

$$
\tilde{I}_{C_m} = j \omega C_m \left( \tilde{V}_s - \tilde{V}_n \right)
$$

The bracket $\left( \tilde{V}_s - \tilde{V}_n \right)$ represents the *voltage across the capacitor* — the difference between the source node potential and the victim node potential.

**Step 3 — Express the load current in phasor form.**

The current flowing from the victim node to ground through $R_v$ is:

$$
\tilde{I}_{R_v} = \dfrac{\tilde{V}_n}{R_v}
$$

**Step 4 — Equate the two currents (KCL).**

$$
j \omega C_m \left( \tilde{V}_s - \tilde{V}_n \right) = \dfrac{\tilde{V}_n}{R_v}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve algebraically for $\tilde{V}_n$.**

Multiply both sides by $R_v$:

$$
j \omega C_m R_v \left( \tilde{V}_s - \tilde{V}_n \right) = \tilde{V}_n
$$

Expand the left side:

$$
j \omega C_m R_v \tilde{V}_s - j \omega C_m R_v \tilde{V}_n = \tilde{V}_n
$$

Collect $\tilde{V}_n$ terms on the right:

$$
j \omega C_m R_v \tilde{V}_s = \tilde{V}_n + j \omega C_m R_v \tilde{V}_n
$$

$$
j \omega C_m R_v \tilde{V}_s = \tilde{V}_n \left( 1 + j \omega C_m R_v \right)
$$

Therefore the **closed-form transfer expression** is:

$$
\tilde{V}_n = \dfrac{j \omega C_m R_v}{1 + j \omega C_m R_v} \cdot \tilde{V}_s
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This is a high-pass transfer function** with a single pole at $\omega_p = 1 / (R_v C_m)$. The same expression reappears in the KTU question bank in Section 5 — memorize it.

**Step 6 — Magnitude response.**

Taking the modulus of the complex fraction:

$$
\left\vert \tilde{V}_n \right\vert = \dfrac{\omega R_v C_m}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega R_v C_m)^2}} \cdot \left\vert \tilde{V}_s \right\vert
$$

**Step 7 — Asymptotic limits.**

- *Low frequency* $\left( \omega R_v C_m \ll 1 \right)$:

$$
\left\vert \tilde{V}_n \right\vert \approx \omega R_v C_m \cdot \left\vert \tilde{V}_s \right\vert
$$

Noise grows linearly with frequency — the classic "20 dB/decade" slope of capacitive coupling.

- *High frequency* $\left( \omega R_v C_m \gg 1 \right)$:

$$
\left\vert \tilde{V}_n \right\vert \approx \left\vert \tilde{V}_s \right\vert
$$

The capacitor becomes a near-short circuit; full source voltage transfers to the victim.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem.** A logic gate with output swing $V_s = 5\,\text{V}$ and rise time $t_r = 5\,\text{ns}$ drives a trace running $10\,\text{cm}$ parallel to a victim trace. The mutual capacitance is $C_m = 2\,\text{pF}$ and the victim load is $R_v = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$. Estimate the peak noise voltage on the victim.

**Solution.**

Step A — Compute the corner frequency:

$$
f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_v C_m} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 10^4 \times 2 \times 10^{-12}} = \dfrac{1}{4\pi \times 10^{-8}} \approx 7.96 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Hz} \approx 8\,\text{MHz}
$$

Step B — Equivalent frequency of the $5\,\text{ns}$ edge (using the rule $f \approx 0.35 / t_r$):

$$
f_{\text{edge}} = \dfrac{0.35}{5 \times 10^{-9}} = 7 \times 10^{7}\,\text{Hz} = 70\,\text{MHz}
$$

Step C — Since $f_{\text{edge}} \gg f_c$, we are in the *high-frequency regime* and the noise approximates to full transfer *for the spectral components near the edge rate*. But the dominant energy is at the edge knee. A more accurate estimate uses the rise-time formula:

$$
V_{n,\text{peak}} \approx C_m R_v \cdot \dfrac{\Delta V_s}{t_r} = (2 \times 10^{-12}) \times (10^4) \times \dfrac{5}{5 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
V_{n,\text{peak}} = 2 \times 10^{-8} \times 10^{9} = 20\,\text{V}
$$

Step D — **Saturation check.** The peak cannot exceed $V_s = 5\,\text{V}$. Our linear estimate overshoots because we are deep in saturation. The clipped peak is therefore:

$$
\boxed{V_{n,\text{peak}} \approx 5\,\text{V} \;\;(\text{fully coupled})}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examination Pitfall:** Students frequently forget that the linear formula $V_n = C_m R_v (dV/dt)$ is only valid in the small-signal, low-coupling regime. Always verify $\omega R_v C_m$ first. If it exceeds 1, the noise is *clamped* by the saturation term.

## 3.3 Time-Domain Step Response

For a step input $V_s(t) = V_0 u(t)$, the noise voltage on a resistive victim evolves as:

$$
V_n(t) = V_0 \left( 1 - e^{-t / (R_v C_m)} \right)
$$

This is the standard first-order step response — it rises from $0$ to $V_0$ with time constant $\tau = R_v C_m$. At $t = \tau$, the noise has reached $63.2\%$ of $V_0$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Physical-to-Electrical Mapping (Block Diagram)

The diagram below maps the physical geometry of two parallel conductors to the equivalent lumped circuit used in capacitive coupling analysis. Each block represents a physically meaningful element of the coupling path.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcDriver[BLOCK A: Source Driver V sub s with source impedance R sub s]:::aggressor --> nodeA[NODE A: Aggressor Conductor at potential V s]
    nodeA --> capStray[BLOCK B: Stray Mutual Capacitance C sub m]:::coupler
    capStray --> nodeB[NODE B: Victim Conductor picks up noise V n]
    nodeB --> loadVictim[BLOCK C: Victim Load Impedance Z sub v]:::victim
    loadVictim --> gnd[BLOCK D: Common Return Ground Reference]:::ground
    nodeA -. returns via .-> gnd
    nodeB -. displacement current closes through .-> gnd

    classDef aggressor fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#b30000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef victim fill:#e5f0ff,stroke:#004080,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef coupler fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#806600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ground fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#404040,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **red block** is the aggressor — a stiff voltage source with low output impedance.
- The **yellow block** is the parasitic element — the only physical bridge between the two circuits.
- The **blue block** is the victim load — the place where the interference is observed.
- The **grey block** is the common return path — without it, displacement current has nowhere to flow.

## 4.2 Functional Flow of Coupling Energy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STAGE 1: Source voltage V sub s changes with time] --> B[STAGE 2: Time varying electric field E established in surrounding dielectric]
    B --> C[STAGE 3: Displacement current density J sub d equals epsilon times dE by dt]
    C --> D[STAGE 4: Displacement current i sub C equals C sub m times dV sub s by dt injected into victim node]
    D --> E[STAGE 5: Current flows through victim impedance Z sub v to ground]
    E --> F[STAGE 6: Noise voltage V sub n equals i sub C times Z sub v appears across victim]
    F --> G[STAGE 7: If V sub n is significant, victim signal to noise ratio degrades]

    A -. mitigation: .-> M1[Reduce dV sub s by dt: slower edges]
    B -. mitigation: .-> M2[Increase physical separation: reduce C sub m]
    C -. mitigation: .-> M3[Insert grounded guard trace between lines]
    D -. mitigation: .-> M4[Use shielded cable with braid tied to ground]
    E -. mitigation: .-> M5[Lower victim impedance Z sub v]
    F -. mitigation: .-> M6[Use differential signalling to cancel common mode]
    G -. mitigation: .-> M7[Add filtering at victim input]
```

## 4.3 Frequency-Response Characteristic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    freqAxis[LOG FREQUENCY AXIS omega increasing to the right] --> lowBand[LOW FREQUENCY BAND: 20 dB per decade rise of V sub n with omega]
    lowBand --> cornerBand[CORNER FREQUENCY omega sub p equals 1 over R sub v C sub m: 3 dB transition point]
    cornerBand --> highBand[HIGH FREQUENCY BAND: noise plateaus at full V sub s saturation]
    highBand --> result[FINAL OUTCOME: V sub n magnitude approaches V sub s magnitude]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The 20 dB/decade low-frequency slope means that for every decade increase in frequency, the capacitive crosstalk voltage *grows by a factor of ten*. This is why instrumentation dealing with fast edges (digital clocks, switching power supplies) suffers disproportionately from capacitive coupling, while low-frequency sensor signals (thermocouples, strain gauges at kHz rates) are largely immune.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the $j$ operator** in phasor expressions — losing 2 marks in Part B derivations.
> 2. **Confusing capacitive (electric-field) coupling with inductive (magnetic-field) coupling** — capacitive is solved by *shorting* the victim node to ground; inductive is solved by *opening* the loop. Mixing these up is the most common conceptual error.
> 3. **Using $C_m = 1 / (\omega X_c)$ incorrectly** when the question demands a phasor transfer function — always state the transfer function *first*, then substitute.
> 4. **Ignoring the saturation limit** in step-response problems — the linear estimate $V_n = C_m R_v (dV/dt)$ can exceed $V_s$, which is physically impossible.
> 5. **Units in numerical problems** — $C_m$ in pF must be converted to Farads **before** any multiplication; examiners specifically test this conversion.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model]** 
Define **capacitive (electrostatic) coupling**. State two practical situations in instrumentation systems where it dominates, and one design technique to suppress it.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Capacitive or electrostatic coupling is the undesired transfer of an AC voltage from one conductor (the source) to another (the victim) through a stray mutual capacitance $C_m$ that exists between them, owing to the time-varying electric field of the source.

**Practical situations (1 mark each, total 1 mark, any two):**
- Adjacent PCB traces carrying high-frequency switching signals coupling into low-level analog signal traces.
- Unshielded high-impedance transducer cables (e.g., pH, ECG) picking up interference from nearby mains wiring.

**Suppression technique (1 mark):** Insert a grounded *guard trace* or shielding conductor between the aggressor and victim, which diverts the displacement current to ground before it reaches the victim. *(Acceptable alternatives: lowering victim impedance, using differential signalling, increasing physical separation, adding a low-pass RC filter at the victim input.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model]**
A sinusoidal source $V_s = 10 \sin(2\pi \times 10^6 t)\,\text{V}$ is coupled to a victim circuit through $C_m = 5\,\text{pF}$. The victim load is $R_v = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$ to ground. Compute the magnitude of the induced noise voltage, and comment on whether the system is in the low- or high-frequency regime.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — Angular frequency:** $\omega = 2\pi \times 10^6 = 6.283 \times 10^6\,\text{rad/s}$

**Step 2 — Compute $\omega R_v C_m$:**
$$
\omega R_v C_m = (6.283 \times 10^6)(10^3)(5 \times 10^{-12}) = 3.142 \times 10^{-2} = 0.0314
$$

**Step 3 — Since $\omega R_v C_m \ll 1$, apply the low-frequency approximation (1 mark):**
$$
\vert V_n \vert \approx \omega R_v C_m \cdot V_s = 0.0314 \times 10 = 0.314\,\text{V (peak)}
$$

**Step 4 — Comment (1 mark):** The system is in the **low-frequency regime** because $\omega R_v C_m = 0.0314 \ll 1$, meaning the coupling is weak and grows linearly with frequency.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme, ESE Module Choice]**

**(a)** With the help of an equivalent circuit, derive the expression for the noise voltage $V_n$ induced on a victim conductor of load impedance $R_v$ when an aggressor conductor carrying sinusoidal voltage $V_s$ couples through a stray mutual capacitance $C_m$. Clearly state the low-frequency and high-frequency approximations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A clock trace of $3.3\,\text{V}$ amplitude, $50\,\text{MHz}$ fundamental, runs parallel to a sensitive analog line for $8\,\text{cm}$. The mutual capacitance is $C_m = 1.5\,\text{pF}$, and the analog line is terminated to ground by $R_v = 50\,\text{k}\Omega$. Calculate: (i) the corner frequency $f_c$, (ii) the noise voltage at the clock fundamental, and (iii) the dB coupling loss. Comment on the severity of the interference. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

1. Draw the equivalent circuit: source $V_s$ in series with $C_m$ driving the parallel combination of $R_v$ to ground. *(1 mark)*
2. Apply KCL at the victim node:
$$
j\omega C_m (V_s - V_n) = \dfrac{V_n}{R_v}
$$
*(1 mark)*
3. Rearrange and solve:
$$
V_n = \dfrac{j\omega C_m R_v}{1 + j\omega C_m R_v} \cdot V_s
$$
*(2 marks)*
4. State the magnitude:
$$
\vert V_n \vert = \dfrac{\omega R_v C_m}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega R_v C_m)^2}} \cdot \vert V_s \vert
$$
*(1 mark)*
5. State the two asymptotic cases. *(2 marks)*
   - *Low frequency* $\left( \omega R_v C_m \ll 1 \right)$: $\vert V_n \vert \approx \omega R_v C_m \vert V_s \vert$ — grows 20 dB per decade.
   - *High frequency* $\left( \omega R_v C_m \gg 1 \right)$: $\vert V_n \vert \approx \vert V_s \vert$ — full coupling, saturated.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Corner frequency (2 marks):**
$$
f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_v C_m} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 5 \times 10^4 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-12}} = \dfrac{1}{4.712 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 2.122 \times 10^6\,\text{Hz} \approx 2.12\,\text{MHz}
$$

**(ii) Noise voltage at $f = 50\,\text{MHz}$ (3 marks):**

Compute the dimensionless product first:
$$
\omega R_v C_m = 2\pi f \cdot R_v \cdot C_m = (2\pi \times 50 \times 10^6)(5 \times 10^4)(1.5 \times 10^{-12})
$$
$$
= (3.142 \times 10^8)(7.5 \times 10^{-8}) = 23.56
$$

Apply the transfer magnitude:
$$
\vert V_n \vert = \dfrac{23.56}{\sqrt{1 + 23.56^2}} \times 3.3 = \dfrac{23.56}{23.58} \times 3.3 \approx 0.999 \times 3.3 \approx 3.297\,\text{V}
$$

**(iii) dB coupling loss (2 marks):**
$$
C_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\left( \dfrac{3.297}{3.3} \right) = 20 \log_{10}(0.9991) \approx -0.0078\,\text{dB}
$$

**Comment (extra credit remark, not in marks):** The interference is **catastrophic** — essentially 100\% of the clock voltage is coupled into the analog line because $f \gg f_c$. Mitigation: add a series ferrite, increase trace spacing, or insert a grounded guard trace.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Module Internal Choice**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the physical origin of stray mutual capacitance between two parallel conductors. Derive an expression for the mutual capacitance of two parallel cylindrical wires of radius $r$ separated by centre-to-centre distance $D$ in air, using the method of images or by direct application of Gauss's law. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two parallel wires of radius $r = 0.5\,\text{mm}$ run for $\ell = 50\,\text{m}$ with centre-to-centre separation $D = 5\,\text{cm}$ in free space. The source end carries a $400\,\text{V}$ AC signal at $f = 50\,\text{Hz}$ and source impedance $R_s = 10\,\Omega$. The victim end is terminated into a high-impedance measuring instrument of $R_v = 1\,\text{M}\Omega$. Compute: (i) the mutual capacitance $C_m$, (ii) the induced noise voltage magnitude, and (iii) whether the measurement will be corrupted by capacitive interference. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

**Part (a) — Physical origin and derivation (7 marks):**

1. **Physical origin (2 marks):** Any two conductors separated by a dielectric (even air) form the plates of a *distributed capacitor*. The electric field lines emanating from conductor 1 terminate partly on conductor 2, and the surface charge on conductor 2 per unit potential of conductor 1 defines $C_m$. In a PCB, $C_m$ is increased by the higher $\varepsilon_r$ of the substrate and by proximity.
2. **Derivation setup (2 marks):** Place conductor 1 (radius $r$) at origin and conductor 2 (radius $r$) at $x = D$. For $D \gg r$, neglect proximity distortion and use the line-charge approximation. The potential at distance $D$ from a line charge $\lambda$ is $V = \dfrac{\lambda}{2\pi \varepsilon_0} \ln(D/r)$.
3. **Apply Gauss's law / superposition (2 marks):** Equating the potential of conductor 2 (which is zero, being the victim reference for coupling purposes) to the sum of contributions from line charges $+\lambda$ on conductor 1 and $-\lambda$ on conductor 2 yields:

$$
C_m \approx \dfrac{\pi \varepsilon_0 \ell}{\cosh^{-1}(D/r)} \;\;\text{for} \; D \gg r
$$

4. **Final statement (1 mark):** For $D/r \gg 1$, $\cosh^{-1}(D/r) \approx \ln(2D/r)$, so:

$$
C_m \approx \dfrac{\pi \varepsilon_0 \ell}{\ln(2D/r)}
$$

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Mutual capacitance (3 marks):**

Compute $\cosh^{-1}(D/r) = \cosh^{-1}(50/0.5) = \cosh^{-1}(100)$.

Using $\cosh^{-1}(x) \approx \ln(2x) = \ln(200) = 5.298$ for large $x$:

$$
C_m = \dfrac{\pi \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 50}{5.298} = \dfrac{1.391 \times 10^{-9}}{5.298} \approx 2.625 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{F} \approx 263\,\text{pF}
$$

**(ii) Induced noise voltage (3 marks):**

Compute the dimensionless product:
$$
\omega R_v C_m = 2\pi \times 50 \times 10^6 \times 2.625 \times 10^{-10} = 8.25 \times 10^{-2} \approx 0.0825
$$

Since $\omega R_v C_m \ll 1$, low-frequency approximation applies:
$$
\vert V_n \vert \approx \omega R_v C_m \cdot V_s = 0.0825 \times 400 = 33\,\text{V}
$$

**(iii) Severity comment (1 mark):**

A 33 V noise on a 1 MΩ victim input is severe — the measurement is completely corrupted. The mitigation: use a coaxial cable with the shield tied to the source-end ground, reducing $C_m$ by a factor of 100 or more and lowering the noise to under 0.5 V.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Capacitive coupling transfers AC noise via stray mutual capacitance $C_m$ between conductors.
- **Master Formula (must memorize):** $\tilde{V}_n = \dfrac{j\omega C_m R_v}{1 + j\omega C_m R_v} \tilde{V}_s$
- **Low-frequency regime** $\left( \omega R_v C_m \ll 1 \right)$: $\vert V_n \vert \approx \omega R_v C_m \vert V_s \vert$ — 20 dB/decade slope.
- **High-frequency regime** $\left( \omega R_v C_m \gg 1 \right)$: $\vert V_n \vert \approx \vert V_s \vert$ — full coupling, saturated.
- **Corner frequency:** $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_v C_m}$ — separates low- and high-frequency behaviour.
- **Physical constants:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{F/m}$; PCB FR-4 has $\varepsilon_r \approx 4.5$.
- **Mutual capacitance of two parallel wires in air:** $C_m \approx \dfrac{\pi \varepsilon_0 \ell}{\cosh^{-1}(D/r)} \approx \dfrac{\pi \varepsilon_0 \ell}{\ln(2D/r)}$ for $D \gg r$.
- **dB coupling loss:** $C_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\left( \dfrac{\vert V_n \vert}{\vert V_s \vert} \right)$.
- **Step-response peak:** $V_{n,\text{peak}} = C_m R_v \dfrac{\Delta V_s}{t_r}$ (linear approximation, valid only when $\omega R_v C_m \ll 1$).
- **Engineering mitigations (in priority order):**
  1. Increase physical separation between conductors.
  2. Insert a grounded guard trace or shield.
  3. Lower the victim input impedance.
  4. Slow down the aggressor edge rate.
  5. Use differential signalling to reject common-mode capacitive noise.
- **Distinguishing feature:** Capacitive coupling is a **high-impedance, electric-field phenomenon** — solved by *shorting* the victim to ground, in contrast to inductive coupling which is solved by *opening* the loop.
- **Most-tested KTU question type:** "Derive the transfer function of capacitive coupling and compute the noise voltage for given $C_m$, $R_v$, and $V_s$ values." Practice the phasor arithmetic until it is second nature.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Noise Due to Multiple Earths
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT205 - BASIC OF INSTRUMENTATION ENGINEERING (INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Noise Due to Multiple Earths

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Multiple Earths** (also called *Multi-point Grounding*) refer to a wiring configuration in an instrumentation or measurement system where two or more distinct points of the signal circuit are independently tied to the earth (ground) reference at physically separated locations.

**Noise Due to Multiple Earths** is the unwanted spurious voltage that appears superimposed on the measured signal when circulating currents flow through the unintended closed conductive loops formed by these multiple earth connections and the signal cable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – Sources of Noise):**
> The KTU 2024 scheme categorizes noise in instrumentation systems into two broad classes:
> 1. **Inherent Noise** – generated within the transducer / circuit (thermal, shot, flicker).
> 2. **Acquired / External Noise** – picked up from the environment. *Noise due to multiple earths falls squarely in this category*, alongside electrostatic and electromagnetic interference.

### The Ground Loop — The Root Cause

When two separated points A and B of a measurement circuit are both connected to "earth", a **ground loop** is created. The loop is physically closed by:

$$\text{Loop} = \text{Source ground} \rightarrow \text{Earth soil} \rightarrow \text{Load ground} \rightarrow \text{Load cable} \rightarrow \text{Source cable}$$

If the two earth points happen to be at *different electrical potentials* $V_A \neq V_B$, a current $i_g$ circulates through this loop. This current produces an IR-drop across the cable resistance and the earth-return path that is algebraically added to the desired signal — hence, **noise**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Earth Potential Difference (EPD):**
> The voltage $V_{AB} = V_A - V_B$ that exists between two physically separated earth electrodes due to large return currents of power systems, leakage currents, chemical action in soil, or unbalanced three-phase loads.
> Typical magnitudes: **$\mathbf{1\ \text{mV}\ \text{to}\ 100\ \text{V}}$**; typical frequency: **$\mathbf{50\ \text{Hz}}$** power-line fundamental and its harmonics.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Plumbing and the Two Drains"

Imagine two water tanks (representing two devices) connected to *the same drain pipe* (representing earth), but the drain pipe has a **long horizontal stretch** between the two connection points.

- If the drain pipe were a *perfect conductor* with **zero resistance**, both drains would settle at the same water level (potential). No water would flow sideways between the tanks through the pipe.
- In reality, the drain pipe has resistance. Heavy water currents from elsewhere (a power substation, a factory machine) flowing through the same soil cause a **pressure difference** between the two drain points.
- Now the two tanks, although "grounded" to the same earth, sit at *different levels*. Water starts *sloshing between the two tanks* through the pipe — exactly the **ground-loop current**.
- This extra sloshing is the **noise**, added to whatever useful flow you wanted to measure between the tanks.

> [!TIP]
> **Student Takeaway:** Grounding more than once is *not* automatically safer — it can introduce a closed loop that the environment can drive with stray currents.

---

## 1.3 Why Multiple Earths Are So Common (and So Harmful)

In industrial plants, instrumentation and power cabling are routinely run in parallel trays. Engineers add earths for *safety* (fault current clearance) without realizing they have inadvertently created an EMC problem. The KTU examiner expects you to clearly list **when** the problem becomes acute:

- Signal levels are **low** ($< 1\ \text{V}$) — typical of thermocouples, strain gauges, pH probes.
- Cable lengths are **long** ($> 10\ \text{m}$).
- The plant has **heavy power equipment** (motors, drives, welding sets) drawing large earth-return currents.
- The two earth electrodes are **physically separated** (different building columns, different floors, different substations).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Equivalent Circuit of a Two-Earth System

Consider a signal source with internal resistance $R_s$ connected to a load/amplifier with input resistance $R_L$ through a cable whose two conductors have resistances $R_{c1}$ and $R_{c2}$ (including shield resistance if shield is also earthed). The source side is earthed at point A and the load side at point B.

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
|--------|---------|---------------|
| $V_s$ | Useful signal voltage | $\mu\text{V}\ \text{to}\ \text{V}$ |
| $V_g$ | Earth Potential Difference between A and B | $\mathbf{1\ \text{mV}\ \text{to}\ 100\ \text{V}}$ |
| $R_s$ | Source internal resistance | $\Omega\ \text{to}\ \text{M}\Omega$ |
| $R_L$ | Load / amplifier input resistance | $\text{k}\Omega\ \text{to}\ \text{M}\Omega$ |
| $R_{c1},\ R_{c2}$ | Cable conductor resistances | $0.1\ \Omega\ \text{to}\ 10\ \Omega$ |
| $R_e$ | Earth-electrode-to-soil resistance at A & B | $1\ \Omega\ \text{to}\ 50\ \Omega$ |
| $R_{sh}$ | Cable shield resistance (if shield also earthed) | $\text{m}\Omega\ \text{to}\ \Omega$ |
| $R_{sg}$ | Soil resistance between A and B | $0.1\ \Omega\ \text{to}\ 10\ \Omega$ |

The Thevenin/Norton equivalent driving the loop is $V_g$ in series with the loop resistance $R_{loop}$.

---

## 2.2 Deriving the Ground-Loop Noise Voltage at the Load

Apply **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** around the ground loop:

$$V_g = i_g\,(R_{e,A} + R_{e,B} + R_{sg} + R_{sh})$$

where $R_{e,A},\ R_{e,B}$ are the two earth-electrode resistances and $R_{sg}$ is the bulk-soil resistance between electrodes A and B.

This current $i_g$ flowing through the shield (or through the signal conductor, if the shield is also used as a return path) produces a noise voltage that appears *in series* with the signal at the load input:

$$V_{n} = i_g \cdot R_{c,return}$$

If the return path is via the cable shield only:

$$V_n = V_g \cdot \frac{R_{c,return}}{R_{c,return} + R_{e,A} + R_{e,B} + R_{sg}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common-Mode to Differential-Mode Conversion**
> Strictly speaking, $V_g$ first appears as a **common-mode voltage** $V_{cm}$ between the two signal conductors and earth. Any imbalance between the two conductor impedances converts a fraction of $V_{cm}$ into a **differential-mode** voltage $V_{d}$, which the amplifier cannot reject.
> $$V_{d,\text{noise}} = V_{cm} \cdot \left(\frac{\Delta Z}{Z}\right)$$
> This is why *twisted-pair, balanced lines* with matched impedances are so effective.

---

## 2.3 Why Floating the Source Does NOT Fully Help

Sometimes a technician "fixes" the noise by disconnecting the source-side earth, leaving only the load earth. This is called a **floating source**. It breaks the DC/low-frequency ground loop — but at **higher frequencies** the capacitance between the source and earth ($C_{p}$) re-closes the loop, and at high source impedance, electrostatic pickup dominates.

A fully floating source also raises a **safety** concern: a fault could raise the entire source to line potential. KTU examiners often deduct marks for ignoring the safety aspect.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning / When to Use |
|---|----------|----------------------|
| 1 | $i_g = \dfrac{V_g}{R_{e,A} + R_{e,B} + R_{sg} + R_{sh}}$ | Ground-loop circulating current |
| 2 | $V_n = i_g \cdot R_{c,return}$ | Noise voltage in series with signal |
| 3 | $V_{cm} = V_g$ | Common-mode voltage impressed on cable |
| 4 | $V_{d} = V_{cm}\cdot \dfrac{\Delta Z}{Z}$ | Differential-mode noise after imbalance |
| 5 | $\text{CMRR (dB)} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio of amplifier |
| 6 | $V_{d,\text{out}} = \dfrac{V_{cm}}{\text{CMRR}}\cdot A_d$ | Residual noise after differential amp |
| 7 | $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C_p}$ | Stray capacitance reactance — sets HF break |
| 8 | $V_n^{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{4 k_B T R\, \Delta f}$ | Thermal noise (added to ground noise) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants used:**
> $k_B = \mathbf{1.38\times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}}$ — Boltzmann constant
> $T$ — absolute temperature in K

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process Industry:** pH meters in chemical plants pick up $50\ \text{Hz}$ hum precisely because of multi-earth loops between the electrode head and the controller cubicle.
- **Biomedical:** ECG/EEG machines must have isolated front-ends (IEC 60601) because the patient and the recording instrument are independently earthed.
- **Audio:** Guitar pickups and stage amplifiers hum audibly when connected through different mains earths.
- **Automotive Sensors:** Wheel-speed sensors share chassis ground with ECU — ground-shift during cranking is a classic noise source.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the Ground-Loop Noise Voltage

We consider the standard two-earth circuit. Let the cable consist of two conductors (signal + return) plus an optional shield. The source $V_s$ with resistance $R_s$ sits at earth point A; the amplifier with input resistance $R_L$ sits at earth point B.

**Step 1 — Identify the noise source.**
The two physically separated earths A and B are not at the same potential. Thevenin's theorem looking *into* the earth path from the cable end gives:

$$V_g = V_A - V_B \quad \text{(earth potential difference, EPD)}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the loop resistance.**
The closed loop is:

Source-earth(A) → soil → earth(B) → cable shield / return conductor → back to A.

The total loop resistance is the series sum of:

$$R_{loop} = R_{e,A} + R_{e,B} + R_{sg} + R_{sh}$$

where:
- $R_{e,A}$ — resistance of electrode A to surrounding soil
- $R_{e,B}$ — resistance of electrode B to surrounding soil
- $R_{sg}$ — bulk soil resistance between A and B
- $R_{sh}$ — resistance of the cable shield / return conductor

**Step 3 — Compute the loop current using Ohm's Law.**
Apply Ohm's law to the loop:

$$i_g = \frac{V_g}{R_{loop}} = \frac{V_g}{R_{e,A} + R_{e,B} + R_{sg} + R_{sh}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the noise voltage picked up by the amplifier.**
The current $i_g$ flowing through the cable shield / return conductor of resistance $R_{sh}$ produces a series voltage drop. By Ohm's law:

$$V_{n} = i_g \cdot R_{sh}$$

Substituting $i_g$:

$$\boxed{\,V_{n} = V_g \cdot \frac{R_{sh}}{R_{sh} + R_{e,A} + R_{e,B} + R_{sg}}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**
This is a **voltage divider** in disguise. If the cable-shield resistance is small compared to the soil and electrode resistances (typical case), then $V_n \ll V_g$ and the noise is small. However, if the cable is long and the electrodes are close to the surface (low $R_{e,A}, R_{e,B}$), a significant fraction of $V_g$ appears at the amplifier input.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** Notice that the *useful* signal $V_s$ flows through $R_s$ and the signal conductor and is *attenuated* by the same divider when measured at $R_L$. So the **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** becomes:
> $$\text{SNR} = \frac{V_s}{V_n}\cdot \frac{R_L}{R_L + R_s + R_c}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Numerical)

**Problem:** A thermocouple instrument is installed in a plant. The thermocouple lead resistance (signal conductor) is $R_{c1} = 5\ \Omega$; the cable-shield resistance is $R_{sh} = 2\ \Omega$. Source resistance $R_s = 10\ \Omega$. The amplifier input resistance $R_L = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$. Earth-electrode resistances $R_{e,A} = R_{e,B} = 5\ \Omega$ each, and soil resistance $R_{sg} = 3\ \Omega$. The measured earth potential difference between the two earth points is $V_g = 1.5\ \text{V}$ (rms, $50\ \text{Hz}$).

Compute: (a) the ground-loop current, (b) the noise voltage picked up by the amplifier, and (c) the percentage of $V_g$ that appears as noise.

**Solution.**

**(a) Loop current:**
$$R_{loop} = R_{e,A} + R_{e,B} + R_{sg} + R_{sh}$$
$$R_{loop} = 5 + 5 + 3 + 2 = 15\ \Omega$$
$$i_g = \frac{V_g}{R_{loop}} = \frac{1.5}{15} = 0.1\ \text{A}$$

**(b) Noise voltage at amplifier input:**
$$V_{n} = i_g \cdot R_{sh} = 0.1 \times 2 = 0.2\ \text{V (rms)}$$

**(c) Percentage of $V_g$ appearing as noise:**
$$\frac{V_n}{V_g} = \frac{0.2}{1.5} = 13.33\%$$

For a thermocouple signal of, say, $40\ \mu\text{V}/^{\circ}\text{C}$ measuring $100^{\circ}\text{C}$, the actual signal is only $4\ \text{mV}$. A noise of $200\ \text{mV}$ would completely swamp it! This is why **single-point grounding** is mandatory for low-level instrumentation.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common-Mode to Differential-Mode Conversion

| Source of Imbalance | Causes | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Different cable conductor resistances | Different lengths, different gauges | Use **identical, twisted-pair** conductors |
| Loose / corroded terminations | Contact resistance varies | Use **crimp / solder** with strain relief |
| Single-ended vs. differential amplifier input | Different input impedances | Use **instrumentation amplifier** with $\text{CMRR} > 80\ \text{dB}$ |
| Capacitive unbalance (proximity to ground plane) | Asymmetric routing | **Symmetric routing**, both conductors equidistant from tray |

The exact differential noise is:

$$V_d = V_{cm}\cdot\left(\frac{Z_1 - Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2}\right)$$

where $Z_1$ and $Z_2$ are the impedances of the two signal conductors to earth. The bracketed term is called the **unbalance factor**.

---

## 3.4 Solution Strategies — Engineering Counter-Measures

1. **Single-Point Grounding:** Connect the cable shield to earth at **only one end** (the load/amplifier end is preferred). This breaks the DC loop.
2. **Isolation Amplifier:** Use transformer- or optically-coupled isolation (e.g., ISO124, AD202) providing $> 1\ \text{kV}$ galvanic isolation.
3. **Differential Amplifier with High CMRR:** An instrumentation amp rejects the common-mode component and amplifies only the differential.
4. **Floating Power Supply:** Power the source using batteries or isolated DC-DC converters so the source has no reference to earth.
5. **Guard Shield / Driven Shield:** A driven shield held at signal potential removes capacitive coupling without creating a DC loop.
6. **Common-Mode Choke:** A toroidal ferrite around the cable attenuates HF common-mode currents.
7. **Use of Twisted Pair:** Reduces magnetic pickup AND balances the loop impedance.

> [!WARNING]
> **Safety vs. EMC trade-off:** A single-point ground at the load end satisfies EMC but may violate safety codes that demand the chassis be earthed for fault clearance. The professional solution is: **single-point signal ground**, plus a **safety/chassis earth** at every enclosure, plus **isolation** where needed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Two-Earth Ground Loop

The following Mermaid diagram maps the physical-to-electrical conversion of a ground loop and shows how each counter-measure interrupts the loop at a different node.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS[Signal Source with Source Resistance Rs]
    nodeA[Earth Electrode A]
    nodeSoil[Soil Bulk Resistance Rsg and Electrode Re]
    nodeB[Earth Electrode B]
    nodeC[Cable Shield with Resistance Rsh]
    nodeAmp[Amplifier with Input RL]
    nodeVg[Earth Potential Difference Vg between A and B]
    nodeCM[Common Mode Voltage Vcm impressed on Cable]
    nodeConv[Imbalance Delta Z over Z converts Vcm to Vd]
    nodeNd[Differential Mode Noise Vd added to Vs at Amplifier]
    nodeFix1[Solution 1 Single Point Earth at Amplifier End Only]
    nodeFix2[Solution 2 Isolation Amplifier breaks Galvanic Loop]
    nodeFix3[Solution 3 Instrumentation Amplifier with High CMRR]
    nodeFix4[Solution 4 Driven Guard Shield around Inner Pair]

    nodeS --> nodeA
    nodeA --> nodeSoil
    nodeSoil --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeAmp
    nodeVg --> nodeCM
    nodeCM --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeConv
    nodeConv --> nodeNd
    nodeAmp --> nodeNd

    nodeFix1 -. interrupts .-> nodeA
    nodeFix2 -. interrupts .-> nodeC
    nodeFix3 -. rejects at .-> nodeNd
    nodeFix4 -. shields .-> nodeC
```

## 4.2 Equivalent Circuit of the Ground Loop (Functional Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeVs[Useful Signal Source Vs internal resistance Rs]
    node1[Conductor Resistance Rc1]
    node2[Conductor Resistance Rc2]
    nodeRsh[Cable Shield Resistance Rsh]
    nodeRA[Earth Electrode Resistance at A equals ReA]
    nodeRB[Earth Electrode Resistance at B equals ReB]
    nodeRsg[Soil Resistance Rsg]
    nodeVg[Earth Potential Difference Vg Source]
    nodeRL[Amplifier Input Resistance RL]
    nodeIn[Noise Voltage Vn appears here in series with Vs]

    nodeVs --- node1
    node1 --- nodeRL
    nodeRL --- node2
    node2 --- nodeVs
    nodeRsh --- nodeRA
    nodeRA --- nodeVg
    nodeVg --- nodeRsg
    nodeRsg --- nodeRB
    nodeRB --- nodeRsh
    nodeIn --- nodeRL
```

## 4.3 Functional Decision Flowchart — How to Choose a Counter-Measure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Start Multi Earth Noise Problem Detected]
    q1{Signal level less than 1 V?}
    q2{Cable length greater than 10 m?}
    q3{Both ends already earthed for safety?}
    s1[Use Single Point Earth at Load End Only]
    s2[Add Twisted Pair and Balanced Routing]
    s3[Use Instrumentation Amplifier with CMRR greater than 80 dB]
    s4[Insert Isolation Amplifier ISO124 type]
    s5[Use Driven Guard Shield plus Battery Powered Source]
    done[Verified Solution]

    start --> q1
    q1 -- Yes --> q2
    q1 -- No --> s2
    q2 -- Yes --> q3
    q2 -- No --> s2
    q3 -- Yes --> s4
    q3 -- No --> s1
    s1 --> s2
    s2 --> s3
    s3 --> s5
    s4 --> s3
    s5 --> done
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**What is meant by "noise due to multiple earths" in an instrumentation system? How does it arise?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Noise due to multiple earths is the unwanted voltage that appears superimposed on a measurement signal when the circuit is connected to earth at **more than one physically separated point**.

It arises because (i) different earth points are generally at *different potentials* (Earth Potential Difference, EPD) due to large return currents of power equipment flowing through finite-resistance soil, and (ii) these separated earths together with the signal cable form a **closed conductive loop** (ground loop). The EPD drives a circulating current through this loop, and the resulting IR drops across the cable impedances add to the signal as noise.

(1 mark — definition; 1 mark — explanation of EPD; 1 mark — closed loop / circulating current.)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4]`

**List any three methods to reduce noise due to multiple earths.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Single-point grounding** of the signal circuit (signal return is tied to earth at only the amplifier end).
2. **Isolation amplifier** (transformer or optical) providing galvanic isolation between source and load.
3. **Differential (instrumentation) amplifier** with high CMRR (≥ 80 dB) to reject common-mode earth noise.

(Other acceptable answers: twisted-pair cable, driven guard shield, common-mode choke, floating battery-powered source.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** Answer **ONE** of the two questions. Each has sub-parts (a) and (b), 7 marks each.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 4]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the phenomenon of noise generation due to multiple earths in an instrumentation system. Derive the expression for the noise voltage picked up at the amplifier input.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A thermocouple with source resistance $R_s = 20\ \Omega$ is connected to an amplifier of input resistance $R_L = 100\ \text{k}\Omega$ through a 50 m long cable. The cable shield resistance is $R_{sh} = 4\ \Omega$. Earth-electrode resistances at both ends are $R_{e,A} = R_{e,B} = 10\ \Omega$ and the soil resistance between them is $R_{sg} = 6\ \Omega$. The earth potential difference measured between the two earth points is $V_g = 0.9\ \text{V}$ (rms, $50\ \text{Hz}$). Compute: (i) the ground-loop current, (ii) the noise voltage at the amplifier input, and (iii) the noise power delivered to the amplifier input.  **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part A(a)

- **Diagram (3 marks):** Draw the source, two cable conductors, two earth electrodes A and B with $V_g$ source between them, the shield, and the amplifier. Label $R_{e,A},\ R_{e,B},\ R_{sg},\ R_{sh}$. (Use the equivalent circuit from Section 4.2 of these notes.)

- **Derivation (4 marks):** Identify the closed loop. Apply KVL:
$$V_g = i_g(R_{e,A} + R_{e,B} + R_{sg} + R_{sh})$$
$$i_g = \frac{V_g}{R_{e,A} + R_{e,B} + R_{sg} + R_{sh}}$$
The noise voltage picked up at the amplifier is the IR drop on the shield/return conductor:
$$\boxed{V_n = i_g \cdot R_{sh} = V_g \cdot \frac{R_{sh}}{R_{e,A} + R_{e,B} + R_{sg} + R_{sh}}}$$

- **Conclusion (extra credit):** Note that the noise is essentially the EPD scaled by a voltage-divider ratio determined by the loop impedances.

#### Model Solution — Part A(b)

**(i) Loop current:**
$$R_{loop} = 10 + 10 + 6 + 4 = 30\ \Omega$$
$$i_g = \frac{0.9}{30} = 0.03\ \text{A} = 30\ \text{mA}$$

**(ii) Noise voltage:**
$$V_n = i_g \cdot R_{sh} = 0.03 \times 4 = 0.12\ \text{V (rms)}$$

**(iii) Noise power delivered to amplifier input:**
$$P_n = \frac{V_n^2}{R_L} = \frac{(0.12)^2}{100\,000} = 1.44 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{W} = 144\ \text{nW}$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Module 4]`

**(a)** What is a ground loop? Explain the conversion of common-mode noise into differential-mode noise due to multiple earths.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **four** practical methods to eliminate or minimize noise caused by multiple earths in an instrumentation system. Justify the use of a **single-point ground** with the help of a circuit.  **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part B(a)

- **Ground loop definition (2 marks):** A ground loop is an unwanted closed conductive path formed when a circuit is connected to earth at two or more separated points, allowing circulating current to flow driven by the Earth Potential Difference.

- **CM-to-DM conversion (5 marks):** The EPD appears between the two signal conductors and earth as a **common-mode voltage** $V_{cm}$. An ideal differential amplifier rejects $V_{cm}$ completely. However, any imbalance between the two conductor impedances (different lengths, terminations, or capacitive coupling to ground) creates an **unbalance factor**:
$$\beta = \frac{Z_1 - Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2}$$
A fraction $\beta \cdot V_{cm}$ is converted into a **differential-mode voltage**:
$$V_d = V_{cm}\cdot\frac{Z_1 - Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2}$$
This $V_d$ appears in series with the useful signal and is amplified, producing noise.

#### Model Solution — Part B(b)

**Four practical methods (4 marks, 1 each):**

1. **Single-point ground** — Connect the signal-return to earth only at the amplifier end. This breaks the DC/low-frequency ground loop.
2. **Isolation amplifier** — Provides galvanic isolation (1 kV or more) so the source and load share no common conductor.
3. **Instrumentation amplifier with high CMRR** — Rejects common-mode component that cannot be removed by grounding alone.
4. **Twisted-pair, balanced cable** with shield earthed at one end only — Reduces magnetic pickup and equalizes conductor impedances.

**Justification of single-point ground (3 marks):**
In a two-earth system, any EPD drives a current $i_g$ through the loop. If we lift the earth at point A, the loop is broken and $i_g \to 0$. The noise voltage $V_n = i_g R_{sh}$ therefore vanishes. The amplifier end (B) remains earthed for safety, while the source end (A) "floats" with respect to earth. For safety, the source chassis is still bonded to a local protective earth, but the *signal* return is taken only to the amplifier-side single point.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks (Examiner Pattern):**
> 1. **Confusing "earth" with "ground reference of the circuit"** — Earth is a physical electrode in soil; circuit ground is a conductor. KTU expects the distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting to draw the closed loop** in diagrams — examiners specifically look for the loop being **explicitly closed** through soil/shield.
> 3. **Treating it as a purely DC problem** — the dominant EPD is at $50\ \text{Hz}$ and harmonics; answers mentioning only DC are incomplete.
> 4. **Ignoring safety** when recommending single-point ground — always qualify with "while maintaining chassis/safety earth separately".
> 5. **Mixing up common-mode and differential-mode noise** — examiners love asking the conversion process; mention both **CMRR** and the **unbalance factor**.
> 6. **Skipping units** in numerical answers — $V_g$ in volts gives $i_g$ in amperes and $V_n$ in volts; *show the units at every step*.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Multiple earths create **closed ground loops** through the soil, electrodes, and cable shield.
- ✅ The Earth Potential Difference $V_g$ (typically **1 mV to 100 V**, **50 Hz**) drives a **circulating current** $i_g$.
- ✅ Loop current formula: $i_g = V_g \,/\, (R_{e,A} + R_{e,B} + R_{sg} + R_{sh})$.
- ✅ Noise voltage: $V_n = i_g \cdot R_{sh}$ — appears in series with the signal.
- ✅ Common-mode $V_g$ becomes differential-mode noise due to **impedance unbalance** — quantified by the **unbalance factor** $(Z_1 - Z_2)/(Z_1 + Z_2)$.
- ✅ **High CMRR** ($\geq 80\ \text{dB}$) of the differential/instrumentation amplifier is the most important device-level specification for rejecting ground noise.
- ✅ **Single-point ground** at the load end is the simplest practical fix; for full galvanic isolation use **isolation amplifiers** (transformer/optical).
- ✅ **Twisted-pair, balanced cable** with shield earthed at one end only — best practice in modern instrumentation.
- ✅ Floating the source is **not** a complete solution — high-frequency capacitance and safety concerns remain.
- ✅ Always distinguish **signal/EMC ground** (single-point) from **safety/protective earth** (multi-point).
- ✅ Common affected systems: thermocouples, pH meters, strain gauges, ECG/EEG, audio, automotive sensors.
- ✅ Numerical must show: $R_{loop}$ calculation → $i_g$ → $V_n$ → SNR or noise power; never skip a step.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Noise in the Form of Voltage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT205 - BASIC OF INSTRUMENTATION ENGINEERING (INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Noise in the Form of Voltage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Noise in the Form of Voltage — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In instrumentation engineering, **noise in the form of voltage** refers to the *unwanted, random, and unpredictable fluctuations in an electrical signal* that interfere with the desired information-bearing signal. It is mathematically treated as a **stochastic (random) voltage** superimposed on the true signal at the input, output, or internal stages of an electronic system, transducer, amplifier, or transmission channel.

Mathematically, the observed (noisy) voltage is expressed as:

$$v_{\text{observed}}(t) = v_{\text{signal}}(t) + v_{\text{noise}}(t)$$

where $v_{\text{noise}}(t)$ is the **noise voltage** — a zero-mean random variable whose statistical properties are described by its **Root Mean Square (RMS) value** and **Power Spectral Density (PSD)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Noise voltage is *not* a deterministic quantity. We never speak of "the" noise voltage; instead we speak of its **RMS value** $\overline{v_n^2}^{1/2}$, its **mean-square value** $\overline{v_n^2}$, or its **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** $S_v(f)$, measured in $\text{V}^2/\text{Hz}$.

## 1.2 The Five Fundamental Sources of Voltage Noise

| # | Noise Type | Origin | Frequency Characteristic |
|---|-----------|--------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Thermal (Johnson) Noise** | Thermal agitation of charge carriers in resistors | White (frequency-independent PSD) |
| 2 | **Shot Noise** | Discrete nature of charge carriers crossing a PN junction | White (frequency-independent) |
| 3 | **Flicker (1/f) Noise** | Conductivity fluctuations, traps in semiconductors | Dominant at low frequencies |
| 4 | **Avalanche Noise** | Zener breakdown / avalanche multiplication in junctions | White in narrow bands |
| 5 | **Burst (Popcorn) Noise** | Imperfections in semiconductor fabrication | Discrete switching events |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hiss in a Quiet Room"

Imagine you are sitting in a **completely dark, silent room** trying to listen to a friend whisper a secret from across the room. The whisper is your **signal** — small, delicate, and easily lost.

Now imagine a noisy air-conditioner in the next room producing a continuous **"hiss…hiss…hiss"**. That hiss is **noise**. You cannot stop it. It is random. Even when your friend is not speaking, the hiss continues.

- If your friend's whisper is **loud** → you hear it clearly above the hiss. (High SNR)
- If your friend's whisper is **soft** → the hiss drowns it out. (Low SNR)
- You cannot "eliminate" the hiss from the AC; you can only **characterize** it (how loud, what frequencies) and use techniques (closer microphone, better insulation = filtering) to **manage** it.

> [!NOTE]
> This analogy maps perfectly to instrumentation: the **signal** is the measured physical quantity converted to voltage by a transducer; the **noise** is the random voltage that always exists. The engineer's job is to maximize **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are **mandatory** for any KTU numerical on noise voltage:

- **Boltzmann's constant**: $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \;\text{J/K}$
- **Electronic charge**: $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \;\text{C}$
- **Absolute temperature**: $T$ in Kelvin, where $T(\text{K}) = T(^\circ\text{C}) + 273.15$
- **Noise bandwidth**: $B$ in Hz (not the $-3\text{ dB}$ bandwidth — it is the **equivalent rectangular bandwidth**)
- **Mean-square noise voltage**: $\overline{v_n^2}$ in $\text{V}^2$
- **RMS noise voltage**: $v_{n,\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\overline{v_n^2}}$ in V
- **Noise Power Spectral Density (PSD)**: $S_v(f)$ in $\text{V}^2/\text{Hz}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Type 1: Thermal (Johnson) Noise Voltage

### Physical Origin
Inside every **resistor** (and therefore every conductor), electrons are in **continuous, random thermal motion** due to the temperature being above absolute zero. This random motion creates a tiny, fluctuating voltage across the terminals — even with **no current flowing** and **no applied voltage**. This was experimentally verified by **J.B. Johnson** in 1928 and theoretically explained by **H. Nyquist** using thermodynamics.

### Key Characteristics
- It is a **white noise** → its PSD is **constant** with frequency.
- It exists in **every passive resistor** at $T > 0\;\text{K}$.
- It is **Gaussian distributed** by the Central Limit Theorem (sum of millions of random electron motions).
- Its **mean value is zero** — only its RMS value is meaningful.

### Mean-Square Thermal Noise Voltage Formula (Nyquist's Formula)

$$\overline{v_n^2} = 4 k_B T R B$$

where:
- $\overline{v_n^2}$ = mean-square noise voltage in $\text{V}^2$
- $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\;\text{J/K}$ (Boltzmann's constant)
- $T$ = absolute temperature in K
- $R$ = resistance in $\Omega$
- $B$ = noise bandwidth in Hz

### RMS Thermal Noise Voltage

$$v_{n,\text{rms}} = \sqrt{4 k_B T R B}$$

### Power Spectral Density of Thermal Noise

$$S_v(f) = 4 k_B T R \quad \text{(in V}^2\text{/Hz)}$$

## 2.2 Type 2: Shot Noise Voltage

### Physical Origin
When a **DC current** flows across a **PN junction** (diode, BJT base-emitter, FET channel), the current is not continuous — it is the result of **discrete charge carriers** ($q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C) arriving at random times. This discreteness produces random fluctuations in current, which manifest as a noise voltage across the source resistance.

### Mean-Square Shot Noise Current Formula (Schottky's Formula)

$$\overline{i_n^2} = 2 q I_{DC} B$$

This is a **current noise** — to convert it to a **voltage noise** when seen across a resistance $R$:

$$\overline{v_n^2} = \overline{i_n^2} \cdot R^2 = 2 q I_{DC} R^2 B$$

### Power Spectral Density of Shot Noise Current

$$S_i(f) = 2 q I_{DC} \quad \text{(in A}^2\text{/Hz)}$$

## 2.3 Type 3: Flicker (1/f) Noise Voltage

### Physical Origin
Caused by **traps** in semiconductor materials that randomly capture and release charge carriers, and by **conductivity fluctuations** in granular materials. Its PSD is **inversely proportional to frequency**, hence the name 1/f noise.

### Empirical Formula for Flicker Noise Voltage

$$\overline{v_n^2} = \frac{K_f \cdot I_{DC}^{\,a}}{f^{b}} \cdot B \cdot R^2$$

where:
- $K_f$ = constant depending on device (1/f noise coefficient)
- $a$ = current exponent (typically 0.5 to 2)
- $b$ = frequency exponent (typically 0.8 to 1.5, often ≈ 1)

> [!NOTE]
> Flicker noise is **dominant at low frequencies** (below ~1 kHz for most devices). It is the reason why precision DC amplifiers and instrumentation systems are so hard to design.

## 2.4 Type 4: Burst (Popcorn) Noise

A discrete switching noise observed in **integrated circuits and op-amps**, appearing as sudden jumps in the output. PSD is typically of the form $\propto 1 / (1 + (f/f_c)^2)$.

## 2.5 Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)

The single most important metric in any noise analysis:

$$\text{SNR} = \frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}} = \frac{V_{\text{signal, rms}}^{\,2}}{V_{\text{noise, rms}}^{\,2}}$$

In **decibels (dB)**:

$$\text{SNR (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{\text{signal, rms}}}{V_{\text{noise, rms}}}\right)$$

## 2.6 Noise Factor and Noise Figure

Used in **amplifiers and communication receivers** to quantify how much the device degrades the SNR:

$$F = \frac{\text{SNR}_{\text{input}}}{\text{SNR}_{\text{output}}}$$

$$\text{NF (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}(F)$$

An **ideal noiseless amplifier** has $F = 1$ and NF = 0 dB.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Mean-square thermal noise voltage | $\overline{v_n^2} = 4 k_B T R B$ | $\text{V}^2$ | Nyquist's formula |
| 2 | RMS thermal noise voltage | $v_{n,\text{rms}} = \sqrt{4 k_B T R B}$ | V | Most commonly asked |
| 3 | Thermal noise PSD | $S_v = 4 k_B T R$ | $\text{V}^2/\text{Hz}$ | White noise |
| 4 | Mean-square shot noise current | $\overline{i_n^2} = 2 q I_{DC} B$ | $\text{A}^2$ | Schottky's formula |
| 5 | Shot noise PSD (current) | $S_i = 2 q I_{DC}$ | $\text{A}^2/\text{Hz}$ | White noise |
| 6 | Flicker noise voltage (empirical) | $\overline{v_n^2} = K_f I^{a} B R^2 / f^{b}$ | $\text{V}^2$ | Low-frequency dominant |
| 7 | SNR (ratio) | $\text{SNR} = V_{s,\text{rms}}^{2} / V_{n,\text{rms}}^{2}$ | dimensionless | Power ratio |
| 8 | SNR (in dB) | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(V_s / V_n)$ | dB | Use for amplifier problems |
| 9 | Noise Factor | $F = \text{SNR}_{\text{in}} / \text{SNR}_{\text{out}}$ | dimensionless | $F \geq 1$ |
| 10 | Noise Figure | $\text{NF} = 10 \log_{10}(F)$ | dB | NF = 0 dB for ideal amp |
| 11 | Total noise (independent sources) | $\overline{v_{n,\text{tot}}^2} = \overline{v_{n1}^2} + \overline{v_{n2}^2} + \cdots$ | $\text{V}^2$ | **Powers add, not voltages** |
| 12 | Noise temperature relation | $T = \overline{v_n^2} / (4 k_B R B)$ | K | Reciprocal of formula 1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** When two or more **independent** noise sources exist, their **mean-square voltages ADD**. You CANNOT add their RMS values directly: $\sqrt{a^2 + b^2} \neq a + b$.

## 2.8 Engineering Utility

Voltage noise analysis is the cornerstone of:
- **Medical instrumentation** (ECG, EEG) — where signals are microvolts and thermal noise of electrodes dominates.
- **Communication receivers** — Noise Figure determines receiver sensitivity.
- **Precision transducer design** — Strain gauge, thermocouple, LVDT all have intrinsic noise floors.
- **Audio engineering** — SNR defines dynamic range.
- **Space instrumentation** — Cosmic background radiation, Johnson noise of cryogenic sensors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mean-Square Thermal Noise Voltage (Nyquist's Formula)

**Step 1 — Starting Point: Equipartition Theorem of Thermodynamics**

For any system in thermal equilibrium at temperature $T$, the average energy stored in each degree of freedom is $\tfrac{1}{2} k_B T$. For a noisy RLC network, this energy oscillates between the inductor (magnetic) and the capacitor (electric). The total mean energy per resonant mode is:

$$\langle E \rangle = k_B T$$

The energy stored in a capacitor at voltage $v$ is $\tfrac{1}{2} C v^2$. Equating:

$$\frac{1}{2} C v^2 = k_B T \quad \Rightarrow \quad \overline{v^2} = \frac{2 k_B T}{C}$$

**Step 2 — Resonant Modes in a Bandwidth $B$**

A transmission line of length $\ell$ supports resonant modes at frequencies $f_n = n / (2\ell\sqrt{LC})$ for $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$. The number of modes in a bandwidth $B$ is:

$$N_{\text{modes}} = 2 B \ell \sqrt{LC} \quad \text{(factor of 2 for two directions)}$$

**Step 3 — Each Mode Contributes $\overline{v^2} = 2 k_B T / C$**

Since each mode is independent and at thermal equilibrium, total mean-square voltage across $R$ is:

$$\overline{v_n^2} = N_{\text{modes}} \cdot \frac{2 k_B T}{C}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $N_{\text{modes}}$**

$$\overline{v_n^2} = 2 B \ell \sqrt{LC} \cdot \frac{2 k_B T}{C}$$

$$\overline{v_n^2} = 4 B \ell k_B T \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$

**Step 5 — Use the Relation $R = \sqrt{L/C}$ (Characteristic Impedance of Lossless Line)**

Since the matched termination equals the line's characteristic impedance, $R = \sqrt{L/C}$:

$$\overline{v_n^2} = 4 k_B T R B \quad \blacksquare$$

This is **Nyquist's celebrated result** — one of the most elegant derivations in electrical engineering.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU-Style Problem)

> **Problem:** A resistor of $R = 10\;\text{k}\Omega$ is at room temperature ($T = 300\;\text{K}$). The measurement bandwidth is $B = 100\;\text{kHz}$. Calculate: (a) the mean-square thermal noise voltage, (b) the RMS thermal noise voltage, and (c) the noise power spectral density.

**Given:**
- $R = 10 \times 10^{3}\;\Omega = 10^{4}\;\Omega$
- $T = 300\;\text{K}$
- $B = 100 \times 10^{3}\;\text{Hz} = 10^{5}\;\text{Hz}$
- $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\;\text{J/K}$

**Part (a) — Mean-Square Noise Voltage**

$$\overline{v_n^2} = 4 k_B T R B$$

$$\overline{v_n^2} = 4 \times (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 300 \times (10^4) \times (10^5)$$

$$\overline{v_n^2} = 4 \times 1.38 \times 3 \times 1 \times 1 \times 10^{-23+4+5}$$

$$\overline{v_n^2} = 4 \times 1.38 \times 3 \times 10^{-14}$$

$$\overline{v_n^2} = 16.56 \times 10^{-14} = 1.656 \times 10^{-13}\;\text{V}^2$$

**Part (b) — RMS Noise Voltage**

$$v_{n,\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\overline{v_n^2}} = \sqrt{1.656 \times 10^{-13}}$$

$$v_{n,\text{rms}} = 4.07 \times 10^{-7}\;\text{V} = 0.407\;\mu\text{V}$$

**Part (c) — Noise Power Spectral Density**

$$S_v = 4 k_B T R = \frac{\overline{v_n^2}}{B} = \frac{1.656 \times 10^{-13}}{10^5}$$

$$S_v = 1.656 \times 10^{-18}\;\text{V}^2/\text{Hz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** The RMS noise of a 10 kΩ resistor at 300 K over 100 kHz is about 0.4 µV. This is why instrumentation amplifiers need such high gain and low offset — the signals we measure (e.g., thermocouples) are often in the same microvolt range as the noise!

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

| Scenario | Dominant Noise | Formula to Apply |
|----------|----------------|------------------|
| Pure resistor at room temperature, no DC current | Thermal | $\overline{v_n^2} = 4 k_B T R B$ |
| DC-biased diode or BJT | Shot | $\overline{i_n^2} = 2 q I_{DC} B$ |
| Low-frequency measurement (< 1 kHz) with semiconductor sensor | Flicker | $\overline{v_n^2} \propto 1/f$ |
| Zener-diode-based voltage reference | Avalanche | Empirical, $\propto I_{DC}$ |
| Multiple independent sources | Total | **Sum mean-squares**, then take square root |
| Cascaded amplifiers (Friis' formula) | Total NF | $\text{NF}_{\text{tot}} = \text{NF}_1 + \frac{\text{NF}_2 - 1}{G_1} + \cdots$ |

## 3.4 Derivation: Friis' Formula for Noise Figure of Cascaded Stages

For a cascade of $N$ amplifier stages, each with power gain $G_i$ and noise factor $F_i$:

$$F_{\text{total}} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \frac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2} + \cdots + \frac{F_N - 1}{G_1 G_2 \cdots G_{N-1}}$$

This is derived from the requirement that SNR degrades monotonically and that noise added by each stage is referred back to the input through the cumulative gain. The first stage dominates — hence the design rule: **"Put the lowest-noise, highest-gain stage first."**

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Shot Noise

> **Problem:** A silicon diode carries a forward DC current of $I_{DC} = 5\;\text{mA}$ at $T = 300\;\text{K}$. The source resistance is $R = 100\;\Omega$. Find the RMS shot noise voltage over a bandwidth of 1 MHz.

**Given:**
- $I_{DC} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{A}$
- $B = 10^6\;\text{Hz}$
- $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\;\text{C}$

**Step 1 — Mean-square shot noise current**

$$\overline{i_n^2} = 2 q I_{DC} B = 2 \times (1.6 \times 10^{-19}) \times (5 \times 10^{-3}) \times (10^6)$$

$$\overline{i_n^2} = 2 \times 1.6 \times 5 \times 10^{-19-3+6} = 16 \times 10^{-16} = 1.6 \times 10^{-15}\;\text{A}^2$$

**Step 2 — Mean-square shot noise voltage across $R$**

$$\overline{v_n^2} = \overline{i_n^2} \cdot R^2 = 1.6 \times 10^{-15} \times (100)^2 = 1.6 \times 10^{-11}\;\text{V}^2$$

**Step 3 — RMS shot noise voltage**

$$v_{n,\text{rms}} = \sqrt{1.6 \times 10^{-11}} = 1.265 \times 10^{-5.5} \approx 4.0\;\mu\text{V}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Noise Sources in a Typical Instrumentation Channel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcX[Physical Quantity e.g. Temperature] --> transX[Transducer e.g. Thermocouple]
    transX --> condX[Signal Conditioning Amplifier with Gain A1 and Noise F1]
    condX --> filtX[Filter Bandwidth B Hz]
    filtX --> adcX[ADC Quantization Noise]
    adcX --> procX[Digital Processor]
    procX --> outX[Output Display]

    n1[Thermal Noise 4kT R B] -.-> condX
    n2[Shot Noise 2q I B] -.-> condX
    n3[Flicker Noise K/f] -.-> condX
    n4[Environmental EMI Coupling] -.-> transX
    n5[Quantization Noise q_step squared / 12] -.-> adcX

    classDef noiseStyle fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef signalStyle fill:#e5f0ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class n1,n2,n3,n4,n5 noiseStyle
    class srcX,transX,condX,filtX,adcX,procX,outX signalStyle
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Hierarchy of Voltage Noise Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootN[Voltage Noise v_n t] --> typeA[White Noise PSD constant with f]
    rootN --> typeB[Colored Noise PSD varies with f]

    typeA --> th[Thermal Johnson Noise]
    typeA --> sh[Shot Noise]
    typeA --> av[Avalanche Noise]

    typeB --> fl[Flicker 1 divided by f Noise]
    typeB --> br[Burst Popcorn Noise]
    typeB --> th2[Thermal Noise at High f approximately flat but rolls off]

    th --> thEq[Mean Square = 4 kB T R B]
    sh --> shEq[Mean Square I = 2 q IDC B]
    fl --> flEq[Empirical K I power a B R squared divided by f power b]

    classDef rootStyle fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b38600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef branchStyle fill:#e6f7ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef leafStyle fill:#f6ffed,stroke:#389e0d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class rootN rootStyle
    class typeA,typeB,th,sh,av,fl,br,th2 branchStyle
    class thEq,shEq,flEq leafStyle
```

## 4.3 Cascaded Amplifier Noise (Friis Cascade)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inA[Source Rs with Thermal Noise 4kT Rs B] --> ampA[Stage 1 Gain G1 Noise Factor F1]
    ampA --> ampB[Stage 2 Gain G2 Noise Factor F2]
    ampB --> ampC[Stage 3 Gain G3 Noise Factor F3]
    ampC --> loadX[Load R_L]

    noteA[SNR in at input] -.-> inA
    noteOut[SNR out less than SNR in by factor F_total] -.-> loadX

    friis[F_total = F1 + F2-1 divided by G1 + F3-1 divided by G1 G2]

    classDef sigNode fill:#d9f7be,stroke:#389e0d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef noteNode fill:#fff1b8,stroke:#d48806,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class inA,ampA,ampB,ampC,loadX sigNode
    class noteA,noteOut,friis noteNode
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the Cascade:** Notice how the **first stage's noise factor $F_1$** enters the total $F_{\text{total}}$ without any denominator — it cannot be "diluted" by gain. This is why the first stage in any low-noise design (e.g., LNA in a radio receiver) is the **most critical**.

## 4.4 Functional Noise Analysis Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startX[Identify All Noise Sources] --> modelX[Model Each as an Independent RMS Voltage Source]
    modelX --> sumX[Add Mean-Squares Independent Sources]
    sumX --> calcX[Compute Total RMS Noise v_n total]
    calcX --> snrX[Compute SNR = V_signal_rms squared divided by V_noise_rms squared]
    snrX --> nfX[Compute Noise Figure NF dB = 10 log10 of SNR_in divided by SNR_out]
    nfX --> decideX{Does SNR meet specification?}
    decideX -- Yes --> shipX[Proceed to Production]
    decideX -- No --> improvX[Reduce Source Resistance, Lower Temperature, Reduce Bandwidth, Use Low-Noise Front End]
    improvX --> startX

    classDef procNode fill:#e6f7ff,stroke:#1890ff,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef critNode fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#ff4d4f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef goalNode fill:#d9f7be,stroke:#52c41a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class startX,modelX,sumX,calcX,snrX,nfX procNode
    class decideX,improvX critNode
    class shipX goalNode
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Define thermal noise. Derive the expression for the RMS value of thermal noise voltage across a resistor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Thermal noise, also called Johnson noise, is the random noise voltage generated across a resistor due to the **thermal agitation of charge carriers** (electrons) at any temperature above absolute zero. It exists even in the absence of any applied voltage or current.

**Expression (2 marks):** As derived by Nyquist using thermodynamic principles, the mean-square thermal noise voltage across a resistor $R$ at absolute temperature $T$ over a noise bandwidth $B$ is:

$$\overline{v_n^2} = 4 k_B T R B$$

Therefore, the RMS thermal noise voltage is:

$$v_{n,\text{rms}} = \sqrt{4 k_B T R B} \;\text{volts}$$

where $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\;\text{J/K}$ is Boltzmann's constant.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Differentiate between thermal noise and shot noise. Mention the formula for the mean-square value of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Thermal Noise | Shot Noise |
|-----------|---------------|------------|
| Origin | Thermal agitation of electrons in a resistor | Discrete nature of charge carriers across a PN junction |
| Requirement | Exists in **any** conductor at $T > 0$ | Requires a **DC current** $I_{DC} > 0$ |
| Mean-square value | $\overline{v_n^2} = 4 k_B T R B$ (voltage) | $\overline{i_n^2} = 2 q I_{DC} B$ (current) |
| PSD | $4 k_B T R$ (in $\text{V}^2/\text{Hz}$) | $2 q I_{DC}$ (in $\text{A}^2/\text{Hz}$) |
| Spectral character | White noise | White noise |
| Depends on | $R$, $T$, $B$ | $I_{DC}$, $B$ |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

#### Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Explain in detail the different types of noise voltages encountered in instrumentation systems. Derive the expression for the mean-square thermal noise voltage.**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Noise voltage is the unwanted random fluctuation in voltage that masks the desired signal. In instrumentation, the four most important types are thermal, shot, flicker, and burst noise.

**Thermal Noise (2 marks):** Generated by thermal agitation of electrons in conductors. It is white (uniform PSD) and Gaussian. The mean-square noise voltage is $\overline{v_n^2} = 4 k_B T R B$. Its RMS value $v_{n,\text{rms}} = \sqrt{4 k_B T R B}$ is the practical metric.

**Shot Noise (1.5 marks):** Occurs in active devices (diodes, BJTs, FETs) when a DC current crosses a potential barrier. Caused by the **discrete** (quantized) nature of charge $q$. Schottky's formula gives the mean-square noise current: $\overline{i_n^2} = 2 q I_{DC} B$. It is also white and Poisson-distributed.

**Flicker Noise (1 mark):** Also called 1/f noise, dominant at low frequencies (< 1 kHz). Caused by crystal defects and surface traps in semiconductors. Empirical PSD: $S_v(f) \propto 1/f$.

**Burst Noise (0.5 marks):** Discrete jumps in output due to fabrication defects in ICs; PSD has a Lorentzian shape.

**Derivation of Mean-Square Thermal Noise Voltage (1 mark):** Using the **equipartition theorem**, each resonant mode in a transmission line of length $\ell$ has energy $\tfrac{1}{2} k_B T$ stored in the capacitor. The number of modes in bandwidth $B$ is $2B\ell\sqrt{LC}$. Combining with the characteristic impedance $R = \sqrt{L/C}$ yields:

$$\overline{v_n^2} = 4 k_B T R B \quad \blacksquare$$

#### Part (b) (7 Marks)

**A strain gauge of resistance $R = 350\;\Omega$ is used in a measurement system. The gauge is at $T = 300\;\text{K}$ and the measurement is carried out over a bandwidth $B = 10\;\text{kHz}$. Compute: (i) the mean-square thermal noise voltage, (ii) the RMS noise voltage, and (iii) the noise power spectral density.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $R = 350\;\Omega$
- $T = 300\;\text{K}$
- $B = 10 \times 10^3 = 10^4\;\text{Hz}$
- $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\;\text{J/K}$

**(i) Mean-square noise voltage (3 marks):**

$$\overline{v_n^2} = 4 k_B T R B$$

$$\overline{v_n^2} = 4 \times (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 300 \times 350 \times 10^4$$

$$\overline{v_n^2} = 4 \times 1.38 \times 300 \times 350 \times 10^{-23+4}$$

$$\overline{v_n^2} = 4 \times 1.38 \times 3 \times 350 \times 10^{-19}$$

$$\overline{v_n^2} = 4 \times 1.38 \times 1050 \times 10^{-19} = 5796 \times 10^{-19}$$

$$\boxed{\overline{v_n^2} = 5.796 \times 10^{-16}\;\text{V}^2}$$

**(ii) RMS noise voltage (2 marks):**

$$v_{n,\text{rms}} = \sqrt{5.796 \times 10^{-16}} = 2.41 \times 10^{-8}\;\text{V} = 24.1\;\text{nV}$$

**(iii) Noise PSD (2 marks):**

$$S_v = 4 k_B T R = \frac{\overline{v_n^2}}{B} = \frac{5.796 \times 10^{-16}}{10^4} = 5.796 \times 10^{-20}\;\text{V}^2/\text{Hz}$$

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

#### Part (a) (7 Marks)

**With a neat block diagram, explain the concept of Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) and Noise Figure in instrumentation systems. Derive Friis' formula for the total noise figure of a cascaded amplifier system.**

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram & SNR Definition (2 marks):**

SNR is the ratio of signal power to noise power. In a cascaded chain of amplifiers, the SNR degrades from input to output due to noise added by each stage.

**Noise Figure (2 marks):** Noise Factor $F$ quantifies the SNR degradation:
$$F = \frac{\text{SNR}_{\text{input}}}{\text{SNR}_{\text{output}}}, \quad \text{NF (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}(F)$$

**Friis' Formula Derivation (3 marks):**

For a two-stage cascade with gains $G_1, G_2$ and noise factors $F_1, F_2$:

- Input signal power: $S_i$
- Input noise power: $N_i = k_B T_0 B$
- Output of stage 1: signal $= G_1 S_i$, noise $= G_1 N_i + G_1 N_{a1}$ where $N_{a1} = (F_1 - 1) N_i$
- Output of stage 2: signal $= G_1 G_2 S_i$, noise $= G_2 (G_1 N_i + G_1 N_{a1}) + G_2 N_{a2}$

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{out}} = \frac{G_1 G_2 S_i}{G_1 G_2 N_i (F_1 + (F_2 - 1)/G_1)}$$

$$\therefore F_{\text{total}} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} \quad \blacksquare$$

Generalizing to $N$ stages:

$$F_{\text{total}} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \frac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2} + \cdots$$

#### Part (b) (7 Marks)

**A three-stage amplifier cascade has the following specifications:**
