| Stage | Power Gain $G_i$ (dB) | Noise Figure $\text{NF}_i$ (dB) |
|-------|------------------------|-------------------------------|
| 1     | 15                     | 3                             |
| 2     | 20                     | 5                             |
| 3     | 10                     | 8                             |

**Calculate the overall noise figure of the cascade in dB.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Convert dB to linear:**

$$G_1 = 10^{15/10} = 31.62$$
$$G_2 = 10^{20/10} = 100$$
$$G_3 = 10^{10/10} = 10$$

$$F_1 = 10^{3/10} = 1.995 \approx 2.0$$
$$F_2 = 10^{5/10} = 3.162$$
$$F_3 = 10^{8/10} = 6.310$$

**Step 2 (4 marks) — Apply Friis' formula:**

$$F_{\text{total}} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \frac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2}$$

$$F_{\text{total}} = 2.0 + \frac{3.162 - 1}{31.62} + \frac{6.310 - 1}{31.62 \times 100}$$

$$F_{\text{total}} = 2.0 + \frac{2.162}{31.62} + \frac{5.310}{3162}$$

$$F_{\text{total}} = 2.0 + 0.0684 + 0.00168$$

$$F_{\text{total}} = 2.070$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Convert back to dB:**

$$\text{NF}_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}(2.070) = 10 \times 0.3160 = \boxed{3.16\;\text{dB}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Adding RMS values instead of mean-squares:** A frequent and fatal error. Independent noise voltages add as $v_{\text{total,rms}} = \sqrt{v_1^2 + v_2^2}$, NOT $v_1 + v_2$.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert temperature to Kelvin:** Always $T(\text{K}) = T(^\circ\text{C}) + 273$.
> 3. **Confusing noise bandwidth with −3 dB bandwidth:** For a single-pole RC filter, $B_n = (\pi/2) \cdot f_{-3\text{dB}}$ — **not equal**.
> 4. **Wrong formula for shot noise units:** Shot noise formula gives **current** squared; conversion to voltage requires $R^2$ multiplication.
> 5. **In Friis' formula, the first stage's noise factor is added without any denominator** — many students wrongly divide it by $G_1$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Noise in the Form of Voltage"**

- **Thermal (Johnson) noise** arises from thermal agitation of electrons; formula: $\overline{v_n^2} = 4 k_B T R B$. It is **white** and **Gaussian**.
- **Shot noise** arises from discrete charge carrier transit; formula: $\overline{i_n^2} = 2 q I_{DC} B$. It is **white** and **Poisson-distributed**.
- **Flicker (1/f) noise** dominates at low frequencies; PSD $\propto 1/f$.
- **Burst (popcorn) noise** appears as discrete jumps in ICs.
- **Boltzmann's constant** $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\;\text{J/K}$ — must be **memorized**.
- **Electronic charge** $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\;\text{C}$ — must be **memorized**.
- **Noise bandwidth** $B$ is the equivalent rectangular bandwidth, **not** the $-3\;\text{dB}$ bandwidth.
- **Independent noise sources add as mean-squares** (powers), not as RMS values.
- **SNR** is signal power to noise power; in dB use $20\log_{10}$ for amplitude ratios.
- **Noise Figure NF** = 0 dB means an ideal noiseless amplifier; typical LNA has NF < 2 dB.
- **Friis' formula** for cascaded amplifiers: $F_{\text{tot}} = F_1 + (F_2-1)/G_1 + (F_3-1)/(G_1 G_2) + \cdots$ — **first stage dominates**.
- **Cascading rule:** Put the **lowest-noise, highest-gain** stage first to minimize total NF.
- **Cooling reduces thermal noise** linearly with $T$ — this is why radio astronomy receivers and SQUIDs operate near absolute zero.
- **Reducing bandwidth** is the most direct way to reduce noise — this is why precision instruments use narrowband filters.
- **Voltage noise is additive** to the signal: $v_{\text{obs}} = v_{\text{signal}} + v_{\text{noise}}$.
- **Mean of noise voltage is zero** — only the mean-square (or RMS) is physically meaningful.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Errors During the Measurement Process: Errors in Measurements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT205 - BASIC OF INSTRUMENTATION ENGINEERING (INSTRUMENT AND CONTROL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Errors During the Measurement Process: Errors in Measurements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of measurement science, a **Measurement Error** is defined as the deviation of a measured value from the true (or actual) value of the measurand. Mathematically, it is expressed as the algebraic difference between the *measured value* $A_m$ and the *true value* $A_t$ of the quantity being measured.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Standard):**
> A measurement error is the difference between the *indicated value* of a measuring instrument and the *true value* of the quantity being measured. Since no measurement can be performed with perfect accuracy (i.e., 100% perfection), every measurement is inherently subject to some degree of uncertainty or error.

The general representation is given by:
$$\text{Error} = \text{Measured Value} - \text{True Value}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Broken Weighing Scale"

Imagine you step onto a bathroom weighing scale and the needle points to **70 kg**, but your actual weight is **68.5 kg**. The difference (**1.5 kg**) is the error. 

Now, if the scale consistently shows 1.5 kg more every single time you weigh yourself, that is a **Systematic Error** (the scale is biased). However, if the scale shows 70 kg, then 69.2 kg, then 70.5 kg on consecutive weighings (even though you haven't moved or eaten), that randomness is a **Random Error**. Finally, if you forget to remove your shoes and the scale reads 75 kg, that blunder is a **Gross (Human) Error**.

This single everyday example maps directly to the three universal classifications of measurement errors that the KTU examiner expects every student to master.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** The examiner does NOT consider the error as a "mistake" or a "defect" in the traditional sense. In metrology, an error is simply the *inherent limitation* of any physical measurement process. The goal of an instrumentation engineer is to **minimize, quantify, and compensate** for these errors — never to completely eliminate them.

## 1.3 Classification of Measurement Errors (Syllabus Snapshot)

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups measurement errors into the following primary categories:

| Category | Core Idea | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Gross Errors** | Human blunders / Carelessness | Misreading scale, recording 21.5 as 12.5 |
| **Systematic Errors** | Consistent, repeatable deviations | Zero error, calibration drift |
| **Random Errors** | Statistical, non-deterministic variations | Thermal noise, friction fluctuations |

These will be explored in deep theoretical detail in the next section.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Comprehensive Classification of Errors

### 2.1.1 Gross Errors (Human Errors)
These originate entirely from the **human operator** handling the instrument, recording data, or interpreting the readings. They are non-repeatable, unpredictable, and have no mathematical pattern.

**Common Causes:**
- Misreading of the instrument scale (parallax error).
- Incorrect recording of the observation (e.g., writing 21.5 instead of 12.5).
- Improper positioning or alignment of the instrument.
- Fatigue, haste, or inattention of the observer.
- Incorrect application of the instrument.

**Mitigation:** Training the operator, taking multiple readings, using automatic data loggers, and cross-verification.

### 2.1.2 Systematic Errors
These are the **most critical** category for KTU 2024 evaluations. Systematic errors are consistent, repeatable, and follow a definite mathematical or physical law. They can be predicted and, more importantly, **eliminated** through proper calibration.

Systematic errors are further subdivided into three types:

#### (a) Instrumental Errors
These errors are caused by the **inherent imperfections, design limitations, or aging** of the measuring instrument itself.

- *Zero Error:* The pointer does not rest on the true zero when no input is applied.
- *Friction in moving parts* (e.g., in analog meters).
- *Hysteresis:* Different readings for the same value when approached from increasing or decreasing side.
- *Dead Zone:* The range of input over which the instrument does not respond.
- *Manufacturing defects*, aging of components (spring stiffness changes over years).

#### (b) Environmental Errors
These arise due to **external conditions** surrounding the measurement setup. They are the trickiest because they often masquerade as part of the signal.

- *Temperature variations* causing expansion/contraction of instrument parts.
- *Humidity, pressure, dust, vibrations, stray magnetic/electric fields.*
- *Atmospheric conditions* affecting radio frequency or optical measurements.

**Remedy:** Use air-conditioned laboratories, magnetic shielding, vibration isolation tables, and temperature compensation networks.

#### (c) Observational Errors
These errors are introduced **during the act of reading** the instrument.

- *Parallax Error:* Reading a scale from an angle instead of perpendicularly.
- *Wrong scale interpolation* (e.g., between two major divisions).
- *Incorrect estimation* of the last digit.

**Remedy:** Use mirrored scales, digital displays, and consistent viewing angles.

### 2.1.3 Random Errors
These are small, residual errors that remain **after all gross and systematic errors have been eliminated**. They obey the laws of probability and are analyzed using statistical methods.

- Caused by unknown, uncontrollable, and unpredictable factors.
- Some readings will be slightly high, some slightly low.
- They are symmetrically distributed around the true value (assuming a large data set).
- **Cannot be eliminated**, but their magnitude can be *reduced* by taking multiple readings and applying averaging.

## 2.2 Distinction Between Error, Accuracy, and Precision

This is a **favorite KTU question (frequently asked for 3 marks)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Accuracy** is the degree of *closeness* of a measurement to the *true value*.
> - **Precision** is the degree of *repeatability* of a measurement (consistency among successive readings).
> - **Error** is the *numerical deviation* from the true value.

A measurement can be precise but not accurate, accurate but not precise, both, or neither.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

The following table consolidates **every essential formula** for the KTU Board Examination on this topic. **Memorize these.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit / Note |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Absolute Error | $e_a = A_m - A_t$ | Same unit as measurand |
| 2 | Relative (Fractional) Error | $e_r = \dfrac{A_m - A_t}{A_t}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 3 | Percentage Error | $e_{\%} = e_r \times 100$ | Expressed in % |
| 4 | True Value (from reading) | $A_t = A_m \pm e_a$ | Always $\pm$ form |
| 5 | Arithmetic Mean | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ | $n$ = number of readings |
| 6 | Deviation of $i^{th}$ reading | $d_i = x_i - \bar{x}$ | Note: $\sum d_i = 0$ |
| 7 | Variance (sample) | $\sigma^{2} = \dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i^{2}$ | For finite samples |
| 8 | Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i^{2}}$ | Spread of random errors |
| 9 | Probable Error | $r = 0.6745\,\sigma$ | 50% probability of occurrence |
| 10 | Average Deviation | $\bar{D} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert d_i \vert$ | Uses absolute value |
| 11 | Range of Errors | $R_e = x_{\max} - x_{\min}$ | Spread between extremes |
| 12 | Limiting Error (Guaranteed Accuracy) | $e_L = \pm (\text{specified accuracy})$ | Given by manufacturer |
| 13 | Error propagation (Sum/Difference) | $e_R = \pm\sqrt{e_1^{2} + e_2^{2} + \dots + e_n^{2}}$ | Independent random errors |
| 14 | Error propagation (Product/Quotient) | $\dfrac{e_R}{R} = \pm\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{e_1}{x_1}\right)^{2} + \dots}$ | Relative form is additive in quadrature |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Formatting Note for KTU Answer Sheets:** Always express an error in the format `Measured Value ± Error` along with the correct unit. Example: $V = 12.50 \pm 0.02\,\text{V}$. Examiners explicitly award marks for the proper $\pm$ notation and unit consistency.

## 2.4 The Concept of Limiting Errors

When a manufacturer produces an instrument, they **guarantee** a maximum permissible error. This is called the **Limiting Error** or **Guaranteed Accuracy Error**. It defines the worst-case boundary within which the instrument is certified to perform.

**Example:** An ammeter with a full-scale reading of 100 A and a guaranteed accuracy of $\pm 2\%$ of full scale. The limiting error is:
$$e_L = \pm (0.02 \times 100) = \pm 2\,\text{A}$$

This means **at any reading between 0 and 100 A**, the error will never exceed $\pm 2$ A. This is a very important concept for combination-of-instruments problems.

## 2.5 Error Propagation — The "Worst-Case" Engineering Tool

When a final result is computed from several measured quantities (each with its own error), the total error in the result must be estimated. There are two scenarios:

### Case 1: Sum or Difference
If $R = x_1 \pm x_2 \pm x_3 \pm \dots$, the worst-case absolute error is the **arithmetic sum**:
$$e_R = \pm(\vert e_1 \vert + \vert e_2 \vert + \dots + \vert e_n \vert)$$
For random errors (statistical independence), the **root-sum-square (RSS)** is used:
$$e_R = \pm\sqrt{e_1^{2} + e_2^{2} + \dots + e_n^{2}}$$

### Case 2: Product or Quotient
If $R = \dfrac{x_1 \cdot x_2}{x_3}$, the relative error is propagated:
$$\dfrac{e_R}{R} = \pm\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{e_1}{x_1}\right)^{2} + \left(\dfrac{e_2}{x_2}\right)^{2} + \left(\dfrac{e_3}{x_3}\right)^{2}}$$
This is **statistical error propagation** and is the most commonly tested form in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Mean and Variance of Repeated Readings

### Step 1: Define the Dataset
Suppose a physical quantity (e.g., a voltage) is measured $n$ times, yielding $n$ independent readings: $x_1, x_2, x_3, \dots, x_n$. Each reading contains a random error, so they are all slightly different.

### Step 2: Define the Best Estimate (Arithmetic Mean)
By the principle of **Least Squares**, the most probable true value is the arithmetic mean of all readings:
$$\bar{x} = \frac{x_1 + x_2 + x_3 + \dots + x_n}{n} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$$

### Step 3: Define Deviations
The deviation of each reading from the mean is:
$$d_i = x_i - \bar{x}$$

### Step 4: Algebraic Property (Verification Step)
A fundamental property of deviations is that their algebraic sum equals zero:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i - n\bar{x}$$

Substituting $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum x_i$:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i = n\bar{x} - n\bar{x} = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> This identity is **frequently asked as a 3-mark proof question** in KTU Module 4 examinations.

### Step 5: Derive the Variance
Variance measures the average of squared deviations. For a finite (sample) dataset, we divide by $(n-1)$ — this is called **Bessel's correction**:
$$\sigma^{2} = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i^{2} = \frac{1}{n-1}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^{2} - \frac{1}{n}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i\right)^{2}\right]$$

The standard deviation is simply the positive square root:
$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i^{2}}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Examples

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following fully worked-out numerical examples are representative of the **exact KTU 2024 problem style** (frequently appearing in Part B).

### Example 1: Computation of Statistical Error Parameters

**Problem:** A set of 8 resistance measurements (in $\Omega$) taken on a precision resistor is: **101.2, 101.4, 101.0, 101.3, 101.1, 101.2, 101.4, 101.3**. Calculate the (a) arithmetic mean, (b) deviations, (c) standard deviation, and (d) probable error.

**Solution:**

**(a) Arithmetic Mean:**
$$\bar{x} = \frac{101.2 + 101.4 + 101.0 + 101.3 + 101.1 + 101.2 + 101.4 + 101.3}{8}$$
$$\bar{x} = \frac{809.9}{8} = 101.2375\,\Omega$$

**(b) Deviations $d_i = x_i - \bar{x}$:**

| Reading $x_i$ ($\Omega$) | Deviation $d_i$ ($\Omega$) | $d_i^{2}$ ($\Omega^{2}$) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 101.2 | $-0.0375$ | $0.001406$ |
| 101.4 | $+0.1625$ | $0.026406$ |
| 101.0 | $-0.2375$ | $0.056406$ |
| 101.3 | $+0.0625$ | $0.003906$ |
| 101.1 | $-0.1375$ | $0.018906$ |
| 101.2 | $-0.0375$ | $0.001406$ |
| 101.4 | $+0.1625$ | $0.026406$ |
| 101.3 | $+0.0625$ | $0.003906$ |
| **Sum** | $\approx 0$ (as expected) | $\sum d_i^{2} = 0.138750$ |

**(c) Standard Deviation:**
$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{0.138750}{8 - 1}} = \sqrt{0.019821} = 0.1408\,\Omega$$

**(d) Probable Error:**
$$r = 0.6745 \times \sigma = 0.6745 \times 0.1408 \approx 0.0950\,\Omega$$

**Final Reported Value:**
$$R = \bar{x} \pm r = 101.2375 \pm 0.0950\,\Omega$$

---

### Example 2: Error Propagation in a Product (Classic KTU Problem)

**Problem:** The power in a DC circuit is calculated using $P = V \times I$. A voltmeter reads $V = 100 \pm 1\,\text{V}$ and an ammeter reads $I = 5 \pm 0.1\,\text{A}$. Find the (a) nominal value of power, (b) limiting error in power, (c) percentage limiting error, and (d) range of power.

**Solution:**

**(a) Nominal Power:**
$$P = V \times I = 100 \times 5 = 500\,\text{W}$$

**(b) Relative Limiting Error in Power (Product Rule):**
$$\frac{e_P}{P} = \pm\sqrt{\left(\frac{e_V}{V}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{e_I}{I}\right)^{2}}$$
$$\frac{e_P}{P} = \pm\sqrt{\left(\frac{1}{100}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{0.1}{5}\right)^{2}}$$
$$\frac{e_P}{P} = \pm\sqrt{(0.01)^{2} + (0.02)^{2}} = \pm\sqrt{0.0001 + 0.0004}$$
$$\frac{e_P}{P} = \pm\sqrt{0.0005} = \pm 0.02236$$

**(c) Percentage Limiting Error:**
$$e_{\%} = 0.02236 \times 100 = \pm 2.236\%$$

**(d) Absolute Error and Range of Power:**
$$e_P = 0.02236 \times 500 = \pm 11.18\,\text{W}$$
$$P = 500 \pm 11.18\,\text{W} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Range: } [488.82\,\text{W},\ 511.18\,\text{W}]$$

> [!NOTE]
> This problem is **exemplary of KTU 2024 Module 4 question patterns**. Students should practice the substitution of $e_P/P$ values carefully and retain at least 3–4 significant figures until the final step.

---

### Example 3: Worst-Case Limiting Error in a Sum

**Problem:** Two resistors are in series: $R_1 = 100\,\Omega \pm 5\%$ and $R_2 = 50\,\Omega \pm 5\%$. Find the total resistance and its worst-case limiting error.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Absolute errors of each resistor:**
$$e_1 = 0.05 \times 100 = \pm 5\,\Omega$$
$$e_2 = 0.05 \times 50 = \pm 2.5\,\Omega$$

**Step 2 — Nominal total resistance:**
$$R_T = R_1 + R_2 = 100 + 50 = 150\,\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Worst-case (arithmetic sum) absolute error:**
$$e_{R_T} = \pm(\vert e_1 \vert + \vert e_2 \vert) = \pm(5 + 2.5) = \pm 7.5\,\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Reported value:**
$$R_T = 150 \pm 7.5\,\Omega \quad \text{or} \quad R_T = 150 \pm 5\%$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Master Classification of Measurement Errors

The diagram below provides the **complete hierarchical tree** of errors in the measurement process, exactly as expected in a KTU descriptive answer.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Measurement Errors] --> B[Gross Errors]
    A --> C[Systematic Errors]
    A --> D[Random Errors]

    B --> B1[Misreading Scale]
    B --> B2[Wrong Recording]
    B --> B3[Improper Instrument Use]

    C --> C1[Instrumental Errors]
    C --> C2[Environmental Errors]
    C --> C3[Observational Errors]

    C1 --> C1a[Zero Error]
    C1 --> C1b[Friction and Hysteresis]
    C1 --> C1c[Aging of Components]
    C1 --> C1d[Manufacturing Defects]

    C2 --> C2a[Temperature Variation]
    C2 --> C2b[Humidity and Pressure]
    C2 --> C2c[Stray EM Fields]
    C2 --> C2d[Mechanical Vibration]

    C3 --> C3a[Parallax Error]
    C3 --> C3b[Wrong Interpolation]
    C3 --> C3c[Estimation of Last Digit]

    D --> D1[Statistical in Nature]
    D --> D2[Cannot be Eliminated]
    D --> D3[Reduced by Averaging]
    D --> D4[Analyzed via Mean and SD]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Accuracy vs Precision (Classic "Target" Concept)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph S1 [TARGET 1: High Accuracy, High Precision]
        S1A[Hole]
        S1A --> S1B[Hole]
        S1A --> S1C[Hole]
        S1A --> S1D[Hole]
    end

    subgraph S2 [TARGET 2: Low Accuracy, High Precision]
        S2A[Hole]
        S2A --> S2B[Hole]
        S2A --> S2C[Hole]
        S2A --> S2D[Hole]
    end

    subgraph S3 [TARGET 3: High Accuracy, Low Precision]
        S3A[Hole]
        S3A --> S3B[Hole]
        S3A --> S3C[Hole]
        S3A --> S3D[Hole]
    end
```

**Interpretation for the KTU answer sheet:**

- **Target 1:** Shots are tightly clustered AND centered on the bullseye → instrument is **accurate and precise** (ideal).
- **Target 2:** Shots are tightly clustered but offset from the bullseye → instrument is **precise but not accurate** (systematic error present).
- **Target 3:** Shots are scattered but their average lies on the bullseye → instrument is **accurate on average but not precise** (random errors present).

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Error Propagation Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Measured Quantity with Error] --> Q{Operation Type?}
    Q --> S[Sum or Difference]
    Q --> M[Product or Quotient]

    S --> S1[Use Arithmetic Sum of Absolute Errors]
    S1 --> S2[Report RT = R plus or minus eR]

    M --> M1[Compute Relative Errors of each input]
    M1 --> M2[Apply RSS Formula on Relative Errors]
    M2 --> M3[Convert Back to Absolute Error]
    M3 --> M4[Report Result with plus or minus]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define the following terms with examples: (i) Absolute Error, (ii) Relative Error, (iii) Percentage Error.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

- **(i) Absolute Error** is the numerical difference between the measured value $A_m$ and the true value $A_t$ of the measurand. 
$$e_a = A_m - A_t$$
*Example:* If a voltmeter reads 10.2 V when the true value is 10.0 V, the absolute error is $+0.2$ V.

- **(ii) Relative Error** (also called fractional error) is the ratio of the absolute error to the true value. It is dimensionless. 
$$e_r = \frac{A_m - A_t}{A_t}$$
*Example:* For the above case, $e_r = 0.2 / 10.0 = 0.02$.

- **(iii) Percentage Error** is simply the relative error expressed as a percentage. 
$$e_{\%} = e_r \times 100$$
*Example:* $e_{\%} = 0.02 \times 100 = 2\%$.

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between Systematic Errors and Random Errors. Give two examples of each.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Systematic Errors | Random Errors |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Nature** | Consistent, deterministic | Statistical, non-deterministic |
| **Cause** | Instrument defect, environment, observer | Unknown, uncontrollable |
| **Predictability** | Predictable and correctable | Unpredictable, follows probability law |
| **Magnitude** | Constant for a given setup | Varies from reading to reading |
| **Removal** | Can be eliminated by calibration | Reduced by averaging, not eliminated |
| **Example 1** | Zero error in ammeter | Thermal noise in resistor |
| **Example 2** | Drift due to temperature change | Slight parallax in reading needle |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> When solving error propagation problems, students **frequently lose marks** by:
> 1. Forgetting to convert percentage errors to fractional form before applying the RSS formula.
> 2. Reporting only the percentage error and **forgetting to compute the absolute error** in the final answer.
> 3. Using the *arithmetic sum* of errors when the *RSS (root-sum-square)* was required, or vice versa.
> 4. Omitting the **$\pm$ sign** in the final reported value — this alone costs **at least 1 mark**.
> 5. Failing to state the final answer in the form `Result = Nominal Value $\pm$ Error (with unit)`.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Part B Option 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Q3. (a)** Explain in detail the various types of systematic errors encountered in the measurement process. Describe, with suitable examples, how each type can be minimized. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Systematic errors are **consistent, repeatable errors** that arise from definite, identifiable causes. They are classified into three categories:

**1. Instrumental Errors** — Caused by imperfections in the instrument itself.
- *Zero Error:* Pointer does not rest on the true zero. **Remedy:** Mechanical zero adjustment before use.
- *Friction in moving parts:* Causes non-repeatable readings. **Remedy:** Use jeweled bearings; tap gently before reading.
- *Hysteresis:* Different output for increasing vs decreasing input. **Remedy:** Use instruments with low-hysteresis materials; standard approach direction.
- *Aging of springs/elements:* Drift over years. **Remedy:** Periodic recalibration.

**2. Environmental Errors** — Caused by external ambient conditions.
- *Temperature:* Expansion of bimetallic strips, change in resistance. **Remedy:** Temperature compensation networks, air-conditioned labs.
- *Humidity:* Affects insulation resistance. **Remedy:** Hermetic sealing, desiccants.
- *Stray magnetic fields:* Induce EMF in moving coils. **Remedy:** Magnetic shielding (mu-metal enclosures).
- *Vibrations:* Cause mechanical fluctuations. **Remedy:** Vibration isolation tables.

**3. Observational Errors** — Caused by the human observer.
- *Parallax:* Reading pointer at an angle. **Remedy:** Mirrored scale, perpendicular viewing.
- *Interpolation errors:* Estimating between two scale divisions. **Remedy:** Use verniers or digital displays.

---

**(b)** The resistance of a coil is measured using the formula $R = V/I$. The voltmeter reads $V = 50 \pm 0.5$ V and the ammeter reads $I = 2 \pm 0.02$ A. Calculate (i) the measured value of resistance, (ii) the relative and absolute errors in resistance, and (iii) the range of resistance. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Measured Value of Resistance:**
$$R = \frac{V}{I} = \frac{50}{2} = 25\,\Omega$$

**(ii) Relative and Absolute Error:**
Since $R$ is a quotient, the relative error follows the RSS formula:
$$\frac{e_R}{R} = \pm\sqrt{\left(\frac{e_V}{V}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{e_I}{I}\right)^{2}}$$
$$\frac{e_R}{R} = \pm\sqrt{\left(\frac{0.5}{50}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{0.02}{2}\right)^{2}}$$
$$\frac{e_R}{R} = \pm\sqrt{(0.01)^{2} + (0.01)^{2}} = \pm\sqrt{0.0001 + 0.0001}$$
$$\frac{e_R}{R} = \pm\sqrt{0.0002} = \pm 0.01414$$

Absolute error:
$$e_R = 0.01414 \times 25 = \pm 0.3535 \approx \pm 0.354\,\Omega$$

**(iii) Range of Resistance:**
$$R = 25 \pm 0.354\,\Omega \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Range: } [24.646\,\Omega,\ 25.354\,\Omega]$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Part B Option 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q4. (a)** What are Random Errors? Explain the statistical methods (Arithmetic Mean, Standard Deviation, Variance, and Probable Error) used to analyze them. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Random Errors** are small, residual errors that persist even after all systematic and gross errors have been eliminated. They originate from unknown, uncontrollable factors such as thermal noise, micro-fluctuations in friction, and quantization noise. They are **unpredictable in sign and magnitude** but follow the laws of probability when many readings are taken.

**Statistical Methods of Analysis:**

**1. Arithmetic Mean ($\bar{x}$):** The best estimate of the true value when $n$ independent readings $x_i$ are taken:
$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$$
By the principle of least squares, this minimizes the sum of squared errors.

**2. Deviations ($d_i$):** The difference between each reading and the mean:
$$d_i = x_i - \bar{x}$$
Their algebraic sum is always zero: $\sum d_i = 0$.

**3. Variance ($\sigma^{2}$):** The mean of squared deviations (with Bessel's correction for finite samples):
$$\sigma^{2} = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i^{2}$$
It quantifies the average spread of readings around the mean.

**4. Standard Deviation ($\sigma$):** The square root of variance, expressed in the same unit as the measurand:
$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i^{2}}$$
A small $\sigma$ indicates high precision.

**5. Probable Error ($r$):** The error value such that there is a **50% probability** that any single random error will be less than this magnitude:
$$r = 0.6745\,\sigma$$
The constant $0.6745$ arises from the normal (Gaussian) probability distribution.

**Reported Final Reading:** $A = \bar{x} \pm r$ (with proper units).

---

**(b)** A set of 10 measurements of a voltage source gave the following values (in volts): 10.12, 10.15, 10.10, 10.13, 10.11, 10.14, 10.12, 10.16, 10.13, 10.11. Calculate the standard deviation and probable error. Report the result in the standard form. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Arithmetic Mean:**
$$\bar{x} = \frac{10.12 + 10.15 + 10.10 + 10.13 + 10.11 + 10.14 + 10.12 + 10.16 + 10.13 + 10.11}{10}$$
$$\bar{x} = \frac{101.27}{10} = 10.127\,\text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Deviations and Squared Deviations:**

| $x_i$ (V) | $d_i = x_i - \bar{x}$ (V) | $d_i^{2}$ (V²) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 10.12 | $-0.007$ | $0.000049$ |
| 10.15 | $+0.023$ | $0.000529$ |
| 10.10 | $-0.027$ | $0.000729$ |
| 10.13 | $+0.003$ | $0.000009$ |
| 10.11 | $-0.017$ | $0.000289$ |
| 10.14 | $+0.013$ | $0.000169$ |
| 10.12 | $-0.007$ | $0.000049$ |
| 10.16 | $+0.033$ | $0.001089$ |
| 10.13 | $+0.003$ | $0.000009$ |
| 10.11 | $-0.017$ | $0.000289$ |
| **Sum** | $\approx 0$ | $\sum d_i^{2} = 0.003210$ |

**Step 3 — Standard Deviation:**
$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{0.003210}{10 - 1}} = \sqrt{0.0003567} = 0.01888\,\text{V} \approx 0.0189\,\text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Probable Error:**
$$r = 0.6745 \times \sigma = 0.6745 \times 0.01888 \approx 0.01273\,\text{V}$$

**Step 5 — Final Reported Voltage:**
$$V = \bar{x} \pm r = 10.127 \pm 0.013\,\text{V}$$

This means that there is a 50% probability that the true value of the voltage lies between $10.114$ V and $10.140$ V.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Errors in Measurement**

- **Three master categories:** Gross (human) Errors, Systematic Errors (Instrumental / Environmental / Observational), and Random Errors.
- **Systematic errors are predictable and can be eliminated; random errors can only be statistically reduced.**
- **Absolute Error** = Measured Value $-$ True Value (same unit as measurand).
- **Relative Error** = Absolute Error / True Value (dimensionless).
- **Percentage Error** = Relative Error $\times$ 100 (in %).
- Always report final answer in the canonical form: **$A = \text{Mean} \pm \text{Error}$** with units.
- **Arithmetic Mean** is the best estimate of the true value (least-squares criterion).
- **Sum of deviations always equals zero** — a key proof-based question.
- **Standard Deviation $\sigma$** measures the spread (precision) of random errors.
- **Probable Error $r = 0.6745\sigma$** — represents the 50% probability boundary.
- **Limiting Error (Guaranteed Accuracy)** is the manufacturer's worst-case specification; it is *not* a random error.
- **Error propagation in sum/difference:** absolute errors add **arithmetically** in worst case, or in **quadrature (RSS)** for random errors.
- **Error propagation in product/quotient:** *relative* errors combine via the **RSS formula** in quadrature.
- **Accuracy** = closeness to true value; **Precision** = closeness among repeated readings.
- Use **mirrored scales, digital displays, calibration, shielding, and averaging** to minimize errors.
- Common KTU traps: missing $\pm$ sign, wrong unit, applying arithmetic sum when RSS is required, and not converting % to fraction.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: True Value
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT205 - BASIC OF INSTRUMENTATION ENGINEERING (INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** True Value

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In metrology and instrumentation engineering, the **True Value (TV)** of a measurand is defined as the *actual, exact, and unique value of a physical quantity that would be obtained if a perfect, ideal measurement were performed under ideal conditions, with no systematic or random errors present*. 

Mathematically, it is the value that is completely free from any kind of measurement error. In practice, since no physical measurement is perfectly ideal, the **True Value remains an idealization** that is never directly known. Instead, we approach it through a hierarchy of standards and statistical averaging.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The term "true value" is a foundational concept upon which all subsequent definitions of *error, accuracy, precision, calibration, and traceability* are constructed. Mastery of this concept is essential to answering Module 4 and Module 5 questions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology Distinction (KTU Board Favourite):**
> - **Measurand:** The physical quantity being measured (e.g., temperature, pressure, voltage).
> - **True Value:** The ideal, error-free value of the measurand.
> - **Measured Value (MV):** The value indicated by the measuring instrument.
> - **Indicated Value:** The reading displayed by the instrument, inclusive of all errors.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an archer aiming at the **bullseye** of a target. The bullseye represents the **True Value** of the measurement.

- Your arrow landing exactly in the center → **High Accuracy** (close to true value).
- Your arrows consistently clustering tightly together (even if slightly off-center) → **High Precision**.
- Your arrows scattered randomly across the target → **Both low accuracy and low precision**.

Since you can never physically place the bullseye exactly where it should be without first knowing the true value, and since no instrument is perfect, the *true value is analogous to a philosophical limit* — we strive to get closer to it, but we can only **estimate** it using calibrated standards and repeated measurements.

## 1.3 Why the True Value Matters in Engineering

| Field | Why True Value is Critical |
|---|---|
| **Process Control** | Ensuring reactor temperature matches design specifications to prevent runaway reactions. |
| **Calibration Labs** | Establishing traceability chains to national/international standards. |
| **Pharmaceuticals** | Drug dosage accuracy is governed by how close measured values are to true values. |
| **Aerospace** | Sensor calibration errors of a fraction of a percent can be catastrophic. |
| **Power Systems** | Voltage and frequency measurements must align with grid standards. |

---

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for KTU Exams:** *"True value is to measurement what infinity is to mathematics — a limit we approach but never reach."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchical Approximation to True Value

Since true value is unobservable directly, instrumentation engineers construct a layered architecture of approximations:

### Step 1: Definition of the Measurand
A physical quantity is defined operationally (e.g., length = distance between two parallel planes at a specified temperature, usually **20 °C** as per ISO 1:2002).

### Step 2: Realization via Primary Standards
- **Primary Standard:** Maintained by national/international laboratories (e.g., **NPL India, NIST USA, PTB Germany, BIPM**).
- A primary standard realizes the *defined unit* with the highest possible accuracy achievable by current science.

### Step 3: Secondary Standards
- Calibrated against primary standards and used to calibrate working standards in industry.

### Step 4: Working Standards
- Routine laboratory standards used to calibrate industrial instruments.

### Step 5: Measuring Instrument
- The instrument under test (IUT) reads the value of the measurand, and its output is a **Measured Value**.

> [!NOTE]
> The closer we go up this hierarchy, the closer the value is to the **True Value**.

## 2.2 True Value and the Error Equation

The relationship between true value, measured value, and error is fundamental to KTU problems:

$$\text{Measured Value (MV)} = \text{True Value (TV)} + \text{Error (e)}$$

Rearranging, the **Absolute Error** is defined as:

$$e = MV - TV$$

The **Relative Error** is:

$$\text{Relative Error} = \frac{e}{TV} = \frac{MV - TV}{TV}$$

The **Percentage Error** is:

$$\%\text{ Error} = \frac{MV - TV}{TV} \times 100$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Some students confuse **% Error** with **% Accuracy**. They are **complementary**:
> $$\% \text{Accuracy} = 100\% - \%\text{ Error}$$
> Always re-read the question to see which is being asked.

## 2.3 Static vs Dynamic Measurements

| Aspect | Static Measurement | Dynamic Measurement |
|---|---|---|
| **Measurand** | Constant with time | Varying with time |
| **True Value** | Well-defined | Time-dependent, requires transient definition |
| **Goal** | Equilibrium reading | Tracking the time-varying signal |
| **Example** | Room temperature | Vibration sensor output |

## 2.4 Statistical Nature of True Value (Repeated Measurements)

When $n$ repeated measurements $M_1, M_2, \dots, M_n$ are taken of the same measurand, the **Best Estimate of True Value (BETV)** is the **arithmetic mean**:

$$\bar{M} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} M_i$$

As $n \to \infty$ and all systematic errors are removed, $\bar{M} \to TV$.

> [!NOTE]
> The **True Value** is the **limiting mean** of an infinite number of measurements with no systematic error.

## 2.5 The Concept of "Conventional True Value"

In practical metrology, the term **Conventional True Value (CTV)** is used. It is the value obtained from a primary or secondary standard, which is *accepted* as the closest approximation to the true value within a specified uncertainty.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Term:** "Conventional True Value" is the more commonly accepted phrasing in engineering problems, because pure true value is unattainable.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Absolute Error | $e = MV - TV$ | Same as measurand | Signed quantity |
| True Value (operationally) | $TV = MV - e$ | Same as measurand | Can be negative or positive |
| Relative Error | $r = \dfrac{MV - TV}{TV}$ | Dimensionless | Often expressed as fraction |
| Percentage Error | $\%e = r \times 100$ | Percent (%) | Most commonly asked in KTU |
| Percentage Accuracy | $\%A = 100 - \%e$ | Percent (%) | KTU-favourite follow-up |
| Best Estimate of True Value (BETV) | $\bar{M} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} M_i$ | Same as measurand | Used when $n \geq 3$ typically |
| Deviation from Mean | $d_i = M_i - \bar{M}$ | Same as measurand | Used in error analysis |
| True Value (conventional) | $\bar{M} + \text{correction}$ | Same as measurand | After applying systematic correction |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Trick:** Whenever a problem gives a *voltmeter reading* and asks for the "actual" value, the actual value = **Measured Value − Error** (or **Measured Value + Correction**, where correction $= -e$).

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The concept of true value drives several engineering realities:

1. **Calibration Hierarchy:** Every industrial pressure transmitter can be traced back through working standards → secondary standards → primary standards → SI definition of the pascal. This chain exists *only* because we accept an approximation to the true value at each level.

2. **Uncertainty Quantification (ISO GUM):** Modern metrology accepts that we never *know* the true value, so we report measurements with an **uncertainty interval** $\bar{M} \pm U$.

3. **Sensor Fusion:** When multiple sensors measure the same quantity, weighted averaging based on individual accuracies is performed to converge to the true value.

4. **Six-Sigma Quality Control:** Process control engineering defines defect rates relative to specifications derived from true value tolerances.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Limiting Mean Equals True Value

**Goal:** Show that the arithmetic mean of an infinite set of error-free measurements equals the true value.

**Assumption:** Each measurement $M_i$ has the form:
$$M_i = TV + e_i$$
where $e_i$ is the random error in the $i$-th measurement. The expected value of $e_i$ is zero, i.e., $E[e_i] = 0$.

**Step 1 — Define the arithmetic mean of $n$ measurements:**

$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} M_i$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $M_i = TV + e_i$:**

$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} (TV + e_i)$$

**Step 3 — Split the summation:**

$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{1}{n}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} TV + \sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i\right)$$

**Step 4 — Factor out $TV$ from the first sum:**

$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{1}{n}\left(n \cdot TV + \sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i\right)$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**

$$\bar{M}_n = TV + \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i$$

**Step 6 — Take the limit as $n \to \infty$:**

By the **Law of Large Numbers**, as $n \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i = E[e_i] = 0$$

Therefore:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \bar{M}_n = TV$$

This proves the **Best Estimate of True Value** is the arithmetic mean when the number of readings is large and systematic errors are absent.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Relationship Between Error, Correction, and True Value

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of measured value:**

$$MV = TV + e$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange for true value:**

$$TV = MV - e$$

**Step 3 — Define correction $c$ as the negative of error:**

$$c = -e \quad \Rightarrow \quad e = -c$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$$TV = MV - (-c) = MV + c$$

$$\boxed{TV = MV + c}$$

This is the **Calibration Equation** in its simplest form, and it is a *guaranteed KTU 14-mark derivation* on the topic of calibration.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A voltmeter reads **119.6 V** when measuring a voltage source. When calibrated against a standard, the instrument has an error of **+0.4 V**. Determine:
- (a) The true value of the voltage.
- (b) The relative error.
- (c) The percentage error.
- (d) The percentage accuracy.

**Solution:**

**(a) True Value:**

$$TV = MV - e = 119.6 \text{ V} - 0.4 \text{ V} = 119.2 \text{ V}$$

**(b) Relative Error:**

$$r = \frac{MV - TV}{TV} = \frac{119.6 - 119.2}{119.2} = \frac{0.4}{119.2} \approx 3.356 \times 10^{-3}$$

**(c) Percentage Error:**

$$\%e = 3.356 \times 10^{-3} \times 100 \approx 0.3356\%$$

**(d) Percentage Accuracy:**

$$\%A = 100\% - 0.3356\% \approx 99.6644\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** Always show the formula *before* substituting values. Examiners deduct marks if you directly write numbers.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Identifying True Value from Repeated Readings

The following table demonstrates how the **Best Estimate of True Value (BETV)** is found by averaging multiple measurements, assuming no systematic error is present.

| Trial | Measured Length (mm) | Deviation from Mean $d_i$ | Squared Deviation $d_i^{2}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 25.02 | +0.01 | 0.0001 |
| 2 | 25.00 | -0.01 | 0.0001 |
| 3 | 25.01 | 0.00 | 0.0000 |
| 4 | 25.03 | +0.02 | 0.0004 |
| 5 | 24.99 | -0.02 | 0.0004 |
| **Sum** | **125.05** | **0.00** | **0.0010** |
| **Mean (BETV)** | **25.01** | — | — |

Here, the **True Value is approximated as 25.01 mm** (the arithmetic mean of 5 trials). The small deviations confirm the instrument is precise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for KTU Boards:** The sum of deviations from the mean is *always* zero (a property of the arithmetic mean). Examiners sometimes use this to verify students' calculations.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: The True Value Estimation Hierarchy

This Mermaid block diagram illustrates the layered flow of calibration through which we approach the *ideal* True Value from a *raw* measured value.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Measurand e.g. Temperature] --> B[Sensor Transducer]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning]
    C --> D[Display Unit]
    D --> E[Measured Value MV]
    E --> F[Apply Correction c]
    F --> G[Best Estimate of True Value]
    G --> H{Error Check}
    H --> I[Conventional True Value CTV]
    H --> J[Rejected Reading]
    I --> K[Reference to Primary Standard]
    K --> L[International Definition SI Unit]
    L --> M[True Value Ideal Limit]
```

**Flow Explanation:**

- **Step A → E:** The physical quantity is converted through a sensor, conditioning circuit, and display into a *Measured Value* containing errors.
- **Step F:** A *correction* (derived from calibration) is added to compensate for known systematic errors.
- **Step H:** The corrected value is checked against a higher-accuracy standard.
- **Step I → L:** The Conventional True Value is anchored to the SI definition of the unit.
- **Step M:** True Value is the asymptotic limit — never fully reached, but approached.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Sources of Error Between True Value and Measured Value

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TV[True Value TV] --> SE[Systematic Error]
    TV --> RE[Random Error]
    SE --> MV1[Measured Value Offset]
    RE --> MV2[Measured Value Noise]
    MV1 --> MV[Final Measured Value]
    MV2 --> MV
    MV --> E[Error e = MV - TV]
```

**Interpretation:**

- **Systematic Error (Bias):** Repeatable, predictable deviation (e.g., zero drift, calibration offset). Can be *corrected*.
- **Random Error (Noise):** Unpredictable fluctuations (e.g., electrical noise, human parallax). Can be *reduced* by averaging.
- The combination yields the final Measured Value, from which the total error $e$ is computed.

## 4.3 Block Diagram: True Value vs. Accuracy vs. Precision (Target Analogy)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph High_Accuracy_High_Precision
    H1[True Value Target Center]
    H2[Tight cluster of hits near center]
    end
    subgraph High_Precision_Low_Accuracy
    L1[Cluster of hits away from center]
    L2[Reproducible but biased]
    end
    subgraph Low_Precision_Low_Accuracy
    P1[Random scatter across target]
    P2[Neither accurate nor precise]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** An instrument with **high accuracy** reflects minimal systematic error (low bias from true value), while **high precision** reflects minimal random error (low scatter).

## 4.4 Concept Map: The Role of True Value in Instrumentation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[True Value Concept] --> B[Measurand Definition]
    A --> C[Calibration Standards]
    A --> D[Error Analysis]
    A --> E[Uncertainty Quantification]
    B --> B1[ISO 1:2002]
    C --> C1[Primary Standards]
    C --> C2[Secondary Standards]
    C --> C3[Working Standards]
    D --> D1[Systematic Error]
    D --> D2[Random Error]
    E --> E1[Confidence Intervals]
    E --> E2[ISO GUM Standard]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "True Value" and "Conventional True Value". Why can the true value never be exactly determined? [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

The **True Value** of a measurand is the value that would be obtained by a perfect, error-free measurement. It is an idealization that is unattainable in practice because no measurement system is perfectly free from systematic and random errors.

The **Conventional True Value (CTV)** is the value of a quantity obtained from a primary or secondary standard, accepted by convention as having an uncertainty appropriate for a given purpose. The CTV is the closest practically achievable approximation to the True Value.

The True Value cannot be exactly determined because:
1. Every measurement instrument has finite resolution and inherent imperfections.
2. Environmental conditions (temperature, humidity, vibration) always affect the measurement.
3. Random errors are stochastic in nature and cannot be entirely eliminated.
4. Systematic errors, even after calibration, leave residual uncertainties.

### Question 2: Differentiate between "True Value", "Measured Value", and "Error" with a suitable example. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

| Term | Definition | Example (Thermometer) |
|---|---|---|
| **True Value (TV)** | The ideal, error-free value of the measurand | Actual room temperature = 25.0 °C |
| **Measured Value (MV)** | The value indicated by the measuring instrument | Thermometer reading = 25.4 °C |
| **Error (e)** | $MV - TV$ | $25.4 - 25.0 = +0.4$ °C |

The error indicates how far the measured value deviates from the true value. A **positive** error means the instrument reads high, and a **negative** error means the instrument reads low.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: True Value — Calibration and Error Analysis [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define True Value. The arithmetic mean of 5 measurements of a resistance is **101.4 Ω**, and each measurement has a known systematic correction of **+0.6 Ω**. Determine:
- (i) The Conventional True Value of the resistance.
- (ii) The absolute error, relative error, and percentage error in the measurement.
- (iii) Comment on the accuracy of the instrument.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A pressure gauge reads **9.82 bar** when the standard pressure is **10.00 bar**.
- (i) Calculate the absolute error and percentage error.
- (ii) Calculate the percentage accuracy.
- (iii) If the same gauge is used to measure a pressure of **75 bar**, estimate the expected measured value, assuming the percentage error remains constant.

---

### Model Solution to Question A:

#### Part (a):

**(i) Conventional True Value:**

$$CTV = \bar{M} + c = 101.4 \text{ Ω} + 0.6 \text{ Ω} = 102.0 \text{ Ω}$$

**(ii) Error computations:**

Absolute error:
$$e = MV - TV = 101.4 - 102.0 = -0.6 \text{ Ω}$$

Relative error:
$$r = \frac{-0.6}{102.0} \approx -5.882 \times 10^{-3}$$

Percentage error:
$$\%e = -0.5882\%$$

**(iii) Accuracy comment:**

$$\%A = 100 - 0.5882 = 99.4118\%$$

The instrument is **highly accurate** since the percentage error is less than 1%. This is acceptable for most industrial applications.

#### Part (b):

**(i) Absolute error and percentage error:**

$$e = 9.82 - 10.00 = -0.18 \text{ bar}$$

$$\%e = \frac{-0.18}{10.00} \times 100 = -1.8\%$$

**(ii) Percentage accuracy:**

$$\%A = 100 - 1.8 = 98.2\%$$

**(iii) Expected measured value at 75 bar:**

Since percentage error remains constant:
$$MV = TV \times \left(1 + \frac{\%e}{100}\right) = 75 \times (1 - 0.018) = 75 \times 0.982 = 73.65 \text{ bar}$$

---

### Question B: True Value — Statistical Averaging and Best Estimate [KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define the "Best Estimate of True Value" (BETV). Five independent measurements of a voltage are: **12.10 V, 12.05 V, 12.12 V, 12.08 V, 12.15 V**.
- (i) Calculate the BETV.
- (ii) Calculate the absolute deviation of each reading from the mean.
- (iii) Verify that the sum of deviations is zero.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is the "Conventional True Value"? Describe the role of **Primary, Secondary, and Working Standards** in establishing the true value. Why is a calibration hierarchy important in industries such as **pharmaceuticals and aerospace**?

---

### Model Solution to Question B:

#### Part (a):

**(i) Best Estimate of True Value (arithmetic mean):**

$$\bar{V} = \frac{12.10 + 12.05 + 12.12 + 12.08 + 12.15}{5}$$

$$\bar{V} = \frac{60.50}{5} = 12.10 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Absolute deviations:**

| Reading $V_i$ (V) | Deviation $d_i = V_i - \bar{V}$ (V) |
|---|---|
| 12.10 | 0.00 |
| 12.05 | -0.05 |
| 12.12 | +0.02 |
| 12.08 | -0.02 |
| 12.15 | +0.05 |
| **Sum** | **0.00** |

**(iii) Verification:** $\sum d_i = 0.00 - 0.05 + 0.02 - 0.02 + 0.05 = 0.00$ ✓

This verifies the mathematical property of the arithmetic mean: the sum of deviations from the mean is always zero.

#### Part (b):

**Conventional True Value (CTV):** It is the value of a measurand obtained from a primary or secondary standard, accepted by convention as the closest practical approximation to the true value with an associated uncertainty.

**Role of Standards:**

| Standard Type | Role | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Standard** | Highest accuracy; maintained by national metrology institutes | NPL India, NIST USA, PTB Germany, BIPM |
| **Secondary Standard** | Calibrated against primary; used by accredited calibration labs | ISO 17025 accredited labs |
| **Working Standard** | Routine use for calibrating industrial instruments | Factory calibration benches |

**Importance of Calibration Hierarchy:**

1. **Pharmaceuticals:** Drug formulation requires extremely tight tolerances. A miscalibrated balance could lead to incorrect drug dosage. The hierarchy ensures traceability to SI units.

2. **Aerospace:** Pressure transducers in aircraft altimeters must be traceable to the SI pascal. A small error at the working standard level can cascade into a 1000-foot altitude error at 35,000 feet.

3. **Legal & Regulatory Compliance:** ISO 9001, FDA 21 CFR Part 11, and other regulations require traceable measurements.

4. **Inter-laboratory Comparability:** The hierarchy ensures that a measurement made in India is comparable to one made in the USA.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing % Error with % Accuracy:** Many students write the final answer as the *percentage error* when the question asks for *percentage accuracy* (or vice versa). Always re-read the question verb.
> 2. **Sign of Error:** Forgetting that the error $e$ is a *signed* quantity. The absolute error is $MV - TV$, not $TV - MV$.
> 3. **Forgetting Units:** The absolute error has the same units as the measurand; the relative error and percentage error are dimensionless. The KTU board deducts 0.5 marks for missing units.
> 4. **Mis-stating "True Value" Definition:** Writing "True Value is the average of readings" is *incorrect*. The True Value is the *ideal* value. The average is the *Best Estimate* of the True Value.
> 5. **Skipping Formula Steps:** Examiners want the formula written symbolically *before* numerical substitution. Skipping this loses 1–2 marks per sub-part.
> 6. **Calibration Hierarchy Confusion:** Students often mix up primary, secondary, and working standards. Remember: **P**rimary is the **P**innacle (highest accuracy).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — True Value**

- **True Value (TV):** Ideal, error-free value of a measurand; never directly knowable.
- **Conventional True Value (CTV):** Accepted approximation from calibrated standards.
- **Measured Value (MV):** Reading from the instrument, containing errors.
- **Absolute Error:** $e = MV - TV$ (signed, same units as measurand).
- **Relative Error:** $r = e / TV$ (dimensionless).
- **Percentage Error:** $\%e = r \times 100$ (in percent).
- **Percentage Accuracy:** $\%A = 100 - \%e$ (complementary to % error).
- **Correction:** $c = -e$, so $TV = MV + c$.
- **Best Estimate of True Value (BETV):** Arithmetic mean of repeated readings, $\bar{M} = \frac{1}{n}\sum M_i$.
- **Limiting Mean Theorem:** As $n \to \infty$, $\bar{M} \to TV$ (when systematic error is absent).
- **Sum of Deviations Property:** $\sum (M_i - \bar{M}) = 0$ always.
- **Calibration Hierarchy:** Primary → Secondary → Working → Field Instrument.
- **International Standards Bodies:** NPL (India), NIST (USA), PTB (Germany), BIPM (international).
- **Systematic vs Random Errors:** Systematic = bias (correctable); Random = scatter (reducible via averaging).
- **KTU Board Terminology:** When the question says "actual value", it usually means the *true* or *conventional* true value.
- **High-Yield Formula Triplet to Memorize:**
  1. $TV = MV - e$
  2. $\%e = \frac{MV - TV}{TV} \times 100$
  3. $\%A = 100 - \%e$

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** *"True Value: Think Vital Accuracy"* — always aim for the true value; quantify your deviation as a percentage of it.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Limiting Errors (Guarantee Errors)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT205 - BASIC OF INSTRUMENTATION ENGINEERING (INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Limiting Errors (Guarantee Errors)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **measurement systems and instrumentation**, a **Limiting Error** (also widely called a **Guarantee Error**) is the maximum permissible error that the manufacturer of an instrument or measuring device **guarantees** will never be exceeded under specified operating conditions. It represents the **worst-case error** that a user can expect from a device, and it is always expressed as a percentage of the true value, the full-scale reading, or the span of the instrument.

Mathematically, limiting error defines a **bounded error window** around the true value of the measured quantity. The actual error of the instrument, under normal operating conditions, will always lie within this guaranteed window.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCICT205 - Module 4):**
> *Limiting Error (Guarantee Error) is the maximum error specified by the manufacturer of an instrument. It is the difference between the guaranteed measured value and the true value, expressed as a percentage of the true value (or full-scale deflection).*

The key terminologies associated with this concept are:
- **Guarantee Error (eᵍ):** The error magnitude that the manufacturer commits (guarantees) will not be exceeded.
- **True Value (Aₜ):** The exact, actual value of the quantity being measured.
- **Measured Value (Aₘ):** The value indicated by the instrument.
- **Full-Scale Value (FSD):** The maximum reading on the instrument scale.
- **Relative Limiting Error:** The ratio of the limiting error to the true value, expressed as a percentage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Idea:** Limiting error is **not** the actual error of the instrument during a specific measurement — it is the **maximum** error that the manufacturer commits to. The actual operational error will always be **less than or equal to** the limiting error.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you walk into a shop to buy a packet of rice advertised as **"1 kg"**. The manufacturer prints a small disclaimer on the packet that says:
*"Guaranteed weight: 1 kg ± 1% (i.e., between 990 g and 1010 g)."*

This ±1% is the **limiting error** of the weight. It is the manufacturer's **guarantee** that the actual weight of rice in the packet will **never** be less than 990 g or more than 1010 g. The actual weight when you check it on a precise scale might be 998 g (an error of 2 g), but this is well within the guaranteed ±10 g (1%) window. If you ever find a packet weighing 985 g, then the manufacturer has **violated their guarantee**.

Similarly, when an ammeter is rated as "0–10 A, ±2% of full scale", it means:
- The manufacturer guarantees that whenever the ammeter reads any value, the **true current** will lie within **±2% of 10 A** = **±0.2 A** of the indicated reading.
- No matter what the ammeter shows — 2 A, 5 A, or 8 A — the error is bounded by ±0.2 A.

This **guarantee** is what allows engineers to **trust** the instrument and to perform **worst-case error analysis** in complex measurement systems where multiple instruments are cascaded.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is it called "Guarantee Error"?**
> Because the manufacturer **guarantees** the user that the error will not exceed this specified limit under normal operating conditions. If it does, the device is considered defective and eligible for replacement/repair under warranty.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Bold

The following standard metrics are universally used when specifying limiting errors in instrumentation:

- **Full-Scale Deflection (FSD)** — The maximum readable value on the instrument's scale.
- **True Value (Aₜ)** — The actual magnitude of the quantity being measured (in SI units).
- **Measured Value (Aₘ)** — The value displayed by the instrument.
- **Limiting Error (eₗ)** — Expressed as **± % of True Value** or **± % of Full-Scale Reading**.
- **Relative Limiting Error (eᵣ)** — Dimensionless ratio: eₗ / Aₜ × 100%.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown

The theory of limiting errors is built upon the concept that **manufacturers cannot produce a perfectly accurate instrument** due to tolerances in components, environmental effects, calibration drift, and manufacturing imperfections. Instead of claiming perfect accuracy (which is practically impossible and economically prohibitive), manufacturers provide a **bounded guarantee** within which the device will perform reliably.

The logical steps involved in the analysis of limiting errors are:

1. **Identify the True Value (Aₜ):** Establish the actual physical quantity being measured, either through a primary standard or reference.
2. **Obtain the Measured Value (Aₘ):** Read the value indicated by the instrument under test.
3. **Determine the Limiting Error Specification:** Read the manufacturer's datasheet to obtain the guaranteed error limit (e.g., ±1% of FSD, ±0.5% of true value).
4. **Compute the Absolute Limiting Error:** Multiply the percentage specification by the appropriate reference value (FSD or true value) to get the absolute error in the measurement unit.
5. **Establish the Error Bounds:** Calculate the upper and lower limits of the true value:
   - **Upper Limit:** Aₘ + |eₗ|
   - **Lower Limit:** Aₘ - |eₗ|
6. **Determine the Worst-Case Range:** The true value lies somewhere within [Aₘ - |eₗ|, Aₘ + |eₗ|].
7. **For Composite Systems:** Apply the **limiting error combination rules** (sum, difference, product, quotient, power) to determine the overall worst-case error when multiple error-contributing components are cascaded.

## 2.2 Mathematical Formulations

### 2.2.1 Absolute Error

The **absolute error** of a measurement is the difference between the measured value and the true value.

$$e_a = A_m - A_t$$

Where:
- $e_a$ = Absolute error
- $A_m$ = Measured (indicated) value
- $A_t$ = True value

### 2.2.2 Relative Error

The **relative error** is the ratio of absolute error to the true value. It is a **dimensionless quantity**, often expressed as a percentage.

$$e_r = \frac{e_a}{A_t} = \frac{A_m - A_t}{A_t}$$

### 2.2.3 Percentage Error (Percentage Limiting Error)

$$e\% = e_r \times 100\% = \frac{A_m - A_t}{A_t} \times 100\%$$

### 2.2.4 Limiting Error Definition

The **limiting error** is the maximum magnitude of error that can occur in any given measurement, as guaranteed by the manufacturer:

$$e_L = \pm \left( \frac{\Delta A_{max}}{A_t} \times 100\% \right)$$

Where $\Delta A_{max}$ is the maximum deviation between measured and true values.

### 2.2.5 Limiting Error in Terms of Full-Scale Deflection (FSD)

If the manufacturer specifies the limiting error as a percentage of FSD:

$$e_L = \pm \left( \eta \times \text{FSD} \right)$$

Where $\eta$ is the percentage error (e.g., 0.02 for ±2%).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction in KTU Problems:**
> Many students confuse "% of True Value" with "% of Full-Scale Value." They are **NOT the same**.
> - **% of FSD** → Constant absolute error regardless of where the needle points.
> - **% of True Value** → Variable absolute error; smaller at the lower end of the scale.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $e_a = A_m - A_t$ | Absolute Error | Same as measured quantity |
| 2 | $e_r = \dfrac{e_a}{A_t}$ | Relative Error | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $e\% = \dfrac{e_a}{A_t} \times 100$ | Percentage Error | % |
| 4 | $e_L = \pm \eta \times \text{FSD}$ | Limiting Error (% of FSD) | Same as measured quantity |
| 5 | $e_L = \pm \eta \times A_t$ | Limiting Error (% of True Value) | Same as measured quantity |
| 6 | $A_{upper} = A_m + \vert e_L \vert$ | Upper bound of true value | Same as measured quantity |
| 7 | $A_{lower} = A_m - \vert e_L \vert$ | Lower bound of true value | Same as measured quantity |
| 8 | $R = A_m(1 \pm \eta)$ | Range (for % of true value spec) | Same as measured quantity |
| 9 | $\varepsilon_R = \pm (\eta_1 + \eta_2)$ | Limiting error of Sum $(R = A+B)$ | % |
| 10 | $\varepsilon_R = \pm (\eta_1 - \eta_2)$ | Limiting error of Difference $(R = A-B)$ | % |
| 11 | $\varepsilon_R = \pm (\eta_1 + \eta_2)$ | Limiting error of Product $(R = A \times B)$ | % |
| 12 | $\varepsilon_R = \pm (\eta_1 + \eta_2)$ | Limiting error of Quotient $(R = A/B)$ | % |
| 13 | $\varepsilon_R = \pm (n \cdot \eta_1)$ | Limiting error of Power $(R = A^n)$ | % |
| 14 | $A_{min} = \dfrac{A_m}{1 + \eta}$ | Minimum possible true value | Same as measured quantity |
| 15 | $A_{max} = \dfrac{A_m}{1 - \eta}$ | Maximum possible true value | Same as measured quantity |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for Combination Rules:** For **sum, difference, product, and quotient**, the limiting errors are always **ADDED (with appropriate sign for difference)**. This is because in worst-case analysis, you assume all individual errors occur in their most unfavourable direction simultaneously.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Limiting Error Analysis

Limiting error analysis is **critical** in the following engineering applications:

1. **Cascaded Measurement Systems:** When multiple transducers and signal conditioners are connected in series (e.g., thermocouple → amplifier → ADC → display), the total error accumulates. Limiting error analysis provides the **worst-case error** of the entire system.
2. **Worst-Case Circuit Design:** In analog electronic circuit design (e.g., amplifiers, filters), the worst-case output due to tolerance of resistors, capacitors, and active devices is computed using the limiting error combination rules.
3. **Quality Control and Calibration:** During acceptance testing of instruments, the measured error is compared against the manufacturer's guarantee to decide pass/fail.
4. **Safety-Critical Systems:** In aerospace, nuclear, and medical instrumentation, worst-case error analysis ensures that the system remains within safe operating limits.
5. **Process Control:** In industries (chemical, petrochemical, manufacturing), the measurement uncertainty in sensors directly affects control accuracy. Limiting error analysis quantifies the **worst-case control deviation**.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Limiting Error of a Sum (R = A + B)

Let us consider two quantities $A$ and $B$ with measured values $A_m$ and $B_m$ and corresponding limiting errors $\eta_A$ and $\eta_B$ expressed as percentages of their true values.

The result of their sum is:

$$R = A + B$$

The measured result is:

$$R_m = A_m + B_m$$

The absolute error in the sum is:

$$e_R = R_m - R_t = (A_m + B_m) - (A_t + B_t) = (A_m - A_t) + (B_m - B_t) = e_A + e_B$$

The maximum possible absolute error occurs when both individual errors are at their **worst-case (limiting) values with the same sign**:

$$e_{R,max} = \vert e_A \vert + \vert e_B \vert$$

The relative limiting error is:

$$\varepsilon_R = \frac{e_{R,max}}{R_t} = \frac{\vert e_A \vert + \vert e_B \vert}{A_t + B_t}$$

Since $e_A = \eta_A \cdot A_t$ and $e_B = \eta_B \cdot B_t$:

$$\varepsilon_R = \frac{\eta_A \cdot A_t + \eta_B \cdot B_t}{A_t + B_t}$$

**Step-by-step expansion:**

$$\varepsilon_R = \frac{\eta_A \cdot A_t}{A_t + B_t} + \frac{\eta_B \cdot B_t}{A_t + B_t}$$

This can be written as a weighted sum of the individual percentage errors. However, the **standard KTU result** assumes worst-case weighting (equal contribution), which simplifies to:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_R = \pm (\eta_A + \eta_B)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The exact weighted form is $\varepsilon_R = \dfrac{\eta_A \cdot A_t + \eta_B \cdot B_t}{A_t + B_t}$, but for the **worst-case (guaranteed) limiting error**, the individual percentage errors are **summed directly**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Limiting Error of a Difference (R = A − B)

Let $R = A - B$. The absolute error is:

$$e_R = R_m - R_t = (A_m - B_m) - (A_t - B_t) = (A_m - A_t) - (B_m - B_t) = e_A - e_B$$

The maximum magnitude of the error occurs when $e_A$ and $e_B$ have **opposite signs** (one positive, one negative), so the magnitudes add:

$$e_{R,max} = \vert e_A \vert + \vert e_B \vert$$

The relative limiting error:

$$\varepsilon_R = \frac{\vert e_A \vert + \vert e_B \vert}{A_t - B_t}$$

Using the same worst-case logic as above:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_R = \pm (\eta_A + \eta_B)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** In a **difference measurement**, the denominators can become very small if $A \approx B$, leading to a **huge relative error** even when each individual error is small. This phenomenon is known as **subtractive cancellation** and is a common source of gross errors in instrumentation. **Always check the difference magnitude first!**

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Limiting Error of a Product (R = A × B)

Let $R = A \cdot B$. Taking the natural logarithm on both sides:

$$\ln R = \ln A + \ln B$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to the true values:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{dA}{A} + \frac{dB}{B}$$

Replacing the differentials with small errors and taking worst-case magnitudes:

$$\frac{\vert e_R \vert}{R_t} = \frac{\vert e_A \vert}{A_t} + \frac{\vert e_B \vert}{B_t}$$

In terms of percentage errors:

$$\varepsilon_R = \eta_A + \eta_B$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_R = \pm (\eta_A + \eta_B)}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Limiting Error of a Quotient (R = A / B)

Let $R = A / B$. Taking the natural logarithm:

$$\ln R = \ln A - \ln B$$

Differentiating:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{dA}{A} - \frac{dB}{B}$$

Taking worst-case magnitudes:

$$\frac{\vert e_R \vert}{R_t} = \frac{\vert e_A \vert}{A_t} + \frac{\vert e_B \vert}{B_t}$$

In terms of percentage errors:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_R = \pm (\eta_A + \eta_B)}$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Limiting Error of a Power (R = Aⁿ)

Let $R = A^n$. Taking the natural logarithm:

$$\ln R = n \ln A$$

Differentiating:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = n \cdot \frac{dA}{A}$$

Taking the worst-case magnitude:

$$\frac{\vert e_R \vert}{R_t} = n \cdot \frac{\vert e_A \vert}{A_t}$$

In terms of percentage errors:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_R = \pm (n \cdot \eta_A)}$$

## 3.6 Analytical Worked Example: Full Derivation

**Problem:** A voltmeter with a full-scale range of 100 V is specified with a limiting error of ±2% of full scale. When the voltmeter reads 75 V, find:
1. The absolute limiting error in the reading.
2. The relative limiting error of the measurement.
3. The range of possible true values.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the given data.**

$$\text{FSD} = 100 \text{ V}, \quad \eta = 2\% = 0.02, \quad A_m = 75 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the absolute limiting error.**

The limiting error is specified as a percentage of FSD:

$$e_L = \pm (\eta \times \text{FSD}) = \pm (0.02 \times 100) = \pm 2 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the relative limiting error.**

The relative limiting error is the ratio of the absolute error to the measured (indicated) value:

$$e_{r,L} = \frac{\vert e_L \vert}{A_m} \times 100\% = \frac{2}{75} \times 100\% = 2.667\%$$

**Step 4: Calculate the range of possible true values.**

The true value lies within the band:

$$A_{lower} = A_m - \vert e_L \vert = 75 - 2 = 73 \text{ V}$$

$$A_{upper} = A_m + \vert e_L \vert = 75 + 2 = 77 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5: Present the result.**

$$\boxed{A_t \in [73 \text{ V}, 77 \text{ V}] \quad \text{with} \quad e_L = \pm 2 \text{ V} \quad (e_{r,L} = \pm 2.667\%)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** The **absolute error** remains ±2 V regardless of the reading, but the **relative error** becomes worse at lower readings. At 75 V, the relative error is 2.667%, but at 25 V, it would be 2/25 × 100% = 8%. This is why **instruments should be operated near full-scale** for better accuracy.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Flowchart of Limiting Error Calculation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[START: Measured Value Am and Limiting Error Spec] --> node1
    node1[Identify Reference: FSD or True Value] --> node2
    node2[Convert Percentage to Decimal: eta] --> node3
    node3{Reference is FSD?}
    node3 -- YES --> node4A[Absolute Error eL = eta x FSD]
    node3 -- NO --> node4B[Absolute Error eL = eta x At]
    node4A --> node5
    node4B --> node5
    node5[Compute Upper Bound: Am + eL] --> node6
    node6[Compute Lower Bound: Am - eL] --> node7
    node7[Compute Relative Limiting Error: eL divided by Am x 100 percent] --> node8
    node8[Present Result: True Value lies in Range Am minus eL to Am plus eL] --> endA
    endA[END]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Architecture of Error Combination in a Cascaded System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SENSOR["SENSOR STAGE - Transducer"]
        S1[Physical Quantity X] --> S2[Transducer with eta1]
        S2 --> S3[Electrical Signal V1]
    end
    subgraph AMPLIFIER["AMPLIFIER STAGE - Signal Conditioner"]
        S3 --> A1[Amplifier with eta2]
        A1 --> A2[Conditioned Signal V2]
    end
    subgraph ADC["ADC STAGE - Analog to Digital Converter"]
        A2 --> C1[ADC with eta3]
        C1 --> C2[Digital Code N]
    end
    subgraph DISPLAY["DISPLAY STAGE - Indicator"]
        C2 --> D1[Display Driver with eta4]
        D1 --> D2[Final Reading Y]
    end
    D2 --> R[Total Limiting Error: eta1 + eta2 + eta3 + eta4]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Decision Tree for Limiting Error Combination Rules

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB[Given: Quantities with Individual Limiting Errors] --> q1{What is the Operation?}
    q1 -- Sum A plus B --> r1[etaR = eta1 + eta2]
    q1 -- Difference A minus B --> r2[etaR = eta1 + eta2]
    q1 -- Product A times B --> r3[etaR = eta1 + eta2]
    q1 -- Quotient A divided by B --> r4[etaR = eta1 + eta2]
    q1 -- Power A raised to n --> r5[etaR = n times eta1]
    r1 --> finalB
    r2 --> finalB
    r3 --> finalB
    r4 --> finalB
    r5 --> finalB
    finalB[Result: Worst Case Limiting Error of Combined Quantity R]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Concept Map of Limiting Error

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Limiting Error] --> B[Absolute Error]
    A --> C[Relative Error]
    A --> D[Percentage Error]
    B --> E[Measured minus True]
    C --> F[Absolute divided by True]
    D --> G[Relative x 100]
    A --> H[Specifications]
    H --> H1[Percent of FSD]
    H --> H2[Percent of True Value]
    A --> I[Combination Rules]
    I --> I1[Sum and Difference]
    I --> I2[Product and Quotient]
    I --> I3[Power]
    A --> J[Applications]
    J --> J1[Instrument Calibration]
    J --> J2[Cascaded System Design]
    J --> J3[Quality Control]
```

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**Define the term "Limiting Error" and "Guarantee Error" in the context of instrumentation. Why is it also called a worst-case error?**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**Model Answer:**

A **limiting error** is the maximum error that the manufacturer of an instrument guarantees will not be exceeded under specified operating conditions. It is expressed as a percentage of the true value or the full-scale deflection of the instrument.

$$\text{Limiting Error}, \quad e_L = \pm \left( \eta \times \text{True Value or FSD} \right)$$

The terms **"limiting error"** and **"guarantee error"** are used interchangeably in instrumentation. The manufacturer **guarantees** that the actual error in any measurement will be **less than or equal to** this limit.

It is called a **worst-case error** because it represents the **maximum possible deviation** between the measured and true values. In a real measurement, the actual error will typically be smaller, but the user is **guaranteed** that it will never exceed the specified limit. Hence, it provides a safe upper bound for error analysis.

> [!TIP]
> **Marking Tip (3 marks):** Definition (1.5 marks) + Mathematical expression (0.5 mark) + Worst-case justification (1 mark).

### Question 2
**State and justify the rules for combining limiting errors in:**
**(a)** Sum or difference of two quantities.
**(b)** Product or quotient of two quantities.
**(c)** A quantity raised to a power $n$.

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Sum or Difference $(R = A \pm B)$:**
The worst-case limiting error is the **algebraic sum** of the individual percentage limiting errors:
$$\varepsilon_R = \pm (\eta_A + \eta_B)$$
**Justification:** In worst-case analysis, both $e_A$ and $e_B$ are assumed to occur with maximum magnitude in the same (or opposite for difference) direction, so their absolute values add.

**(b) Product or Quotient $(R = A \times B \text{ or } R = A/B)$:**
The worst-case limiting error is again the **sum** of the individual percentage errors:
$$\varepsilon_R = \pm (\eta_A + \eta_B)$$
**Justification:** Using logarithmic differentiation, $\dfrac{dR}{R} = \dfrac{dA}{A} \pm \dfrac{dB}{B}$. In worst case, the magnitudes add.

**(c) Power $(R = A^n)$:**
The worst-case limiting error is $n$ times the individual percentage error:
$$\varepsilon_R = \pm (n \cdot \eta_A)$$
**Justification:** From $\ln R = n \ln A$, we get $\dfrac{dR}{R} = n \cdot \dfrac{dA}{A}$.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** A voltmeter with a full-scale range of 200 V reads 150 V when the true voltage is 148 V. Calculate:
1. The absolute error of the measurement.
2. The relative error.
3. The percentage error.

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Full-Scale Range: $\text{FSD} = 200 \text{ V}$
- Measured Value: $A_m = 150 \text{ V}$
- True Value: $A_t = 148 \text{ V}$

**Step 1: Calculate the Absolute Error.**

The absolute error is the difference between the measured value and the true value:

$$e_a = A_m - A_t = 150 - 148 = +2 \text{ V}$$

The positive sign indicates that the voltmeter reads **higher** than the actual voltage.

**Step 2: Calculate the Relative Error.**

The relative error is the ratio of the absolute error to the true value:

$$e_r = \frac{e_a}{A_t} = \frac{2}{148} = 0.01351$$

This is a dimensionless quantity.

**Step 3: Calculate the Percentage Error.**

The percentage error is the relative error multiplied by 100:

$$e\% = e_r \times 100\% = 0.01351 \times 100\% = 1.351\%$$

**Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{e_a = +2 \text{ V}, \quad e_r = 0.01351, \quad e\% = 1.351\%}$$

**(b) [7 Marks]** A pressure gauge with a full-scale range of 500 kPa has a manufacturer-guaranteed limiting error of ±1.5% of full scale. When the gauge reads 250 kPa, determine:
1. The absolute limiting error in kPa.
2. The range of possible true pressure values.
3. The relative limiting error as a percentage of the measured value.
4. Comment on whether the gauge should be used at this range or at full scale for better accuracy.

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
