**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Full-Scale Range: $\text{FSD} = 500 \text{ kPa}$
- Limiting Error: $\eta = \pm 1.5\% = \pm 0.015$ of FSD
- Measured Value: $A_m = 250 \text{ kPa}$

**Step 1: Calculate the Absolute Limiting Error.**

The limiting error is specified as a percentage of FSD. Therefore:

$$e_L = \pm (\eta \times \text{FSD}) = \pm (0.015 \times 500) = \pm 7.5 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Range of Possible True Pressure Values.**

The true pressure lies within the band:

$$A_{lower} = A_m - \vert e_L \vert = 250 - 7.5 = 242.5 \text{ kPa}$$

$$A_{upper} = A_m + \vert e_L \vert = 250 + 7.5 = 257.5 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the Relative Limiting Error.**

The relative limiting error (as a percentage of the measured value) is:

$$e_{r,L} = \frac{\vert e_L \vert}{A_m} \times 100\% = \frac{7.5}{250} \times 100\% = 3.0\%$$

**Step 4: Comparison with Full-Scale Operation.**

If the same gauge were used at full scale (i.e., reading 500 kPa), the absolute limiting error would still be ±7.5 kPa, but the relative error would be:

$$e_{r,L,FS} = \frac{7.5}{500} \times 100\% = 1.5\%$$

This is **half** the relative error compared to reading at half-scale (3.0%).

**Conclusion:** The gauge should ideally be used **at or near the full-scale value** to minimize the relative error and obtain the most accurate measurement. Operating it at 50% of full scale doubles the relative error.

**Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{e_L = \pm 7.5 \text{ kPa}, \quad A_t \in [242.5, 257.5] \text{ kPa}, \quad e_{r,L} = \pm 3.0\%}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** A resistor is specified with a nominal value of $1000 \, \Omega$ and a tolerance of $\pm 5\%$. A capacitor is specified with a nominal value of $2 \, \mu\text{F}$ and a tolerance of $\pm 10\%$. These components are used in a circuit to compute a time constant $\tau = R \times C$. Determine:
1. The nominal value of the time constant.
2. The worst-case (limiting) error in the time constant.
3. The range of possible time constant values.

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Resistor: $R = 1000 \, \Omega$ with $\eta_R = \pm 5\% = \pm 0.05$
- Capacitor: $C = 2 \, \mu\text{F}$ with $\eta_C = \pm 10\% = \pm 0.10$

**Step 1: Calculate the Nominal Time Constant.**

The time constant of an RC circuit is given by:

$$\tau = R \times C = 1000 \, \Omega \times 2 \, \mu\text{F} = 2000 \, \mu\text{s} = 2 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Worst-Case Limiting Error in the Time Constant.**

Since $\tau = R \times C$ is a **product**, the worst-case limiting error is the sum of the individual percentage errors:

$$\varepsilon_\tau = \pm (\eta_R + \eta_C) = \pm (5\% + 10\%) = \pm 15\%$$

**Step 3: Calculate the Range of Possible Time Constant Values.**

The worst-case absolute error in the time constant is:

$$\vert e_\tau \vert = \varepsilon_\tau \times \tau = 0.15 \times 2 \text{ ms} = 0.3 \text{ ms}$$

The range of possible values:

$$\tau_{min} = 2 \text{ ms} - 0.3 \text{ ms} = 1.7 \text{ ms}$$

$$\tau_{max} = 2 \text{ ms} + 0.3 \text{ ms} = 2.3 \text{ ms}$$

**Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{\tau_{nominal} = 2 \text{ ms}, \quad \varepsilon_\tau = \pm 15\%, \quad \tau \in [1.7 \text{ ms}, 2.3 \text{ ms}]}$$

**(b) [7 Marks]** In a measurement system, the power dissipated in a resistor is calculated using the formula $P = V^2 / R$, where $V = 50 \pm 1\% \, \text{V}$ and $R = 1000 \pm 2\% \, \Omega$. Determine:
1. The nominal power dissipation.
2. The worst-case limiting error in the calculated power.
3. The range of possible power values.

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Voltage: $V = 50 \text{ V}$ with $\eta_V = \pm 1\% = \pm 0.01$
- Resistance: $R = 1000 \, \Omega$ with $\eta_R = \pm 2\% = \pm 0.02$

**Step 1: Calculate the Nominal Power.**

$$P_{nominal} = \frac{V^2}{R} = \frac{(50)^2}{1000} = \frac{2500}{1000} = 2.5 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Worst-Case Limiting Error in Power.**

The expression $P = V^2 / R$ is a combination of a **power** and a **quotient**.

- For the numerator $V^2$: the limiting error is $\pm (2 \cdot \eta_V) = \pm (2 \times 1\%) = \pm 2\%$.
- For the quotient $V^2 / R$: the limiting error is the **sum** of the numerator and denominator errors:

$$\varepsilon_P = \pm (2 \cdot \eta_V + \eta_R) = \pm (2 \times 1\% + 2\%) = \pm (2\% + 2\%) = \pm 4\%$$

**Step 3: Calculate the Range of Possible Power Values.**

The worst-case absolute error in power is:

$$\vert e_P \vert = \varepsilon_P \times P_{nominal} = 0.04 \times 2.5 \text{ W} = 0.1 \text{ W}$$

The range of possible power values:

$$P_{min} = 2.5 - 0.1 = 2.4 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{max} = 2.5 + 0.1 = 2.6 \text{ W}$$

**Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{P_{nominal} = 2.5 \text{ W}, \quad \varepsilon_P = \pm 4\%, \quad P \in [2.4 \text{ W}, 2.6 \text{ W}]}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing "% of FSD" with "% of True Value":** Many students apply the percentage directly to the measured value, which is only correct when the spec is "% of true value." When the spec is "% of FSD," you must multiply by FSD, not the measured value.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by the exponent in power rules:** For $V^2$, the error contribution is $2 \eta_V$, not $\eta_V$. For $V^{1/2}$ (square root), it is $0.5 \eta_V$.
> 3. **Sign errors in difference:** The relative error of a difference can be enormous if the two quantities are close. Always check the magnitude of the denominator.
> 4. **Mixing up absolute and percentage errors:** Carry units carefully. Absolute error has the same units as the measurement; percentage error is dimensionless.
> 5. **Not converting percentage to decimal:** Always convert "2%" to "0.02" before using in formulas.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Limiting Error = Guarantee Error = Worst-Case Error:** All three terms refer to the **maximum error** that the manufacturer commits will not be exceeded.
- **Two Reference Bases:**
  - **% of Full-Scale Deflection (FSD):** Constant absolute error; relative error worsens at low readings.
  - **% of True Value:** Absolute error scales with the reading; relative error is approximately constant.
- **Combination Rules (Worst-Case Limiting Error):**
  - **Sum or Difference:** $\varepsilon_R = \pm (\eta_1 + \eta_2)$
  - **Product or Quotient:** $\varepsilon_R = \pm (\eta_1 + \eta_2)$
  - **Power $(A^n)$:** $\varepsilon_R = \pm (n \cdot \eta_1)$
  - **Square Root $(A^{1/2})$:** $\varepsilon_R = \pm (0.5 \cdot \eta_1)$
- **Range of True Value:** $A_t \in [A_m - \vert e_L \vert, \, A_m + \vert e_L \vert]$
- **Always operate instruments near full-scale** for best relative accuracy.
- **Difference measurements are inherently error-prone** (subtractive cancellation).
- **Worst-case analysis assumes all errors occur simultaneously in the most unfavourable direction.**
- **KTU 2024 Scheme frequently tests:** Combination of errors in product/quotient/power forms, calculation of error ranges, and identification of the worst-case scenario in cascaded systems.

topics.title: Relative (Fractional) Limiting Error
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT205 - BASIC OF INSTRUMENTATION ENGINEERING (INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Relative (Fractional) Limiting Error

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 | Relative (Fractional) Limiting Error

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Anchor (PCICT205 - Module 4):** *Static and Dynamic Characteristics of Instruments — Errors in Measurement: Absolute Error, Relative Error, Limiting Error, and Relative (Fractional) Limiting Error.*

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Relative Limiting Error** (also called **Fractional Limiting Error**) is defined as the ratio of the **maximum absolute limiting error** in a measurement to the **nominal (true) value** of the quantity being measured, expressed either as a **dimensionless fraction** or as a **percentage**. It quantifies the *worst-case* measurement uncertainty normalized with respect to the measured quantity, thereby providing a scale-independent figure of merit for instrument accuracy.

Mathematically, the relative limiting error is given by:

$$\varepsilon_{r} = \frac{\delta A}{A} \quad \text{(dimensionless fraction)}$$

$$\varepsilon_{r}(\%) = \frac{\delta A}{A} \times 100 \quad \text{(percentage form)}$$

where:
- $\delta A$ = Magnitude of the absolute limiting error
- $A$ = Magnitude of the true (nominal) value of the measurand

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Two Friends Buying Mangoes" Analogy:**
>
> Imagine **Friend A** buys 10 mangoes for ₹100, but the shopkeeper gives him 1 extra mango by mistake. The *absolute error* is **1 mango**, and the *relative error* is **1/10 = 10%**.
>
> Now **Friend B** buys 100 mangoes for ₹1000, and gets 1 extra mango by mistake. The *absolute error* is still **1 mango**, but the *relative error* is only **1/100 = 1%**.
>
> Who is more accurately served? Clearly **Friend B**, because the same physical mistake (1 extra mango) is *relatively* much smaller compared to his bulk purchase.
>
> The **Relative Limiting Error** is the instrument designer's promise: *"No matter what the magnitude of the measurand, the maximum error I allow will be no more than a certain fixed fraction (or percentage) of that measurand."* This is the metric engineers use to compare instruments of different ranges and physical scales.

### 1.3 Significance in Instrumentation

> [!TIP]
> - **Absolute Error** depends on the *units* (Volts, °C, kg, etc.) and is therefore **not dimensionless**.
> - **Relative Error** is a **pure ratio** (no units) and is **scale-independent**, making it the gold standard for comparing two instruments with different ranges.
> - **Limiting** implies *guaranteed worst-case* — typically a manufacturer-specified tolerance (e.g., ±1% of reading, ±0.5% of full-scale).

---

### 1.4 Hierarchy of Error Terms (Foundation Recap)

| Term | Symbol | Definition | Dimensionality |
|:-----|:------:|:-----------|:--------------:|
| Absolute Error | $e_a$ | $e_a = A_m - A_t$ | Same as measurand |
| Absolute Limiting Error | $\delta A$ | Maximum guaranteed $e_a$ | Same as measurand |
| Relative Error | $e_r$ | $e_r = e_a / A_t$ | **Dimensionless** |
| Relative (Fractional) Limiting Error | $\varepsilon_r$ | $\varepsilon_r = \delta A / A$ | **Dimensionless** |
| Percentage Limiting Error | $\varepsilon_r(\%)$ | $\varepsilon_r \times 100$ | Expressed in % |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Trap:** A percentage of **1%** is *equal to* a fractional error of **0.01**, **NOT** 1. KTU examiners actively test this conversion. Always write: *Fraction = Percentage / 100.*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why We Need "Limiting" Errors

In practice, the **true value** $A_t$ is never known exactly. The **measured value** $A_m$ always carries a small but unknown deviation caused by:
- Manufacturing imperfections of the sensing element
- Calibration drift over time
- Environmental noise (temperature, humidity, EMI)
- Loading effect of the measuring instrument
- Observer parallax and quantization (in digital systems)

Because the *exact* error in a single reading is unknown, instrument manufacturers specify a **guaranteed maximum bound** on the error. This bound is the **Limiting Error**. When this bound is normalized by the true value, we obtain the **Relative (Fractional) Limiting Error**.

## 2.2 Two Reference Bases for the Denominator

KTU problems frequently hinge on which "A" sits in the denominator:

### Case A — Denominator = True (Measured) Value
$$\varepsilon_r = \frac{\delta A}{A_m} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \varepsilon_r = \frac{\delta A}{A_t}$$

This is used when the instrument accuracy is specified **"as a percentage of the reading"** (common in precision digital multimeters).

### Case B — Denominator = Full-Scale Value
$$\varepsilon_r = \frac{\delta A}{A_{FS}}$$

This is used when the instrument accuracy is specified **"as a percentage of full-scale deflection"** (common in analog panel meters, Bourdon gauges).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this distinction matters:** A voltmeter specified as ±2% of full-scale reading will be **most accurate at full-scale** and **least accurate near zero**. The same instrument at 1/10th of full-scale has a relative error of 20%! This is a frequently tested KTU concept.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **complete, exam-ready** formula set for this topic. Memorize it.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Conditions / Notes |
|:-:|:-------------|:-----------------------:|:------------------|
| 1 | Absolute Error | $e_a = A_m - A_t$ | Signed quantity (can be + or −) |
| 2 | Relative Error (True) | $e_r = e_a / A_t$ | $\vert e_r \vert \ll 1$ for "good" measurement |
| 3 | Relative Error (Measured) | $e_r \approx e_a / A_m$ | Used since $A_t$ is unknown |
| 4 | Absolute Limiting Error | $\delta A = \varepsilon_r \cdot A$ | Guaranteed worst-case |
| 5 | **Relative Limiting Error (Fractional)** | $\varepsilon_r = \delta A / A$ | Dimensionless fraction |
| 6 | **Relative Limiting Error (% Form)** | $\varepsilon_r(\%) = (\delta A / A) \times 100$ | Always express in % unless asked |
| 7 | Limiting Error referenced to Full-Scale | $\delta A = \varepsilon_r(\%) \times A_{FS} / 100$ | For analog meters |
| 8 | Resultant Limiting Error (product) | $\varepsilon_{r,total} = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,1}^2 + \varepsilon_{r,2}^2}$ | Independent random errors (RSS) |
| 9 | Resultant Limiting Error (sum) | $\varepsilon_{r,total} = \varepsilon_{r,1} + \varepsilon_{r,2}$ | Independent worst-case (absolute sum) |
| 10 | Reading at worst-case minimum | $A_{min} = \delta A / \varepsilon_{r,specified}$ | Used to find safe operating range |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sensor Datasheets:** Almost all industrial sensors (RTD, thermocouple, strain gauge, LVDT) quote accuracy as a *relative limiting error*, e.g., *"Class A PT100 — ±0.15 °C at 0 °C"*, which when normalized to 100 °C gives a relative error of 0.15%.
- **Process Control:** In a cascade of instruments (sensor → transmitter → ADC → controller → DAC → final control element), each block's **relative** error is added (RSS method) to predict overall loop accuracy.
- **Calibration Strategy:** Helps in deciding whether calibration is needed based on whether the *relative* error exceeds a process tolerance.
- **Quality Assurance (ISO 9001):** Measurement uncertainty budgets are universally expressed in *relative* terms to allow comparison across different physical quantities.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Absolute Error to Relative Limiting Error

**Starting Premise:** A manufacturer guarantees that any single measurement $A_m$ will not deviate from the true value $A_t$ by more than a stated tolerance, which we call the **absolute limiting error** $\delta A$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Definition of absolute limiting error} \quad & \delta A = \vert A_m - A_t \vert_{max} \\
\text{Step 2: Definition of true value} \quad & A_t \approx A_m \quad (\text{true value is approximately the measured value for a "good" instrument}) \\
\text{Step 3: Forming the ratio} \quad & \frac{\delta A}{A} = \frac{\delta A}{A_t} \approx \frac{\delta A}{A_m} \\
\text{Step 4: Define the relative limiting error} \quad & \varepsilon_{r} \equiv \frac{\delta A}{A} \\
\text{Step 5: Percentage conversion} \quad & \varepsilon_{r}(\%) = \varepsilon_{r} \times 100 = \frac{\delta A}{A} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The **relative limiting error** is *dimensionless* and represents the fractional deviation of the measurand from its true value under worst-case conditions.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Absolute Error in Terms of Percentage Accuracy

**Given:** An instrument is rated at $\pm \eta \,\%$ of full-scale $A_{FS}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Convert percentage to fraction} \quad & \eta_{frac} = \frac{\eta}{100} \\
\text{Step 2: Maximum absolute error} \quad & \delta A = \eta_{frac} \cdot A_{FS} = \frac{\eta}{100} \cdot A_{FS} \\
\text{Step 3: Relative error at a reading } A \quad & \varepsilon_{r} = \frac{\delta A}{A} = \frac{\eta \cdot A_{FS}}{100 \cdot A} \\
\text{Step 4: Substitute } A = A_{FS} \text{ (full-scale case)} \quad & \varepsilon_{r}\vert_{FS} = \frac{\eta \cdot A_{FS}}{100 \cdot A_{FS}} = \frac{\eta}{100} \\
\text{Step 5: Substitute } A = A_{FS}/n \text{ (n-th of full-scale)} \quad & \varepsilon_{r}\vert_{A_{FS}/n} = \frac{\eta \cdot A_{FS}}{100 \cdot (A_{FS}/n)} = \frac{n \eta}{100}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Key Insight:** As $n$ increases (i.e., as the reading moves further from full-scale), the **relative error grows linearly**. At 1/10th of full-scale, the relative error is **10 times** the rated accuracy — this is the **"downscale accuracy degradation"** principle.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 1 (KTU-Style 3-Mark Problem)

**Problem:** A voltmeter reads **117 V** when the true voltage is **120 V**. Calculate the (a) absolute error, (b) relative error, and (c) relative error as a percentage.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(a) Absolute Error:} \quad e_a &= A_m - A_t = 117 - 120 = -3 \text{ V} \\
\textbf{(b) Relative Error (fractional):} \quad e_r &= \frac{e_a}{A_t} = \frac{-3}{120} = -0.025 \\
\textbf{(c) Relative Error (percentage):} \quad e_r(\%) &= -0.025 \times 100 = -2.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> The **negative sign** indicates the meter is *under-reading*. In KTU answers, you may write "the meter reads 2.5% low" instead of carrying the sign.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 2 (KTU-Style 14-Mark Sub-Problem)

**Problem:** A pressure gauge with a full-scale range of **0–10 bar** is specified with an accuracy of **±2% of full-scale**. The gauge is currently reading **4 bar**. Determine:
(a) The absolute limiting error and the percentage limiting error at this reading.
(b) The range of true pressures possible.

**Solution (a):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given:} \quad & A_{FS} = 10 \text{ bar}, \quad \eta = 2\%, \quad A_m = 4 \text{ bar} \\
\text{Step 1: Absolute limiting error} \quad & \delta A = \frac{\eta}{100} \cdot A_{FS} = \frac{2}{100} \times 10 = \pm 0.2 \text{ bar} \\
\text{Step 2: Percentage limiting error at 4 bar} \quad & \varepsilon_r(\%) = \frac{\delta A}{A_m} \times 100 = \frac{0.2}{4} \times 100 = 5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution (b):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lower bound: } A_t^{min} &= A_m - \delta A = 4 - 0.2 = 3.8 \text{ bar} \\
\text{Upper bound: } A_t^{max} &= A_m + \delta A = 4 + 0.2 = 4.2 \text{ bar} \\
\text{Range of true pressure} \quad & \boxed{3.8 \text{ bar} \leq A_t \leq 4.2 \text{ bar}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that the **same physical instrument** is described as **±2% of full-scale** (manufacturer's quote) but acts as **±5% of reading** (user-experienced accuracy) at 4 bar. KTU questions often ask you to compute both views.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Error Magnitude vs. Reading Position

| Reading $A$ (bar) | $\delta A$ (bar) | Relative Error $\varepsilon_r(\%)$ |
|:-----------------:|:----------------:|:----------------------------------:|
| 10 (Full-Scale)   | ±0.20            | ±2.0%                              |
| 8                 | ±0.20            | ±2.5%                              |
| 5                 | ±0.20            | ±4.0%                              |
| 4                 | ±0.20            | ±5.0%                              |
| 2                 | ±0.20            | ±10.0%                             |
| 1                 | ±0.20            | ±20.0%                             |
| 0.5               | ±0.20            | ±40.0%                             |

> [!WARNING]
> **Visual Lesson:** The table above shows that using an analog meter in the **lower 1/4 of its scale is engineering malpractice** — a 40% relative error is unacceptable for any control loop. Always operate analog meters in the **upper 1/3 of the scale**.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 3: Combination of Independent Relative Limiting Errors (RSS Method)

When two independent measurements (e.g., resistance $R$ and current $I$) are combined to compute power $P = I^2 R$, the relative error in $P$ must be derived carefully.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Differentiate } P = I^2 R \quad & dP = 2IR\,dI + I^2\,dR \\
\text{Step 2: Form the relative differential} \quad & \frac{dP}{P} = \frac{2IR\,dI}{I^2 R} + \frac{I^2\,dR}{I^2 R} = 2\frac{dI}{I} + \frac{dR}{R} \\
\text{Step 3: Replace infinitesimals with limiting errors} \quad & \frac{\delta P}{P} = 2\frac{\delta I}{I} + \frac{\delta R}{R} \\
\text{Step 4: Convert to relative limiting errors} \quad & \varepsilon_{r,P} = 2\varepsilon_{r,I} + \varepsilon_{r,R} \quad \text{(absolute worst-case sum)}\\
\text{Step 5: RSS form for random errors} \quad & \varepsilon_{r,P} = \sqrt{(2\varepsilon_{r,I})^2 + (\varepsilon_{r,R})^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Application:** This is the standard technique used in **uncertainty propagation** for power, energy, and impedance measurements, and appears frequently in KTU Part B problems.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Figure 1 — Error Hierarchy & Conversion Flowchart

The following Mermaid diagram maps the *conceptual hierarchy* of all measurement errors discussed in this topic, showing the exact transformation path from a physical measurand to a relative limiting error percentage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Measurand True Value At] --> B[Instrument Reads Measured Value Am]
    B --> C{Compute Absolute Error}
    C --> D["ea = Am - At (Volts, bar, degC, etc.)"]
    D --> E{Specify Worst Case Bound}
    E --> F["Absolute Limiting Error delta A = max |ea|"]
    F --> G{Normalize by True Value}
    G --> H["Fractional Error = delta A / At (dimensionless)"]
    H --> I[Convert to Percentage]
    I --> J["Relative Limiting Error percent = delta A over At times 100 percent"]
    F --> K{Reference: Full Scale or Reading}
    K --> L["delta A as percent of AFS (analog meters)"]
    K --> M["delta A as percent of A reading (digital meters)"]
    L --> H
    M --> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide for Students:** Follow the arrows top-to-bottom. Note the two parallel branches (L and M) at the bottom — they represent the **two reference bases** discussed in Section 2.2, both converging into the same dimensionless ratio.

---

## 4.2 Figure 2 — Downscale Accuracy Degradation Block Diagram

This schematic illustrates *why* analog-panel-meter specifications must always be qualified by the operating point on the scale.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Manufacturer Label: 2 percent of Full Scale] --> B0[FS value = 10 bar]
    B0 --> C0[Constant delta A = 0.2 bar everywhere on scale]
    C0 --> D0{Operating Point A}
    D0 --> E1[A = 10 bar FS]
    D0 --> E2[A = 5 bar half scale]
    D0 --> E3[A = 1 bar tenth scale]
    E1 --> F1[Relative Error = 2 percent]
    E2 --> F2[Relative Error = 4 percent]
    E3 --> F3[Relative Error = 20 percent]
    F1 --> G[Rule: Operate in upper third of scale for minimum relative error]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** This is the **single most-tested visual concept** in KTU Module 4. A 2%-of-full-scale instrument is *2% accurate only at full scale*; everywhere else it is *worse*. The block diagram above is your revision-ready mnemonic.

---

## 4.3 Figure 3 — Functional Block Architecture of an Error Propagation Chain

When multiple instruments are cascaded in a measurement chain (sensor → signal conditioner → ADC → display), their **relative limiting errors combine** following the architecture below.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Sensor Block er1] --> S2[Signal Conditioner er2]
    S2 --> S3[ADC Block er3]
    S3 --> S4[Display Block er4]
    S4 --> X{Combination Rule}
    X --> Y1[Absolute Sum er total = er1 + er2 + er3 + er4 worst case]
    X --> Y2[Root Sum Square er total = sqrt of sum of er squared random]
    Y1 --> Z[Final Reported Value with Total Relative Limiting Error]
    Y2 --> Z
```

> [!TIP]
> **Decision Rule for KTU Problems:**
> - If the problem says **"guaranteed maximum"** or **"worst-case"** → use the **absolute sum** (Y1).
> - If the problem says **"independent random errors"** or **"statistical"** → use the **RSS** formula (Y2).
> - If not specified, KTU convention is to use the **absolute sum** unless RSS is explicitly requested.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** Define *Relative Limiting Error* and *Fractional Limiting Error*. Show that they are mathematically equivalent.

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Distribution: 1 + 1 + 1):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** The **relative limiting error** is the ratio of the absolute limiting error $\delta A$ to the true value $A_t$ of the measurand, expressed as a fraction or percentage.
- **Definition (1 mark):** The **fractional limiting error** is the same quantity expressed in decimal (non-percentage) form.
- **Equivalence (1 mark):** Both refer to the same dimensionless ratio $\varepsilon_r = \delta A / A_t$. The "fractional" form is just $\varepsilon_r$, while the "relative limiting error percentage" is $\varepsilon_r \times 100$. Hence, *"Fractional Limiting Error = (Relative Limiting Error in %) / 100."*

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** A voltmeter has a full-scale range of **300 V** and an accuracy class of **1.0**. Calculate the relative limiting error (in %) when the meter reads **(i) 300 V** and **(ii) 50 V**.

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Distribution: 1.5 + 1.5):**

- **Step 1 (0.5 mark):** Absolute limiting error $\delta A = (1.0/100) \times 300 = \pm 3 \text{ V}$.
- **(i) At 300 V (1 mark):** $\varepsilon_r(\%) = (3/300) \times 100 = \pm 1.0\%$.
- **(ii) At 50 V (1 mark):** $\varepsilon_r(\%) = (3/50) \times 100 = \pm 6.0\%$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Convention:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks split into sub-parts (typically 7 + 7). Both alternative choices **A and B** below are full 14-mark questions. Solve **either A or B** as per the question paper.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**Q:**
**(a)** Derive the expression for the *relative limiting error* of a quantity $y = (A^2 \cdot B) / C^{1/2}$, where $A$, $B$, $C$ are independent measured quantities with absolute limiting errors $\delta A$, $\delta B$, $\delta C$ respectively. State clearly any assumption made. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In a circuit, the power dissipated is given by $P = V^2 / R$. A voltmeter reads $V = 100 \pm 1 \text{ V}$ and an ohmmeter reads $R = 50 \pm 0.5 \text{ }\Omega$. Determine:
  - **(i)** The nominal value of $P$.
  - **(ii)** The absolute limiting error in $P$.
  - **(iii)** The relative limiting error in $P$ (in %). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1 (1 mark):} \quad & y = \frac{A^2 \cdot B}{C^{1/2}} \\
\textbf{Step 2 (2 marks):} \quad & \text{Take natural log: } \ln y = 2 \ln A + \ln B - \tfrac{1}{2} \ln C \\
\textbf{Step 3 (2 marks):} \quad & \text{Differentiate: } \frac{dy}{y} = 2\frac{dA}{A} + \frac{dB}{B} - \frac{1}{2}\frac{dC}{C} \\
\textbf{Step 4 (1 mark):} \quad & \text{Replace differentials with limiting errors:} \\
& \frac{\delta y}{y} = 2\frac{\delta A}{A} + \frac{\delta B}{B} - \frac{1}{2}\frac{\delta C}{C} \\
\textbf{Step 5 (1 mark):} \quad & \text{Assumption: errors are small, independent, and worst-case (absolute sum).}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Expression (boxed for 7th mark):**
$$\varepsilon_{r,y} = 2\varepsilon_{r,A} + \varepsilon_{r,B} + \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_{r,C}$$

**Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(i) Nominal P (2 marks):} \quad & P = \frac{V^2}{R} = \frac{100^2}{50} = \frac{10000}{50} = 200 \text{ W} \\
\textbf{(ii) Absolute error (3 marks):} \quad & \varepsilon_{r,P} = 2\varepsilon_{r,V} + \varepsilon_{r,R} = 2\left(\frac{1}{100}\right) + \left(\frac{0.5}{50}\right) \\
& \varepsilon_{r,P} = 0.02 + 0.01 = 0.03 \\
& \delta P = \varepsilon_{r,P} \times P = 0.03 \times 200 = \pm 6 \text{ W} \\
\textbf{(iii) Relative error in \% (2 marks):} \quad & \varepsilon_{r,P}(\%) = 0.03 \times 100 = 3\%
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**Q:**
**(a)** A temperature sensor with a full-scale range of **0–200 °C** has an accuracy specification of **±0.5% of full-scale**. It is used to measure a temperature of **80 °C**. Calculate:
  - **(i)** The absolute limiting error.
  - **(ii)** The relative limiting error in percentage.
  - **(iii)** The minimum temperature for which the relative error does not exceed **1%**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain, with a suitable diagram, the *downscale accuracy degradation* of an analog panel meter. Show mathematically why operating near full-scale is preferred. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(i) Absolute error (2 marks):} \quad & \delta A = \frac{0.5}{100} \times 200 = \pm 1.0 \text{ °C} \\
\textbf{(ii) Relative error at 80 °C (2 marks):} \quad & \varepsilon_r(\%) = \frac{1.0}{80} \times 100 = 1.25\% \\
\textbf{(iii) Minimum temperature for 1% relative error (3 marks):} \quad & A_{min} = \frac{\delta A}{\varepsilon_{r,specified}} = \frac{1.0}{0.01} = 100 \text{ °C}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** The meter must read at least **100 °C** for the relative error to stay at or below 1%. Below that, the relative error exceeds the allowed tolerance.

**Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

**Explanation (4 marks):**
Analog panel meters are typically specified as "*±X% of full-scale*". This means the **absolute limiting error $\delta A$ is constant** across the entire scale (it does not shrink as the reading gets smaller). Because the relative error is $\varepsilon_r = \delta A / A$, as the reading $A$ decreases, the relative error increases **inversely**. Hence, the instrument becomes progressively less accurate as we move from full-scale toward zero — this is called **downscale accuracy degradation**.

**Mathematical Proof (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_r(A) &= \frac{\delta A}{A} = \frac{\eta \cdot A_{FS} / 100}{A} = \frac{\eta \cdot A_{FS}}{100 \cdot A} \\
\frac{d\varepsilon_r}{dA} &= -\frac{\eta \cdot A_{FS}}{100 \cdot A^2} < 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the derivative is **negative**, $\varepsilon_r$ is a **monotonically decreasing function of $A$** — i.e., error falls as we go up-scale. Hence, operating near full-scale minimizes relative error.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in This Topic (Recurring KTU Errors):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "% of full-scale" with "% of reading" (–2 marks):** A 1% FS meter at half-scale gives 2% relative error, not 1%. Always re-derive $\varepsilon_r$ from the actual reading.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert percentage to fraction (–1 mark):** When plugging $\eta$ into $\delta A = (\eta/100) \times A_{FS}$, students often write $\eta \times A_{FS}$ and get a value 100× too large.
> 3. **Sign handling (–1 mark):** Absolute error is signed ($+$, $-$, or $\pm$); relative error magnitude is taken as positive even when absolute error is negative. The KTU marking scheme deducts for sign inconsistency.
> 4. **Mixing RSS and Absolute Sum (–2 marks):** KTU exam questions *state explicitly* which method to use. If "worst-case" is mentioned, use sum; if "random/statistical" is mentioned, use RSS. Default = absolute sum.
> 5. **Skipping units in the final answer (–0.5 mark):** Always state the unit of $\delta A$ (V, °C, bar, etc.). Percentage errors are dimensionless.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Print-Ready):**

- [ ] **Definition:** Relative Limiting Error = $\delta A / A$ (dimensionless fraction) or $\times 100$ for percentage.
- [ ] **Limiting = Worst-Case:** Always assume the *maximum* allowed deviation, not an average.
- [ ] **Two Reference Bases:** % of Full-Scale (analog meters) vs. % of Reading (digital meters). Both feed into the *same* $\delta A$.
- [ ] **Downscale Degradation:** $\delta A$ is constant; $\varepsilon_r$ grows as $1/A$. Operate in the upper third of any analog scale.
- [ ] **Conversion Rule:** Fraction = (Percentage) / 100. Never reverse this.
- [ ] **Combination Formulae:**
    - Sum: $\varepsilon_{r,total} = \varepsilon_{r,1} + \varepsilon_{r,2} + \dots$ (worst-case)
    - RSS: $\varepsilon_{r,total} = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,1}^2 + \varepsilon_{r,2}^2 + \dots}$ (random independent)
- [ ] **Power Rule:** For $P = I^n R^m$, $\varepsilon_{r,P} = n \varepsilon_{r,I} + m \varepsilon_{r,R}$ (absolute sum form).
- [ ] **Minimum Reading Formula:** $A_{min} = \delta A / \varepsilon_{r,allowed}$ — useful for selecting the right instrument range.
- [ ] **Sign Convention:** Absolute error is signed; relative-error magnitude is positive; always quote the *magnitude* unless sign is asked.
- [ ] **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** For measurement chains, *never let the worst single-block error exceed 1/3 of the total budget* (3-sigma heuristic).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Combination of Quantities with Limiting Errors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT205 - BASIC OF INSTRUMENTATION ENGINEERING (INSTRUMENT AND CONTROL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Combination of Quantities with Limiting Errors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In any physical measurement system, no instrument can produce a value that is **exactly equal** to the true value of the measurand. Every reading carries an inherent uncertainty, known as the **Error** of measurement. The **Limiting Error** (also called the **Guaranteed Accuracy Error** or **Worst-Case Error**) of an instrument is the maximum deviation of the indicated value from the true value, as specified by the manufacturer under standard reference conditions.

If a measured quantity is denoted as $A_m$ and the limiting (absolute) error is $\delta A$, then the true value $A_t$ is guaranteed to lie within a band defined by:

$$A_t = A_m \pm \delta A$$

This means the manufacturer **guarantees** that the true value lies somewhere in the closed interval $[A_m - \delta A,\; A_m + \delta A]$. The corresponding **Relative Limiting Error** and **Percentage Limiting Error** are defined as:

$$\epsilon_r = \frac{\delta A}{A_m} \quad ; \quad \% \epsilon_r = \frac{\delta A}{A_m} \times 100$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Limiting errors are *deterministic bounds*, not statistical standard deviations. They represent the **worst-case** scenario and use simple **arithmetic addition** (not root-sum-square) of component errors during combination.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Tolerance" Picture

Imagine you are a pharmacist compounding a medicine. The active ingredient is labeled "500 mg ± 5 mg." This ± 5 mg is the **limiting error**. The manufacturer is *guaranteeing* the pill contains between 495 mg and 505 mg of the active drug.

Now, suppose your prescription requires the **sum of two drug masses** (say, drug A and drug B). The *worst-case* total mass you could actually be giving the patient is not simply the sum of nominal values — it is the sum of nominal values **plus the sum of the two limiting errors**. This is the essence of error combination: in the absence of statistical data, you must assume every component is at its worst extreme simultaneously.

A second analogy is **engineering tolerances on mechanical parts**. If shaft A has tolerance ± 0.1 mm and bore B has tolerance ± 0.1 mm, the clearance between them (their difference) can vary by up to ± 0.2 mm — the absolute errors **add up** directly, but because the clearance might be small in magnitude, the *relative* error can become dangerously large.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Instrumentation

In real measurement chains (transducers, signal conditioners, ADC, displays), the **final result is almost always a combination of several sub-measurements**. The voltmeter might multiply current and resistance; the flow computer might multiply differential pressure by a square-root and a calibration constant. Knowing how individual errors combine allows the engineer to:

- **Predict** the overall accuracy of a measurement chain *before* building it.
- **Allocate** the error budget intelligently to sub-components.
- **Identify** which sub-measurement contributes most to the total error (the **sensitivity rule**).
- **Choose** the proper combination rule (sum/difference vs. product/quotient).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Reference Constants used in this module:**
> - Standard gravity $g = 9.80665 \; \text{m/s}^2$ (used in manometer / pressure problems)
> - Density of water at 4 °C: $\rho_{water} = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
> - Standard atmospheric pressure $P_{atm} = 101.325 \; \text{kPa}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Canonical Combination Cases

The KTU 2024 syllabus focuses on **five fundamental error-combination operations**. Every complex expression can be reduced to a sequence of these five. They are:

1. **Sum of quantities** $\rightarrow$ $R = A + B + C + \dots$
2. **Difference of quantities** $\rightarrow$ $R = A - B$
3. **Product of quantities** $\rightarrow$ $R = A \cdot B \cdot C$
4. **Quotient of quantities** $\rightarrow$ $R = A / B$
5. **Power of a quantity** $\rightarrow$ $R = A^n$

A sixth, more general, case is the **arbitrary multivariable function** $R = f(A, B, C, \dots)$ handled via the **partial-derivative rule**.

## 2.2 Logical Reasoning Behind Each Combination

- **Case 1 & 2 (Sum / Difference):** The absolute limiting error of the result is the **arithmetic sum of the absolute limiting errors** of the individual quantities. This is because, in the worst case, *every* component deviates in the direction that *maximises* the deviation of the result. For the sum, both positive deviations add; for the difference, a positive deviation in the minuend *and* a negative deviation in the subtrahend both push the difference in the same worst-case direction.
- **Case 3 & 4 (Product / Quotient):** The **relative limiting errors add** (magnitudes only). Physically, this happens because multiplying or dividing scales the *magnitude* of the error along with the result, so the *fractional* uncertainty is what accumulates.
- **Case 5 (Power):** The relative limiting error is multiplied by the power index. This follows from a first-order Taylor expansion of $A^n$ around $A_m$.

> [!TIP]
> **The Golden Rule for KTU Board Exams:** Whenever you see a question combining measured quantities:
> - If the combination is a **sum or difference** → work with **absolute errors** (add them).
> - If the combination is a **product, quotient, or power** → work with **relative errors** (add them).
> - The final step is always to convert back to the form asked by the question (absolute or percentage).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Combination Form | Absolute Limiting Error $\delta R$ | Relative Limiting Error $\epsilon_R = \delta R / R_m$ | Percentage Limiting Error $\% \epsilon_R$ |
|---|------------------|------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------|
| 1 | $R = A + B$ | $\pm (\delta A + \delta B)$ | $\pm \dfrac{\delta A + \delta B}{A_m + B_m}$ | $\pm \dfrac{\delta A + \delta B}{A_m + B_m} \times 100$ |
| 2 | $R = A - B$ | $\pm (\delta A + \delta B)$ | $\pm \dfrac{\delta A + \delta B}{A_m - B_m}$ | $\pm \dfrac{\delta A + \delta B}{A_m - B_m} \times 100$ |
| 3 | $R = A \cdot B$ | $B_m \, \delta A + A_m \, \delta B$ | $\pm \left( \dfrac{\delta A}{A_m} + \dfrac{\delta B}{B_m} \right)$ | $\pm \left( \epsilon_A + \epsilon_B \right) \times 100$ |
| 4 | $R = A / B$ | $\dfrac{B_m \, \delta A - A_m \, \delta B}{B_m^{\,2}}$ | $\pm \left( \dfrac{\delta A}{A_m} + \dfrac{\delta B}{B_m} \right)$ | $\pm \left( \epsilon_A + \epsilon_B \right) \times 100$ |
| 5 | $R = A^n$ | $\pm n \, A_m^{\,n-1} \, \delta A$ | $\pm \, n \, \dfrac{\delta A}{A_m} = n \, \epsilon_A$ | $\pm \, n \, \epsilon_A \times 100$ |
| 6 (General) | $R = f(A, B, C)$ | $\pm \left( \left\vert \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial A} \right\vert \delta A + \left\vert \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial B} \right\vert \delta B + \left\vert \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial C} \right\vert \delta C \right)$ | $\dfrac{\delta R}{R_m}$ | $\dfrac{\delta R}{R_m} \times 100$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, the notation $\left\vert \cdot \right\vert$ denotes absolute magnitude (used when adding worst-case errors). The pipe symbol is **deliberately avoided inside the table** to preserve markdown structure — read it as mathematical "modulus."

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of These Rules

- **Power meter design:** Active power $P = V \cdot I \cdot \cos \phi$ — relative errors of V, I, and $\cos\phi$ add directly.
- **Orifice-plate flow metering:** Volumetric flow $Q = C_d \cdot A \sqrt{2 g h}$ — the discharge coefficient $C_d$ and orifice area $A$ appear linearly (relative error contribution), while $h$ appears under a square root (its error contribution is halved).
- **Resistance measurement by volt-amp method:** $R = V/I$ — relative errors of voltmeter and ammeter add.
- **Strain-gauge bridge calibration:** Output $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot (\Delta R / 4R)$ — relative errors of excitation voltage and gauge factor add.

These rules are the **backbone of every instrument data-sheet** that publishes a "total accuracy" figure, because the manufacturer has already combined the individual error sources using exactly these formulas.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Convention:** In all derivations, capital letters $A, B, R$ denote **nominal (measured) values**, and $\delta A, \delta B, \delta R$ denote their **absolute limiting errors**. We assume all errors are *small* (first-order), so products of two errors ($\delta A \cdot \delta B$) are neglected as second-order infinitesimals.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sum of Two Quantities $R = A + B$

**Step 1 — Express true values with their errors:**
The true value of $A$ is $A + \delta A$, and of $B$ is $B + \delta B$, where $\delta A$ and $\delta B$ are the individual limiting errors.

**Step 2 — Compute the true value of the sum:**
By definition, the true value of $R$ is the true value of $A$ plus the true value of $B$:

$$R + \delta R = (A + \delta A) + (B + \delta B)$$

**Step 3 — Group nominal terms and error terms:**

$$R + \delta R = (A + B) + (\delta A + \delta B)$$

**Step 4 — Identify the nominal and the error parts:**
Since $R = A + B$ is the nominal result, the *additional* part is the limiting error of the result:

$$\delta R = \delta A + \delta B$$

In worst-case (limiting) form, we attach the ± sign to denote both extremes:

$$\boxed{\delta R = \pm (\delta A + \delta B)}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the relative error:**

$$\epsilon_R = \frac{\delta R}{R} = \pm \frac{\delta A + \delta B}{A + B}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** The absolute errors *add directly*. The relative error is *smaller* when the sum is large, but *larger* when the sum is small. This is why instrument engineers try to *measure small signals additively* rather than subtractively.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Difference of Two Quantities $R = A - B$

**Step 1 — Set up the true values:**
Same as before: true $A = A + \delta A$, true $B = B + \delta B$.

**Step 2 — Compute true value of the difference:**

$$R + \delta R = (A + \delta A) - (B + \delta B)$$

**Step 3 — Group terms:**

$$R + \delta R = (A - B) + (\delta A - \delta B)$$

**Step 4 — Identify the error:**
With $R = A - B$, the residual error is $\delta R = \delta A - \delta B$.

**Step 5 — Take the worst-case (limiting) magnitude:**
A positive $\delta A$ and a negative $\delta B$ both *increase* $R$ in magnitude (in opposite directions of the sign convention), so the worst case is:

$$\boxed{\delta R = \pm (\delta A + \delta B)}$$

**Step 6 — Relative error of the difference:**

$$\epsilon_R = \pm \frac{\delta A + \delta B}{A - B}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Subtractive Cancellation Pitfall:** If $A \approx B$ (the two readings are nearly equal), the denominator $A - B$ is small, so the relative error $\epsilon_R$ can become **huge** even if the absolute errors are modest. This is the well-known **subtractive-cancellation** problem in differential measurements. KTU examiners frequently test this with a "two-resistor subtraction" or "manometer reading" problem.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Product of Two Quantities $R = A \cdot B$

**Step 1 — True values:** true $A = A + \delta A$, true $B = B + \delta B$.

**Step 2 — True value of the product:**

$$R + \delta R = (A + \delta A)(B + \delta B)$$

**Step 3 — Expand the product:**

$$R + \delta R = AB + A\,\delta B + B\,\delta A + \delta A \cdot \delta B$$

**Step 4 — Neglect the second-order term:**
Since $\delta A$ and $\delta B$ are both small, $\delta A \cdot \delta B$ is *second-order* (much smaller) and is dropped:

$$R + \delta R \approx AB + A\,\delta B + B\,\delta A$$

**Step 5 — Recognise $R = AB$ and isolate the error:**

$$\boxed{\delta R = \pm \big( B\,\delta A + A\,\delta B \big)}$$

**Step 6 — Form the relative error by dividing by $R = AB$:**

$$\epsilon_R = \frac{\delta R}{R} = \frac{B\,\delta A + A\,\delta B}{A \cdot B} = \frac{\delta A}{A} + \frac{\delta B}{B}$$

In worst-case limiting form:

$$\boxed{\epsilon_R = \pm \big( \epsilon_A + \epsilon_B \big)}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Quotient of Two Quantities $R = A / B$

**Step 1 — True values:** true $A = A + \delta A$, true $B = B + \delta B$.

**Step 2 — True value of the quotient:**

$$R + \delta R = \frac{A + \delta A}{B + \delta B}$$

**Step 3 — Normalise by dividing numerator and denominator by $B$:**

$$R + \delta R = \frac{\dfrac{A}{B} + \dfrac{\delta A}{B}}{1 + \dfrac{\delta B}{B}}$$

**Step 4 — Let $x = \dfrac{\delta B}{B} \ll 1$ and use the series expansion $\dfrac{1}{1+x} \approx 1 - x$ for small $x$:**

$$R + \delta R \approx \left( \frac{A}{B} + \frac{\delta A}{B} \right) \left( 1 - \frac{\delta B}{B} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Expand and drop second-order terms:**

$$R + \delta R \approx \frac{A}{B} + \frac{\delta A}{B} - \frac{A}{B} \cdot \frac{\delta B}{B} = \frac{A}{B} + \frac{1}{B}\left( \delta A - \frac{A\,\delta B}{B} \right)$$

**Step 6 — Recognise $R = A/B$ and isolate the error term:**

$$\delta R = \pm \frac{1}{B}\left( \delta A + \frac{A\,\delta B}{B} \right) = \pm \left( \frac{\delta A}{B} + \frac{A\,\delta B}{B^2} \right)$$

Equivalently:

$$\boxed{\delta R = \pm \frac{B\,\delta A - A\,\delta B}{B^2}}$$

**Step 7 — Form the relative error (divide by $R = A/B$):**

$$\epsilon_R = \frac{\delta R}{R} = \frac{\dfrac{B\,\delta A - A\,\delta B}{B^2}}{\dfrac{A}{B}} = \frac{\delta A}{A} - \frac{\delta B}{B}$$

Taking worst-case magnitudes (since the deviation of the divisor in the *opposite* direction from the dividend maximises the result):

$$\boxed{\epsilon_R = \pm \big( \epsilon_A + \epsilon_B \big)}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Power of a Quantity $R = A^n$

**Step 1 — True value of the base:** $A_{true} = A + \delta A$.

**Step 2 — True value of the power:**

$$R + \delta R = (A + \delta A)^n$$

**Step 3 — Apply the binomial expansion** (valid for small $\delta A$):

$$(A + \delta A)^n = A^n + n A^{n-1}\,\delta A + \underbrace{\frac{n(n-1)}{2}A^{n-2}(\delta A)^2 + \dots}_{\text{second and higher order}} $$

**Step 4 — Drop higher-order terms:**

$$R + \delta R \approx A^n + n A^{n-1}\,\delta A$$

**Step 5 — Identify $R = A^n$ and isolate the error:**

$$\boxed{\delta R = \pm \, n \, A^{n-1} \, \delta A}$$

**Step 6 — Form the relative error:**

$$\epsilon_R = \frac{\delta R}{R} = \frac{n A^{n-1}\,\delta A}{A^n} = n \cdot \frac{\delta A}{A} = n\,\epsilon_A$$

$$\boxed{\epsilon_R = \pm \, n \, \epsilon_A}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Special case $n = 1/2$ (square root):** The relative error is *halved*. This is why square-root extraction (e.g., in flow measurement from differential pressure) is *error-friendly* — the squareroot compresses the uncertainty.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — General Function $R = f(A, B, C)$ (Partial Derivative Rule)

**Step 1 — First-order Taylor expansion of the multivariable function about the nominal point $(A, B, C)$:**

$$R + \delta R = f(A + \delta A,\; B + \delta B,\; C + \delta C)$$

**Step 2 — Expand to first order:**

$$R + \delta R \approx f(A, B, C) + \frac{\partial f}{\partial A}\delta A + \frac{\partial f}{\partial B}\delta B + \frac{\partial f}{\partial C}\delta C$$

**Step 3 — With $R = f(A, B, C)$, isolate the error term:**

$$\boxed{\delta R = \pm \left( \left\vert \frac{\partial f}{\partial A} \right\vert \delta A + \left\vert \frac{\partial f}{\partial B} \right\vert \delta B + \left\vert \frac{\partial f}{\partial C} \right\vert \delta C \right)}$$

**Step 4 — Relative error is then $\epsilon_R = \delta R / R$:**

$$\epsilon_R = \pm \frac{1}{R}\left( \left\vert \frac{\partial f}{\partial A} \right\vert \delta A + \left\vert \frac{\partial f}{\partial B} \right\vert \delta B + \left\vert \frac{\partial f}{\partial C} \right\vert \delta C \right)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sensitivity Coefficients:** The partial derivatives $\partial f / \partial A$, $\partial f / \partial B$, etc., are called the *sensitivity coefficients* of the result $R$ with respect to each input. The largest one tells the engineer which sub-measurement needs the tightest calibration.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Worked Mini-Examples

**Example A (Sum):** Two resistors in series: $R_1 = 100 \pm 2 \; \Omega$, $R_2 = 50 \pm 1 \; \Omega$. Find total resistance and its percentage error.

$$R_{total} = 100 + 50 = 150 \; \Omega$$
$$\delta R = 2 + 1 = 3 \; \Omega$$
$$\% \epsilon_R = \frac{3}{150} \times 100 = 2\%$$

**Example B (Product):** Power $P = V \cdot I$, with $V = 200 \pm 2 \; \text{V}$, $I = 5 \pm 0.1 \; \text{A}$.

$$P = 200 \times 5 = 1000 \; \text{W}$$
$$\epsilon_P = \frac{2}{200} + \frac{0.1}{5} = 0.01 + 0.02 = 0.03$$
$$\% \epsilon_P = 3\% \quad \Rightarrow \quad P = 1000 \pm 30 \; \text{W}$$

**Example C (Quotient):** Resistance $R = V/I$, with $V = 10 \pm 0.2 \; \text{V}$, $I = 2 \pm 0.05 \; \text{A}$.

$$R = 10/2 = 5 \; \Omega$$
$$\epsilon_R = \frac{0.2}{10} + \frac{0.05}{2} = 0.02 + 0.025 = 0.045$$
$$\% \epsilon_R = 4.5\% \quad \Rightarrow \quad R = 5 \pm 0.225 \; \Omega$$

**Example D (Power — square root):** Flow $Q = K \sqrt{h}$, with $K = 10 \pm 0.2$, $h = 25 \pm 0.5 \; \text{m}$.

$$Q = 10 \sqrt{25} = 50 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$
$$\epsilon_Q = \frac{0.2}{10} + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{0.5}{25} = 0.02 + 0.01 = 0.03$$
$$\% \epsilon_Q = 3\% \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q = 50 \pm 1.5 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Tree — Which Combination Rule Applies?

This flowchart is the **first mental step** every KTU student should perform when reading a combination-of-errors question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Measured Result R = f(A, B, C, ...)"] --> q1{"What is the\nmathematical form?"}
    q1 -- "A plus or minus B" --> sumnode["SUM or DIFFERENCE rule"]
    q1 -- "A times or divided by B" --> prodnode["PRODUCT or QUOTIENT rule"]
    q1 -- "A raised to power n" --> pownode["POWER rule"]
    q1 -- "Mixed / general function" --> gennode["GENERAL PARTIAL DERIVATIVE rule"]
    sumnode --> sumwork["Work with ABSOLUTE errors.\nAdd delta A and delta B directly.\nCompute relative error = delta R / R."]
    prodnode --> prodwork["Work with RELATIVE errors.\nAdd epsilon A and epsilon B.\nCompute absolute error = R * epsilon R."]
    pownode --> powwork["Multiply the base relative error by n.\nGeneral case: relative error of R = sum of n_i * epsilon_i."]
    gennode --> genwork["Compute partial derivatives.\nAdd weighted absolute errors.\nCompute relative error = delta R / R."]
    sumwork --> final["Express answer in\nabsolute, relative, or\npercentage form as asked"]
    prodwork --> final
    powwork --> final
    genwork --> final
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Error Flow in a Measurement Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src["Physical Measurand\n(TRUE VALUE)"] --> tr["Transducer /\nSensor"]
    tr -->|"delta T"| sig["Signal Conditioner\nAmplifier / Filter"]
    sig -->|"delta S"| adc["ADC /\nDisplay Unit"]
    adc -->|"delta D"| out["Final Indicated\nValue R_m"]
    out --> comb["Combination of Quantities\nError Propagation Engine"]
    tr -.-> comb
    sig -.-> comb
    adc -.-> comb
    comb --> report["Total Limiting Error\ndelta R and percent epsilon R"]
```

## 4.3 Hierarchy of Combination Rules

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0["Error Combination Master Theorem"] --> A1["Sum / Difference:\ndelta R = delta A + delta B"]
    A0 --> A2["Product / Quotient:\nepsilon R = epsilon A + epsilon B"]
    A0 --> A3["Power:\nepsilon R = n * epsilon A"]
    A0 --> A4["General Function:\ndelta R = sum of abs partial f / partial x * delta x"]
    A1 --> S1["Example: Resistors in series"]
    A1 --> S2["Example: Manometer height subtraction"]
    A2 --> Q1["Example: P = V * I"]
    A2 --> Q2["Example: R = V / I"]
    A3 --> P1["Example: Q = K * sqrt h"]
    A3 --> P2["Example: A = pi * r squared"]
    A4 --> G1["Example: Flow coefficient with\nmultiple cal terms"]
```

## 4.4 Sensitivity Map — Which Input Error Dominates?

For the general case $R = f(A, B, C)$, the **sensitivity coefficient** of $R$ to each input is $\partial f / \partial x_i$. The largest coefficient identifies the **dominant error source** that should be calibrated first.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inpA["Input A with delta A"] -->|"partial f / partial A"| sA["Sensitivity sA"]
    inpB["Input B with delta B"] -->|"partial f / partial B"| sB["Sensitivity sB"]
    inpC["Input C with delta C"] -->|"partial f / partial C"| sC["Sensitivity sC"]
    sA --> sum["delta R = sA delta A + sB delta B + sC delta C"]
    sB --> sum
    sC --> sum
    sum --> max["Identify MAX of s_i delta x_i\nthat dominates total error"]
```
