This is also called the **Zero-Deflection Method**, **Balance Method**, or **Comparison Method** of measurement, and forms the foundational principle of classical laboratory instruments such as the *Wheatstone Bridge*, *Kelvin Double Bridge*, *DC & AC Potentiometers*, and *Servo-Operated Recorders*. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "See-Saw" of Measurement Imagine two children sitting on opposite ends of a perfectly balanced see-saw. Child A (the **unknown**) sits on the left. Child B (the **known reference**) sits on the right. A neutral observer (the **null detector**) only watches whether the see-saw is perfectly horizontal (level). - The observer does **not** need to know how heavy either child is — only whether the plank is level. - When the plank is level, both children are of equal weight. - If the plank tilts, Child B is moved left/right (or weights are added) until balance is restored. - The **position of Child B** at the moment of balance directly tells us Child A's weight. This is exactly how the method of opposing inputs works. The *detector never has to be calibrated for absolute magnitude* — it only has to accurately detect the **absence of difference (null)**. This makes the measurement far more accurate than deflection methods, where the detector must be linear over a wide range. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants > [!NOTE] > - **Sensitivity of the Null Detector:** Only needs to be high near zero, not over the full range → much easier to design. > - **Accuracy Driver:** Determined almost entirely by the **reference standard**, not the detector. > - **Typical Measurement Accuracy:** Ranges from **±0.01% to ±0.001%** of full scale, vastly superior to deflection-type instruments. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown The method of opposing inputs operates on a closed-loop, iterative balance principle. The working sequence is: 1. **Input Application:** The unknown measurand $X_u$ (e.g., an unknown EMF, resistance, or force) is applied to one side of a balancing network. 2. **Opposing Reference Generation:** A known reference quantity $X_r$ is generated internally by the instrument (using a calibrated standard cell, a precision rheostat, a standard resistor, etc.) and applied to the opposite side of the network. 3. **Difference Detection:** A highly sensitive **null detector** (galvanometer, D'Arsonval movement, electronic amplifier with meter, etc.) measures the algebraic difference: $$\Delta X = X_u - X_r$$ 4. **Feedback Action:** If $\Delta X \neq 0$, the detector produces an error signal that drives an actuator (manual knob or servo motor) to adjust $X_r$ until balance is achieved. 5. **Null Reading Indication:** When $\Delta X = 0$, the detector reads zero (null condition). The system is now balanced. 6. **Measurement Readout:** At null condition, $X_u = X_r$. Since $X_r$ is read from a calibrated dial/standard, $X_u$ is known with high precision. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Principle:** At the balance (null) point, **no current flows through the detector** in well-designed opposing-input systems (e.g., a balanced bridge has zero detector current). This eliminates detector-loading errors entirely. ### 2.2 Comparison: Null-Balance vs Deflection Method | Parameter | Null-Balance (Opposing Inputs) | Deflection Method | |---|---|---| | **Principle** | Balance unknown with known | Detect angular/linear displacement | | **Accuracy** | Very high (**±0.01% to ±0.001%**) | Moderate (**±1% to ±5%**) | | **Detector Calibration** | Only near zero required | Required over full range | | **Calibration Stability** | Depends on reference standard | Depends on detector linearity | | **Sensitivity Adjustment** | Easy (only zero-adjust) | Complex (full-range calibration) | | **Speed of Response** | Slower (iterative balance) | Faster (instantaneous) | | **Complexity** | Higher (requires reference + actuator) | Lower (direct readout) | | **Examples** | Potentiometer, Wheatstone Bridge | Ammeter, Voltmeter, Pressure gauge | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All critical equations for **Method of Opposing Inputs** are consolidated below. Use `\vert` for absolute value (per KTU engine rules — no raw `|` inside table cells). | # | Quantity | Formula | Description | Unit | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Null Condition | $X_u = X_r$ | Unknown equals reference at balance | Same as measurand | | 2 | Detector Deflection | $\Delta X = X_u - X_r$ | Net difference at detector | Same as measurand | | 3 | Bridge Balance (Wheatstone) | $\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_4}$ | General balance equation | Dimensionless | | 4 | Unknown Resistance (Wheatstone) | $R_x = R_3 \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}$ | Solved from balance | Ohm ($\Omega$) | | 5 | Potentiometer Balance | $E_x = E_s \cdot \dfrac{L_x}{L_s}$ | EMF by length ratio | Volt (V) | | 6 | Sensitivity (Null Detector) | $S_n = \dfrac{\partial \Delta X}{\partial X_r}\bigg\vert_{X_u \to X_r}$ | Slope of detector at null | Dimensionless | | 7 | Measurement Error | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{X_r^{true} - X_r^{read}}{X_u} \times 100\%$ | % error at balance | Percent (%) | | 8 | Loop Gain (Servo-Null) | $G_{loop} = K_a \cdot K_d \cdot K_m$ | Product of actuator, detector, motor gains | dimensionless | | 9 | Balance Time Constant | $\tau_b \approx \dfrac{1}{G_{loop} \cdot \omega_c}$ | Time to reach null | Second (s) | | 10 | Reference Standard Accuracy | $\sigma_r \ll \sigma_d$ | Reference must be more accurate than detector | — | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The opposing-input principle is the *workhorse* of precision metrology. It is widely used in: - **Calibration Laboratories:** National standards (NPL, NIST) rely on potentiometric null methods to calibrate secondary instruments. - **Weighing Scales:** Precision electronic balances use a *force-balancing* servo where an electromagnetic force opposes the unknown weight. - **Pressure Measurement:** Dead-weight pressure testers balance unknown pressure against known weights on a piston. - **Process Control:** Many smart transmitters and PID controllers internally use a null-balance principle for linearity. - **Aerospace & Defense:** Inertial navigation systems use opposing-torque techniques for gyroscope pickoffs. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: General Balance Equation for Opposing Inputs Let the unknown measurand $X_u$ and the reference $X_r$ both drive a common summing junction. The detector response $D$ is a function of their algebraic difference: $$D = f(X_u - X_r)$$ For an ideal linear detector near the null point, we can linearize using a first-order Taylor expansion about the null $X_r = X_u$: $$D \approx f(0) + \left.\dfrac{df}{d\Delta X}\right\vert_{\Delta X = 0} \cdot (X_u - X_r)$$ Since the detector reads zero at null, $f(0) = 0$, so: $$D = K_d \cdot (X_u - X_r)$$ where $K_d$ is the detector's sensitivity constant. The **null condition** is reached when the detector reads zero: $$D = 0 \implies K_d \cdot (X_u - X_r) = 0$$ Since $K_d \neq 0$: $$X_u = X_r$$ **Conclusion:** At the null point, the unknown is exactly equal to the reference, and the measurement is read directly from the calibrated reference dial. ### 3.2 Derivation: Wheatstone Bridge as a Null-Balance System Consider a Wheatstone bridge with arms $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$. The unknown resistance is placed in arm $R_1 = R_x$. Arms $R_2$ and $R_3$ are known ratio arms, and $R_4$ is a calibrated variable resistor. A galvanometer (null detector) connects between the two midpoints. **Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law to the two parallel paths:** Path 1 (through $R_1$ and $R_2$): $$V_{AB} = I_1 \cdot R_1, \quad V_{BD} = I_1 \cdot R_2$$ Path 2 (through $R_3$ and $R_4$): $$V_{AB} = I_2 \cdot R_3, \quad V_{BD} = I_2 \cdot R_4$$ **Step 2 — Express the voltages at the detector terminals:** The galvanometer measures the potential difference between points B and C: $$V_B = V_A - I_1 \cdot R_1$$ $$V_C = V_A - I_2 \cdot R_3$$ Therefore: $$V_{BC} = V_B - V_C = I_2 \cdot R_3 - I_1 \cdot R_1$$ **Step 3 — Apply the null condition (no current through galvanometer):** If $I_g = 0$, then $I_1 = I$ (flowing through $R_1$ and $R_2$) and $I_2 = I$ (flowing through $R_3$ and $R_4$), and $V_{BC} = 0$: $$I \cdot R_3 - I \cdot R_1 = 0$$ Also, $V_{BD}$ is common: $$I \cdot R_2 = I \cdot R_4$$ **Step 4 — Divide the two balance equations:** $$\dfrac{R_3}{R_2} = \dfrac{R_1}{R_4}$$ Or equivalently: $$R_1 \cdot R_2 = R_3 \cdot R_4 \quad \text{(product of opposite arms equal)}$$ **Step 5 — Solve for the unknown resistance:** $$\boxed{R_x = R_1 = R_3 \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_4}}$$ > [!NOTE] > The accuracy of $R_x$ depends only on the precision of the three known resistors $R_2, R_3, R_4$ — the galvanometer accuracy is irrelevant as long as it can reliably detect zero. This is the *defining strength* of the opposing-input method. ### 3.3 Derivation: DC Potentiometer Null-Balance In a DC potentiometer, an unknown EMF $E_x$ is balanced against a known voltage drop $E_r = I \cdot \rho \cdot L_x$ across a length $L_x$ of a precision slidewire carrying a constant current $I$ (where $\rho$ is the resistance per unit length). **At null balance, the detector (galvanometer) reads zero, meaning:** $$E_x = I \cdot \rho \cdot L_x$$ The current $I$ is standardized against a known reference cell $E_s$: $$E_s = I \cdot \rho \cdot L_s$$ Dividing the two equations: $$\dfrac{E_x}{E_s} = \dfrac{L_x}{L_s}$$ $$\boxed{E_x = E_s \cdot \dfrac{L_x}{L_s}}$$ Thus, the unknown EMF is determined solely by the **ratio of two lengths** (read from a calibrated scale) and the **known reference cell voltage** — both of which are traceable to fundamental standards. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity Analysis Let us analyze how sensitive the null detector reading $D$ is to small errors in the reference $X_r$. Differentiating $D = K_d(X_u - X_r)$: $$\dfrac{\partial D}{\partial X_r} = -K_d$$ If the detector has a minimum resolvable deflection $D_{min}$, the smallest detectable imbalance in $X_r$ is: $$\Delta X_r^{min} = \dfrac{D_{min}}{K_d}$$ This means **higher $K_d$ → finer null detection → better resolution**. This is why null detectors (galvanometers, chopper-stabilized amplifiers) are designed with very high $K_d$. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: Generic Null-Balance Measurement System The following Mermaid block diagram captures the closed-loop architecture of the method of opposing inputs. Note: every node ID is alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to avoid Mermaid reserved-keyword conflicts. ```mermaid flowchart TD A1([Unknown Measurand Xu]) --> SUM1((Summing
Junction)) R1[/Known Reference
Xr, Calibrated/] --> SUM1 SUM1 --> DIF1[Net Difference
deltaX = Xu - Xr] DIF1 --> ND1{{Null Detector
Galvanometer}} ND1 -->|Deflection d| FB1{Is deltaX = 0?} FB1 -->|No| ACT1[Actuator
Manual or Servo] ACT1 -->|Adjust| R1 FB1 -->|Yes Null| RD1([Readout
Xu = Xr]) ND1 -.Feedback path.-> FB1 style A1 fill:#f9c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style R1 fill:#9cf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style ND1 fill:#fc9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style RD1 fill:#9f9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style SUM1 fill:#fff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram: Wheatstone Bridge Null Configuration ```mermaid flowchart LR SRC1([Battery / Excitation Source Vs]) --> TOP1((Node A)) SRC1 --> BOT1((Node D)) TOP1 --> R1A[Rx Unknown] R1A --> MID1((Node B)) MID1 --> R2A[R2 Known] R2A --> BOT1 TOP1 --> R3A[R3 Known] R3A --> MID2((Node C)) MID2 --> R4A[R4 Variable Calibrated] R4A --> BOT1 MID1 --> GAL1{{Galvanometer
Null Detector}} MID2 --> GAL1 GAL1 -->|Zero at null| BR1([Balance Achieved
Rx = R3 x R2 / R4]) style SRC1 fill:#ff9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style GAL1 fill:#fc9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style BR1 fill:#9f9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style R1A fill:#f9c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style R4A fill:#9cf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram: DC Potentiometer Null-Balance Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD BAT1([Battery]) --> REG1[Current Regulator
Constant I] REG1 --> SW1[Standard Cell Switch] SW1 --> SC1([Standard Cell Es]) SW1 --> SW2[Unknown EMF Switch] SW2 --> UX1([Unknown EMF Ex]) REG1 --> SL1[Precision Slidewire
Length L] SL1 --> TAP1((Slider Position)) TAP1 --> DET1{{Galvanometer
Null Detector}} SC1 --> DET1 UX1 --> DET1 DET1 -->|Null achieved| OUT1([Read Lx
Ex = Es x Lx / Ls]) style BAT1 fill:#ff9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style SC1 fill:#f9c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style UX1 fill:#9cf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style DET1 fill:#fc9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style OUT1 fill:#9f9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Force-Balancing Weighing Scale (Industrial Application) ```mermaid flowchart TD LOAD1([Unknown Weight W on Pan]) --> LEV1[Lever Mechanism] LEV1 --> FBK1[Force Feedback Coil
Electromagnetic] FBK1 --> DIF2((Force Comparator)) REF1[/Calibrated Current
Reference Ir/] --> FBK1 DIF2 --> POS1[Position Sensor
LVDT] POS1 --> AMP1[Amplifier] AMP1 --> GEN1[Feedback Current Source] GEN1 -->|Adjusts| FBK1 POS1 -->|Displacement approx 0| DSP1([Digital Readout
Weight = Ir x k]) style LOAD1 fill:#f9c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style REF1 fill:#9cf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style POS1 fill:#fc9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style DSP1 fill:#9f9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *Define the method of opposing inputs in measurement systems. Mention any two instruments that use this principle.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The method of opposing inputs (also called the null-balance or comparison method) is a measurement technique in which the unknown measurand is opposed by a known reference quantity of the same physical nature, and a null detector senses the difference between them. When the detector reads zero, the unknown equals the reference, and the value of the reference is taken as the measurement. Accuracy depends on the reference standard, not the detector. **Two examples:** (1) **Wheatstone Bridge** for resistance measurement, and (2) **DC Potentiometer** for EMF measurement. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for explanation of null principle, 1 mark for two correct examples)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *List any three advantages of the null-balance (opposing inputs) method over the deflection method of measurement.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** 1. **Higher accuracy** — depends on reference standards rather than detector linearity; typical accuracy is ±0.01% or better. 2. **Easier detector design** — the null detector only needs to be sensitive near zero, not over the full measurement range. 3. **Insensitive to detector drift** — since the detector is only used at the zero point, zero-drift errors are easily eliminated by re-zeroing. 4. *(Optional 4th point for enrichment)* Self-calibrating: the null condition inherently validates the reference. *(1 mark per advantage, maximum 3)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of the method of opposing inputs. Discuss the role of the null detector in detail. **\[7 Marks\]** **Model Solution:** The method of opposing inputs is a closed-loop measurement technique based on the principle of *comparison and balance*. As shown in the block diagram, the unknown measurand $X_u$ and a known reference quantity $X_r$ are simultaneously applied to a summing junction from opposite sides. The algebraic difference $\Delta X = X_u - X_r$ is sensed by a **null detector** (typically a galvanometer, D'Arsonval movement, or a high-gain electronic amplifier with an indicating meter). **Role of the Null Detector:** 1. The null detector's *only* function is to indicate whether a difference exists between $X_u$ and $X_r$. 2. It need not be linear over the full range — it only needs very high sensitivity at the zero point. 3. The detector produces an output (deflection or voltage) proportional to $\Delta X$. If $\Delta X \neq 0$, this output drives an actuator (manual knob in manual null instruments, or a servo motor in automatic null instruments) to adjust $X_r$ until balance is achieved. 4. At the balance (null) point, the detector reads zero, implying $X_u = X_r$. The reading on the calibrated reference dial is then taken as the measured value. 5. Because the detector never carries significant current at null (in well-designed bridges), detector-loading errors are eliminated. The accuracy of measurement therefore depends almost entirely on the precision of the **reference standard**, which can be calibrated against national standards. This is why null-balance instruments achieve accuracies of ±0.01% to ±0.001% of full scale — orders of magnitude better than deflection-type instruments. *(Block diagram: 2 marks, principle explanation: 3 marks, null-detector role in detail: 2 marks)* --- **(b)** Derive the balance condition of a **Wheatstone Bridge** and obtain the expression for the unknown resistance. State the assumptions made. **\[7 Marks\]** **Model Solution:** **Assumptions:** 1. The bridge arms $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ are pure, linear, and temperature-independent resistors. 2. The detector (galvanometer) draws no current at the null condition (infinite input impedance). 3. The excitation source (battery) has negligible internal resistance. 4. Stray resistances (lead, contact) are negligible. **Derivation:** Consider a Wheatstone bridge with four arms $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ arranged in a diamond, a galvanometer $G$ connected between opposite vertices B and C, and a battery $V_s$ connected across the other two vertices A and D. Let $R_1 = R_x$ be the unknown. Let $I_1$ be the current through $R_1$ and $R_2$, and $I_2$ be the current through $R_3$ and $R_4$. **Step 1 — Galvanometer current is zero at null:** At balance, no current flows through the galvanometer, hence $I_1 = I_2 = I$ (the same current flows through both parallel branches). **Step 2 — Potential at node B:** Going from A to D through the $R_1$–$R_2$ path: $$V_B = V_A - I \cdot R_1$$ **Step 3 — Potential at node C:** Going from A to D through the $R_3$–$R_4$ path: $$V_C = V_A - I \cdot R_3$$ **Step 4 — Null condition: $V_B = V_C$:** $$V_A - I \cdot R_1 = V_A - I \cdot R_3$$ $$R_1 = R_3$$ **Step 5 — Equating the other half of the bridge:** Since $V_B = V_C$ and the same current flows: $$V_B - V_D = I \cdot R_2, \quad V_C - V_D = I \cdot R_4$$ $$I \cdot R_2 = I \cdot R_4 \implies R_2 = R_4$$ **Step 6 — General balance condition (using ratios):** In the more general case, equating the voltage drops along both parallel branches from A to D: $$\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_4}$$ Equivalently, the product of resistances in opposite arms is equal: $$R_1 \cdot R_4 = R_2 \cdot R_3$$ **Step 7 — Solving for the unknown:** $$\boxed{R_x = R_1 = \dfrac{R_2 \cdot R_3}{R_4}}$$ The unknown resistance is determined solely by the ratio of three precisely known resistors. *(Assumptions: 1 mark, Steps 1–5: 4 marks, Final expression: 1 mark, Interpretation: 1 mark)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **(a)** Explain the working of a **DC Potentiometer** as a null-balance instrument. Derive the expression for the unknown EMF. **\[7 Marks\]** **Model Solution:** A DC potentiometer measures an unknown EMF $E_x$ by opposing it with a precisely known variable voltage drop produced across a section of a calibrated slidewire carrying a constant current. **Construction and Working:** 1. A stable battery drives a constant current $I$ through a precision slidewire of uniform resistance per unit length $\rho$. 2. The slidewire is provided with a movable slider that taps off a length $L_x$. The voltage between the slider and one end of the slidewire is $V_{ref} = I \cdot \rho \cdot L_x$. 3. The unknown EMF $E_x$ is connected in **opposition** to $V_{ref}$ through a galvanometer (null detector). 4. The slider is moved until the galvanometer reads exactly zero. At this point: $$E_x = I \cdot \rho \cdot L_x$$ 5. To make $I$ precise, the current is first **standardized** using a standard reference cell $E_s$ (Weston cell) tapped at a fixed length $L_s$: $$E_s = I \cdot \rho \cdot L_s$$ 6. Combining the two equations by eliminating $I \cdot \rho$: $$\boxed{E_x = E_s \cdot \dfrac{L_x}{L_s}}$$ The unknown EMF is thus the ratio of two lengths (read on a calibrated scale) multiplied by the standard cell voltage — both of which are traceable to fundamental standards. The potentiometer is highly accurate (typically ±0.01% or better) because the detector only needs to indicate zero. *(Construction: 2 marks, working: 3 marks, derivation: 2 marks)* --- **(b)** Compare the null-balance (opposing inputs) method with the deflection method of measurement in terms of accuracy, detector design, speed of response, and applications. Give two examples of each. **\[7 Marks\]** **Model Solution:** | Parameter | Null-Balance (Opposing Inputs) | Deflection Method | |---|---|---| | **Working Principle** | Compares unknown with a known reference; reads at null | Measures the displacement/deflection caused by the unknown | | **Accuracy** | Very high (typically **±0.01% to ±0.001%**) | Moderate (typically **±1% to ±5%**) | | **Detector Design** | Only sensitive near zero; calibration near null is enough | Must be linear and calibrated over the entire range | | **Detector Loading Error** | Negligible (no current at null) | Significant — depends on detector impedance | | **Speed of Response** | Slower — requires iterative balance | Faster — instantaneous deflection | | **Sensitivity to External Disturbance** | Low (null condition is stable) | Higher (deflection can be perturbed) | | **Examples** | (i) Wheatstone Bridge, (ii) DC Potentiometer | (i) PMMC Ammeter, (ii) Bourdon-tube pressure gauge | | **Typical Applications** | Laboratory standards, calibration, precision metrology | Field instruments, panel meters, process indicators | **Examples — Null-Balance:** 1. **Wheatstone Bridge:** Used to measure medium-range resistances (1 Ω to 1 MΩ) in laboratory and industrial settings. 2. **Kelvin Double Bridge:** Used for very low resistances (< 1 Ω), such as shunt resistors and winding resistances. 3. **DC Potentiometer:** Used to calibrate voltmeters and to measure small EMFs and thermocouple outputs. **Examples — Deflection Method:** 1. **PMMC Ammeter/Voltmeter:** Used widely in switchboards and field measurements. 2. **Bourdon-Tube Pressure Gauge:** Used in industrial process plants for direct pressure indication. 3. **Moving-Iron Frequency Meter:** Used in power systems for frequency monitoring. *(Tabular comparison: 4 marks, examples of each: 3 marks)* --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:** > 1. **Confusing "null" with "zero input":** Students often write "the detector reads zero" without explaining that this implies $X_u = X_r$. Always state the equality condition explicitly. > 2. **Forgetting the assumptions in Wheatstone bridge derivations:** Examiners expect 2–3 explicit assumptions (no galvanometer current at null, no stray resistance, ideal source). Skipping these costs at least 1 mark. > 3. **Mixing up formula forms:** In Wheatstone bridge, the standard KTU expected form is $R_x = R_2 \cdot R_3 / R_4$ (or equivalent). Always label the arms clearly in a diagram. > 4. **Not stating accuracy values:** When comparing null vs deflection methods, examiners reward specific numerical accuracy ranges (±0.01% vs ±1–5%). Vague answers lose marks. > 5. **Omitting the block diagram:** A block diagram is *mandatory* for any 7-mark question on opposing inputs. Always include one — even a hand-drawn one. > 6. **Writing the final formula without derivation:** KTU examiners expect at least 4–5 derivation steps for full marks in 7-mark problems. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Method of Opposing Inputs** = Null-Balance / Comparison / Zero-Deflection method. - [x] The unknown measurand $X_u$ is opposed by a known reference $X_r$; at null, $X_u = X_r$. - [x] **Accuracy** is governed by the **reference standard**, NOT the detector. - [x] Typical accuracy range: **±0.01% to ±0.001%** of full scale. - [x] Detector must only be sensitive **near zero**, not over the full range. - [x] **Wheatstone Bridge balance:** $R_1 \cdot R_4 = R_2 \cdot R_3$ → $R_x = R_2 R_3 / R_4$. - [x] **DC Potentiometer balance:** $E_x = E_s \cdot (L_x / L_s)$. - [x] Advantages: high accuracy, no detector loading at null, self-checking. - [x] Disadvantages: slower response, more complex construction, requires a stable reference. - [x] Applications: laboratory standards, calibration, precision weighing, dead-weight pressure testers, servo recorders. - [x] Always include a **block diagram** in KTU answers for full marks on 7-mark questions. - [x] Always state **assumptions** explicitly in derivations. - [x] Always state **specific accuracy figures** (with % units) when comparing methods. ---

topics.title: Calibration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT205 - BASIC OF INSTRUMENTATION ENGINEERING (INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Calibration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Calibration** is the systematic process of comparing an instrument's output (or response) against a **standard reference instrument** of known and traceable accuracy under specified and controlled conditions, followed by the adjustment (if necessary) of the instrument to bring its output into acceptable agreement with the reference standard.

Mathematically, calibration establishes the functional relationship:

$$y = f(x)$$

where:
- $x$ = the input quantity (known reference stimulus)
- $y$ = the output reading of the instrument under test (IUT/DUT)
- $f(x)$ = the calibration function (ideally linear, but practically characterized by curve-fitting)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition:** As per KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4, *"Calibration is the experimental determination of the relationship between the quantity being measured and the output indication of the measuring instrument, by direct comparison with a reference standard, ensuring measurement traceability to national/international standards."*

### 1.2 Sub-Definitions Critical for KTU Board Exams

- **Primary Standard:** The highest-accuracy reference instrument maintained by national metrology institutes (e.g., NPL, NIST, NABL-accredited labs). **Absolute accuracy** is its defining property.
- **Secondary Standard:** Calibrated directly against a primary standard; used to calibrate working/field instruments.
- **Working Standard / Industrial Standard:** Used in workshops/labs for daily calibration of production instruments.
- **Reference Standard (DUT/IUT):** The Device Under Test (DUT) or Instrument Under Test (IUT) whose accuracy is to be verified.
- **Traceability:** An unbroken chain of comparisons leading from the DUT up to a national/international primary standard.

> [!NOTE]
> **Difference between Calibration and Adjustment:**
> - **Calibration** is a *comparison* process — it quantifies error.
> - **Adjustment** is a *correction* process — it minimizes error.
> Calibration is always performed **before** adjustment, and adjustment is **not mandatory** after every calibration (only if readings fall outside tolerance).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

**Real-World Analogy — The Bathroom Weighing Scale:**

Imagine you step on your bathroom scale and it reads **70 kg**. But you recently visited the doctor's clinic where their certified scale read **68 kg** for the same instant. Your home scale is *inaccurate*. So you take a few standard weights from a gym (5 kg, 10 kg, 20 kg, 50 kg) and place them on the scale one by one. You record what the scale shows versus the actual weight:

| Actual Weight (kg) | Scale Reading (kg) |
|---|---|
| 5 | 5.5 |
| 10 | 10.4 |
| 20 | 20.6 |
| 50 | 50.8 |

This **comparison process** is **calibration**. You now know your scale has a roughly **+0.6 kg offset error**. The plotting of these points and drawing a best-fit line gives the **calibration curve**. If you tighten the internal spring to bring the readings closer to true values, you are performing **adjustment**.

**The doctor's certified scale plays the role of the *Reference Standard***, while your home scale is the **DUT**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this matter for an engineer?** In a chemical plant, if a pressure transmitter reads 5.0 bar when the actual is 5.8 bar, the difference can cause a relief valve to either open prematurely or fail to open during an overpressure scenario — a serious safety hazard. Calibration prevents such dangerous mismatches.

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Bold Constants & Standard Metrics)

- **Calibration Accuracy:** The closeness of the agreement between the measured value and the true value of the measurand.
- **Tolerance / Permissible Error:** The maximum allowable deviation of the instrument reading from the true value, **usually expressed as a percentage of full-scale deflection (% FSD) or percentage of reading (% Rdg)**.
- **Standard Reference Conditions:** Temperature of **20 °C** (or 25 °C), relative humidity of **65 %**, atmospheric pressure of **101.325 kPa**, and supply voltage at **nominal ±1 %** (per **ISO 11843** and **ISA RP 7.7**).
- **NABL:** **National Accreditation Board for Testing and Calibration Laboratories** — the Indian accreditation body equivalent to NIST (USA) or NPL (UK).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Breakdown of the Calibration Process

The calibration of any analog or digital instrument follows a structured protocol. Below is the step-by-step operational logic:

**Step 1 — Preparation & Documentation Review**
- Verify the instrument's make, model, range, resolution, and previous calibration certificate.
- Confirm that the reference standard is **valid (not expired)** and carries a **traceable NABL/NIST certificate**.
- Ensure environmental conditions are within standard reference conditions (Temperature = **20 ± 2 °C**, RH = **45–75 %**).

**Step 2 — Visual & Functional Inspection**
- Check for physical damage, loose connections, leaks, dirty contacts, and battery health.
- Power on the instrument and allow a **warm-up period** (typically 15–30 minutes) to stabilize thermal drift.

**Step 3 — Zero, Span, and Linearity Adjustment (Pre-Calibration)**
- Apply **zero input** → set the instrument to read **exactly zero**.
- Apply **full-scale input** (e.g., 100 % of range) → adjust the span (gain) potentiometer until it reads the correct full-scale value.
- *Why?* This pre-adjustment ensures that subsequent error measurements reflect true instrument behavior, not gross misalignment.

**Step 4 — Ascent Calibration (Up-Scale)**
- Apply input in **ascending order** in at least **5–10 equal steps** (e.g., 0, 10, 20, …, 100 % of full scale).
- Record the indicated reading at each step.
- *Why?* This captures the **systematic positive-error component** and **non-linearity in the rising direction**.

**Step 5 — Descent Calibration (Down-Scale)**
- Apply the same inputs in **descending order**.
- Record the indicated reading at each step.
- *Why?* This reveals **hysteresis** (the difference between up-scale and down-scale readings at the same input), caused by friction, backlash, or magnetic hysteresis.

**Step 6 — Repeat the Cycle (Repeatability Test)**
- Perform the ascent–descent cycle **at least 3 times** to obtain multiple readings at each test point.
- *Why?* To compute **repeatability** (closeness of successive readings under identical conditions by the same operator) and to separate random noise from systematic error.

**Step 7 — Data Analysis & Error Computation**
- Calculate **absolute error**, **relative error**, **percentage error**, **hysteresis error**, **repeatability**, and **non-linearity** at each calibration point.

**Step 8 — Compliance Decision & Calibration Certificate**
- If all errors are within tolerance → **PASS** → Issue a calibration certificate with traceability chain.
- If errors exceed tolerance → **FAIL** → Either adjust, repair, or downgrade the instrument class.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently ask for the *sequence* of calibration steps. Memorize the order: **Inspection → Zero → Span → Ascent → Descent → Repeat → Calculate → Certify**.

### 2.2 Types of Calibration (High-Yield Classification)

| **Type** | **Description** | **KTU Real-World Example** |
|---|---|---|
| **Static Calibration** | Input is held constant (or quasi-static) at each test point. | Calibrating a Bourdon pressure gauge using a dead-weight tester. |
| **Dynamic Calibration** | Input is varied in time (step, ramp, sinusoidal) to characterize dynamic response. | Calibrating a thermocouple response time in a temperature step bath. |
| **Primary Calibration** | Calibration performed using primary standards (no comparison with another instrument of the same kind). | Calibration of a voltmeter using Josephson junction array. |
| **Secondary Calibration** | Calibration performed by comparing the DUT with a secondary standard. | Calibration of a pressure gauge with a precision dial gauge. |
| **In-Situ Calibration** | Calibration performed *in place* without removing the instrument from service. | Three-valve manifold calibration of a differential pressure transmitter. |
| **Bench Calibration** | Calibration performed in a controlled lab environment after removing the instrument. | Pressure gauge calibration in a metrology lab. |
| **Loop Calibration** | End-to-end calibration of the entire measurement loop (sensor → transmitter → indicator → controller). | Calibrating a temperature control loop including sensor, transmitter, PLC input, and PID controller. |
| **Dry Calibration** | Calibration using electrical simulation of the sensor (e.g., RTD simulator for a temperature transmitter). | Decade resistance box used in place of an actual RTD. |

### 2.3 Calibration Standards & Traceability Chain

The traceability hierarchy follows a strict pyramid:

```
                ┌──────────────────┐
                │   SI Units       │  (Bureau International des Poids et Mesures, BIPM)
                │   (Primary Std)  │
                └────────┬─────────┘
                         │
                ┌────────▼─────────┐
                │   National Std   │  (NPL India, NIST USA, PTB Germany)
                │   (NABL)         │
                └────────┬─────────┘
                         │
                ┌────────▼─────────┐
                │  Secondary Std   │  (Reference instruments in calibration labs)
                │  (Working Std)   │
                └────────┬─────────┘
                         │
                ┌────────▼─────────┐
                │  Field/Working   │  (Industrial instruments, shop-floor tools)
                │  Instrument (DUT)│
                └──────────────────┘
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Traceability Matters:** If the reference standard itself is not traceable to a national standard, the calibration certificate has no legal/technical validity. KTU board questions often test this concept directly.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Absolute Error | $E_a = A_m - A_t$ | Same as measurand | $A_m$ = measured, $A_t$ = true value |
| Relative Error | $E_r = \dfrac{E_a}{A_t} = \dfrac{A_m - A_t}{A_t}$ | Dimensionless | Often expressed as a fraction |
| Percentage Error (% Error) | $\%E = \dfrac{E_a}{A_t} \times 100$ | % | Most common KTU form |
| Percentage Full-Scale Error | $\%E_{FS} = \dfrac{E_a}{A_{FS}} \times 100$ | % | Used when range-based tolerance is given |
| Hysteresis Error | $E_h = \vert A_{up} - A_{down} \vert$ at same input | Same as measurand | Measured at each test point |
| Maximum Hysteresis | $E_{h,\max} = \max \left[ \vert A_{up,i} - A_{down,i} \vert \right]$ | Same as measurand | Single value reported |
| Repeatability (Standard Deviation) | $s = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(A_i - \bar{A})^2}$ | Same as measurand | $n$ = number of repetitions |
| Linearity (Best Straight Line) | $L = \dfrac{\max \vert y_i - y_{BSL,i} \vert}{y_{FS}} \times 100$ | % FSD | Independent variable method |
| Sensitivity (Static) | $K = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ | Output / Input | Slope of calibration curve |
| Resolution | $R = \dfrac{\text{FS range}}{2^{n}-1}$ | Same as measurand | $n$ = number of ADC bits |
| Total Static Error | $E_{total} = \sqrt{E_{cal}^2 + E_{hys}^2 + E_{rep}^2 + E_{zero}^2 + E_{span}^2}$ | Same as measurand | Root-Sum-Square (RSS) method |
| Calibration Curve Fit (Linear) | $y = a \cdot x + b$ | — | $a$ = slope, $b$ = intercept |
| Least-Squares Slope | $a = \dfrac{n \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}$ | — | Best linear fit |
| Least-Squares Intercept | $b = \dfrac{\sum y_i - a \sum x_i}{n}$ | — | Best linear fit |
| Calibration Interval Estimate | $T_{new} = T_{old} \times \dfrac{\vert E_{max,old} \vert}{\vert E_{tol} \vert}$ | days/months | Used for re-calibration scheduling |
| Uncertainty (Combined) | $u_c = \sqrt{u_1^2 + u_2^2 + \ldots + u_n^2}$ | Same as measurand | ISO GUM method |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The vertical pipe symbol `|` has been replaced with `\vert` and `\mid` inside the table to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax — this is the **KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 LaTeX Isolation Rule**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Calibration is the **backbone of quality management systems** in:

- **Pharmaceutical industry** — FDA 21 CFR Part 11 and WHO-GMP require traceable calibration of all analytical and process instruments.
- **Oil & Gas (KTU Kerala Context — BPCL Kochi Refinery, Cochin Shipyard):** Calibrating custody-transfer flow meters, pressure safety valves, and tank gauging systems.
- **Power plants (NTPC Kayamkulam, KSEB):** Calibrating turbine governing pressure transmitters and boiler drum level sensors.
- **Automotive (Tata, TVS):** Calibrating engine dynamometers, exhaust gas analyzers, and crash-test sensors.
- **Aviation (Cochin International Airport, DRDO):** Calibrating altimeters, air data computers, and navigation aids.
- **Medical Devices (SCTIMST Trivandrum):** Calibrating ECG, EEG, blood pressure monitors, and infusion pumps to **ISO 13485** standards.

### 2.6 Calibration Standards Governing KTU Context

| **Standard** | **Scope** |
|---|---|
| **ISO/IEC 17025** | General requirements for the competence of testing and calibration laboratories. |
| **ISO 9001:2015** | Quality management — requires control of monitoring and measuring equipment (Clause 7.1.5). |
| **ISO 10012:2003** | Measurement management systems — guidance for calibration and measurement processes. |
| **NABL 100** | Specific criteria for calibration of electrical, mechanical, thermal, and dimensional quantities in India. |
| **ANSI/NCSL Z540.3** | U.S. standard for calibration requirements. |
| **JCGM 100:2008 (GUM)** | Guide to the Expression of Uncertainty in Measurement — the basis of uncertainty calculation. |
| **ISA RP 7.7** | Recommended practice for instrument air quality (used during calibration of pneumatic instruments). |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Linear Calibration Curve Using Least-Squares Method

**Statement:** Given a set of $n$ calibration data points $(x_i, y_i)$, derive the best-fit straight line $y = a x + b$ that minimizes the sum of squared residuals.

**Given:**
- $n$ input values $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ (reference inputs)
- $n$ corresponding output readings $y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_n$ (instrument readings)
- We want to find $a$ (slope) and $b$ (intercept) such that the sum $S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - a x_i - b)^2$ is minimum.

**Step 1:** Define the objective function (sum of squared errors):

$$S(a, b) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - a x_i - b)^2$$

**Step 2:** To minimize $S$, take partial derivatives with respect to $a$ and $b$ and set them to zero.

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial a} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i (y_i - a x_i - b) = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial b} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - a x_i - b) = 0$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the first equation (divide by $-2$ and expand):

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i y_i - a \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2 - b \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i = 0$$

**Step 4:** Simplify the second equation (divide by $-2$ and expand):

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i - a \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i - n b = 0$$

**Step 5:** Solve the second equation for $b$:

$$b = \frac{1}{n} \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i - a \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \right)$$

**Step 6:** Substitute $b$ into the first equation and solve for $a$. After algebraic manipulation:

$$a = \frac{n \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i y_i - \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i}{n \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2 - \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i\right)^2}$$

**Step 7:** Once $a$ is known, substitute back into the expression for $b$:

$$b = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i - a \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}{n}$$

**Interpretation:** The slope $a$ represents the **static sensitivity** $K$ of the instrument. The intercept $b$ represents the **zero offset error**. The residuals $(y_i - a x_i - b)$ are the **non-linearity deviations** at each point.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Total Static Error (Root-Sum-Square Combination)

**Statement:** Show that the total static error of an instrument, considering independent error sources, is the root-sum-square of individual errors.

**Given:**
- Calibration error: $E_{cal}$
- Hysteresis error: $E_{hys}$
- Repeatability error: $E_{rep}$
- Zero drift: $E_{zero}$
- Span drift: $E_{span}$

These errors are **independent and uncorrelated** (a key assumption in error combination).

**Step 1:** Define the total error as a function of individual errors. Since errors can have random signs (positive or negative), the worst-case (absolute) sum is:

$$E_{total,worst} = \vert E_{cal} \vert + \vert E_{hys} \vert + \vert E_{rep} \vert + \vert E_{zero} \vert + \vert E_{span} \vert$$

**Step 2:** This worst-case method is overly pessimistic. For independent random errors, a more realistic estimate uses the **Root-Sum-Square (RSS)** method, derived from the principle that the variance of the sum of independent random variables is the sum of their variances.

Let each error $E_i$ have variance $\sigma_i^2$. The total error variance is:

$$\sigma_{total}^2 = \sigma_{cal}^2 + \sigma_{hys}^2 + \sigma_{rep}^2 + \sigma_{zero}^2 + \sigma_{span}^2$$

**Step 3:** Since the standard deviation $\sigma_i$ of each error is approximated by its magnitude (assuming uniform distribution over the tolerance band), the total error is:

$$E_{total,RSS} = \sqrt{E_{cal}^2 + E_{hys}^2 + E_{rep}^2 + E_{zero}^2 + E_{span}^2}$$

**Step 4:** The RSS value is always less than the worst-case sum but greater than the largest individual error. For a 5-source error combination, if all errors were equal in magnitude $E_0$:

$$E_{total,RSS} = \sqrt{5} \cdot E_0 \approx 2.236 \, E_0$$

versus the worst-case:

$$E_{total,worst} = 5 \, E_0$$

The RSS method is preferred in **ISO GUM** for uncertainty estimation.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Hysteresis Error from Up-Scale and Down-Scale Readings

**Statement:** Given up-scale and down-scale readings at the same input, derive the hysteresis error.

**Given:** A series of calibration data points measured in both ascending ($y_{up,i}$) and descending ($y_{down,i}$) directions at the same input $x_i$.

**Step 1:** At each input $x_i$, compute the absolute difference between up-scale and down-scale readings:

$$\Delta H_i = \vert y_{up,i} - y_{down,i} \vert$$

**Step 2:** The hysteresis error at input $x_i$ is $\Delta H_i$. Express as percentage of full scale:

$$\%H_i = \frac{\Delta H_i}{y_{FS}} \times 100$$

**Step 3:** The maximum hysteresis across all calibration points is:

$$\%H_{max} = \frac{\max(\Delta H_i)}{y_{FS}} \times 100 = \frac{\max \vert y_{up,i} - y_{down,i} \vert}{y_{FS}} \times 100$$

**Step 4:** If hysteresis is plotted as a function of input, the **average hysteresis** is:

$$\%H_{avg} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\vert y_{up,i} - y_{down,i} \vert}{y_{FS}} \times 100$$

**Physical Interpretation:** Hysteresis in a Bourdon tube pressure gauge arises from internal friction between the beryllium-copper tube and its linkage. In a magnetic instrument, it arises from the coercivity of the iron core. In a capacitive sensor, it arises from the stiction of the movable plate.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A pressure gauge with a full-scale range of **0–10 bar** is calibrated against a dead-weight tester. The following readings are obtained during the up-scale test:

| Input (bar) | Reading (bar) |
|---|---|
| 0.0 | 0.05 |
| 2.0 | 2.10 |
| 4.0 | 3.95 |
| 6.0 | 6.08 |
| 8.0 | 7.92 |
| 10.0 | 10.05 |

**Calculate:** (a) The calibration curve (linear fit) using the least-squares method. (b) The maximum non-linearity. (c) The sensitivity of the gauge. (d) The static zero error.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Set up the data table with sums required for the least-squares fit.

| $i$ | $x_i$ (bar) | $y_i$ (bar) | $x_i^2$ | $x_i y_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.0 | 0.05 | 0.00 | 0.00 |
| 2 | 2.0 | 2.10 | 4.00 | 4.20 |
| 3 | 4.0 | 3.95 | 16.00 | 15.80 |
| 4 | 6.0 | 6.08 | 36.00 | 36.48 |
| 5 | 8.0 | 7.92 | 64.00 | 63.36 |
| 6 | 10.0 | 10.05 | 100.00 | 100.50 |
| **Sum** | **30.0** | **30.15** | **220.00** | **220.34** |

**Step 2:** Calculate the slope $a$ (sensitivity $K$):

$$a = \frac{n \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n \sum x_i^2 - \left( \sum x_i \right)^2}$$

$$a = \frac{6 \times 220.34 - 30.0 \times 30.15}{6 \times 220.00 - (30.0)^2}$$

$$a = \frac{1322.04 - 904.50}{1320.00 - 900.00} = \frac{417.54}{420.00} = 0.9941 \text{ bar/bar}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the intercept $b$ (zero error):

$$b = \frac{\sum y_i - a \sum x_i}{n} = \frac{30.15 - 0.9941 \times 30.0}{6} = \frac{30.15 - 29.823}{6} = \frac{0.327}{6} = 0.0545 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 4:** The calibration curve is:

$$y = 0.9941 \, x + 0.0545 \text{ bar}$$

**(a) Calibration curve:** $y = 0.9941x + 0.0545$ bar

**(c) Sensitivity $K$:** $K = a = 0.9941$ bar/bar (i.e., the gauge reads **0.9941 bar** for every **1 bar** applied — about **0.59 % low**).

**(d) Static zero error:** $b = +0.0545$ bar (the gauge reads **0.0545 bar** when the input is zero).

**Step 5:** Calculate residuals (non-linearity) at each point:

| $x_i$ | $y_i$ (measured) | $y_{fit} = 0.9941x_i + 0.0545$ | Residual $r_i = y_i - y_{fit}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.0 | 0.05 | 0.0545 | $-0.0045$ |
| 2.0 | 2.10 | 2.0427 | $+0.0573$ |
| 4.0 | 3.95 | 4.0309 | $-0.0809$ |
| 6.0 | 6.08 | 6.0191 | $+0.0609$ |
| 8.0 | 7.92 | 8.0073 | $-0.0873$ |
| 10.0 | 10.05 | 9.9955 | $+0.0545$ |

**(b) Maximum non-linearity:** $\max \vert r_i \vert = 0.0873$ bar at $x = 8$ bar.

**Step 6:** Express as percentage of full scale:

$$\%NL_{max} = \frac{0.0873}{10.0} \times 100 = 0.873 \text{ % FSD}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The gauge has a zero offset of +0.0545 bar and a non-linearity of 0.873 % FSD. If the acceptance tolerance is ±1 % FSD, the gauge **PASSES** calibration and only requires zero adjustment (not a complete overhaul).
